AI Foundations

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: AI is like giving software a trained assistant instead of only a rulebook. A normal program follows exact instructions; an AI system learns patterns from examples and makes a best-effort prediction. New learners need this distinction first: AI is useful when rules are hard to write but examples are available.

What is AI?

Artificial Intelligence is software that learns patterns from data and uses those patterns to make decisions, rather than following a fixed set of hand-written rules.

Why this matters

AI matters for product engineers because it unlocks capabilities that are impossible to program explicitly: understanding natural language, recognising images, generating coherent text, and predicting outcomes from messy data.

In plain terms

Traditional software is like a recipe: you write every step. AI is like hiring someone who has cooked a thousand meals and can adapt to whatever ingredients are in front of them.

For non-technical readers

If you're not technical, the one distinction worth keeping is this: normal software does exactly what it's told, every time; AI software makes an educated guess based on examples it has seen before, and can be wrong in ways a traditional program never would be. That's not a flaw to fear, it's the trade you're making for handling tasks too fuzzy or too varied to write exact rules for, like reading a customer's email or judging whether a photo is appropriate. When evaluating any 'AI-powered' pitch, the useful question is what examples the system learned from, not whether it sounds impressive in a demo.

How it works

The internal flow of any AI system is: input → feature representation → model prediction → post-processing → output. Understanding each handoff helps you diagnose failures.

  • Rule-based systems break when inputs fall outside the rules. AI systems generalise from examples to handle inputs they have never seen exactly before.
  • AI learns a function that maps inputs to outputs. The function is discovered from data, not programmed by hand.
  • There are three broad paradigms: supervised learning (labelled examples), unsupervised learning (finding structure in unlabelled data), and reinforcement learning (learning from reward signals).
  • Modern AI products are not magic — they are statistical pattern matchers. Understanding that makes debugging, evaluation, and trust much easier.
  • AI is most useful when: rules are too complex to write, enough labelled examples exist, and the task has a measurable definition of success.

A concrete example

A traditional spam filter uses rules: 'block emails with the word FREE in capitals'. An AI spam filter learns from millions of labelled spam and non-spam emails, discovering hundreds of subtle signals — sender reputation, link patterns, writing style — without any rule being explicitly written.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Key architectural trade-off: rule-based systems are predictable and auditable but brittle. Learned systems are flexible but require data, evaluation, and monitoring to stay reliable.

Hands-on lab

Task: Pick any repetitive decision in a product you know — categorising support tickets, flagging reviews, routing requests. Write down: what the input is, what the output should be, what 'correct' means, and what bad data would look like.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production AI feature needs: a clear definition of success, a labelled evaluation set, monitoring for distribution shift, and a fallback for low-confidence predictions.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • AI does not 'understand' in the human sense. It finds statistical patterns. This is why it can be confidently wrong.
  • More data is not always better. Low-quality, biased, or mislabelled data makes models worse.
  • AI is not a replacement for product thinking. You still need to define the problem, the users, and the success criteria.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about AI Foundations?

  • Once AI Foundations is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: AI does not 'understand' in the human sense. It finds statistical patterns. This is why it can be confidently wrong.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing AI Foundations into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: A production AI feature needs: a clear definition of success, a labelled evaluation set, monitoring for distribution shift, and a fallback for low-confidence predictions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain AI Foundations in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

AI product thinking

AI product thinking is the discipline of designing a product around what an AI system can reliably do and where it will fail, rather than treating AI as a magic feature that can be bolted onto any existing workflow.

In plain terms

It is like designing a car around an engine's actual torque curve and fuel efficiency, not an idealized engine that doesn't exist.

For non-technical readers

If you're not technical, think of this as the difference between buying a tool and designing a job around a new hire. A traditional software feature always does exactly what it's told; an AI feature behaves more like a capable but occasionally wrong employee. Good AI product thinking means designing the surrounding process, checks, human sign-off, clear escalation, so the business doesn't inherit the AI's mistakes directly. When someone pitches you an AI feature, the first question worth asking isn't how accurate is the model, it's what happens to my business when it's wrong, and who catches that.

How it works

In practice it comes down to starting from the user's actual job to be done, identifying which parts of that job are well suited to probabilistic AI versus deterministic logic, and designing the product's UX, guardrails, and fallback behavior around the AI's realistic failure modes rather than assuming it will always work.

A concrete example

A resume-screening product built with AI product thinking doesn't let the model auto-reject candidates; it uses AI to rank and summarize, with a human making the final call, because the team recognized screening errors have real consequences a purely automated flow would hide.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Designing conservatively around AI's failure modes produces a more trustworthy product, but it can feel slower and less magical than a product that oversells full automation, so the trade-off is credibility and safety versus a flashier initial pitch

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real or hypothetical product idea, identify one part of the user's job that's well suited to AI and one part that should stay deterministic or human-led, and explain why.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • AI product thinking is not the same as adding a chatbot to an existing product; it means redesigning the actual workflow around what the AI can and cannot reliably do, which often changes the UX significantly from a traditional deterministic feature.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about AI product thinking?

  • Correct answer: AI product thinking is not the same as adding a chatbot to an existing product; it means redesigning the actual workflow around what the AI can and cannot reliably do, which often changes the UX significantly from a traditional deterministic feature.
  • Once AI product thinking is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain AI product thinking in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

First principles

First principles thinking about AI means reasoning from the basic mechanics of how these systems actually work, learning statistical patterns from data, rather than from marketing claims or assumptions borrowed from science fiction.

In plain terms

It is like understanding how an engine actually converts fuel into motion, instead of just knowing that pressing the gas pedal makes the car go.

For non-technical readers

For a non-technical reader, the practical version of first-principles thinking is a habit: before believing an AI vendor's pitch, ask what the tool actually learned from and what happens with an example the salesperson didn't show you. AI vendors are often selling a demo, not a guarantee, and the gap between the two is exactly where budget gets wasted. This one habit, asking what's it actually doing under the hood, in plain terms, before signing a contract, prevents most of the expensive AI-adoption mistakes companies make.

How it works

In practice it comes down to breaking down a claim about an AI system into its actual mechanism, what data it learned from, what pattern it's matching, what it cannot know, and evaluating capability claims against that mechanism rather than against how confident or fluent the system sounds.

A concrete example

When a vendor claims their AI understands customer intent, first-principles thinking asks what data the model was trained on, what patterns it's actually matching, and what happens when a real customer's phrasing falls outside those patterns.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Reasoning from first principles takes more effort than accepting a vendor's capability claims at face value, but it's the only reliable way to predict how a system will behave on your specific, real-world cases rather than the vendor's demo cases

Hands-on lab

Task: Take one AI capability claim you've encountered, whether in marketing or a coworker's assumption, and break it down into what the system is actually doing mechanically, and what that mechanism implies it can't reliably do.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A system's confident, fluent output is not evidence that it understands or reasons the way marketing materials often imply; first-principles thinking specifically resists inferring capability from confidence or fluency alone.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about First principles?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once First principles is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A system's confident, fluent output is not evidence that it understands or reasons the way marketing materials often imply; first-principles thinking specifically resists inferring capability from confidence or fluency alone.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain First principles in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

AI system lifecycle

The AI system lifecycle is the full sequence of stages an AI feature moves through, problem framing, data collection, model selection, evaluation, deployment, and ongoing monitoring, each with its own risks and required checks, distinct from a traditional software lifecycle's simpler build-test-ship cycle.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between manufacturing a fixed product, built once and shipped, versus raising a working animal that needs ongoing training, feeding, and monitoring even after its initial training is complete.

How it works

In practice it comes down to moving through problem framing and success criteria, data collection and preparation, model selection and training or configuration, evaluation against representative cases, deployment with monitoring in place, and continuous monitoring for drift or degradation after launch, with each stage's output feeding the next.

A concrete example

A team building a support-ticket classifier moves from defining what counts as correct classification, to collecting labeled examples, to selecting and evaluating a model, to deploying it with a dashboard, to monitoring for drift as new ticket types appear over time.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Treating every stage of the lifecycle with real rigor takes longer than rushing straight to deployment, but skipping stages like evaluation or monitoring setup means problems surface in production instead of being caught earlier and more cheaply

Try it in code

const lifecycle = [
  "problem framing + success criteria",
  "data collection",
  "model selection + training",
  "evaluation",
  "deployment + monitoring setup",
  "ongoing monitoring for drift"
];
// The lifecycle doesn't end at deployment — the last stage never stops.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real AI feature idea, sketch its lifecycle stages from problem framing through ongoing monitoring, and identify the one stage most likely to be skipped under time pressure.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • An AI system's lifecycle does not end at deployment the way a typical one-time software release might feel like it does; ongoing monitoring for data drift and model degradation is a required, continuing stage, not an optional add-on.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about AI system lifecycle?

  • Once AI system lifecycle is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: An AI system's lifecycle does not end at deployment the way a typical one-time software release might feel like it does; ongoing monitoring for data drift and model degradation is a required, continuing stage, not an optional add-on.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain AI system lifecycle in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework

Data and signals

Data and signals refers to the raw information an AI system learns from or acts on, and the specific measurable indicators extracted from that data that the system actually uses to make decisions.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between a company's raw sales records and the specific dashboard metrics, like conversion rate or churn, that leadership actually watches to make decisions; the metrics are a distilled signal extracted from the raw data.

For non-technical readers

In plain terms: an AI system can only be as smart as the information it's actually shown, no more. If you're evaluating an AI proposal, the most useful non-technical question is what data would this actually learn from, and do we really have that data, in good shape, today. A shockingly common reason AI projects stall isn't the model, it's discovering six months in that the data everyone assumed existed was incomplete, inconsistent, or locked in a system nobody accounted for.

How it works

In practice it comes down to identifying what raw data is available, what signals can be reliably extracted from it, and how representative and clean that data is, since a model's behavior is fundamentally shaped by the data and signals it was trained or operated on, not by intent alone.

A concrete example

A churn-prediction model is only as good as the signals available to it; if the data doesn't capture a key reason customers actually leave, like a competitor's new pricing, no amount of modeling skill will let it predict that kind of churn.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Using more signals can improve a model's accuracy, but each additional data source adds collection cost, privacy exposure, and potential noise, so signal selection is a real trade-off, not simply more data is always better

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real business decision you'd want an AI system to help with, list what data is actually available, and identify one signal that might be missing that would matter a lot.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Having a lot of data does not automatically mean having the right signals; a company can have millions of records and still lack the specific signal that actually predicts the outcome it cares about.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Data and signals?

  • Once Data and signals is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Having a lot of data does not automatically mean having the right signals; a company can have millions of records and still lack the specific signal that actually predicts the outcome it cares about.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Data and signals in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Rule-based vs learned systems

Rule-based systems encode expert knowledge as explicit conditions. Learned systems discover patterns from labelled examples. Production AI often uses both together.

Why this matters

Understanding this distinction matters because it determines how you build, test, debug, and explain the system to compliance teams.

In plain terms

A rule-based system is a legal contract: every scenario must be explicitly covered. A learned system is an experienced judge: it has seen enough cases to generalise to new ones, but its reasoning is harder to audit.

For non-technical readers

For a business reader, this choice maps to a familiar trade-off: a strict policy manual is predictable and easy to audit but breaks the moment reality doesn't match the manual; an experienced judgment call handles novel situations well but is harder to explain after the fact. Most real AI products blend both, hard rules for anything with legal or safety stakes, like never revealing a password, and learned judgment for the fuzzier parts, like understanding what a customer actually meant. When reviewing an AI proposal, ask which parts are hard rules you can rely on absolutely, and which parts are 'best guess' judgment that needs a safety net.

How it works

The internal flow differs: rules branch on conditions, models transform features through learned weights. Knowing which is which helps you place logging in the right place.

  • Rules are deterministic and auditable. Given the same input, you always get the same output and you can trace exactly why.
  • Learned models generalise to unseen inputs but can fail silently when inputs drift away from training data.
  • Hybrid systems use rules for high-stakes or regulated decisions and ML for flexible pattern matching.
  • Rules degrade gracefully: when a rule is wrong, you fix the rule. Models degrade through data drift: when the world changes, model performance drops silently.
  • Production systems need monitoring in both cases — rules need change management, models need performance tracking.

A concrete example

A fraud detection system might use a rule to instantly block transactions from known fraud IP addresses (deterministic, auditable), while using an ML model to score unusual spending patterns (flexible, generalising). An alert goes to human review when the score is in the uncertain middle range.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Rules are cheap to explain and audit; models handle complexity but require evaluation sets, monitoring, and retraining pipelines.

Hands-on lab

Task: For a content moderation system, list five rules you could write explicitly and three situations where rules would fail but a model might succeed. Then flip it: write three situations where a model would fail but a rule would be safer.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production slice should include both a rule-based baseline (for comparison and fallback) and a model, with evals that measure each.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Rules do not scale: a real fraud system would need millions of rules to match what a model learns from data.
  • Models are not always better: for simple, stable, high-stakes decisions, an explicit rule is easier to audit and explain.
  • You cannot replace all rules with models in regulated industries — some decisions legally require explainable deterministic logic.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Rule-based vs learned systems?

  • Once Rule-based vs learned systems is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Rules do not scale: a real fraud system would need millions of rules to match what a model learns from data.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Rule-based vs learned systems into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: A production slice should include both a rule-based baseline (for comparison and fallback) and a model, with evals that measure each.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Rule-based vs learned systems in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Probabilistic thinking

Probabilistic thinking means reasoning about an AI system's output as a likelihood or confidence rather than a guaranteed fact, since most AI models produce a best statistical estimate that can be wrong, not a certainty.

In plain terms

It is like a weather forecast that says 80 percent chance of rain; it's useful, actionable information, but treating it as a guarantee that it will or won't rain would be a mistake.

How it works

In practice it comes down to interpreting a model's output alongside its confidence or probability, and building product logic that behaves differently at different confidence levels, such as auto-approving high-confidence predictions while routing low-confidence ones to a human, rather than treating every output as equally certain.

A concrete example

A content moderation system auto-removes content the model flags with 98 percent confidence, but routes content flagged at 60 percent confidence to a human reviewer, since a single fixed threshold-blind action would be wrong more often.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Building product logic that accounts for confidence levels produces a safer, more calibrated system, but it adds design complexity compared to a system that treats every model output as equally trustworthy

Try it in code

function handlePrediction(result) {
  if (result.confidence > 0.98) return autoApprove(result);
  if (result.confidence < 0.6) return escalateToHuman(result);
  return flagForReview(result); // not every output gets treated as equally certain
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real AI feature, describe how its behavior should differ between a high-confidence prediction and a low-confidence one, rather than treating every prediction identically.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A model's output is not a fact just because it's presented confidently; even a well-performing model is fundamentally producing a probabilistic best guess, and treating its output as certain rather than likely is a common and costly design mistake.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Probabilistic thinking?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Probabilistic thinking is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A model's output is not a fact just because it's presented confidently; even a well-performing model is fundamentally producing a probabilistic best guess, and treating its output as certain rather than likely is a common and costly design mistake.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Probabilistic thinking in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework

Model capabilities

Model capabilities refers to the specific, testable things an AI model can and cannot reliably do, which vary significantly by task and are best understood through actual evaluation rather than general reputation or marketing.

In plain terms

It is like a job candidate's actual demonstrated skills versus their resume's claimed skills; the only reliable way to know is to test them on the specific tasks you actually need done.

For non-technical readers

The business-relevant version of this: never approve an AI tool for a real workflow based on a demo or a vendor's claim alone. Ask to test it on your own actual, messy examples, the weird customer email, the ambiguous contract clause, not their curated showcase. A model that looks brilliant in a sales demo can fail quietly on exactly the edge cases your business deals with every day, and that gap is where AI projects lose money and trust after launch.

How it works

In practice it comes down to evaluating a model on tasks representative of the actual intended use case, rather than relying on general benchmark scores or a vendor's broad capability claims, since a model can excel at one task type while performing poorly on a superficially similar one.

A concrete example

A model that writes excellent marketing copy might perform poorly at extracting precise numbers from a legal contract, because the two tasks require very different kinds of reliability, even though both are generating text.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Testing a model specifically on your own representative tasks takes more upfront effort than trusting a vendor's general capability claims, but it's the only way to know how it will actually perform on your real use case rather than their demo

Hands-on lab

Task: For one AI capability you're considering relying on, describe the specific test you'd run using your own real examples before trusting it for that use case.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A model performing impressively on one task, or scoring well on a public benchmark, is not evidence it will perform well on a different, even superficially similar, task; capability doesn't transfer as generally as it might seem.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Model capabilities?

  • Correct answer: A model performing impressively on one task, or scoring well on a public benchmark, is not evidence it will perform well on a different, even superficially similar, task; capability doesn't transfer as generally as it might seem.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Model capabilities is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Model capabilities in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?
