  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Failure modes

Failure modes are the specific, predictable ways an AI system gets things wrong, such as confidently stating incorrect information, being manipulated by malicious input, or performing worse on cases underrepresented in its training data.

In plain terms

It is like knowing a car's specific weak points, brakes that fade on long descents, tires that wear unevenly, so you can watch for those specific problems rather than being surprised when something eventually breaks.

For non-technical readers

For a non-technical decision-maker, the useful question isn't could this AI system fail, since the honest answer is always yes, it's what specifically does it get wrong, and what does that cost us when it happens. A support bot that occasionally gives a slightly awkward answer is a minor cost; one that occasionally invents a refund policy that doesn't exist is a real liability. Understanding the specific failure mode, not just that failure is possible, is what lets you size the actual business risk correctly.

How it works

In practice it comes down to cataloging the specific ways a given AI system is known to fail, such as hallucinating plausible-sounding but false information, being vulnerable to adversarial input, or degrading on inputs unlike its training data, and designing detection and mitigation for each specific failure mode rather than treating failure as one undifferentiated risk.

A concrete example

A customer service AI's known failure modes might include confidently answering questions outside its knowledge base and being tricked by cleverly worded requests to bypass its refund policy; each of these needs its own specific detection and mitigation, not one generic safety net.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Cataloging and designing for specific failure modes takes more upfront analysis than a generic disclaimer that the AI might be wrong sometimes, but it's what actually lets a team build effective, targeted safeguards instead of vague ones

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real AI feature, name two distinct failure modes it's likely to have, and describe one specific way you'd detect each.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • AI failure is not one uniform risk that a single generic safeguard can address; different failure modes, hallucination, manipulation, poor performance on unusual inputs, require different specific detections and different specific fixes.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Failure modes?

  • Correct answer: AI failure is not one uniform risk that a single generic safeguard can address; different failure modes, hallucination, manipulation, poor performance on unusual inputs, require different specific detections and different specific fixes.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Failure modes is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Failure modes in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Human-in-the-loop

Human-in-the-loop is a design pattern where a person reviews, approves, or can override an AI system's output before it takes effect, used specifically for decisions where the cost of an AI error is high enough to warrant a human checkpoint.

In plain terms

It is like a pilot and autopilot: the autopilot handles routine flying, but the pilot stays in the loop and takes over for anything unusual or high-stakes, rather than the plane flying itself with nobody watching.

For non-technical readers

If you're deciding how much to trust an AI system in your business, human-in-the-loop is your main lever for managing risk without giving up the efficiency gains entirely. The practical question to ask any team proposing an AI feature is: which specific decisions still get a human's eyes before they take effect, and which are fully automated? A thoughtful answer names specific decision types and dollar thresholds; a vague answer like we monitor it is a sign the risk hasn't actually been thought through.

How it works

In practice it comes down to identifying decision points where an AI error would be costly or hard to reverse, routing those specific decisions to a human reviewer with enough context to make a real judgment, while allowing lower-stakes, easily reversible decisions to proceed automatically.

A concrete example

An AI system drafts responses to customer complaints, but a human reviews and approves any response involving a refund over a certain amount, while routine, low-stakes responses go out automatically.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More human review reduces the risk of a costly automated mistake, but it also slows down the process and requires staffing, so human-in-the-loop should be reserved for genuinely high-stakes decisions, not applied to everything by default

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real AI-assisted process, identify the single decision point where a human review would catch the costliest possible mistake, and describe what context that reviewer would need.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Human-in-the-loop is not the same as a human passively glancing at outputs after the fact; for it to actually catch errors, the human needs real context and enough time to make a genuine judgment, not just rubber-stamp a high volume of automated recommendations.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Human-in-the-loop?

  • Once Human-in-the-loop is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Human-in-the-loop is not the same as a human passively glancing at outputs after the fact; for it to actually catch errors, the human needs real context and enough time to make a genuine judgment, not just rubber-stamp a high volume of automated recommendations.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Human-in-the-loop in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Product discovery

Product discovery for AI features means validating that a real user problem exists and that AI is genuinely the right way to solve it, before investing in building, since AI capability is easy to be seduced by even when it doesn't fit the actual problem.

In plain terms

It is like confirming there's real demand for a restaurant in a specific neighborhood before signing a long lease, rather than opening because you personally love the cuisine.

For non-technical readers

In plain business terms: just because AI can do something doesn't mean your customers need it or will use it. Before funding an AI initiative, ask what evidence exists that this is a real, validated pain point, not just an assumption that AI would be impressive here. The AI industry is full of technically working features nobody asked for; the discipline of confirming real demand first is the same discipline that applies to any new product investment, AI or not.

How it works

In practice it comes down to talking to real users about their actual workflow and pain points, testing whether a lightweight prototype or even a manual, faked version of the AI feature actually solves their problem, before committing engineering time to a full AI-powered build.

A concrete example

Before building an AI-powered meeting summarizer, a team manually writes summaries for a few real users' meetings for two weeks to confirm summarization is actually the bottleneck users care about, rather than assuming it based on internal excitement about the technology.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Validating the problem before building takes extra time upfront and can feel like it's delaying visible progress, but it prevents the far more expensive outcome of building a technically impressive AI feature that nobody actually needed

Hands-on lab

Task: For one AI feature idea, describe the cheapest possible way you could test whether users actually want it before writing any code.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Having an impressive AI capability available is not the same as having discovered a real product opportunity; product discovery specifically guards against building something because the technology is exciting rather than because a validated user need exists.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Product discovery?

  • Once Product discovery is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Having an impressive AI capability available is not the same as having discovered a real product opportunity; product discovery specifically guards against building something because the technology is exciting rather than because a validated user need exists.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Product discovery in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Operational readiness

Operational readiness for an AI feature means having the monitoring, support processes, and incident response in place to actually run the feature reliably in production, distinct from having a version of it that works in a demo or test environment.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between a restaurant's kitchen being able to cook one great dish for a food critic, versus having the staffing, supply chain, and health inspections in place to serve hundreds of customers every night.

For non-technical readers

For a business stakeholder, the question to ask before any AI feature goes live to customers is: if this starts behaving badly at 2am, how would we know, and how fast could we turn it off? A feature that impressed everyone in the demo can still cause real damage in production if there's no one watching and no plan to intervene. Operational readiness is what separates a science project from something the business can actually stand behind.

How it works

In practice it comes down to establishing monitoring for the AI feature's quality, cost, and latency, a support process for handling user complaints about wrong or unexpected AI behavior, and a documented incident-response plan for when the AI system behaves badly at scale, before launching to real users.

A concrete example

Before launching an AI-powered support assistant broadly, a team confirms there's a dashboard tracking answer accuracy, a process for a human to review flagged bad responses, and a plan for temporarily disabling the assistant if it starts giving harmful advice.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Building out full operational readiness takes real time and resourcing before launch, but skipping it to launch faster means the team has no way to detect or respond to problems until they've already affected many real users

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real AI feature about to launch, name the one monitoring signal that would most quickly reveal if something has gone wrong, and describe what the team should do when it fires.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • An AI feature that works correctly in testing is not the same as one that's operationally ready; without monitoring, support processes, and an incident plan, the same feature can behave unpredictably or expensively at real scale with nobody positioned to catch it in time.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Operational readiness?

  • Once Operational readiness is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: An AI feature that works correctly in testing is not the same as one that's operationally ready; without monitoring, support processes, and an incident plan, the same feature can behave unpredictably or expensively at real scale with nobody positioned to catch it in time.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Operational readiness in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Ethics baseline

An ethics baseline for AI is the minimum set of commitments an organization makes about fairness, transparency, and harm prevention before deploying an AI system, treated as a non-negotiable starting point rather than an afterthought added if time permits.

In plain terms

It is like a restaurant's baseline food-safety practices: not a competitive differentiator to brag about, just the non-negotiable floor below which the business shouldn't operate at all.

For non-technical readers

The practical takeaway for a non-technical leader: an ethics baseline isn't corporate values language for a webpage, it's a specific checklist, like we tested this doesn't discriminate by group or users can appeal an automated decision, that either gets done before launch or doesn't. The real business risk isn't looking bad in a press release; it's shipping a system that treats certain customers unfairly and discovering that only after it's already caused harm and made the news. Treating the ethics baseline as a launch gate, the same way you'd treat a legal or security sign-off, is far cheaper than the alternative.

How it works

In practice it comes down to defining specific, checkable commitments, such as testing for disparate outcomes across user groups, disclosing when a user is interacting with AI, and having a process for users to contest or escalate an AI decision, and treating these as launch requirements rather than optional enhancements.

A concrete example

A company's ethics baseline requires that any AI system making decisions about people, such as loan approvals or hiring, be tested for outcome disparities across demographic groups before launch, regardless of how much schedule pressure exists.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Holding a strict ethics baseline can slow down launches and add real testing overhead, but skipping it to move faster risks harm to real people and significant legal and reputational damage to the business if problems surface after launch

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real or hypothetical AI system your organization might build, write two specific, testable ethics baseline requirements it would need to meet before launch.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Ethics in AI is not a public relations exercise separate from the actual engineering work; a real ethics baseline consists of specific, testable requirements that either get verified before launch or don't, not a values statement that exists independently of what actually ships.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Ethics baseline?

  • Correct answer: Ethics in AI is not a public relations exercise separate from the actual engineering work; a real ethics baseline consists of specific, testable requirements that either get verified before launch or don't, not a values statement that exists independently of what actually ships.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Ethics baseline is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Ethics baseline in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Foundation lab

The foundation lab is a hands-on exercise to take one real repetitive decision, frame it as an AI problem with clear inputs, outputs, and success criteria, and identify what data would be needed, without yet building anything.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to picking one real repetitive decision, explicitly writing out its input, expected output, what counts as a correct result, and what data would be needed to learn the pattern, surfacing gaps in that framing before any model is built.

It is like sketching a blueprint before construction begins, catching design problems on paper where they're cheap to fix rather than in a half-built structure.

The brief

Take one real repetitive decision you're familiar with, and frame it explicitly with input, output, success criteria, and what data would be needed, without building anything yet.

A worked reference

A lab might take the decision of routing customer emails to the right team, framing the input as email text, the output as a team label, success as matching what a human would have chosen, and identifying that historical routing logs would be the needed training data.

Starting point in code

const problemFrame = {
  input: "incoming customer email text",
  output: "team label (billing, technical, shipping)",
  successCriteria: "matches what a human router would have chosen",
  neededData: "historical routing logs with labels"
};
// Written out BEFORE any model is built

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Spending time on this framing exercise before writing any code feels slower than jumping straight to building, but it surfaces problems, like missing data or unclear success criteria, far more cheaply than discovering them after a model is already built

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • Framing a problem clearly is not a bureaucratic formality that can be skipped for a simple-seeming AI feature; a surprising number of AI projects fail specifically because the problem was never framed with clear inputs, outputs, and success criteria before work began.

Going deeper

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework

Mini project

The mini project ships a small, complete demonstration of one AI concept for a real, framed problem, with a documented decision about whether a rule-based or learned approach fits, and what evidence would prove it's working.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a clearly framed problem with an explicit choice between a rule-based or learned approach, justified by the nature of the problem, and a definition of what evidence would prove the solution is actually working, into one small, documented demonstration.

It is like building a small working proof of concept that clearly documents why you chose the approach you did, not just that something runs.

The brief

Frame one real small problem, decide and justify whether a rule-based or learned approach fits better, and define what evidence would prove your solution actually works.

A worked reference

A mini project might frame the problem of flagging potentially spam form submissions, decide a small set of explicit rules would work better than a learned model given the very limited scope, and define what evidence, like a false-positive rate below one percent, would prove it's working.

Starting point in code

const decision = {
  problem: "flag potentially spam form submissions",
  approach: "rule-based", // scope is narrow enough that rules beat a learned model
  reason: "small, well-defined pattern space; rules are auditable and free to run",
  successEvidence: "false-positive rate below 1%"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A small project with a clearly justified approach choice and defined success evidence is more valuable to demonstrate real judgment than a flashier build with no clear reasoning behind its design choices

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • Choosing to use a learned model is not automatically the more impressive or correct choice; for some real problems, a simple rule-based approach is genuinely the better engineering decision, and knowing when to make that call is itself the skill being demonstrated.

Going deeper

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete AI product proposal from first principles: problem framing, data and signal assessment, an approach recommendation, expected failure modes, and where human oversight belongs, as one coherent proposal.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a clearly framed problem with success criteria, an honest assessment of what data and signals are actually available, a recommended approach with its trade-offs, a list of expected failure modes, and specific points where human oversight is required into one complete, documented proposal.

It is like writing a complete feasibility study and project plan before a company commits real budget to building something, not just a pitch for why the idea sounds exciting.

The brief

Write a one-page AI product proposal for a real idea: problem framing with success criteria, a data and signal assessment, a recommended approach, and one specific human-oversight requirement.

A worked reference

A capstone for an AI-assisted resume screening tool might frame the problem as ranking, not rejecting, candidates, assess available historical hiring data for bias risk, recommend AI-assisted ranking with mandatory human final review, and name specific failure modes like demographic disparity to watch for.

Starting point in code

const proposal = {
  problem: "rank candidates, not auto-reject them",
  dataAssessment: "historical hiring data — checked for demographic skew",
  approach: "AI-assisted ranking + mandatory human final review",
  failureModes: ["demographic disparity", "overconfidence on thin resumes"],
  humanOversight: "final decision always made by a person"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A complete, honest capstone proposal takes more work than an enthusiastic pitch focused only on the upside, but it's what actually lets a team or stakeholder make an informed decision about whether and how to proceed

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • An AI project proposal is not complete once it explains what the AI could do; without an honest assessment of data availability, expected failure modes, and where human oversight is required, it's a pitch, not a real feasibility analysis.

Going deeper

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework

Interview pack

The AI foundations interview pack prepares you to explain first-principles AI reasoning, problem framing, and probabilistic thinking credibly, backed by the ability to reason through a described scenario rather than reciting definitions.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain not just what a tool is, but how you'd decide whether it's even the right tool for a specific described job.

How it works

In practice it comes down to practicing clear explanations of when AI fits a problem versus a rule-based approach, and a concrete story about framing an ambiguous problem or reasoning about a model's confidence in a real decision.

A concrete example

A strong answer to would you use AI for this problem starts by clarifying what data is actually available and what success would look like, rather than assuming AI is the right answer by default just because the question mentions it.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const reasoning = [
  "what data would this actually need, and do we have it?",
  "what does success look like, concretely?",
  "would a simple rule-based approach solve this just as well?",
  "only then: is AI actually the right tool here?"
];

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Practice explaining, out loud, how you would decide whether a described repetitive business decision is a good fit for an AI-based solution or a simpler rule-based one.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Being enthusiastic about AI's capabilities is not the same as being able to reason about when it's actually the right tool for a specific problem versus when a simpler, rule-based approach would genuinely serve better; interviewers probe for that judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • Correct answer: Being enthusiastic about AI's capabilities is not the same as being able to reason about when it's actually the right tool for a specific problem versus when a simpler, rule-based approach would genuinely serve better; interviewers probe for that judgment specifically.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework

Machine Learning

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: Machine learning is like training a new teammate with labeled examples. Instead of writing every rule, you show enough examples until the teammate learns the pattern. ML is the foundation for understanding why models need data, metrics, validation, and monitoring.

What is ML?

Machine learning is a branch of AI where a system learns to make predictions or decisions by finding patterns in historical data, rather than following explicitly programmed rules for every case.

In plain terms

It is like training a new employee by showing them thousands of past examples of good and bad decisions, rather than handing them a rulebook covering every possible situation.

For non-technical readers

For a non-technical reader, the simplest useful mental model is: machine learning is pattern-matching from historical examples, not reasoning from rules. That's exactly why it's powerful for things too complex to write rules for, like judging whether a photo contains a specific object, and exactly why it can fail in surprising ways on situations unlike anything in its training history. When evaluating an ML-based proposal, ask what historical examples it actually learned from, since that history is effectively the model's entire worldview.

How it works

In practice it comes down to feeding a model many examples of inputs paired with known correct outputs, letting an optimization process adjust the model's internal parameters to reduce prediction error on those examples, then evaluating whether it generalizes correctly to new, unseen examples.

A concrete example

A spam classifier learns from millions of labeled emails which patterns, such as certain phrases, sender reputation, and formatting, correlate with spam, rather than a human writing an explicit rule for every possible spam tactic.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Machine learning can handle patterns too complex or numerous for a human to hand-code as rules, but it requires substantial representative data and ongoing evaluation, and its decisions can be harder to explain than an explicit rule would be

Hands-on lab

Task: For one repetitive decision made in a business you know, describe what past examples of good and bad outcomes would look like, and what pattern a machine learning model might learn from them.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Machine learning does not memorize a lookup table of every training example; a well-trained model generalizes patterns to new examples it has never seen, which is both its main value and the source of its unpredictability on unusual cases.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Machine Learning?

  • Correct answer: Machine learning does not memorize a lookup table of every training example; a well-trained model generalizes patterns to new examples it has never seen, which is both its main value and the source of its unpredictability on unusual cases.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Machine Learning is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Machine Learning in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Supervised learning

Supervised learning trains a model using labelled examples — pairs of input and correct output — so the model learns to predict the output for new inputs.

Why this matters

Supervised learning matters because most AI products — classifiers, extractors, rankers, risk scorers — are supervised learning problems at their core.

In plain terms

Supervised learning is like teaching with an answer key. You show the student (model) a question and the correct answer, repeat thousands of times, and the student learns to answer new questions correctly.

How it works

Internal flow: collect data → label data → split into train/val/test → choose model → train → evaluate → deploy → monitor for drift.

  • The training set contains labelled examples: (input, correct_output) pairs. The model learns a function that maps inputs to outputs.
  • During training, the model makes predictions on training data, a loss function measures how wrong the predictions are, and optimisation adjusts the model to reduce the loss.
  • Validation data (not used in training) measures generalisation — whether the model works on new inputs it has not seen.
  • Common tasks: classification (predict a category), regression (predict a number), ranking (order items by relevance).
  • The quality of labels is the most important factor. Noisy or inconsistent labels produce unreliable models regardless of architecture.

A concrete example

To build an email priority classifier, you collect 10,000 emails labelled as 'urgent' or 'not urgent' by your team. The model trains on 8,000, is validated on 1,000, and tested on a final held-out set of 1,000. After deployment, you monitor whether its predictions still match human judgement on a sample of live emails.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More labelled data improves performance but labelling is expensive; complex models fit training data better but may overfit; faster models trade accuracy for latency.

Hands-on lab

Task: You want to classify customer support tickets into five categories. Write down: the input features, what 'correct label' means and who decides, how you would catch labelling disagreements, how you would measure whether the deployed model is still working six months later.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production slice needs: a held-out test set that is never used during development, a baseline (e.g. majority class), error analysis on failure cases, and a retraining trigger when performance drops.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Accuracy on training data is meaningless. Only held-out test set performance matters.
  • More model complexity does not always help. A simple model with clean data often beats a complex model with noisy data.
  • Deploying a model is not the end — distribution shift means model performance degrades over time as the real world changes.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Supervised learning?

  • Correct answer: Accuracy on training data is meaningless. Only held-out test set performance matters.
  • Once Supervised learning is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Supervised learning into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: A production slice needs: a held-out test set that is never used during development, a baseline (e.g. majority class), error analysis on failure cases, and a retraining trigger when performance drops.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Supervised learning in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Unsupervised learning

Unsupervised learning finds structure or patterns in data without labeled outputs, such as grouping similar items together or reducing data to its most important dimensions, distinct from supervised learning which learns from labeled input-output pairs.

In plain terms

It is like sorting a box of mixed items into piles based on similarity without being told the categories in advance, versus being handed a labeled answer key for each item.

How it works

In practice it comes down to algorithms such as clustering that group similar data points together based on distance or density, or dimensionality reduction that compresses data into fewer dimensions while preserving its most important structure, both discovering patterns without any labeled target to predict.

A concrete example

A retailer clusters customers by purchasing behavior without predefined segments, discovering that a previously unnoticed group of customers behaves distinctly enough to warrant its own marketing strategy.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Unsupervised learning doesn't require expensive labeled data, but its discovered patterns, like clusters, don't come with a built-in definition of correct or incorrect, making results harder to validate than supervised learning's clear accuracy metrics

Try it in code

const clusters = kMeans(customerVectors, { k: 5 });
clusters.forEach((c, i) => console.log(`Cluster ${i}: ${c.size} customers`));
// No labels needed — discovering structure, not predicting a known target

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real dataset without labels, describe what unsupervised technique, clustering or dimensionality reduction, you would use and what question it would help answer.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Unsupervised learning is not automatically less useful or accurate than supervised learning; it solves a different problem, discovering structure with no labels, that supervised learning can't address at all, since supervised learning requires labels that may not exist or be feasible to create.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Unsupervised learning?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Unsupervised learning is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Unsupervised learning is not automatically less useful or accurate than supervised learning; it solves a different problem, discovering structure with no labels, that supervised learning can't address at all, since supervised learning requires labels that may not exist or be feasible to create.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Unsupervised learning in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

Feature engineering

Feature engineering is the process of selecting, transforming, or creating the specific input signals a model actually learns from, since raw data rarely arrives in the form that best exposes the patterns a model needs to find.

In plain terms

It is like a chef prepping ingredients before cooking, chopping, measuring, combining, rather than throwing raw, whole ingredients directly into the pot and hoping for the best.

How it works

In practice it comes down to transforming raw data into more useful representations, such as extracting the day of week from a timestamp, combining two raw fields into a meaningful ratio, or encoding a categorical variable numerically, so the model receives signals more directly related to what it needs to predict.

A concrete example

Instead of feeding a raw transaction timestamp to a fraud model, feature engineering extracts the time since the customer's last transaction and whether it occurred outside their usual hours, both far more predictive signals than the raw timestamp alone.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Well-engineered features can dramatically improve a simpler model's performance, but feature engineering takes domain knowledge and manual effort, and deep learning models can sometimes learn useful features automatically from raw data, reducing the need for it

Try it in code

function engineerFeatures(transaction, customerHistory) {
  return {
    timeSinceLastPurchase: transaction.date - customerHistory.lastPurchaseDate,
    isOffHours: isOutsideUsualHours(transaction.date, customerHistory),
    rawAmount: transaction.amount // keep alongside engineered features, not instead of
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real prediction problem, identify one raw data field that could be transformed into a more predictive feature, and explain why the transformed version would help more.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Feature engineering is not obsolete just because deep learning can learn features automatically; for many real-world tabular data problems, thoughtful feature engineering still meaningfully outperforms feeding raw fields into even a sophisticated model.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Feature engineering?

  • Once Feature engineering is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Feature engineering is not obsolete just because deep learning can learn features automatically; for many real-world tabular data problems, thoughtful feature engineering still meaningfully outperforms feeding raw fields into even a sophisticated model.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Feature engineering in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

Training data

Training data is the labeled examples a supervised model learns from, and its quality, representativeness, and labeling accuracy directly determine the ceiling on how good the resulting model can possibly be.

In plain terms

It is like a student's textbook; if the textbook has errors or covers only part of the subject, the student's knowledge will reflect those same gaps and mistakes no matter how well they study.

How it works

In practice it comes down to collecting examples that represent the actual distribution of cases the model will see in production, ensuring labels are accurate and consistently applied, and checking for gaps where certain real-world cases are underrepresented or entirely missing from the training set.

A concrete example

A model trained mostly on daytime traffic camera images performs poorly at night, not because of a modeling flaw, but because nighttime images were underrepresented in the training data.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More training data generally improves a model's ability to generalize, but collecting and accurately labeling more data costs real time and money, and a large amount of biased or mislabeled data can be worse than a smaller, carefully curated set

Try it in code

const coverage = trainingSet.groupBy(row => row.timeOfDay);
console.log(coverage); // { day: 8400, night: 120 } — night is badly underrepresented

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real model or prediction task, identify one category of real-world case that might be underrepresented in its training data, and describe how you'd check.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A model performing poorly is not always a sign the modeling approach is wrong; it's very often a sign the training data doesn't adequately represent the cases the model is actually being asked to handle in production.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Training data?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A model performing poorly is not always a sign the modeling approach is wrong; it's very often a sign the training data doesn't adequately represent the cases the model is actually being asked to handle in production.
  • Once Training data is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Training data in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework

Loss functions

A loss function is a mathematical measure of how wrong a model's prediction is. Training minimises the loss, steering the model toward better predictions.

Why this matters

Loss functions matter because they define what 'better' means during training. Wrong choice leads to a model that optimises for the wrong thing.

In plain terms

A loss function is like a score on a test that gets lower the more correct you are — except the model takes millions of small tests and adjusts itself after each one to bring the score down.

How it works

Internal flow: model predicts → loss computes error → gradient descent adjusts weights in the direction that reduces loss → repeat for all training examples.

  • Cross-entropy loss is standard for classification: it penalises confident wrong predictions very heavily.
  • Mean squared error is standard for regression: it penalises large errors more than small ones.
  • The loss landscape is the mathematical space the optimiser explores. Deep learning works because neural networks create loss surfaces that can be navigated with gradient descent.
  • Loss on training data can keep decreasing even as loss on validation data starts rising — this is overfitting.
  • Custom losses can encode business requirements: e.g. penalise false negatives more than false positives in a medical screening model.

A concrete example

For a loan default predictor, you might use cross-entropy as the training loss but report AUC-ROC and the false negative rate to business stakeholders — because missing a default (false negative) has much higher cost than a false alarm.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Simple losses are easy to optimise but may not align with business value. Custom losses better match real costs but are harder to tune and debug.

Hands-on lab

Task: For a medical diagnosis model, write down the cost of a false positive (wrong alarm) vs a false negative (missed case). Now explain how you would modify the loss function to make the model penalise false negatives more heavily.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-ready model needs both a training loss and a separate set of business metrics. Never ship a model evaluated only on its training objective.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Low training loss does not mean the model is good. You need validation and test loss.
  • Accuracy is a misleading metric on imbalanced data — a model that always predicts the majority class can have 99% accuracy but zero useful predictions.
  • Changing the loss function is one of the highest-leverage interventions in ML. Do not assume the default loss is correct for your problem.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Loss functions?

  • Correct answer: Low training loss does not mean the model is good. You need validation and test loss.
  • Once Loss functions is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Loss functions into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: A production-ready model needs both a training loss and a separate set of business metrics. Never ship a model evaluated only on its training objective.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Loss functions in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Optimization

Optimization in machine learning is the process of adjusting a model's parameters to minimize a loss function, using an algorithm like gradient descent that iteratively moves parameters in the direction that reduces prediction error.

In plain terms

It is like walking downhill in fog by feeling the slope under your feet and taking small steps toward lower ground, without being able to see the whole landscape at once.

How it works

In practice it comes down to computing the gradient of the loss function with respect to each parameter, indicating which direction would reduce the loss, and updating parameters by a small step in that direction, repeated many times until the loss stops meaningfully decreasing.

A concrete example

Training a model to predict house prices adjusts its parameters step by step, each adjustment slightly reducing the average error between predicted and actual prices across the training set, until further steps produce negligible improvement.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Larger optimization steps, a higher learning rate, converge faster when they work, but they risk overshooting the best solution or becoming unstable, while smaller steps are more stable but take longer to converge

Try it in code

function gradientStep(weights, gradient, learningRate) {
  return weights.map((w, i) => w - learningRate * gradient[i]);
}
// too large a learningRate -> overshoot; too small -> slow convergence

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Explain in your own words why taking optimization steps that are too large can cause training to become unstable rather than converging faster.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Optimization finding a loss value that stops decreasing does not guarantee the model has found the best possible solution; it may have settled into a locally good but not globally optimal set of parameters, which is why initialization and optimizer choice both matter.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Optimization?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Optimization is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Optimization finding a loss value that stops decreasing does not guarantee the model has found the best possible solution; it may have settled into a locally good but not globally optimal set of parameters, which is why initialization and optimizer choice both matter.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Optimization in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

Validation

Validation checks whether AI inputs, outputs, or tool calls satisfy explicit rules before the system trusts or acts on them.

In plain terms

It is like a compiler and safety inspector for model behavior.

How it works

In practice it comes down to schema checks, policy checks, semantic checks, source checks, confidence thresholds, and rejection handling.

A concrete example

An extraction workflow validates date format, total amount range, and source evidence before writing to billing.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Strict validation catches errors but can increase false rejects.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write validation rules for a refund approval response before it reaches payment code.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not validate only after side effects have already happened.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Validation?

  • Correct answer: Do not validate only after side effects have already happened.
  • Once Validation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Validation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Overfitting

Overfitting happens when a model learns the training data too precisely — including its noise and quirks — and performs worse on new, unseen data.

Why this matters

Overfitting matters because it is the most common reason a model performs well in development but fails in production.

In plain terms

Overfitting is like memorising last year's exam answers instead of understanding the subject. You score 100% on the practice paper but fail the real exam because the questions are slightly different.

For non-technical readers

This is why a vendor demo or an internal test result can be misleading: a model can look perfect on the exact examples it was shown and still perform poorly on your real customers the moment it goes live. If a team reports a suspiciously perfect accuracy number, the right non-technical follow-up question is: was this tested on data the model never saw during training? If not, that impressive number may not survive contact with reality.

How it works

Internal flow to detect it: track training loss and validation loss across epochs. If training loss keeps falling while validation loss rises or plateaus, overfitting has begun.

  • The gap between training performance and validation performance is the clearest signal of overfitting.
  • Regularisation techniques (L1, L2, dropout, early stopping) reduce overfitting by adding penalties for model complexity or randomly disabling neurons during training.
  • More training data is the most reliable cure. More data gives the model genuine patterns to learn rather than fitting to noise.
  • Model capacity (number of parameters) should match the complexity of the problem. Tiny datasets cannot train huge models reliably.
  • Cross-validation gives a more robust estimate of generalisation by averaging performance across multiple train/validation splits.

A concrete example

A recommendation model trained on two weeks of data for a seasonal product may overfit to Christmas-specific patterns. When deployed in March, it performs poorly because the learned patterns don't generalise to year-round behaviour.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Reducing overfitting (regularisation, smaller model) may increase bias. The goal is the best generalisation, not the lowest training error.

Hands-on lab

Task: You train a model for three days and it reaches 99% training accuracy and 72% validation accuracy. Write down five experiments you would run to diagnose and fix the gap.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production checklist: hold out a test set before starting. Never tune on the test set. Log real-world performance after deployment. Compare to baseline.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • High training accuracy is not a success signal — it is expected and often meaningless.
  • Bigger models on small datasets almost always overfit. A logistic regression with clean features often beats a deep network with limited data.
  • Overfitting is not always visible in validation metrics. Silent overfitting to specific demographic groups or time periods is common in production.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Overfitting?

  • Correct answer: High training accuracy is not a success signal — it is expected and often meaningless.
  • Once Overfitting is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Overfitting into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: Production checklist: hold out a test set before starting. Never tune on the test set. Log real-world performance after deployment. Compare to baseline.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Overfitting in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Model selection

Model selection chooses the smallest, safest, fastest model that satisfies the product's quality and operational requirements.

In plain terms

It is like choosing a vehicle: a truck, bike, and race car are each right for different jobs.

For non-technical readers

For a budget owner, the practical rule is: don't default to the newest, most expensive model just because it's the most capable one available. A simpler, cheaper model is often good enough for a well-defined task like sorting emails into categories, while only your hardest problems, like drafting a nuanced legal argument, actually need the top-tier, more expensive model. Asking your team to justify model choice against actual task difficulty, not just default to the flagship model, is one of the simplest ways to control AI costs.

How it works

In practice it comes down to task fit, context length, tool use, multilingual ability, latency, cost, safety, license, and deployment target.

A concrete example

A classifier may use a small fast model while a complex legal drafting workflow uses a larger model with stronger eval performance.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Larger models improve capability but increase cost, latency, and governance burden.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create a model scorecard with quality, latency, cost, privacy, and operations columns.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not pick a model from hype; pick it from eval results on your tasks.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Model selection?

  • Correct answer: Do not pick a model from hype; pick it from eval results on your tasks.
  • Once Model selection is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Model selection in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Metrics

Metrics are the specific, quantitative measures used to evaluate a model's performance, chosen based on what actually matters for the task, since a single generic accuracy number can be misleading or even meaningless for some problems.

In plain terms

It is like judging a job candidate on the specific skills the role actually requires, rather than one generic overall impression score that hides where they're strong or weak.

How it works

In practice it comes down to selecting metrics that reflect the specific cost of different error types, such as precision and recall separately for an imbalanced classification problem, rather than a single aggregate accuracy number that can hide poor performance on the minority class.

A concrete example

A fraud-detection model with 99 percent accuracy might still be useless if fraud only occurs in 1 percent of transactions and the model simply predicts no fraud every time; precision and recall on the fraud class reveal this failure that accuracy alone hides.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Precision and recall often trade off against each other, improving one by adjusting a decision threshold typically worsens the other, so the right balance depends on the specific cost of false positives versus false negatives for the actual use case

Try it in code

const confusion = { truePositive: 8, falsePositive: 45, falseNegative: 2, trueNegative: 9945 };
const precision = confusion.truePositive / (confusion.truePositive + confusion.falsePositive);
const recall = confusion.truePositive / (confusion.truePositive + confusion.falseNegative);
// accuracy alone (99.5%) would have hidden this precision problem

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real classification problem, explain why accuracy alone might be misleading, and name a more appropriate metric given the specific cost of different error types.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A high accuracy score is not automatically evidence of a good model, especially on imbalanced data where a naive model that always predicts the majority class can achieve deceptively high accuracy while being completely useless.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Metrics?

  • Correct answer: A high accuracy score is not automatically evidence of a good model, especially on imbalanced data where a naive model that always predicts the majority class can achieve deceptively high accuracy while being completely useless.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Metrics is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Metrics in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena

Experiment tracking

Experiment tracking means systematically recording each model training run's configuration, data version, and resulting metrics, so different approaches can be compared reliably and past results can be reproduced.

In plain terms

It is like a lab notebook that records exactly what was changed in each experiment and what the result was, rather than relying on memory to recall which of a dozen attempts actually worked best.

How it works

In practice it comes down to logging each training run's hyperparameters, code version, dataset version, and resulting metrics to a shared, searchable record, so comparing two approaches means comparing recorded facts rather than relying on memory or scattered notes.

A concrete example

A team tries five different feature sets for a model, and their experiment tracking system records each run's exact configuration and validation score, making it easy to see which specific feature set actually performed best rather than guessing from memory.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Rigorous experiment tracking takes a small amount of extra setup for every training run, but without it, teams lose the ability to reliably compare past approaches or reproduce a result that worked well months earlier

Try it in code

await experimentLog.record({
  runId: "exp-042",
  features: ["time_since_purchase", "is_off_hours"],
  hyperparams: { learningRate: 0.01 },
  metrics: { precision: 0.61, recall: 0.80 }
});

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real modeling project, list the specific fields you would record for every training run to make results genuinely comparable later.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Remembering that one approach worked better than another is not the same as having a reliable, comparable record; without tracking the exact configuration and data version, a result can be difficult or impossible to reproduce later.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Experiment tracking?

  • Once Experiment tracking is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Remembering that one approach worked better than another is not the same as having a reliable, comparable record; without tracking the exact configuration and data version, a result can be difficult or impossible to reproduce later.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Experiment tracking in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

Deployment patterns

Deployment patterns are the different ways a trained machine learning model is actually put into production use, such as batch predictions run periodically, real-time predictions served on demand, or embedded predictions running directly on a user's device.

In plain terms

It is like choosing between mailing out a monthly report, staffing a help desk that answers on demand, or giving someone a reference book to look things up themselves; each pattern fits a different need for speed and context.

For non-technical readers

The business-relevant question here is cost versus need for speed. Real-time AI feels more impressive, but it's meaningfully more expensive to run than a system that just recalculates predictions once a day or once a week. Before your team defaults to real-time, ask whether the actual business decision this feeds into truly needs instant answers, or whether yesterday's answer would work just as well at a fraction of the cost.

How it works

In practice it comes down to choosing batch prediction for large-scale, non-time-sensitive scoring such as nightly customer churn scores, real-time serving for interactive user-facing predictions needing an immediate response, or on-device deployment for privacy-sensitive or offline use cases, based on latency, cost, and data-freshness requirements.

A concrete example

A fraud-detection model runs in real time to approve or flag each transaction as it happens, while a marketing segmentation model runs as an overnight batch job since same-day freshness isn't required for that use case.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Real-time serving gives the freshest, most interactive predictions but costs more per prediction and requires more serving infrastructure, while batch prediction is cheaper and simpler to operate but introduces staleness between runs

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real prediction use case, decide whether it genuinely needs real-time serving or could use cheaper batch processing, and justify the choice based on how quickly the prediction is actually needed.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Real-time serving is not automatically the right choice just because it's more capable-sounding; many business use cases genuinely don't need instant predictions, and choosing batch processing where it fits can save substantial infrastructure cost without hurting the actual business outcome.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Deployment patterns?

  • Once Deployment patterns is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Real-time serving is not automatically the right choice just because it's more capable-sounding; many business use cases genuinely don't need instant predictions, and choosing batch processing where it fits can save substantial infrastructure cost without hurting the actual business outcome.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Deployment patterns in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

ML lab

The ML lab is a hands-on exercise to train a simple model on a real small dataset, evaluate it with an appropriate metric, then engineer one new feature and measure whether it actually improves the result.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to training a baseline model on a small real dataset, evaluating it with a metric matched to the task, then adding one engineered feature and re-evaluating to see whether it measurably improved performance.

It is like running a controlled before-and-after experiment on your own recipe, changing one ingredient at a time to see its actual effect.

The brief

Train a baseline model on one real dataset, measure it with an appropriate metric, then add one engineered feature and measure whether it actually improved the result.

A worked reference

A lab might train a baseline model to predict customer churn, measure precision and recall, then add a feature like days since last purchase and re-measure to see if it improved recall on the churn class specifically.

Starting point in code

const baseline = trainAndEval(features_v1);
const withNewFeature = trainAndEval([...features_v1, "days_since_last_purchase"]);
console.log({ baseline, withNewFeature }); // did recall actually improve?

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Testing one feature at a time with a real before-and-after comparison takes more discipline than adding several changes at once, but it's the only way to know which specific change actually helped

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • The lab is not complete once a model trains and produces some accuracy number; without comparing before and after an engineered feature using an appropriate metric, you have no evidence the feature actually helped.

Going deeper

References

  • Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

Mini project

The mini project ships a small, complete ML model for one real task, with documented feature engineering, an appropriate evaluation metric, and tracked experiments, that another engineer could reproduce.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a documented feature engineering approach, a metric chosen for the specific task, and tracked experiment records showing what was tried and what worked into one complete, reproducible project.

It is like publishing a complete, reproducible lab report, not just a final result with no record of how you got there.

The brief

Train a small model on one real dataset, document your feature engineering choices, track your experiments, and report the metric most appropriate to the task.

A worked reference

A mini project might build a small churn-prediction model, documenting which features were engineered and why, tracking three experiment runs, and reporting precision and recall rather than accuracy alone given the class imbalance.

Starting point in code

export const churnModel = {
  features: ["days_since_last_purchase", "support_tickets_last_30d"],
  metric: "precision_recall", // not accuracy — classes are imbalanced
  experiments: require("./experiment-log.json")
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document
