  • The core tension: A small, fully documented and tracked project is more valuable and more trustworthy than a bigger model with an unclear evaluation metric and no record of what was tried

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A model with a good-looking accuracy number is not the same as a complete mini project; without the right metric for the task and a tracked record of what was tried, there's no way to know if the result is actually meaningful or reproducible.

Going deeper

References

  • Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete ML workflow for a real problem: training data assessment, feature engineering, optimization and metric choices, experiment tracking, and a deployment pattern, as one coherent system.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining an assessment of training data representativeness, a documented feature engineering approach, justified metric and optimization choices, a tracked experimentation process, and a deployment pattern appropriate to the use case into one complete, documented workflow.

It is like designing a complete research-to-production pipeline, not just training one model in isolation.

The brief

Write a one-page ML workflow design for a real problem: training data assessment, one engineered feature, metric choice with justification, and a deployment pattern.

A worked reference

A capstone for a credit-risk model might specify an assessment of training data representativeness across demographic groups, engineered features from transaction history, precision-recall tuned for the cost of false negatives, tracked experiments, and a batch deployment pattern.

Starting point in code

const mlWorkflow = {
  dataAssessment: "checked representation across demographic groups",
  features: ["transaction_velocity", "time_since_last_purchase"],
  metric: "precision-recall, tuned for false-negative cost",
  deployment: "batch scoring, nightly"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A complete ML workflow spanning data, features, metrics, tracking, and deployment takes substantially more work than training one model, but it reflects what's actually needed to ship a trustworthy ML system

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • An ML project is not complete once a model achieves a good metric on a held-out set; without assessing training data representativeness and choosing a deployment pattern that fits the use case, it's not ready for real production use.

Going deeper

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework

Interview pack

The machine learning interview pack prepares you to discuss metric selection, feature engineering, and training data quality with concrete reasoning, backed by the ability to diagnose a described model performance problem.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain not just a car's specs, but how you'd actually diagnose why it's underperforming on a specific road.

How it works

In practice it comes down to practicing clear explanations of why a specific metric fits a specific problem, and a concrete story about diagnosing a real or realistic model performance issue tied to data or feature quality.

A concrete example

A strong answer to why is this model's accuracy high but it's still not useful in production explains the class imbalance issue, names precision and recall as better metrics, and describes how you'd verify the training data represents real production cases.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const diagnosis = {
  symptom: "95% accuracy but useless in production",
  check: "class balance — what % is the majority class?",
  fix: "switch to precision/recall, check training data representativeness"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Practice explaining, out loud, why a model with 95 percent accuracy might still be a bad model, using a specific example of class imbalance.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing metric definitions is not the same as knowing which metric fits a specific problem's actual cost structure; interviewers often describe a scenario and expect you to reason toward the right metric, not recite a list.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Knowing metric definitions is not the same as knowing which metric fits a specific problem's actual cost structure; interviewers often describe a scenario and expect you to reason toward the right metric, not recite a list.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena

Deep Learning

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: Deep learning is like an assembly line of pattern detectors. Early stations notice simple patterns; later stations combine them into richer understanding. It explains how modern systems learn from images, audio, text, and complex signals without hand-coding every feature.

Deep learning overview

Deep learning is a subset of machine learning that uses neural networks with many layers to automatically learn complex patterns and representations directly from raw data, without a human manually engineering which features matter.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between a chef following a recipe that lists exact pre-measured ingredients, versus one who tastes the raw ingredients themselves and figures out what matters; deep learning figures out the important features itself rather than a human specifying them in advance.

For non-technical readers

For a non-technical reader: deep learning is the technology underneath most of what's called AI in the news today, including the large language models behind tools like ChatGPT and Claude. Its key business advantage is handling messy, unstructured information, text, images, audio, that used to require either an army of manual rules or wasn't automatable at all. The trade-off worth remembering is that this power costs real money in data and computing resources, so we'll use deep learning should always come with a rough sense of what that compute and data cost actually looks like.

How it works

In practice it comes down to stacking many layers of simple computational units, each learning increasingly abstract representations of the input, trained end to end on large amounts of data so the network discovers useful patterns automatically rather than relying on hand-engineered features.

A concrete example

A traditional machine learning image classifier might require an engineer to manually define features like edge patterns or color histograms, while a deep learning model learns useful visual features like edges, shapes, and textures automatically from raw pixel data during training.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Deep learning can automatically discover far more nuanced patterns than manually engineered features, especially for unstructured data like images, audio, and text, but it typically requires much more data and compute to train well than simpler traditional machine learning approaches

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real business problem involving unstructured data, such as images, audio, or free-text, explain why deep learning's ability to learn features automatically would likely outperform an approach requiring hand-engineered rules.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Deep learning is not a completely different kind of intelligence from other machine learning; it's the same fundamental idea, learning patterns from data, applied with a much deeper, more automatic architecture that's particularly well suited to messy, unstructured data like images and language.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Deep learning overview?

  • Correct answer: Deep learning is not a completely different kind of intelligence from other machine learning; it's the same fundamental idea, learning patterns from data, applied with a much deeper, more automatic architecture that's particularly well suited to messy, unstructured data like images and language.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Deep learning overview is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Deep learning overview in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Tensors

A tensor is a container for numbers arranged in one or more dimensions. A scalar has zero dimensions, a vector has one, a matrix has two, and model inputs often use three or more dimensions such as batch, time, height, width, or channels.

Why this matters

Tensors matter because every neural network input, activation, gradient, and weight is represented as a tensor. If you cannot read shapes, you cannot debug deep-learning code.

In plain terms

Think of tensors as spreadsheets that can have more than two dimensions. A normal spreadsheet has rows and columns. A tensor can add pages, batches, colour channels, time steps, or any other axis the model needs.

How it works

The internal flow is: raw data becomes numeric tensors, layers transform those tensors, the loss compares predictions with labels, and gradients update weight tensors.

  • Rank means the number of dimensions. A scalar has rank 0, a vector rank 1, a matrix rank 2, and an image batch might be rank 4.
  • Shape tells you the size of each dimension, such as [32, 224, 224, 3] for 32 colour images that are 224 by 224 pixels.
  • Dtype tells you how each number is stored, such as float32, float16, int64, or bool. Dtype affects memory, speed, and numerical behaviour.
  • Operations such as addition, multiplication, reshaping, slicing, and matrix multiplication are the basic moves deep-learning systems use.
  • Broadcasting lets smaller tensors participate in operations with larger tensors when dimensions are compatible.

A concrete example

In an image classifier, one image can be stored as [height, width, channels]. A training batch becomes [batch, height, width, channels]. The model does not see 'cat' or 'dog' directly; it sees tensors of pixel values and learns patterns from them.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Shape and dtype choices affect latency, memory, accuracy, and hardware efficiency. For example, float16 can be faster on GPUs but may require care with numerical stability.

Try it in code

const batch = {
  shape: [32, 224, 224, 3],
  dtype: "float32",
  values: "pixel intensities"
};

function assertImageBatch(tensor) {
  const [batchSize, height, width, channels] = tensor.shape;
  if (channels !== 3) throw new Error("expected RGB images");
  return { batchSize, pixelsPerImage: height * width * channels };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create three tensors on paper: a customer feature vector [age, spend, visits], a table of 10 customers, and a batch of 64 tables. Write the shape of each one, then describe what one row or cell means.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production slice should log input shapes, validate expected dimensions, catch NaN values, track memory usage, and include tests for bad shapes.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A tensor is not automatically intelligent. It is just structured numbers.
  • More dimensions do not always mean better data. Extra dimensions must have meaning.
  • Shape errors are not minor syntax problems; they often reveal a mismatch in the mental model of the data.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Tensors?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A tensor is not automatically intelligent. It is just structured numbers.
  • Once Tensors is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Tensors into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: A production slice should log input shapes, validate expected dimensions, catch NaN values, track memory usage, and include tests for bad shapes.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Tensors in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Gradient descent

Gradient descent is the optimisation algorithm that trains neural networks by repeatedly adjusting weights in the direction that reduces the loss function.

Why this matters

Gradient descent matters because it is the engine of learning in nearly every neural network. Without it, we could not train models from data.

In plain terms

Imagine you are blindfolded on a hilly landscape and want to reach the lowest valley. Gradient descent means you feel the slope under your feet and take a small step downhill. Repeat millions of times and you eventually reach a low point.

How it works

Internal flow: forward pass (compute prediction and loss) → backward pass (compute gradients via backpropagation) → update weights → repeat.

  • The gradient is the direction of steepest increase in loss. Moving in the opposite direction (negative gradient) reduces the loss.
  • Learning rate controls step size. Too large: you overshoot and oscillate. Too small: training is slow and may get stuck.
  • Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) computes the gradient on a random mini-batch rather than the full dataset, which is much faster and often finds better solutions.
  • Momentum carries some direction from previous steps, helping escape shallow local minima and noise.
  • Modern optimisers like Adam adapt the learning rate per parameter automatically, which is why they are the default in most deep learning.

A concrete example

Training a text classifier: the model reads a batch of 32 reviews, predicts sentiment, computes how wrong it was (loss), calculates the gradient of that loss with respect to every weight, then nudges every weight slightly in the direction that would have reduced the loss. After 10,000 such steps on different batches, the model has learned useful patterns.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Batch size affects gradient noise and memory; learning rate schedule affects convergence speed and final quality; optimiser choice affects training stability.

Hands-on lab

Task: Draw a simple curve (like a bowl shape). Mark a starting point on one side. Draw five arrows showing gradient descent steps toward the minimum. Now add a second curve with a false minimum — where would gradient descent get stuck?

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production signal: monitor the training loss curve. Smooth steady decrease is healthy. Spikes or plateau may indicate learning rate issues, bad batches, or data problems.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Gradient descent does not find the global minimum — it finds a good local minimum, which is usually sufficient for deep networks.
  • A training loss that reaches near zero is not a goal — it likely means overfitting.
  • Gradient descent does not make the model 'understand' anything. It minimises a number. The intelligence comes from what that number measures.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Gradient descent?

  • Correct answer: Gradient descent does not find the global minimum — it finds a good local minimum, which is usually sufficient for deep networks.
  • Once Gradient descent is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Gradient descent into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: Production signal: monitor the training loss curve. Smooth steady decrease is healthy. Spikes or plateau may indicate learning rate issues, bad batches, or data problems.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Gradient descent in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Backpropagation

Backpropagation is the algorithm that computes how much each weight in a neural network contributed to the prediction error, so gradient descent knows which direction to adjust each weight.

Why this matters

Backpropagation matters because without it, training large networks from data would be computationally infeasible.

In plain terms

Backpropagation is like a post-match debrief. After the team loses (makes a wrong prediction), the coach works backwards through every decision made in the game and assigns credit or blame to each player (weight). Players who made bad decisions get coached harder.

How it works

Internal flow: loss → output layer gradient → hidden layer gradients (via chain rule) → weight updates. Deeper layers get gradients that are products of many terms.

  • The chain rule of calculus allows the gradient of the final loss to be decomposed into the contributions of every weight in every layer.
  • Forward pass: inputs flow through layers to produce a prediction and loss. Backward pass: gradients flow from the loss back through layers to every weight.
  • Gradients can vanish (become tiny) in very deep networks, making early layers learn very slowly. This was a major problem before residual connections and good initialisations.
  • Gradients can explode (become huge) in RNNs, causing numerical instability. Gradient clipping limits the maximum gradient magnitude.
  • Automatic differentiation (autograd) in PyTorch and JAX computes backpropagation automatically — you write the forward pass, and the framework handles gradients.

A concrete example

A three-layer network predicts house price incorrectly. Backpropagation starts at the loss (e.g. predicted $400k, actual $300k — error is $100k), moves backward through the output layer, then the hidden layers, computing how much each weight contributed to the $100k error. Each weight is then adjusted in proportion to its contribution.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Deeper networks are more expressive but gradients weaken with depth unless you use residual connections, normalisation, or careful initialisation.

Hands-on lab

Task: Sketch a two-layer network with two inputs, two hidden neurons, and one output. Trace what information flows forward (prediction) and what flows backward (gradients). Label where vanishing gradients could be a problem.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production-relevant: vanishing gradients are why LSTMs, GRUs, and residual networks were invented. If your model's early layers are not learning, gradient flow is the first thing to check.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Backpropagation does not 'understand' which features are meaningful — it blindly follows the gradient of a loss function.
  • Automatic differentiation is not the same as symbolic differentiation — it computes numerical gradient values, not formulas.
  • Backpropagation does not require the network to be differentiable everywhere — methods like subgradients handle non-smooth activations like ReLU.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Backpropagation?

  • Correct answer: Backpropagation does not 'understand' which features are meaningful — it blindly follows the gradient of a loss function.
  • Once Backpropagation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Backpropagation into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: Production-relevant: vanishing gradients are why LSTMs, GRUs, and residual networks were invented. If your model's early layers are not learning, gradient flow is the first thing to check.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Backpropagation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Activation functions

Activation functions introduce non-linearity into neural networks. Without them, stacking layers would just be matrix multiplication — the entire network would collapse into a single linear transformation.

Why this matters

Activation functions matter because they determine whether a neural network can learn complex, non-linear patterns at all.

In plain terms

Activation functions are like decision gates at each neuron: 'fire or don't fire, and how strongly.' Without them, every layer is just multiplying numbers — no matter how many layers you add, the result is still just a straight line.

How it works

Internal flow: each neuron computes a weighted sum of its inputs, adds a bias, then passes the result through its activation function before sending it to the next layer.

  • ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit): outputs 0 for negative inputs, passes positive inputs unchanged. Fast to compute, works well in practice, and avoids the vanishing gradient problem for positive activations.
  • Sigmoid: squashes any value to 0–1. Used in binary classification output layers. Has vanishing gradient problems in deep hidden layers.
  • Softmax: converts a vector of numbers into a probability distribution (all values sum to 1). Standard for multi-class classification output layers.
  • GELU (Gaussian Error Linear Unit): a smooth version of ReLU used in transformers. Empirically works better for language models.
  • The choice of activation function matters more for hidden layers than output layers. The output layer activation depends on the task: sigmoid for binary, softmax for multi-class, linear for regression.

A concrete example

In an image classifier, ReLU activation in hidden layers allows the network to detect edges, shapes, and objects. Negative values (not a detected feature) become zero. Positive values (feature detected) pass through and grow stronger in later layers. The final softmax layer converts the scores for each class into probabilities like {cat: 0.82, dog: 0.15, bird: 0.03}.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: ReLU is fast but can cause 'dead neurons' (always outputting zero). Sigmoid is smooth but has vanishing gradients. GELU is expensive but works well for transformers.

Hands-on lab

Task: On paper, compute ReLU for these values: -3, 0, 2.5, -0.1, 7. Now compute sigmoid for the same values (sigmoid(x) = 1/(1+e^-x), approximate it). What is the main difference in how these two functions handle large positive values?

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production signal: if large portions of a layer's neurons always output zero (dead ReLU), your learning rate may be too high or initialisation is bad.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Adding more layers without non-linear activations is equivalent to having a single layer — the network cannot learn more complex patterns.
  • Sigmoid and tanh are rarely used in hidden layers of modern deep networks due to vanishing gradients — they are mostly used in output layers or gates.
  • The activation function is a design choice that affects both training dynamics and the types of patterns the model can represent.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Activation functions?

  • Once Activation functions is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Adding more layers without non-linear activations is equivalent to having a single layer — the network cannot learn more complex patterns.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Activation functions into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: Production signal: if large portions of a layer's neurons always output zero (dead ReLU), your learning rate may be too high or initialisation is bad.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Activation functions in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Regularization

Regularization is a set of techniques that constrain a model during training to reduce overfitting, trading a small amount of training-set accuracy for better generalization to new data.

In plain terms

It is like a coach who limits how perfectly a student can memorize practice questions, forcing them to actually learn the underlying pattern instead of the exact answers.

How it works

In practice it comes down to adding a penalty to the loss function based on weight magnitude, L1 or L2 regularization, randomly disabling neurons during training with dropout, or stopping training early once validation performance stops improving, each discouraging the model from fitting noise in the training data.

A concrete example

A model prone to overfitting on a small dataset applies dropout, randomly zeroing 20 percent of neurons on each training pass, which prevents any single neuron from becoming overly specialized to specific training examples.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Stronger regularization improves generalization to new data, but too much can prevent the model from learning genuinely useful patterns, underfitting even the training data

Try it in code

function dropout(activations, rate = 0.2, training = true) {
  if (!training) return activations;
  return activations.map(a => (Math.random() < rate ? 0 : a / (1 - rate)));
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one model you're training or have access to, describe one regularization technique you would add if it shows signs of overfitting, and how you'd tell if the strength is right.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Regularization is not a fix applied after a model overfits; it's a training-time constraint that has to be tuned alongside model capacity and data size, and picking a fixed regularization strength without validation testing is itself a common mistake.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Regularization?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Regularization is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Regularization is not a fix applied after a model overfits; it's a training-time constraint that has to be tuned alongside model capacity and data size, and picking a fixed regularization strength without validation testing is itself a common mistake.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Regularization in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift

CNNs

Convolutional Neural Networks are an architecture that applies small, shared filters across an input, exploiting the assumption that local spatial patterns matter and the same pattern can appear anywhere in the input, making them well suited to images.

In plain terms

It is like scanning a photo with a small magnifying glass looking for the same specific pattern, edge, color, texture, everywhere across the image, rather than examining every pixel independently with no shared knowledge.

How it works

In practice it comes down to sliding small learned filters across the input to produce feature maps, stacking multiple convolutional layers so early layers detect simple patterns like edges and later layers combine those into more complex shapes and objects, with pooling layers reducing spatial resolution between stages.

A concrete example

An early CNN layer might learn filters that detect horizontal and vertical edges, while a much later layer combines many such features to recognize a specific object like a face or a stop sign.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Sharing filters across spatial positions dramatically reduces the number of parameters compared to a fully connected network on the same image, but this spatial-locality assumption can hurt performance on data where that assumption doesn't hold, like unordered tabular data

Try it in code

function conv2d(image, filter) {
  // Same small filter slides across every position — shared weights
  return slideWindow(image, filter.shape).map(patch => dotProduct(patch, filter));
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Explain in your own words why sharing the same filter across every position in an image uses far fewer parameters than connecting every input pixel to every neuron individually.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A CNN's filters are not manually designed to detect specific features like edges; the filter values are learned from data during training, and what each filter ends up detecting is discovered, not programmed.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about CNNs?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once CNNs is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A CNN's filters are not manually designed to detect specific features like edges; the filter values are learned from data during training, and what each filter ends up detecting is discovered, not programmed.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain CNNs in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition

RNNs

Recurrent Neural Networks process sequences by maintaining a hidden state updated at each step, carrying information from earlier elements forward, which made them the standard approach for sequential data before transformers became dominant.

In plain terms

It is like reading a book one word at a time while keeping a running mental summary of everything read so far, rather than being able to glance back at the whole page at once.

How it works

In practice it comes down to processing a sequence one element at a time, updating a hidden state at each step that combines the current input with the previous hidden state, so information from earlier steps influences later ones, though this influence weakens over long distances.

A concrete example

An RNN processing a long sentence can struggle to relate a pronoun near the end back to a noun mentioned many words earlier, since the relevant information has to survive being repeatedly compressed into the hidden state across every intermediate step.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: RNNs process sequences with a compact, fixed-size hidden state and less memory than attention over the full sequence, but they process one step at a time, which is slower to train than transformers' fully parallel processing, and they lose long-range information more easily

Try it in code

function rnnStep(input, prevHidden, W) {
  return tanh(matmul(W.input, input) + matmul(W.hidden, prevHidden));
  // hidden state compresses everything seen so far into one fixed vector
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Explain why an RNN might struggle to connect a word at the very end of a long paragraph to a related word at the very beginning, in terms of how its hidden state works.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • RNNs are not obsolete because they're conceptually wrong for sequences; transformers largely replaced them because attention parallelizes better and preserves long-range dependencies more reliably, not because the recurrent idea itself was flawed.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about RNNs?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: RNNs are not obsolete because they're conceptually wrong for sequences; transformers largely replaced them because attention parallelizes better and preserves long-range dependencies more reliably, not because the recurrent idea itself was flawed.
  • Once RNNs is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain RNNs in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition

Optimization tricks

Optimization tricks are practical techniques, learning rate schedules, gradient clipping, momentum-based optimizers, that make training deep networks faster and more stable beyond plain gradient descent.

In plain terms

It is like adjusting your driving technique, easing off the accelerator near a turn, gradually building speed on a straightaway, rather than always pressing the pedal at one fixed rate regardless of the road.

How it works

In practice it comes down to using an optimizer like Adam that adapts the effective learning rate per parameter based on recent gradient history, scheduling the learning rate to start higher and decay over training, and clipping gradients that grow too large to prevent a single bad batch from destabilizing training.

A concrete example

A training run using a learning rate warm-up gradually increases the learning rate over the first few hundred steps, avoiding the instability that can occur if a large learning rate is applied to randomly initialized weights immediately.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Adaptive optimizers and learning rate schedules generally train faster and more reliably than plain gradient descent, but they add hyperparameters, like the schedule shape or clipping threshold, that themselves need tuning for a specific model and dataset

Try it in code

const schedule = {
  warmupSteps: 500,
  learningRate: (step) => step < 500 ? baseLR * (step / 500) : baseLR * decay(step),
  gradientClipNorm: 1.0
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real training run you're familiar with, or a hypothetical one, identify one optimization trick that would most likely help, and explain what specific instability or slowness it addresses.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Optimization tricks are not just minor implementation details that any configuration would work equally well without; skipping gradient clipping or a learning rate schedule on a genuinely difficult training run can be the difference between converging and diverging entirely.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Optimization tricks?

  • Once Optimization tricks is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Optimization tricks are not just minor implementation details that any configuration would work equally well without; skipping gradient clipping or a learning rate schedule on a genuinely difficult training run can be the difference between converging and diverging entirely.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Optimization tricks in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

Hardware basics

Hardware basics for deep learning covers the fundamental difference between CPUs and GPUs and why AI training and inference workloads specifically benefit from specialized, highly parallel processors rather than general-purpose ones.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between a single highly skilled chef doing everything sequentially, a CPU, versus a large kitchen brigade all doing the same simple chopping task simultaneously, a GPU; the second approach wins decisively when the work is millions of simple, repetitive, parallelizable operations.

For non-technical readers

The budget-relevant fact here: specialized AI hardware, GPUs and similar chips, is expensive, and it's the single biggest line item in many AI infrastructure budgets. When someone proposes training a custom AI model in-house, a fair non-technical question is what does the compute cost look like, and have we compared that to just using an existing AI provider's API instead of buying or renting our own hardware? Often, renting access to someone else's model is dramatically cheaper than the hardware and expertise required to train your own from scratch.

How it works

In practice it comes down to deep learning training and inference is dominated by large-scale matrix multiplication, an operation that's highly parallelizable, so GPUs and specialized AI accelerators, which have thousands of simple cores working simultaneously, dramatically outperform CPUs, which have few but more flexible cores, for this specific type of workload.

A concrete example

Training a large image classification model might take weeks on a CPU but only hours or days on a modern GPU, because the underlying matrix math parallelizes so effectively across the GPU's many cores.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: GPUs and specialized AI hardware dramatically speed up AI workloads, but they're significantly more expensive to buy or rent than general-purpose CPU compute, so hardware choice is a real cost lever, not just a performance one

Hands-on lab

Task: Explain in plain terms why a task made of millions of simple, repeated calculations benefits more from many simple parallel processors than from one very fast, flexible processor.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • More powerful hardware is not a substitute for a well-designed model or good data; hardware determines how fast and how cheaply you can train or run a model, not whether the underlying approach or data is actually sound.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Hardware basics?

  • Once Hardware basics is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: More powerful hardware is not a substitute for a well-designed model or good data; hardware determines how fast and how cheaply you can train or run a model, not whether the underlying approach or data is actually sound.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Hardware basics in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Training loops

A training loop is the repeated cycle of feeding a batch of data through the model, computing loss, running backpropagation, and updating weights, repeated across many batches and epochs until the model converges or a stopping criterion is met.

In plain terms

It is like a factory's repeated production cycle: build one unit, inspect it, adjust the machine settings based on the defect found, then repeat with the next unit.

How it works

In practice it comes down to sampling a batch of training data, running a forward pass to get predictions, computing the loss against true labels, running backpropagation to get gradients, updating weights via the optimizer, and periodically evaluating on a held-out validation set to track real progress, repeated until a stopping condition is reached.

A concrete example

A training loop processes batches of 32 images at a time, updates weights after each batch, and every epoch pauses to measure accuracy on a validation set that was never used for training, stopping when validation accuracy stops improving.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Larger batch sizes make each step's gradient estimate more stable and can use hardware more efficiently, but they require more memory and sometimes need learning rate adjustments to train as effectively as smaller batches

Try it in code

for (const batch of dataLoader) {
  const preds = model.forward(batch.x);
  const loss = lossFn(preds, batch.y);
  const grads = backprop(loss);
  optimizer.step(grads);
  if (step % evalEvery === 0) evaluate(model, validationSet);
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Sketch the exact steps of a training loop for one small model, including where validation evaluation fits and what would trigger stopping training early.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Watching training loss decrease is not sufficient evidence a model is improving in a way that matters; only validation performance on data the model hasn't been trained on tells you whether it's actually learning to generalize rather than memorizing.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Training loops?

  • Correct answer: Watching training loss decrease is not sufficient evidence a model is improving in a way that matters; only validation performance on data the model hasn't been trained on tells you whether it's actually learning to generalize rather than memorizing.
  • Once Training loops is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Training loops in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

Distributed training

Distributed training splits a model's training workload across multiple GPUs or machines, either by dividing the data, data parallelism, or dividing the model itself, model parallelism, to train faster or handle models too large for a single device.

In plain terms

It is like a large printing job split across multiple printers running simultaneously, versus one printer handling the entire job alone, either splitting the pages, data parallelism, or splitting a single oversized document across machines, model parallelism.

How it works

In practice it comes down to data parallelism running the same model on multiple devices with different data batches and synchronizing gradients afterward, while model parallelism splits the model's layers or components across devices when the model itself is too large to fit on one device, each approach with different communication overhead.

A concrete example

A large model too big to fit in a single GPU's memory is split across four GPUs, with each holding a portion of the model's layers, while a smaller model that fits easily is instead replicated across the same four GPUs with each processing a different data batch.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Distributing training speeds up large training runs and enables training models too big for one device, but coordinating multiple devices adds real communication overhead and engineering complexity that isn't worth it for models that train fast enough on a single device

Try it in code

// Data parallelism: same model, different data shards
const gradients = await Promise.all(
  shards.map(shard => worker.computeGradients(model, shard))
);
model.applyGradients(averageGradients(gradients));

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real or hypothetical large model, decide whether it would need model parallelism, data parallelism, or neither, and justify the choice based on whether it fits on a single device.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Adding more GPUs to a training run does not always produce a proportional speedup; communication overhead between devices can eat into the gains, especially if the workload isn't large enough to justify the coordination cost.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Distributed training?

  • Once Distributed training is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Adding more GPUs to a training run does not always produce a proportional speedup; communication overhead between devices can eat into the gains, especially if the workload isn't large enough to justify the coordination cost.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Distributed training in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • NVIDIA Triton Inference Server docs

Model serving

Model serving is the infrastructure that runs a trained deep learning model against live requests, handling batching, hardware allocation, and latency requirements, distinct from the training process that produced the model.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between a chef perfecting a recipe in a test kitchen versus the full restaurant operation needed to serve that dish reliably to customers every night.

How it works

In practice it comes down to deploying a trained model behind a serving layer that batches concurrent requests for hardware efficiency, selects appropriate hardware such as GPU or CPU based on the model's size and latency requirements, and monitors latency and error rates in production.

A concrete example

A real-time image classification service batches multiple incoming requests together before running them through the model on a GPU, improving hardware utilization compared to processing each request one at a time.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Batching multiple requests together improves throughput and hardware efficiency, but it adds a small latency delay while waiting to fill a batch, which matters for latency-sensitive single-request use cases

Try it in code

const batchQueue = new RequestBatcher({ maxBatchSize: 32, maxWaitMs: 10 });
app.post("/predict", async (req, res) => {
  const result = await batchQueue.add(req.body); // batched for GPU efficiency
  res.json(result);
});

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real deep learning model, decide whether request batching would help or hurt its specific latency requirements, and explain why.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A model that trains and evaluates well offline is not guaranteed to serve well in production; real-time serving introduces latency, batching, and hardware constraints that training and offline evaluation don't have to account for.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Model serving?

  • Once Model serving is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A model that trains and evaluates well offline is not guaranteed to serve well in production; real-time serving introduces latency, batching, and hardware constraints that training and offline evaluation don't have to account for.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Model serving in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • NVIDIA Triton Inference Server docs

DL lab

The deep learning lab is a hands-on exercise to train a small network with one regularization technique, deliberately compare it against an unregularized version, and observe the difference in the train-validation gap.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to training the same small network twice, once without regularization and once with a technique like dropout, and comparing training loss versus validation loss for both to see the overfitting gap directly.

It is like running a controlled experiment with and without one variable changed, to see its actual effect rather than assuming it works.

The brief

Train a small network with and without one regularization technique, and compare the gap between training and validation performance in each case.

A worked reference

A lab might train a small image classifier on a limited dataset twice, once with no dropout and once with 20 percent dropout, and compare the gap between training and validation accuracy in each case.

Starting point in code

const withoutDropout = trainModel({ dropout: 0 });
const withDropout = trainModel({ dropout: 0.2 });
console.log({
  withoutDropout: { train: withoutDropout.trainAcc, val: withoutDropout.valAcc },
  withDropout: { train: withDropout.trainAcc, val: withDropout.valAcc }
});

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Running the same training twice to compare with and without a technique takes more compute time than training once, but it's the only way to see the actual effect rather than assuming a technique like dropout is helping

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • The lab is not complete once a model trains successfully with regularization added; the point is comparing it against the unregularized version to see whether it actually reduced the train-validation gap.

Going deeper

References

  • Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift

Mini project

The mini project ships a small, complete deep learning model for one real task, with a documented architecture choice, regularization applied and justified, and a genuine train/validation evaluation.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining an architecture choice appropriate to the data type, at least one regularization technique with a stated reason, and a documented train/validation split evaluation into one complete, reusable project.

It is like building and validating one working prototype device, complete with the safety margins that make it trustworthy, not just a version that works once in ideal conditions.

The brief

Train a small deep learning model on one real dataset with a genuine train/validation split, apply one regularization technique, and report the results with a real explanation of any train/validation gap.

A worked reference

A mini project might train a small CNN on a limited image dataset, apply dropout to address observed overfitting, and report both training and validation accuracy with the gap explained.

Starting point in code

export const smallClassifier = {
  architecture: "3-layer CNN",
  regularization: "dropout 0.2, added after observing train/val gap",
  evaluation: { trainAcc: 0.94, valAcc: 0.89 }
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A small, fully validated project with regularization and honest evaluation is more valuable than a larger, unregularized model that was never checked for overfitting

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • High training accuracy alone is not evidence a deep learning mini project succeeded; without validation performance and a check for overfitting, a model with excellent training accuracy could simply have memorized the data.

Going deeper

References

  • Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete deep learning solution for a real problem: architecture choice with justification, a training loop with regularization and optimization tricks, a distributed training plan if needed, and a model-serving plan including latency budget.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a justified architecture choice, a documented training loop with regularization and optimization decisions, a distributed training plan scoped to the model's actual size, and a production serving design with a latency budget into one coherent, documented system.

It is like designing not just a working prototype device, but the full manufacturing, quality-testing, and deployment plan needed to ship it reliably at scale.

The brief

Write a one-page deep learning solution design for a real task: architecture choice with justification, one regularization technique, and a production latency budget and serving plan.

A worked reference

A capstone for a real-time image moderation classifier might specify a lightweight CNN architecture chosen for its latency budget, dropout regularization justified by an observed overfitting gap, single-GPU training since the model is small, and a serving plan with request batching.

Starting point in code

const solutionDesign = {
  architecture: "lightweight CNN, justified by latency budget",
  regularization: "dropout 0.2",
  training: "single-GPU, no distribution needed at this model size",
  serving: "batched inference, 32 requests per batch"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A complete capstone spanning architecture, training, and serving takes substantially more work than training a model alone, but it reflects what's actually required to ship a deep learning system reliably in production

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A deep learning project is not complete once a model achieves good validation accuracy in a notebook; without a serving plan and a latency budget, there is no path from a trained model to a reliable production system.

Going deeper

References

  • Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
  • NVIDIA Triton Inference Server docs

Interview pack

The deep learning interview pack prepares you to explain regularization, architecture choices like CNNs and RNNs, and training stability tricks with concrete reasoning, backed by the ability to diagnose a described training problem.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain not just what a machine's safety features do, but how you'd actually diagnose it if it started producing defective output.

How it works

In practice it comes down to practicing clear, first-principles explanations of regularization, architecture trade-offs, and optimization tricks, paired with a concrete story about diagnosing or fixing a real or realistic deep learning training issue.

A concrete example

A strong answer to why is this model overfitting badly names specific regularization techniques to try, explains the mechanism behind each, and describes how you'd verify which one actually helped using validation data.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const diagnosisOrder = [
  "check the train/validation gap (is it overfitting or underfitting?)",
  "if overfitting: try dropout, weight decay, or more data",
  "if underfitting: increase model capacity or train longer",
  "only then consider a different architecture"
];

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Practice explaining, out loud, how you would diagnose and fix a described case of a model that performs well on training data but poorly on validation data.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing the names of regularization techniques or architectures is not the same as being able to reason about which one fits a specific described problem; interviewers often present a symptom and expect you to reason toward the right technique, not recite definitions.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • Correct answer: Knowing the names of regularization techniques or architectures is not the same as being able to reason about which one fits a specific described problem; interviewers often present a symptom and expect you to reason toward the right technique, not recite definitions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization

Neural Networks

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: A neural network is like a panel of tiny voters. Each neuron votes weakly based on the signals it sees, and layers combine those votes into a final decision. Understanding neurons, weights, and layers makes training behavior less mysterious.

Neuron model

A neuron in a neural network is a small computational unit that takes a weighted sum of its inputs, adds a bias term, and passes the result through a nonlinear activation function, producing one output value that feeds into the next layer.

In plain terms

It is like a single voter who weighs several pieces of evidence with different importance, adds their own baseline lean, and then decides yes or no rather than reporting a raw undecided score.

How it works

In practice it comes down to computing output equals activation of the sum of each input multiplied by its weight plus a bias term, where the weights and bias are learned parameters and the activation function introduces the nonlinearity that lets networks model more than simple linear relationships.

A concrete example

A single neuron deciding whether an email is spam might weight the presence of the word free positively, weight a known sender domain negatively, sum those weighted signals plus a bias, and pass the result through an activation function to produce a spam-likelihood signal.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A single neuron can only represent a limited, essentially linear-plus-threshold decision boundary; real predictive power comes from combining many neurons across layers, not from making any one neuron more complex

Try it in code

function neuron(inputs, weights, bias, activation) {
  const z = inputs.reduce((sum, x, i) => sum + x * weights[i], bias);
  return activation(z); // e.g. sigmoid, relu
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Compute by hand the output of one neuron with two inputs, given specific weights, a bias, and a simple activation function like a step function, for one example input.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A neuron's weights are not manually assigned meaning like this weight represents spam words; they're learned jointly through training and their individual values are rarely individually interpretable in isolation.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Neuron model?

  • Correct answer: A neuron's weights are not manually assigned meaning like this weight represents spam words; they're learned jointly through training and their individual values are rarely individually interpretable in isolation.
  • Once Neuron model is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Neuron model in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition

Layers

Layers are groups of neurons that process the same input in parallel and pass their combined output forward to the next layer, forming the depth and structure of a neural network.

In plain terms

It is like an assembly line where each station transforms the product a bit further, and a deeper line with more stations can build more complex products than a single station ever could.

How it works

In practice it comes down to an input layer receiving raw features, one or more hidden layers each transforming the previous layer's output through weighted sums and activations, and an output layer producing the final prediction in the task-appropriate format, such as a probability distribution for classification.

A concrete example

An image classifier's early layers might learn to detect simple edges and colors, middle layers combine those into shapes and textures, and later layers combine shapes into recognizing whole objects like a specific animal.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Deeper networks with more layers can represent more complex functions and patterns, but they are harder to train, more prone to overfitting on limited data, and require more compute per prediction

Try it in code

function denseLayer(input, weightsMatrix, biases, activation) {
  return weightsMatrix.map((weights, i) => neuron(input, weights, biases[i], activation));
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Sketch a 3-layer network for one simple real task, and describe in plain terms what kind of pattern each layer is likely learning to detect.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Adding more layers to a network is not a free way to improve accuracy; beyond a certain depth without enough data or proper training techniques, additional layers can make training harder and actually hurt performance rather than help it.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Layers?

  • Once Layers is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Adding more layers to a network is not a free way to improve accuracy; beyond a certain depth without enough data or proper training techniques, additional layers can make training harder and actually hurt performance rather than help it.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Layers in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition

Forward pass

A forward pass is the process of pushing an input through every layer of a neural network in sequence, from input to output, producing the network's prediction for that specific input.

In plain terms

It is like tracing water flowing through a series of connected pipes and valves from the source to the tap, following exactly one path through the whole system for one specific flow of water.

How it works

In practice it comes down to computing each layer's output from the previous layer's output in order, applying weights, biases, and activation functions at each step, until the final layer produces the network's output, which is then compared against the true label to compute a loss value.

A concrete example

For an image of a handwritten digit, the forward pass computes activations through each convolutional and fully connected layer in sequence, ending in an output layer that assigns a probability to each possible digit, 0 through 9.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A forward pass alone can only produce a prediction; it says nothing about how wrong that prediction is or how to improve it, which is why training also requires computing loss and running backpropagation afterward

Try it in code

function forwardPass(input, layers) {
  return layers.reduce((activations, layer) => layer.forward(activations), input);
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Trace the forward pass for one simple 2-layer network by hand: compute the hidden layer's output from a sample input, then compute the final output from that hidden layer's output.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A forward pass is not the same as training; running a forward pass through a network with random, untrained weights produces a real but essentially meaningless output, since no learning has happened yet.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Forward pass?

  • Correct answer: A forward pass is not the same as training; running a forward pass through a network with random, untrained weights produces a real but essentially meaningless output, since no learning has happened yet.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Forward pass is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Forward pass in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition

Backpropagation internals

Backpropagation internals describe how the chain rule from calculus is applied layer by layer, from the output back to the input, to compute exactly how much each individual weight contributed to the network's final prediction error.

In plain terms

It is like tracing a mistake in a group project backward through every step of the process, assigning specific responsibility to each person's specific contribution rather than blaming the whole team equally.

How it works

In practice it comes down to computing the loss gradient with respect to the output layer's weights first, then propagating that gradient backward through each preceding layer using the chain rule, so that each weight throughout the network receives a gradient signal proportional to its actual contribution to the final error.

A concrete example

If a network wrongly predicts a cat image as a dog, backpropagation computes exactly how much each weight in every layer, from the final classification layer back to the earliest edge-detecting layer, contributed to that specific wrong answer, and by how much each should be adjusted.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Backpropagation is efficient because it reuses intermediate computations from the forward pass rather than recomputing gradients from scratch for every weight individually, but very deep networks can suffer from vanishing or exploding gradients as the chain rule multiplies many terms together across many layers

Try it in code

// Chain rule, one layer at a time (simplified)
function backprop(lossGrad, layers) {
  let grad = lossGrad;
  for (const layer of [...layers].reverse()) {
