    grad = layer.backward(grad); // dL/dW_layer computed here
  }
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For a tiny 2-layer network with made-up numbers, manually compute the gradient of the loss with respect to one specific weight in the first layer, applying the chain rule step by step.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Backpropagation does not directly update weights by itself; it only computes the gradient, the direction and magnitude of adjustment needed, while a separate optimization step, like gradient descent, actually applies that gradient to update the weights.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Backpropagation internals?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Backpropagation internals is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Backpropagation does not directly update weights by itself; it only computes the gradient, the direction and magnitude of adjustment needed, while a separate optimization step, like gradient descent, actually applies that gradient to update the weights.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Backpropagation internals in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition

Initialization

Initialization is the choice of starting values for a neural network's weights before training begins, which meaningfully affects whether training converges quickly, slowly, or fails entirely due to vanishing or exploding signals early on.

In plain terms

It is like the starting position in a negotiation; starting from a wildly unreasonable position makes it much harder to reach a good outcome, even if the process afterward is sound.

How it works

In practice it comes down to sampling initial weights from a distribution scaled according to the number of inputs and outputs of each layer, such as Xavier or He initialization, so that the variance of activations and gradients stays roughly stable as signals pass forward and backward through many layers.

A concrete example

Initializing all weights to exactly zero would make every neuron in a layer compute the identical output and receive the identical gradient, so the network would never break the symmetry between neurons and effectively behave as if it had far fewer parameters than it does.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Careful, scale-aware initialization makes deep networks train faster and more reliably, but poor initialization can be partially compensated for by other techniques like normalization layers, at the cost of a slower or less stable training process

Try it in code

// He initialization for ReLU networks
function heInit(fanIn) {
  const std = Math.sqrt(2 / fanIn);
  return () => gaussianRandom(0, std);
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Explain why initializing every weight in a layer to the same value, even a nonzero one, would still be a problem for training, and what specifically would fail to happen during learning.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Random initialization does not mean the specific random values don't matter; the scale and distribution of the initial values are chosen deliberately based on network architecture, and naive random initialization, like drawing from too wide or too narrow a range, can make deep networks fail to train at all.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Initialization?

  • Correct answer: Random initialization does not mean the specific random values don't matter; the scale and distribution of the initial values are chosen deliberately based on network architecture, and naive random initialization, like drawing from too wide or too narrow a range, can make deep networks fail to train at all.
  • Once Initialization is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Initialization in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift

Normalization

Normalization in neural network training rescales the intermediate activations within a network, typically to have zero mean and unit variance per layer or per batch, which stabilizes and speeds up training of deep networks.

In plain terms

It is like recalibrating an instrument partway through a long measurement process, so small drifts in scale don't compound and throw off every reading that follows.

How it works

In practice it comes down to computing the mean and variance of activations across a batch or layer, rescaling those activations to a standard distribution, and applying learned scale and shift parameters so the network can still represent the original range if that turns out to be optimal.

A concrete example

Batch normalization applied after a layer in an image classifier keeps activation distributions stable as training progresses, which lets the network use a higher learning rate and converge faster than it would without normalization.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Normalization layers make deep networks measurably easier and faster to train, but they add computational overhead per layer and introduce a dependency on batch statistics that can behave differently between training and inference if not handled carefully

Try it in code

function batchNorm(activations, gamma, beta, eps = 1e-5) {
  const mean = mean(activations), variance = variance(activations);
  return activations.map(a => gamma * (a - mean) / Math.sqrt(variance + eps) + beta);
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Explain what specifically could go wrong during training of a 50-layer network if activation values were allowed to grow or shrink unchecked layer after layer, without any normalization.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Normalization layers are not just a minor implementation detail; removing them from a deep network architecture that was designed and tuned with them can significantly destabilize training, since the network's other hyperparameters were chosen assuming normalization's stabilizing effect.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Normalization?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Normalization is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Normalization layers are not just a minor implementation detail; removing them from a deep network architecture that was designed and tuned with them can significantly destabilize training, since the network's other hyperparameters were chosen assuming normalization's stabilizing effect.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Normalization in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift

Residuals

Residual connections add a layer's input directly to its output, letting the network learn only the difference, or residual, between input and desired output, which makes very deep networks dramatically easier to train.

In plain terms

It is like editing a document by marking only the changes you want to make to the existing text, rather than rewriting the entire document from scratch for every revision.

How it works

In practice it comes down to adding the identity of a layer's input to the output of that layer's transformation, so the layer only needs to learn a residual adjustment rather than the full mapping from scratch, which lets gradients flow through the identity path directly to earlier layers during backpropagation.

A concrete example

A very deep image classification network uses residual connections so that if a particular set of layers isn't helpful for a given input, the network can effectively let the identity path dominate, passing information through mostly unchanged, rather than being forced to learn a full non-trivial transformation at every single layer.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Residual connections make very deep networks trainable where they would otherwise fail due to vanishing gradients, but they add a small amount of extra computation and require the input and output dimensions of a residual block to match or be explicitly reshaped

Try it in code

function residualBlock(x, transform) {
  return add(x, transform(x)); // learn only the residual, not the full mapping
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Explain in your own words why a network with residual connections can be trained successfully at 100+ layers deep, while an equivalent network without them often cannot.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Residual connections are not simply a shortcut that skips computation entirely; the layer still learns and contributes a meaningful transformation, the residual connection just makes it easier for gradients to flow and for the network to default toward the identity when that's the better choice.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Residuals?

  • Correct answer: Residual connections are not simply a shortcut that skips computation entirely; the layer still learns and contributes a meaningful transformation, the residual connection just makes it easier for gradients to flow and for the network to default toward the identity when that's the better choice.
  • Once Residuals is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Residuals in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition

Architectures

Neural network architectures are specific, named patterns of how layers are structured and connected, such as convolutional networks for spatial data or transformers for sequential data, each designed with structural assumptions that fit particular kinds of problems.

In plain terms

It is like choosing between different specialized tools, a scalpel, a saw, a wrench, each shaped for a specific job rather than one universal tool trying to do everything adequately.

How it works

In practice it comes down to each architecture family encoding a specific inductive bias, such as convolutional networks assuming nearby pixels are related and sharing weights across spatial positions, or transformers assuming any token might relate to any other token and using attention to let the model learn those relationships directly from data.

A concrete example

A convolutional network is well suited to image classification because it assumes local spatial patterns matter and reuses the same small filters across the whole image, while a transformer is well suited to language because it can directly model relationships between words that are far apart in a sentence.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: An architecture with structural assumptions that match the data, like convolution for images, trains faster and needs less data than a more general-purpose architecture, but if the assumption doesn't fit the actual data, the same structural bias can hurt performance instead of helping

Try it in code

const architectureFit = {
  images: "convolutional (local spatial pattern assumption)",
  sequences: "transformer (any-token-to-any-token attention)"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For two different real tasks, one involving images and one involving sequential text, explain which architecture family's structural assumption fits each and why.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • There is no single best neural network architecture across all tasks; choosing an architecture is a decision about which structural assumptions actually match your data and problem, not about picking whichever architecture is currently most popular or state-of-the-art in a different domain.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Architectures?

  • Correct answer: There is no single best neural network architecture across all tasks; choosing an architecture is a decision about which structural assumptions actually match your data and problem, not about picking whichever architecture is currently most popular or state-of-the-art in a different domain.
  • Once Architectures is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Architectures in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Attention Is All You Need
  • Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition

Debugging training

Debugging training is the process of diagnosing why a neural network isn't learning well, distinguishing between problems in the data, the architecture, the optimization setup, and the evaluation itself, rather than randomly adjusting hyperparameters.

In plain terms

It is like a mechanic diagnosing why a car won't start by checking fuel, battery, and ignition separately, instead of randomly replacing parts and hoping one fix works.

How it works

In practice it comes down to checking the training loss curve for signs of specific failure modes, such as loss not decreasing at all suggesting a bug or learning-rate problem, loss decreasing on training data but not validation data suggesting overfitting, or loss oscillating wildly suggesting an unstable learning rate, and testing on a small sample the network should easily overfit as a sanity check.

A concrete example

A network that fails to reduce training loss below a high plateau even on a tiny sample of 10 examples, which it should easily be able to memorize, points to a bug in the code or a badly misconfigured learning rate, not a data or generalization problem.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A systematic debugging checklist, sanity checks, loss curve inspection, small-sample overfitting tests, takes more disciplined time upfront than randomly trying different hyperparameters, but it converges on the actual root cause far faster than trial and error

Try it in code

// Sanity check: can the network overfit 10 examples?
const tinyBatch = trainingData.slice(0, 10);
trainOn(tinyBatch, { epochs: 200 });
// If loss doesn't approach zero, it's a bug or learning-rate problem, not a data problem.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Given a network that has flat, unchanging training loss from the very first epoch, list three specific things you would check before considering changing the architecture.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A network that fails to train well is not automatically a sign the architecture is wrong; the most common causes are much more mundane, such as a data loading bug, mismatched labels, or a learning rate that's off by an order of magnitude.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Debugging training?

  • Correct answer: A network that fails to train well is not automatically a sign the architecture is wrong; the most common causes are much more mundane, such as a data loading bug, mismatched labels, or a learning rate that's off by an order of magnitude.
  • Once Debugging training is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Debugging training in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift

Interpretability

Interpretability in neural networks is the practice of understanding what internal representations and features a trained network has learned and why it produces a specific output, rather than treating the network purely as an unexaminable black box.

In plain terms

It is like being able to open up a complex machine and trace which internal gears actually drove a specific outcome, rather than only observing what goes in and what comes out.

How it works

In practice it comes down to techniques such as visualizing which input regions most influenced a specific output through gradient-based attribution, or probing intermediate layer activations to see what concepts they respond to, used to build a human-understandable account of the network's internal decision process.

A concrete example

A saliency map for an image classifier highlights the specific pixels that most influenced its prediction, revealing whether the model is genuinely focusing on the relevant object or spuriously relying on background context, like snow, that happens to correlate with a class in the training data.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Interpretability tools can reveal genuinely useful insight into model behavior and catch spurious correlations, but many interpretability techniques produce approximate or partial explanations that can themselves be misleading if over-trusted as complete accounts of the model's true internal reasoning

Try it in code

function saliencyMap(model, image, predictedClass) {
  const grad = gradientOfOutputWrtInput(model, image, predictedClass);
  return normalize(Math.abs(grad)); // highlights influential pixels
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one image or text classifier you have access to, or a hypothetical one, describe one interpretability check you would run to test whether it's relying on a spurious correlation rather than the genuinely relevant signal.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A plausible-looking saliency map or attribution result is not proof of what the model is actually doing internally; interpretability techniques are approximations, and treating their output as ground truth without skepticism can lead to false confidence in a flawed model.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Interpretability?

  • Correct answer: A plausible-looking saliency map or attribution result is not proof of what the model is actually doing internally; interpretability techniques are approximations, and treating their output as ground truth without skepticism can lead to false confidence in a flawed model.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Interpretability is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interpretability in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition

Latency trade-offs

Latency trade-offs in neural networks are the deliberate design decisions, model size, architecture choice, precision, that trade prediction accuracy for faster inference time, which matters directly whenever a model serves real-time user requests.

In plain terms

It is like choosing between a highly detailed, slow-to-prepare meal and a faster, simpler one when a customer is waiting hungrily at the counter; the right choice depends entirely on how much the wait actually costs you.

How it works

In practice it comes down to reducing model size through fewer layers or parameters, using lower numerical precision like float16 or int8 instead of float32, or choosing an architecture with lower computational complexity, each trading some accuracy for measurably faster inference time per request.

A concrete example

A real-time fraud-detection system might use a smaller, quantized version of a model that runs in 20 milliseconds with a small accuracy cost, rather than the full-precision, more accurate version that takes 300 milliseconds and would delay every transaction unacceptably.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Reducing model size or precision for lower latency almost always costs some accuracy, so the right trade-off point depends entirely on how much latency the actual product can tolerate and how much accuracy loss the specific use case can absorb

Try it in code

const configs = {
  fullPrecision: { dtype: "float32", latencyMs: 300 },
  quantized: { dtype: "int8", latencyMs: 20 } // small accuracy cost
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real-time AI feature, state the maximum latency it can tolerate, and describe one specific technique, such as quantization or a smaller model, you would use to meet that budget.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A larger, more accurate model is not automatically the better choice for a production system; if the added latency pushes response time past what users or downstream systems can tolerate, a smaller, faster, slightly less accurate model can be the objectively better production decision.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Latency trade-offs?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A larger, more accurate model is not automatically the better choice for a production system; if the added latency pushes response time past what users or downstream systems can tolerate, a smaller, faster, slightly less accurate model can be the objectively better production decision.
  • Once Latency trade-offs is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Latency trade-offs in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • vLLM PagedAttention paper

Production serving

Production serving of neural networks is the infrastructure and practices for running a trained model reliably against live traffic, covering batching, hardware allocation, versioning, and fallback behavior when the model or its infrastructure fails.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between a chef who can cook one great meal in a home kitchen, versus the full kitchen operation needed to serve that same meal reliably to hundreds of customers a night.

How it works

In practice it comes down to deploying a trained model behind a serving layer that batches concurrent requests for hardware efficiency, monitors latency and error rates, supports versioned rollouts so a new model can be tested against a fraction of traffic before full rollout, and defines a fallback behavior if the serving infrastructure fails.

A concrete example

A recommendation model is deployed with canary rollout, sending 5 percent of traffic to a newly trained version and comparing its business metrics against the existing production version before gradually increasing its traffic share.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Robust serving infrastructure, canary rollouts, monitoring, fallbacks, reduces the risk of a bad model update causing a widespread production incident, but it adds real infrastructure complexity and engineering time compared to simply swapping a model file directly into production

Try it in code

const canaryRollout = {
  newModelTrafficPercent: 5,
  monitor: "business metric vs. current production model",
  rollbackTrigger: "metric regression beyond 2%"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a canary rollout plan for deploying one new model version to production: what percentage of traffic you'd start with, what metric you'd monitor, and what would trigger a rollback.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A model that performs well in offline evaluation is not guaranteed to behave the same in production serving; differences in real-time input distributions, latency constraints, and infrastructure can all cause a model to underperform its offline metrics once actually serving live traffic.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Production serving?

  • Correct answer: A model that performs well in offline evaluation is not guaranteed to behave the same in production serving; differences in real-time input distributions, latency constraints, and infrastructure can all cause a model to underperform its offline metrics once actually serving live traffic.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Production serving is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Production serving in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • NVIDIA Triton Inference Server docs

Monitoring

Monitoring tracks live AI behavior so teams notice quality, safety, latency, cost, and reliability changes after deployment.

In plain terms

It is like a dashboard for a system whose behavior can drift without code changes.

How it works

In practice it comes down to metrics, alerts, traces, feedback, drift signals, eval sampling, and dashboards.

A concrete example

A dashboard shows answer thumbs-down rate, citation failures, p95 latency, token cost, and guardrail block rate.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More monitoring creates noise unless alerts map to action.

Hands-on lab

Task: Choose five production metrics and one alert threshold for a RAG product.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not monitor only uptime for an AI feature.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Monitoring?

  • Once Monitoring is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not monitor only uptime for an AI feature.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Monitoring in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

NN lab

The neural network lab is a hands-on exercise to train a small network on a simple real dataset, deliberately diagnose one training problem, such as an unstable learning rate or overfitting, and fix it, connecting theory to observable behavior.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to training a small network on a simple dataset, deliberately introducing one common training problem, observing its effect on the training loss curve, and applying the specific fix that addresses that root cause.

It is like a flight simulator exercise where an instructor deliberately introduces one specific failure so you practice recognizing and correcting it under realistic conditions.

The brief

Train a small network on a simple dataset, deliberately set the learning rate too high or too low, observe the loss curve, and then find and apply the specific fix that resolves it.

A worked reference

A lab might train a small network with a learning rate set too high, observe the resulting oscillating or diverging loss curve, then fix it by reducing the learning rate and observe the loss curve stabilize and decrease.

Starting point in code

// Deliberately break it, then fix it
train(model, { learningRate: 10.0 });  // watch loss diverge/oscillate
train(model, { learningRate: 0.001 }); // watch loss stabilize and decrease

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Deliberately breaking training to diagnose a specific problem takes more setup than just training a model successfully on the first try, but it builds the diagnostic skill needed to fix real training failures later

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • The lab is not complete once a model trains successfully; the point is specifically practicing diagnosis, so deliberately introducing and then correctly identifying a specific failure mode is the actual learning objective.

Going deeper

References

  • Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift

Mini project

The mini project ships a small, complete trained neural network for one real task, with a documented architecture choice, a working training loop, and an evaluation showing it actually generalizes beyond the training data.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a specific architecture choice justified for the task, a working training loop with a validation split, and a documented evaluation on held-out data into one reusable, documented project.

It is like building and validating one working prototype device, in a way that documents enough of the process that someone else could build a similar one for a related task.

The brief

Train a small neural network on one real dataset with a genuine train/validation split, and report both training and validation performance, explaining any gap between them.

A worked reference

A mini project might train a small image classifier on a limited dataset, using a validation split to confirm it generalizes rather than just memorizing training examples, and document the specific architecture and hyperparameter choices made.

Starting point in code

const { trainLoss, valLoss } = trainWithValidationSplit(model, data, { valFraction: 0.2 });
console.log({ trainLoss, valLoss, gap: valLoss - trainLoss }); // large gap = overfitting

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A small, fully validated project proves the training process genuinely works, which is more valuable than a larger, more ambitious model that was never properly checked for overfitting or true generalization

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • Low training loss alone is not evidence a mini project succeeded; without checking performance on a held-out validation or test set, a model with excellent training loss could simply have memorized the training data.

Going deeper

References

  • Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete neural network solution for a real problem: architecture choice with justification, a training and debugging plan, an evaluation methodology, and a production serving plan including latency budget and rollout strategy.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a justified architecture choice, a documented training and debugging approach, a rigorous held-out evaluation methodology, and a production serving design with latency budget and canary rollout plan into one coherent, documented system.

It is like designing not just a working prototype device, but the full manufacturing, quality-testing, and rollout plan needed to actually ship it reliably.

The brief

Write a one-page neural network solution design for a real task: architecture choice with justification, evaluation methodology, and a production latency budget and rollout plan.

A worked reference

A capstone for a real-time content-moderation classifier might specify a lightweight architecture chosen for its latency budget, a training plan with a held-out evaluation set representative of production traffic, and a canary rollout plan with a defined rollback trigger.

Starting point in code

const solutionDesign = {
  architecture: "lightweight CNN, justified by latency budget",
  evaluation: "held-out set representative of production traffic",
  serving: "canary rollout, 5% traffic, rollback on regression"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A complete capstone spanning architecture, training, evaluation, and serving takes substantially more work than training a model alone, but it reflects what's actually required to ship a neural network system safely and reliably in production

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A neural network project is not complete once a model achieves good accuracy in an offline notebook; without a serving and rollout plan and a latency budget, there is no path from a trained model to a reliable production system.

Going deeper

References

  • Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
  • NVIDIA Triton Inference Server docs

Interview pack

The neural networks interview pack prepares you to explain how networks actually learn and fail: backpropagation mechanics, why initialization and normalization matter, and how you'd debug a training run that isn't converging, backed by concrete reasoning rather than memorized definitions.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain not just what an engine does, but how you'd actually diagnose it when it won't start.

How it works

In practice it comes down to practicing clear, first-principles explanations of forward pass, backpropagation, initialization, and normalization, paired with a concrete story about diagnosing a real or realistic training failure.

A concrete example

A strong answer to why doesn't this network's loss decrease starts by checking for the most common causes, a data bug, a learning rate issue, or a dead activation function, before ever suggesting an architecture change.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const diagnosisOrder = [
  "check data loading / label correctness",
  "check learning rate (off by 10x is common)",
  "check for dead activations (e.g. all-negative ReLU inputs)",
  "only then consider architecture changes"
];

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Practice explaining, out loud, why a network's training loss might plateau immediately at a high value, working through the most likely causes in order of how common they actually are.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that backpropagation uses the chain rule is not the same as being able to reason through why a specific network isn't training; interviewers often probe with a described symptom and expect you to reason toward likely causes, not recite theory.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • Correct answer: Knowing that backpropagation uses the chain rule is not the same as being able to reason through why a specific network isn't training; interviewers often probe with a described symptom and expect you to reason toward likely causes, not recite theory.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Batch Normalization: Accelerating Deep Network Training by Reducing Internal Covariate Shift

Transformers

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: A transformer is like a meeting where every word can look at every other word before deciding what it means. Attention decides which words deserve focus. Transformers power most modern LLMs, so attention, tokens, and context are core vocabulary.

Why transformers?

Transformers are a neural network architecture that processes entire sequences in parallel using attention mechanisms, replacing the sequential processing of RNNs. They became the foundation of modern LLMs because they scale efficiently with data and compute.

Why this matters

Transformers matter because every modern LLM, embedding model, and multimodal model is a transformer. Understanding the architecture explains capabilities and limitations.

In plain terms

RNNs read text like a person reading word by word, keeping only a summary of what they have read so far. Transformers are more like a group discussion where every word can immediately consult every other word — no information gets lost in a long chain of handoffs.

For non-technical readers

You don't need the engineering details to know why this matters commercially: this is the specific architecture breakthrough, dating to around 2017, that made today's ChatGPT- and Claude-style assistants possible at all. Before it, AI language systems lost track of context in longer text; transformers largely solved that, which is why AI chat tools only became genuinely useful for real business writing and analysis in the last few years, not decades ago. When someone says a product is 'powered by a transformer model,' it's shorthand for 'built on the same family of technology behind ChatGPT and Claude.'

How it works

Internal flow: input tokens → embeddings → positional encoding → repeated attention + feed-forward layers → output. Each layer refines the token representations.

  • RNNs and LSTMs process sequences one token at a time and carry a fixed-size hidden state. Long-range dependencies get compressed and lost.
  • Transformers process all tokens simultaneously with attention. Every token attends to every other token in a single operation.
  • This parallelism means transformers train much faster on GPUs and can scale to billions of parameters.
  • The 'Attention is All You Need' paper (2017) introduced the transformer, originally for machine translation. GPT and BERT showed it generalises to virtually every language task.
  • Modern LLMs (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini) are all transformer-based, specifically decoder-only or encoder-decoder variants.

A concrete example

Translating 'The animal did not cross the street because it was too tired' to French: the model needs to know that 'it' refers to 'animal', not 'street'. An RNN might lose this link over the long sequence. A transformer can directly attend from 'it' back to 'animal' regardless of distance.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Attention is O(n²) in sequence length — doubling context length quadruples compute. This is why context windows have limits and why KV caching is important.

Hands-on lab

Task: Draw the difference between how an RNN and a transformer read the sentence 'The bank by the river was muddy'. Show where each architecture processes the word 'bank' and how it connects to 'river' to resolve the ambiguity.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production implications: transformer inference cost scales with context length and model size. Knowing this helps you make token-budget decisions in product design.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Transformers are not magic — they are still pattern matchers. The 'attention' mechanism is mathematical, not cognitive.
  • More layers are not always better — at some point, the law of diminishing returns applies and compute is better spent on more data or better data.
  • Transformers were not invented for chatbots — the original paper was about translation. The conversational ability comes from the training data and RLHF fine-tuning.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Transformers?

  • Correct answer: Transformers are not magic — they are still pattern matchers. The 'attention' mechanism is mathematical, not cognitive.
  • Once Transformers is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Transformers into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: Production implications: transformer inference cost scales with context length and model size. Knowing this helps you make token-budget decisions in product design.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Transformers in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Attention Is All You Need

Tokenization

Tokenization is the process of splitting text into tokens — sub-word units that the model processes. Each token is mapped to a unique integer ID. The model never sees characters or words directly; it sees sequences of integer IDs.

Why this matters

Tokenisation matters because it determines cost, context limits, multilingual behaviour, and why models struggle with counting letters or characters.

In plain terms

Tokenization is like turning a sentence into a bar code for each word fragment. 'Running' might become ['Run', '##ning'] — two tokens. The model only sees the bar code numbers, not the original words.

How it works

Internal flow: raw text → tokeniser (BPE) → integer ID sequence → embedding lookup → model processing. Detokenisation reverses this on the output side.

  • Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) is the most common tokenisation method. It starts with characters and merges frequent pairs until a vocabulary of ~50,000–100,000 tokens is built.
  • Common words become single tokens ('the', 'is'). Rare words and technical terms are split into multiple tokens ('electroencephalogram' → several tokens).
  • Tokens are not words. A single word can be 1–5 tokens. Code, numbers, non-English languages, and special characters often use more tokens per character.
  • Longer token sequences cost more: model inference cost is approximately linear in token count. A task that takes 100 tokens in English may take 150+ in a less-represented language.
  • The context window (e.g. 128k tokens) limits how much text the model can process in one request. Understanding tokenisation helps predict when you will hit limits.

Topic diagram

Diagram: From text to tokens for Tokenization

A concrete example

The sentence 'The GPT-4 model tokenizes text.' might tokenise as: ['The', ' GPT', '-', '4', ' model', ' token', 'izes', ' text', '.']. That is 9 tokens for 7 words. Counting tokens matters because API costs are per token and long documents require chunking.

Trade-offs

  • Vocabulary size vs sequence length: A larger vocabulary reduces token count but increases embedding-table memory.
  • Exact text fidelity vs normalized text: Aggressive normalization simplifies inputs but can erase code, numbers, or multilingual meaning.
  • Cost vs context quality: Shorter token sequences are cheaper, but over-compressing inputs removes useful signal.

Try it in code

const text = "The GPT-4 model tokenizes text.";
const tokens = ["The", " GPT", "-", "4", " model", " token", "izes", " text", "."];
const tokenIds = [791, 480, 12, 19, 1646, 4037, 4861, 1495, 13];

console.log({
  words: text.split(/\s+/).length,
  tokens: tokens.length,
  estimatedCostUnits: tokenIds.length
});

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Use Tiktokenizer (tiktokenizer.vercel.app) or any tokeniser playground. Paste in: (1) a short English sentence, (2) the same sentence in Japanese, (3) a code snippet, (4) a number like 2847391. Count the tokens for each and explain the differences.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production checklist: measure token counts for your typical inputs, not just character counts. Test with your actual language, code, and domain vocabulary — not just English prose.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • The model does not process characters — it processes token IDs. This is why models often fail at counting letters: 'strawberry' may be three tokens with no character-level signal.
  • Token count ≠ word count. Budget by tokens, not words.
  • Tokenisation is frozen after training. You cannot change the tokeniser without retraining the entire model.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Tokenization?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: The model does not process characters — it processes token IDs. This is why models often fail at counting letters: 'strawberry' may be three tokens with no character-level signal.
  • Once Tokenization is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Tokenization into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: Production checklist: measure token counts for your typical inputs, not just character counts. Test with your actual language, code, and domain vocabulary — not just English prose.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Tokenization in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Vocabulary size vs sequence length" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Attention Is All You Need

Embeddings

In a transformer, embeddings are the learned lookup table that converts each discrete input token into a dense numeric vector, giving the model a continuous representation it can perform math on instead of an arbitrary token ID.

In plain terms

It is like assigning every word in a dictionary a specific location on a map, so that mathematical operations like distance and direction between locations start to reflect real relationships between the words.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a learned embedding matrix with one row per vocabulary token, where looking up a token's ID retrieves its corresponding vector, and these vectors are updated during training so that tokens used in similar contexts end up with similar vector representations.

A concrete example

The token for cat and the token for kitten end up with similar embedding vectors after training, because they appear in similar contexts across the training data, even though their token IDs are unrelated numbers.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A larger embedding dimension can capture more nuanced distinctions between tokens, but it increases the model's parameter count and computation cost, particularly since the embedding matrix scales with both vocabulary size and dimension

Try it in code

const embeddingMatrix = new Float32Array(vocabSize * embedDim);
function embed(tokenId) {
  return embeddingMatrix.slice(tokenId * embedDim, (tokenId + 1) * embedDim);
}
// embed("cat") and embed("kitten") end up close after training

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Explain why the tokens for run and running might end up with more similar embeddings than the tokens for run and bank, in terms of how they're used across typical training text.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Token embeddings inside a transformer are not fixed or hand-assigned; they start as random vectors and are learned jointly with the rest of the model during training, which is why a model trained on a different domain can end up with meaningfully different embeddings for the same token.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Embeddings?

  • Correct answer: Token embeddings inside a transformer are not fixed or hand-assigned; they start as random vectors and are learned jointly with the rest of the model during training, which is why a model trained on a different domain can end up with meaningfully different embeddings for the same token.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Embeddings is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Embeddings in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Attention Is All You Need

Positional encoding

Positional encoding adds information about the position of each token in a sequence. Because transformers process all tokens in parallel, they have no inherent sense of order — positional encoding is how they know token 1 came before token 2.

Why this matters

Positional encoding matters because language is fundamentally ordered — the meaning of a sentence changes when word order changes.

In plain terms

Positional encoding is like numbering the seats in a cinema before scrambling everyone's tickets. The transformer sees all the tickets at once and uses the seat numbers to reconstruct who sat where.

How it works

Internal flow: token embedding + positional encoding → combined representation → fed into attention. The attention mechanism can now use position as one signal among many.

  • Original transformers used sinusoidal positional encodings: fixed mathematical signals added to token embeddings. Each position gets a unique pattern of sine and cosine waves.
  • Learned positional embeddings: the model learns position representations from data. Used in BERT.
  • Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) and Alibi are modern variants that encode relative positions and generalise better to longer sequences than the model was trained on.
  • Without positional encoding, 'dog bites man' and 'man bites dog' would look identical to the transformer — the same tokens, different order.
  • The choice of positional encoding affects how well the model handles sequences longer than those seen during training.

A concrete example

For the sentence 'Alice told Bob she trusted him', the model uses positional information to know that 'she' follows 'Bob' and that 'him' refers back to 'Bob' not 'Alice'. Without position, every arrangement of these words would produce the same representation.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Fixed sinusoidal encoding is simple and generalises to longer sequences in theory but not in practice. Learned embeddings work well but only up to trained length. RoPE handles longer contexts better.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write out the sentence 'The cat sat on the mat' and assign each word a position (0–5). Now remove the words and keep only positions. Explain why the transformer needs to combine both the word identity and the position to make sense of the sentence.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production relevance: when a model fails on very long inputs, positional encoding is one root cause. Newer models using RoPE or ALiBi handle long context better.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Positional encoding is added to the token embedding, not stored separately. The model sees one combined vector, not two.
  • Positional encoding does not tell the model the absolute meaning of each position — it just provides a distinct, consistent signal for each position.
  • Some models (like certain vision transformers) use 2D positional encodings for images — the concept extends beyond 1D sequences.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Positional encoding?

  • Correct answer: Positional encoding is added to the token embedding, not stored separately. The model sees one combined vector, not two.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Positional encoding is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Positional encoding into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: Production relevance: when a model fails on very long inputs, positional encoding is one root cause. Newer models using RoPE or ALiBi handle long context better.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Positional encoding in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Attention Is All You Need

Attention

Attention is a transformer mechanism that lets each token decide which other tokens in the context are most relevant before updating its own representation.

Why this matters

Attention matters because it is the core operation that lets transformers use context instead of treating each token independently.

In plain terms

Attention is like reading a sentence with a highlighter. For each word you are trying to understand, you highlight the other words that give it meaning.

How it works

The internal flow is query/key comparison, score normalisation, value mixing, and repeated refinement across layers.

  • Queries represent what a token is looking for.
  • Keys represent what information each token can offer.
  • Values carry the information that gets mixed into the output.
  • Attention scores compare queries to keys, then softmax turns those scores into weights.
  • The weighted sum of values becomes the updated representation for the token.

Topic diagram

Diagram: Attention in one pass for Attention

A concrete example

In 'the trophy would not fit in the suitcase because it was too large', attention helps connect 'it' to 'trophy' rather than 'suitcase'.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Attention improves quality but can be expensive for long context windows because naive attention cost grows quadratically with sequence length.

Try it in code

function scaledDotProductAttention(query, keys, values) {
  const scores = keys.map((key) => dot(query, key) / Math.sqrt(query.length));
  const weights = softmax(scores);
  return values.reduce((mix, value, i) =>
    mix.map((n, j) => n + weights[i] * value[j]), Array(values[0].length).fill(0));
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Pick a sentence with a pronoun. Mark which earlier words the pronoun depends on, then explain how attention would need to focus to resolve the reference.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production slice should measure latency and answer quality as context length changes, especially for long-document tasks.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Attention is not the same as human understanding. It is a learned weighting operation.
  • More context is not always better if irrelevant tokens distract the model.
  • Attention weights are useful clues, but they are not a complete explanation of model behaviour.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Attention?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Attention is not the same as human understanding. It is a learned weighting operation.
  • Once Attention is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Attention into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: A production slice should measure latency and answer quality as context length changes, especially for long-document tasks.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Attention in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Attention Is All You Need
  • The Illustrated Transformer

Multi-head attention

Multi-head attention runs multiple attention operations in parallel, each learning to focus on different types of relationships in the input. The outputs are combined to create a richer representation.

Why this matters

Multi-head attention matters because single attention cannot simultaneously capture all the different relationship types needed for language understanding.

In plain terms

Single attention is like one expert reviewer reading a document. Multi-head attention is like eight reviewers reading the same document simultaneously, each focused on different aspects — one on grammar, one on facts, one on tone, one on references — then combining their notes.

How it works

Internal flow: input → split into h parallel attention heads (each with smaller dimension) → each head computes attention independently → concatenate all heads → linear projection → output.

  • Each head has its own learned query, key, and value weight matrices. It learns to attend to different relationships.
  • In practice, some heads learn syntactic roles (subject-verb agreement), others learn semantic similarity, others learn coreference ('it' → 'the cat').
  • All heads run in parallel, which is computationally efficient on GPUs.
  • The outputs of all heads are concatenated and projected through a linear layer to produce the final attention output.
  • GPT-3 uses 96 attention heads. More heads allow more relationship types to be captured simultaneously.

A concrete example

Processing 'The lawyer argued the case and she won.' — one head might focus on 'she' → 'lawyer' (coreference), another on 'argued' → 'case' (verb-object relationship), another on sentence structure. Together they produce a rich understanding of who did what.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More heads capture more relationship types but increase compute and memory. Head dimension (d_model / num_heads) must stay large enough for each head to be expressive.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take the sentence 'The professor marked the exam quickly because he was late.' Identify three different relationships a reader tracks to understand it. Label each one with what type of 'head' might be responsible: syntactic, semantic, or coreference.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production relevance: multi-head attention is the main compute cost in transformer inference. Optimisations like Flash Attention and grouped-query attention (GQA) reduce this cost significantly.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Heads do not have fixed, predefined roles — they discover useful patterns from training data. Interpretability research reveals what they learned, but nothing is pre-assigned.
  • Not all heads are equally important. Research shows many heads can be pruned with minimal quality loss — 'head importance' varies by task.
  • Multi-head attention is not the same as an ensemble of models. It is multiple perspectives within a single forward pass.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Multi-head attention?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Heads do not have fixed, predefined roles — they discover useful patterns from training data. Interpretability research reveals what they learned, but nothing is pre-assigned.
  • Once Multi-head attention is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Multi-head attention into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: Production relevance: multi-head attention is the main compute cost in transformer inference. Optimisations like Flash Attention and grouped-query attention (GQA) reduce this cost significantly.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Multi-head attention in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Attention Is All You Need
  • FlashAttention

Feed forward networks

The feed-forward network (FFN) is the second sub-layer in each transformer block. After attention mixes information across tokens, the FFN processes each token independently through two linear transformations with a non-linear activation in between.

Why this matters

FFN layers matter because they store most of the factual knowledge and reasoning patterns learned during pretraining.

In plain terms

If attention is a discussion where every participant hears everyone else, the FFN is each participant going away to think privately about what they just heard — no further exchange, just individual processing and transformation.

How it works

Internal flow within one transformer block: token embeddings → multi-head attention (cross-token mixing) → residual + layer norm → FFN (per-token transformation) → residual + layer norm → output.

  • The FFN has two weight matrices: one expands the dimension (usually 4x), one contracts it back. The expansion gives the model room to represent complex intermediate states.
  • A non-linearity (GELU or ReLU) between the two matrices allows the FFN to learn complex functions, not just linear ones.
  • The FFN accounts for roughly two-thirds of the parameter count in most transformer models — it is where much of the model's 'knowledge' is stored.
  • Research suggests FFN layers act as key-value memories: they can recall factual associations learned during training.
  • The FFN is applied identically and independently to each token position — no cross-token communication happens here.

A concrete example

After attention has determined that in 'Paris is the capital of France', 'capital' is closely linked to 'Paris' and 'France', the FFN layer can apply deeper transformations — for instance recalling facts about Paris and activating representations of European capitals, geography, and population statistics from its 'memorised' weights.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Larger FFN dimension (higher expansion ratio) increases capacity but increases memory and compute linearly. MoE models address this by activating only some FFN parameters per token.

Hands-on lab

Task: In a transformer block, draw the flow of information for a single token from input to output. Label the attention sub-layer (where cross-token information merges) and the FFN sub-layer (where individual processing happens). Mark the two residual connections.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production implication: when a model lacks factual knowledge, it is often because the FFN layers for that topic were under-represented in training data, not because attention failed.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • The FFN does not let tokens communicate — that is the attention layer's job. FFN transforms each token in isolation.
  • The FFN expansion is not wasted capacity — the higher-dimensional intermediate space allows the model to represent more complex patterns.
  • Removing FFN layers degrades model quality significantly. They are not optional even though attention is often the focus of research.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Feed forward networks?

  • Once Feed forward networks is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: The FFN does not let tokens communicate — that is the attention layer's job. FFN transforms each token in isolation.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Feed forward networks into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: Production implication: when a model lacks factual knowledge, it is often because the FFN layers for that topic were under-represented in training data, not because attention failed.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Feed forward networks in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Attention Is All You Need

Layer norm

Layer normalisation (LayerNorm) stabilises training by normalising the activations within each layer to have mean 0 and standard deviation 1, then scaling with learned parameters.

Why this matters

LayerNorm matters because it is what makes it possible to train very deep transformers (96+ layers) without numerical instability.

In plain terms

Layer norm is like calibrating all your instruments to the same scale before reading them. If one layer outputs values between 0.001 and 0.003 and another outputs values between 1000 and 3000, training becomes unstable. Layer norm brings them to the same range.

How it works

Internal flow: residual connection adds the sub-layer output → LayerNorm normalises → feeds into the next sub-layer. This happens twice per transformer block.

  • Without normalisation, activations can grow or shrink exponentially as they pass through many layers, causing vanishing or exploding gradients.
  • LayerNorm computes the mean and variance of activations across the feature dimension for each token independently.
  • After normalisation, two learnable parameters (gamma and beta) allow the layer to restore representational capacity — the network learns how much scale it needs.
  • Pre-norm (apply LayerNorm before attention/FFN) has become standard in GPT-style models — it makes training more stable than post-norm.
  • Layer normalisation is applied at every transformer block, before the attention sub-layer and before the FFN sub-layer.

A concrete example

During training, if the attention layer's output values drift from range [-1,1] to range [-100, 100], gradients become unstable. LayerNorm continually rescales outputs back to a stable range, allowing gradients to flow predictably through 96 layers of a large model.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: LayerNorm adds a small compute and parameter cost. RMSNorm (used in Llama) drops the mean-centering step for 10-30% speed improvement with similar quality.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write out five numbers: [-50, -20, 0, 30, 100]. Compute their mean, then compute how far each is from the mean, then scale them so they have mean 0 and standard deviation 1. This is the core operation of LayerNorm.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production relevance: if you are serving models with custom inference code and see NaN outputs, unstable LayerNorm or a missing layer norm is a common culprit.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • LayerNorm is not Batch Norm. BatchNorm normalises across the batch dimension. LayerNorm normalises within each individual sample — it works for any batch size including 1.
  • LayerNorm does not remove the model's ability to represent large or small values — the gamma and beta parameters restore the range the model needs.
  • The position of LayerNorm (pre-norm vs post-norm) has significant effects on training stability. Modern models almost universally use pre-norm.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Layer norm?
