  • Correct answer: LayerNorm is not Batch Norm. BatchNorm normalises across the batch dimension. LayerNorm normalises within each individual sample — it works for any batch size including 1.
  • Once Layer norm is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Layer norm into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: Production relevance: if you are serving models with custom inference code and see NaN outputs, unstable LayerNorm or a missing layer norm is a common culprit.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Layer norm in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Attention Is All You Need

Residual stream

The residual stream is the continuously updated vector representation that flows through every layer of a transformer, with each attention and feed-forward block reading from it and adding their computed update back into it, rather than replacing it entirely.

In plain terms

It is like a running conversation notebook that every specialist consulted adds notes to, without erasing what previous specialists wrote, so information accumulates rather than being overwritten at each step.

How it works

In practice it comes down to each transformer layer reading the current residual stream, computing an update through attention and a feed-forward network, and adding that update back to the stream via a residual connection, so information from early layers remains accessible to later layers unless a later layer's update actively counteracts it.

A concrete example

A fact identified by an early attention layer, such as which pronoun refers to which noun, remains present in the residual stream and available for a later layer to use when it needs to generate a grammatically consistent verb form.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: The residual stream design makes very deep transformers trainable by letting gradients and information flow directly across many layers, but it also means every layer's contribution is additive to a shared representation, which is part of why individual layers can be hard to interpret in isolation

Try it in code

function decoderLayer(residualStream) {
  const attnOut = selfAttention(residualStream);
  const afterAttn = add(residualStream, attnOut); // residual connection
  const ffnOut = feedForward(afterAttn);
  return add(afterAttn, ffnOut); // residual connection again
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Explain in your own words why adding updates to a shared residual stream, rather than replacing it at each layer, makes very deep transformers easier to train.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Each transformer layer does not replace the residual stream's content with its own new computation; it only adds an update to what's already there, which is why information can persist and be reused across many layers rather than being lost after each one.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Residual stream?

  • Correct answer: Each transformer layer does not replace the residual stream's content with its own new computation; it only adds an update to what's already there, which is why information can persist and be reused across many layers rather than being lost after each one.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Residual stream is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Residual stream in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
  • Attention Is All You Need

Decoder stack

The decoder stack is the sequence of stacked decoder layers in a transformer, each combining self-attention over previously generated tokens with a feed-forward network, whose repeated depth is what allows the model to build increasingly abstract representations before producing an output.

In plain terms

It is like a series of editors each reviewing and refining a draft in sequence, where each pass can catch and add something the previous pass missed.

How it works

In practice it comes down to stacking multiple identical decoder layers, each applying masked self-attention, so a token can only attend to earlier tokens, followed by a feed-forward network and residual connections, with the output of one layer becoming the input to the next until the final layer's output is projected into a probability distribution over the vocabulary.

A concrete example

In a 32-layer decoder stack, early layers might primarily process local syntactic patterns like grammar, while later layers combine that with longer-range context to decide the most semantically appropriate next word.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A deeper decoder stack can represent more complex patterns and generally improves quality, but it increases the compute cost and latency of every single forward pass, since every layer's computation happens for every generated token

Try it in code

let stream = embed(tokens) + positionalEncoding(tokens);
for (const layer of decoderLayers) { // e.g. 32 layers
  stream = decoderLayer(stream);
}
const logits = outputProjection(stream);

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Explain why increasing the number of layers in a decoder stack increases both model quality potential and per-token generation latency, and describe one situation where that trade-off would not be worth it.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Each layer in a decoder stack is not solving an independent, separate problem; the layers build on each other through the residual stream, so a deeper stack's benefit comes from progressive refinement across layers, not from parallel independent contributions.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Decoder stack?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Each layer in a decoder stack is not solving an independent, separate problem; the layers build on each other through the residual stream, so a deeper stack's benefit comes from progressive refinement across layers, not from parallel independent contributions.
  • Once Decoder stack is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Decoder stack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Attention Is All You Need

KV cache

KV cache (key-value cache) is an inference optimisation that stores the attention keys and values computed for previously generated tokens, so they do not need to be recomputed on every new token.

Why this matters

KV cache matters because it is what makes interactive LLM applications with long contexts practical. Without it, response latency would scale with context length.

In plain terms

KV cache is like a note-taking assistant who keeps a running transcript. Every time the speaker says a new word, you only transcribe the new word — not the entire speech from the beginning. The previous notes are saved and ready to reference.

How it works

Internal flow: prefill phase computes K/V for the entire prompt and caches them. Decode phase uses cached K/V to generate tokens one at a time.

  • Without KV cache: generating each new token requires recomputing attention over all previous tokens. Cost grows as O(n²) with sequence length.
  • With KV cache: keys and values for all previous tokens are stored in GPU memory. Each new token only adds its own K and V, then attends over the full stored cache.
  • KV cache trades memory for compute. A long context window means a large cache. At 128k tokens, the KV cache can be several GB per batch.
  • Paged attention (used in vLLM) manages KV cache memory like virtual memory pages — allowing more efficient sharing between requests.
  • KV cache is only valid for the same prompt prefix. If the prompt changes, the cache must be invalidated from the point of change.

Topic diagram

Diagram: KV cache reuse for KV cache

A concrete example

Generating a 500-token response to a 2000-token document: without KV cache, each of the 500 new tokens requires attention over all 2000 + previous tokens — 500 expensive operations. With KV cache, the first token computes over 2000 tokens, each subsequent token reuses cached K/V and only adds one new pair. 50–100x faster.

Trade-offs

  • Memory vs latency: Caching keys and values speeds decoding but consumes GPU memory per active request.
  • Long context vs batch size: Large caches reduce how many users fit on the same accelerator.
  • Cache reuse vs correctness: Prompt-prefix reuse is fast only when the prefix is exactly valid for the request.

Try it in code

const cache = new Map();

function decodeNextToken(prefixKey, token) {
  const previous = cache.get(prefixKey) || [];
  const nextKV = transformer.computeKeyValue(token);
  cache.set(prefixKey, previous.concat(nextKV));
  return transformer.sampleWithCache(cache.get(prefixKey));
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For a chat application where users send short messages but receive long responses: estimate when KV cache memory becomes the bottleneck vs compute. At 128k context and 8k tokens per user session, how many simultaneous sessions fit in 80GB GPU memory if each token takes ~200 bytes of KV cache?

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production checklist: measure time-to-first-token (TTFT) and tokens-per-second (TPS) separately. TTFT is dominated by prefill; TPS is dominated by decode. KV cache size limits your batch size.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • KV cache does not cache model weights — it caches the intermediate attention representations for a specific input.
  • KV cache is per-sequence. You cannot reuse cache between different users' requests (unless they share a prompt prefix, which prefix caching exploits).
  • Disabling KV cache makes autoregressive generation impractically slow. It is enabled by default in all serving frameworks.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about KV cache?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: KV cache does not cache model weights — it caches the intermediate attention representations for a specific input.
  • Once KV cache is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing KV cache into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: Production checklist: measure time-to-first-token (TTFT) and tokens-per-second (TPS) separately. TTFT is dominated by prefill; TPS is dominated by decode. KV cache size limits your batch size.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain KV cache in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Memory vs latency" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • vLLM PagedAttention paper
  • Hugging Face KV cache guide
  • Attention Is All You Need

Speculative decoding

Speculative decoding speeds up LLM inference by using a small 'draft' model to propose multiple tokens at once, then using the large model to verify them in parallel — accepting correct predictions and discarding the first wrong one.

Why this matters

Speculative decoding matters because it reduces generation latency without changing model quality — the output is identical to what the large model would produce alone.

In plain terms

Speculative decoding is like a junior employee (draft model) drafting a paragraph for a senior editor (main model) to review. If the paragraph is good, the editor approves it all at once — saving time compared to writing it word by word together. If a sentence is wrong, the editor fixes from that point forward.

How it works

Internal flow: draft model autoregressively generates k tokens → large model verifies all k tokens in one forward pass → accept prefix up to first rejection → continue.

  • Autoregressive generation produces one token at a time, and each step requires a full forward pass through the large model. This is the bottleneck.
  • A small draft model (e.g. 7B parameters) can propose 4–8 tokens quickly. The large model (e.g. 70B) verifies all proposed tokens in one forward pass.
  • If all proposed tokens match what the large model would have generated, all are accepted — a 4x speedup for that step.
  • When a draft token is rejected, generation reverts to the large model's prediction at that point. The sequence is always exactly what the large model would have produced.
  • Typical acceptance rates are 70–90% depending on the draft model quality and the task. This translates to 2–4x real-world speedup.

A concrete example

Generating a code comment: the draft model proposes ['This', ' function', ' calculates', ' the', ' average']. The large model checks all five in parallel. If it agrees with all five, it accepts them and asks for five more. If it disagrees with 'average' (maybe 'mean' is correct), it accepts the first four and generates its own token 5 — saving four token generation steps.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Using a draft model adds memory and inference overhead. If the acceptance rate is low (draft quality is poor), you spend more compute on rejected proposals than saved.

Hands-on lab

Task: Model a simplified speculative decoding step on paper: draft model proposes 4 tokens with probabilities [0.9, 0.85, 0.7, 0.4]. At what probability threshold does the large model 'reject' a token? How many tokens get accepted on average with a 0.75 threshold?

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production checklist: measure acceptance rate by task type. Code generation typically has high acceptance rates (repetitive structure). Creative writing has lower acceptance rates (more diverse outputs).

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Speculative decoding does not change the model's outputs. The final sequence is statistically identical to greedy or sampling from the large model alone.
  • The draft model does not need to be the same architecture as the large model, but it must share the same vocabulary.
  • Speculative decoding helps most with latency (time-to-completion), not throughput (tokens/second across many parallel requests). Batching is better for throughput.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Speculative decoding?

  • Once Speculative decoding is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Speculative decoding does not change the model's outputs. The final sequence is statistically identical to greedy or sampling from the large model alone.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Speculative decoding into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: Production checklist: measure acceptance rate by task type. Code generation typically has high acceptance rates (repetitive structure). Creative writing has lower acceptance rates (more diverse outputs).
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Speculative decoding in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Attention Is All You Need

Serving transformers

Serving transformers means operating transformer models behind APIs with batching, KV cache, routing, memory management, streaming, and observability.

In plain terms

It is like running a power plant for tokens: generation quality matters, but capacity, heat, queues, and failures matter too.

How it works

In practice it comes down to prefill, decode, batching, KV cache, streaming, model replicas, scheduler policy, and telemetry.

A concrete example

A chat endpoint separates prefill-heavy long prompts from decode-heavy generation and tracks time-to-first-token plus tokens per second.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Aggressive batching improves throughput but can hurt interactive latency.

Hands-on lab

Task: Sketch the serving path for one chat request and identify where latency is spent.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not monitor only request count; token volume, queue time, and context length drive load.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Serving transformers?

  • Correct answer: Do not monitor only request count; token volume, queue time, and context length drive load.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Serving transformers is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Serving transformers in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Attention Is All You Need

Transformer lab

The transformer lab is a hands-on exercise to trace a short input sequence through tokenization, embedding lookup, and one attention computation by hand or with a small script, connecting the abstract architecture to concrete numbers.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to tokenizing a short real sentence, looking up or approximating its embeddings, computing a simplified attention score between two specific tokens, and observing how that score reflects a real relationship between the words.

It is like manually tracing one signal through a circuit diagram to confirm you understand what each component actually does to it.

The brief

Tokenize one short sentence, and manually trace or script a simplified attention computation between one ambiguous pronoun and its candidate referents, observing which one scores higher.

A worked reference

A lab might tokenize the sentence the cat sat because it was tired, then compute attention scores between the token it and the candidate tokens cat and tired, observing which one the mechanism weights more heavily and why that reflects the sentence's actual meaning.

Starting point in code

const tokens = tokenize("the cat sat because it was tired");
const scores = attentionScores(tokens, query = "it");
console.log(scores); // "cat" should score higher than "tired" here

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Working through the computation by hand or with a minimal script for a short sequence takes more effort than reading a description of attention, but it builds concrete intuition that a purely conceptual explanation doesn't

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • Understanding attention conceptually as tokens attending to relevant other tokens is not the same as being able to trace an actual attention score computation; the lab's value is specifically in connecting the concept to real numbers.

Going deeper

References

  • The Illustrated Transformer
  • Attention Is All You Need

Mini project

The mini project implements or clearly explains one small transformer component, such as a single attention head or a positional encoding scheme, from scratch, with a working example that demonstrates it operating on real input.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to implementing or precisely tracing through one transformer component in isolation, such as scaled dot-product attention for a small sequence, and demonstrating it produces the expected output on a concrete example.

It is like building a working scale model of one specific part of a larger machine, to prove you understand exactly how that part functions.

The brief

Implement scaled dot-product attention from scratch for a small example sequence, and verify your computed attention weights match a hand-calculated expectation.

A worked reference

A mini project might implement scaled dot-product attention from scratch in a few dozen lines of code, run it on a 5-token example sequence, and verify the attention weights match hand-calculated expectations.

Starting point in code

function scaledDotProductAttention(Q, K, V) {
  const scores = matmul(Q, transpose(K)) / Math.sqrt(K[0].length);
  const weights = softmax(scores);
  return matmul(weights, V);
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Implementing one component thoroughly from scratch builds deeper understanding than describing the whole transformer architecture at a high level, though it takes more focused time than a broader but shallower overview

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • Being able to name transformer components like attention and feed-forward networks is not the same as being able to implement or precisely trace through one of them; the mini project's value is in that concrete, verifiable understanding.

Going deeper

References

  • Attention Is All You Need

Capstone

The capstone explains a complete transformer forward pass for a real short input, from tokenization through the decoder stack to a final output token, tracing what happens at each stage and why the architecture is structured that way.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to walking through tokenization, embedding lookup, positional encoding, each decoder layer's self-attention and feed-forward computation via the residual stream, and the final projection to an output token probability distribution, for one concrete short input sequence.

It is like giving a complete, narrated tour of a factory floor for one specific product moving through every station, rather than just describing the factory's layout in the abstract.

The brief

Write a complete, staged walkthrough of a transformer's forward pass for one short real input sentence, explaining what happens and why at each stage.

A worked reference

A capstone might trace the phrase the weather today is through tokenization, embedding, several decoder layers, and the final output distribution, explaining at each stage what information is being added to the residual stream and why.

Starting point in code

// Traced forward pass (annotated)
tokens -> embeddings -> + positional encoding
  -> [layer 1: attention + FFN via residual stream]
  -> ... -> [layer N]
  -> output projection -> next-token distribution

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A complete, traced walkthrough of a real forward pass takes considerably more effort than summarizing the architecture at a high level, but it demonstrates genuine mechanistic understanding rather than surface familiarity with the component names

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • Being able to list a transformer's components in order is not the same as being able to explain what specific information flows through the residual stream at each stage and why it matters for the final output; the capstone requires that deeper tracing.

Going deeper

References

  • Attention Is All You Need
  • The Illustrated Transformer

Interview pack

The transformers interview pack prepares you to explain attention, the residual stream, and the decoder stack mechanistically and precisely, backed by the ability to trace a concrete example rather than reciting high-level definitions.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain not just what an engine does, but how you'd trace a specific signal through it on request.

How it works

In practice it comes down to practicing clear, precise, first-principles explanations of attention, the residual stream, and decoder depth, paired with the ability to walk through a concrete short example under follow-up questioning.

A concrete example

A strong answer to explain how attention works walks through query, key, and value vectors and a concrete similarity computation for a short example, rather than only saying it lets tokens attend to relevant other tokens.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const explainAttention = {
  query: "what am I looking for?",
  key: "what do I contain?",
  value: "what do I contribute if matched?",
  score: "similarity(query, key) -> softmax -> weighted sum of values"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Practice explaining attention out loud using a concrete short example sentence, tracing through query, key, and value vectors and the resulting attention weights.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that transformers use attention and are stacked in layers is not the same as being able to trace what specifically flows through the residual stream or why a deeper decoder stack helps; interviewers often probe for that mechanistic depth specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Knowing that transformers use attention and are stacked in layers is not the same as being able to trace what specifically flows through the residual stream or why a deeper decoder stack helps; interviewers often probe for that mechanistic depth specifically.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Attention Is All You Need

LLM Fundamentals

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: Think of an LLM as a very fast technical editor. It reads the words already on the page, predicts what should come next, and keeps revising that prediction as more context appears. This matters because every AI product eventually pays for tokens, latency, context quality, and model behavior. If you understand the inference path, you can debug bad answers instead of guessing.

The running example

Throughout this track, imagine you own the model gateway for a product team: every complaint about cost, latency, weird answers, or flaky behavior lands on your desk. Each chapter is one of the dials you'll be asked to explain or turn.

LLM mental model

A large language model is a neural network trained to predict the next token given all previous tokens. The 'intelligence' emerges from learning statistical patterns across vast text — it does not retrieve facts, reason formally, or understand the world the way humans do.

When it goes wrong

The model just told your user the company was founded in 1987. It wasn't. Nothing 'broke' — a next-token predictor did exactly its job. Until you internalize that, every failure will look like a mystery.

Why this matters

The LLM mental model matters because it prevents over-trusting model outputs. If you know it is a statistical pattern matcher, you know when to add retrieval, verification, and guardrails.

In plain terms

An LLM is like a very well-read autocomplete. It has read most of the internet, absorbed statistical patterns about how language works and what tends to follow what, and uses that to generate the most plausible continuation of whatever you type. It does not look things up — it reconstructs from learned patterns.

For non-technical readers

The single most useful thing for a non-technical person to internalize about tools like ChatGPT and Claude: they are not looking up facts in a database, they are generating the statistically most plausible next words based on patterns learned from enormous amounts of text. That's why they can sound completely confident while stating something false, they're not 'lying,' they're producing a plausible-sounding continuation that happens to be wrong. This is exactly why business-critical uses of these tools need a way to verify claims, like citing real sources, rather than trusting the model's confident tone at face value.

How it works

Internal flow: prompt → tokenise → embedding lookup → 96 transformer layers (each doing attention + FFN) → sample next token → repeat until stop token.

  • LLMs generate text one token at a time. Each token is chosen based on a probability distribution over the vocabulary, conditioned on all previous tokens.
  • They are not databases. They cannot reliably retrieve specific facts — they reconstruct plausible facts from pattern memory, which is why they hallucinate.
  • The training objective (predict the next token) causes the model to learn language, reasoning patterns, and world knowledge as a side effect of getting good at prediction.
  • LLMs have no persistent memory across conversations unless it is explicitly stored in the context window or an external system.
  • Capability and reliability are different. An LLM might be capable of writing correct code while being unreliable — doing it 70% of the time, not 99.9% of the time.

A concrete example

Ask an LLM 'What is the capital of France?' and it answers 'Paris' not because it retrieved a fact, but because 'Paris' statistically follows 'capital of France' with near certainty in its training data. Ask it about a fictional country and it will invent a plausible-sounding capital — same mechanism, no way to distinguish.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Larger models are more capable but slower and more expensive. Smaller models can be fine-tuned for specific tasks and be more reliable in a narrow domain.

Hands-on lab

Task: Ask an LLM the same factual question five times with slightly different phrasing. Record the five answers. Do they agree? Where they differ, what does that tell you about relying on the model as a fact source?

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production implication: never use an LLM as your only source of truth for factual claims, calculations, or code correctness. Always add retrieval, tool calls, or deterministic verification.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • LLMs do not 'know' things in the way a database does. They reconstruct plausible text. This is why they hallucinate with confidence.
  • Longer prompts do not always produce better results. Irrelevant context can degrade performance by distracting the model.
  • A well-written confident response is not evidence of correctness. Evaluate outputs, do not read tone.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about LLM mental model?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: LLMs do not 'know' things in the way a database does. They reconstruct plausible text. This is why they hallucinate with confidence.
  • Once LLM mental model is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing LLM mental model into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: Production implication: never use an LLM as your only source of truth for factual claims, calculations, or code correctness. Always add retrieval, tool calls, or deterministic verification.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain LLM mental model in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Pretraining

Pretraining is the first and largest phase of LLM training, where the model learns to predict the next token across hundreds of billions of tokens of text from the internet, books, and code — with no human-provided labels.

When it goes wrong

Why does the model know Python but not your internal API? Pretraining decides what's in the box before you ever write a prompt — and its cutoff date is why the model confidently describes last year's world.

Why this matters

Pretraining matters because it determines the ceiling of the model's capability. Fine-tuning adjusts behaviour but cannot add knowledge that was absent in pretraining.

In plain terms

Pretraining is like a student spending years reading everything in a vast library — not memorising it, but absorbing how language works, what topics exist, how reasoning flows, how code behaves. The student does not pass exams yet; they are building the foundation everything else depends on.

For non-technical readers

The business-relevant fact: pretraining is the enormously expensive part, tens or hundreds of millions of dollars in compute for the largest models, which is exactly why only a handful of companies build these foundation models from scratch, and why almost every business, including yours, should be renting access to one via an API rather than trying to build its own from the ground up. It also explains the 'knowledge cutoff' you may have heard about: a model only knows about the world up to whenever its training data was collected, which is why it can be confidently out of date on recent events.

How it works

Internal flow: web-scraped and curated text → tokenise → train transformer to predict next token → update all ~billions of weights → repeat on trillions of tokens.

  • The training objective is self-supervised: predict the next token. No labels are required because the next word in any text is the label.
  • Pretraining requires enormous compute: GPT-3 was trained on 300 billion tokens. This typically takes thousands of GPU-days.
  • The result is a 'base model' — capable of completing text but not yet instruction-following or safe.
  • Emergent capabilities appear at scale: reasoning, arithmetic, code, multilingual ability all emerge above certain parameter thresholds without being explicitly trained.
  • The quality, diversity, and filtering of pretraining data has a larger effect on the model than most architectural choices.

A concrete example

A base model pretrained on a large code corpus learns syntax, common patterns, APIs, and debugging idioms without any explicit programming curriculum. When later fine-tuned to follow instructions, it can answer 'fix this bug' because the knowledge was already there from pretraining.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More compute and data improve capability but have diminishing returns. Chinchilla scaling laws suggest training a smaller model on more data outperforms a larger model on less data.

Hands-on lab

Task: Find a topic you know well (your technical domain). Write 10 facts about it that would be rare or absent in general internet text. For each, explain whether a general pretrained model would know it and why.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Product implication: when a model consistently fails on your domain (medical, legal, scientific), the root cause is often insufficient domain representation in pretraining — fine-tuning or RAG is the fix.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Pretraining does not make a model safe or instruction-following. That requires RLHF and instruction tuning.
  • A base model does not 'refuse' requests — it just continues text. It might complete a prompt for malware because that is what statistically follows.
  • Pretraining data quality matters more than quantity beyond a point. Models trained on cleaner, higher-quality data with fewer tokens often outperform those trained on messy large datasets.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Pretraining?

  • Once Pretraining is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Pretraining does not make a model safe or instruction-following. That requires RLHF and instruction tuning.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Pretraining into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: Product implication: when a model consistently fails on your domain (medical, legal, scientific), the root cause is often insufficient domain representation in pretraining — fine-tuning or RAG is the fix.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Pretraining in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Chinchilla scaling laws
  • GPT-3 Language Models are Few-Shot Learners

Instruction tuning

Instruction tuning is the training phase that turns a raw text-completion model into one that answers questions and follows directions — by fine-tuning on curated instruction-response pairs until 'continue the text' becomes 'do what was asked'.

When it goes wrong

Ask a raw pretrained model 'What is the capital of France?' and it may reply with three more geography questions — continuing the pattern, not answering. Instruction tuning is why chat models answer instead of autocomplete.

Why this matters

Understanding instruction tuning tells you what kind of machine you're prompting: a system trained to prefer instruction-shaped responses — which is why clear, well-placed instructions work and why that following is probabilistic, not guaranteed.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between someone who has read the whole library and someone who has read the whole library and then worked a year at the reference desk: the knowledge was already there; the reflex to answer the question in front of them was trained.

How it works

The pipeline position: pretraining (knowledge and language) → instruction tuning (task-following behavior) → preference tuning like RLHF (style, helpfulness, refusals) — most 'why does it act like that?' questions trace to one of these three layers.

  • A raw pretrained model is a continuation engine: asked 'What is the capital of France?', it may plausibly continue with more geography questions, because question-lists are a common pattern in its training data — nothing in pretraining says 'answer'.
  • The tuning data is tens or hundreds of thousands of (instruction, good response) pairs across tasks — summarize, extract, explain, refuse — and its diversity and quality shape the assistant more than most people expect; data curation is the craft here.
  • Instruction tuning is where format instincts come from: answering in the asked-for structure, following system prompts at all, respecting 'reply only with JSON' — when a model follows your format reliably, you're cashing in this phase.
  • It generalizes beyond its examples: tuned on 'summarize this article', models follow 'summarize this in pirate voice as three haikus' — the phase teaches the relationship between instructions and outputs, not a fixed task list.
  • Its limits explain daily production behavior: instruction following is a trained tendency, not a parser — instructions buried mid-context, or competing with strongly-patterned data, lose sometimes, which is why prompt placement matters and why injection works at all.

A concrete example

The same base model, before and after: prompt 'Explain what an API is to a new engineer.' Base model output: 'Explain what a database is to a new engineer. Explain what a queue is…' — it recognized a list of exam questions and continued it. Instruction-tuned output: a clear three-paragraph explanation. Same weights underneath, same knowledge; the reference-desk year is the whole difference.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Heavier instruction tuning makes models more compliant and format-reliable but can sand off capability and calibration — labs balance it, and model updates shift the balance, which is one reason behavior changes across versions.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write five prompts and predict how a raw base model would 'continue' each versus how an instruction-tuned model would 'answer'. Then explain which production behavior you rely on daily — format compliance, refusals, system-prompt obedience — comes from this phase.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production judgment: when a model won't follow instructions, diagnose against this layer — instruction placement, competing patterns in context, or a task genuinely outside tuned behavior — before reaching for fine-tuning yourself.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Instruction tuning does not add knowledge; it changes behavior — the facts came from pretraining, the answering reflex came from here.
  • Instruction following is not parsing; it is a trained statistical tendency, which is why 'the model ignored my instruction' is a probability statement, not a bug report.
  • Chat ability is not intelligence added on top; it is the same completion engine steered by tuning — knowing that demystifies both the fluency and the failures.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Instruction tuning?

  • Once Instruction tuning is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Instruction tuning does not add knowledge; it changes behavior — the facts came from pretraining, the answering reflex came from here.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Instruction tuning into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: Production judgment: when a model won't follow instructions, diagnose against this layer — instruction placement, competing patterns in context, or a task genuinely outside tuned behavior — before reaching for fine-tuning yourself.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Instruction tuning in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

RLHF

Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is a technique that fine-tunes a language model using human preferences. Human raters rank model outputs, those rankings train a reward model, and then the language model is optimised to maximise the reward signal using reinforcement learning (PPO).

When it goes wrong

Your assistant agrees with users even when they're wrong: 'You're right, that config should be YAML' (it shouldn't). Optimizing for human approval taught it to be agreeable, not correct — an artifact you have to design around.

Why this matters

RLHF matters because it is the primary technique that turns a capable but raw base model into a safe, helpful, instruction-following assistant.

In plain terms

RLHF is like training a chef with a panel of tasters. The chef makes multiple versions of a dish, tasters rank them, a food critic learns the tasters' preferences, and then coaches the chef using those preferences — not just the recipe. Over time the chef internalises what makes food taste good to humans.

How it works

Internal flow: base model → SFT on demonstrations → reward model trained on preference pairs → PPO optimisation → aligned model (Claude, ChatGPT, etc.).

  • Step 1 — Supervised fine-tuning (SFT): fine-tune the base model on high-quality human-written examples of desired behaviour.
  • Step 2 — Reward model training: human raters compare pairs of model outputs and pick the better one. These preferences train a reward model that predicts a 'human preference score' for any output.
  • Step 3 — RL optimisation (PPO): the language model is updated using Proximal Policy Optimization to generate responses that score higher on the reward model.
  • A KL divergence penalty prevents the model from drifting too far from the supervised fine-tuned checkpoint (to avoid reward hacking).
  • DPO (Direct Preference Optimisation) is a more recent alternative that achieves similar results without the RL step, using preference data directly.

A concrete example

Before RLHF, a base GPT model asked 'How do I break into a car?' might complete the text with a step-by-step guide (statistically it follows). After RLHF, the model has learned that human raters prefer responses that decline harmful requests, so it refuses or redirects instead.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: RLHF is expensive (human annotation at scale) and can introduce subtle alignment problems if the reward model captures rater preferences imperfectly (reward hacking).

Hands-on lab

Task: Write three different responses to the question 'What is the most effective way to persuade someone?' — one that is unhelpful, one that is helpful but unsafe, one that is both helpful and safe. Explain what RLHF is trying to select for, and what it might miss.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Product implication: RLHF is why Claude and ChatGPT behave so differently from GPT-base. The alignment comes from the RLHF phase, not the pretraining.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • RLHF does not make a model 'understand' right and wrong. It makes it statistically resemble outputs that humans rated highly.
  • RLHF can be gamed. If the reward model is flawed, the language model will find behaviours that score well on the reward model but are not actually good.
  • RLHF does not remove all risks — it reduces them. Adversarial prompting, edge cases, and reward model blind spots still produce harmful outputs.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about RLHF?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: RLHF does not make a model 'understand' right and wrong. It makes it statistically resemble outputs that humans rated highly.
  • Once RLHF is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing RLHF into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: Product implication: RLHF is why Claude and ChatGPT behave so differently from GPT-base. The alignment comes from the RLHF phase, not the pretraining.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain RLHF in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Learning from Human Preferences
  • Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback

Fine tuning

Fine-tuning takes a pretrained model and continues training it on a smaller, task-specific dataset, adjusting weights to make the model better at a particular behaviour or domain.

When it goes wrong

Three months of prompt engineering couldn't make the model write claim summaries in your exact regulated format. Two thousand labeled examples and one fine-tune did. Knowing which problems live in prompts and which live in weights saves quarters.

Why this matters

Fine-tuning matters when prompt engineering and RAG are insufficient — typically when you need consistent style, format, or specialised vocabulary.

In plain terms

Fine-tuning is like an experienced consultant spending two weeks embedded with a specific company. They already have deep general knowledge from years of work; the two-week immersion makes them far more effective at this specific client's problems without forgetting their general expertise.

How it works

Internal flow: pretrained model weights → load on training infrastructure → run gradient descent on task-specific dataset → save fine-tuned checkpoint → evaluate on held-out examples.

  • Full fine-tuning updates all model parameters. This is expensive (same hardware as pretraining for a smaller model) but produces the best task-specific performance.
  • Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) updates only a small subset of parameters — either LoRA adapters, prompt tuning, or prefix tuning. Much cheaper, often 90%+ of full fine-tuning quality.
  • Fine-tuning is most valuable for: style/format alignment, domain-specific vocabulary, specific instruction formats, and reducing verbosity or hallucination on known topics.
  • Fine-tuning is not retrieval. It bakes knowledge into weights. If knowledge changes frequently, RAG is a better choice than fine-tuning.
  • Catastrophic forgetting is a risk: fine-tuning on a narrow dataset can degrade general capabilities. Regularisation or mixed training with general data mitigates this.

A concrete example

A general-purpose model might write medical notes in a conversational style. Fine-tuning on 50,000 real physician notes teaches it the specific format, terminology, and abbreviations of clinical documentation — without retraining the entire model from scratch.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Full fine-tuning is expensive and may cause catastrophic forgetting. LoRA is cheaper but slightly less powerful. RAG is cheaper still and more updatable but adds retrieval latency.

Hands-on lab

Task: You have a customer service chatbot that gives correct answers but in the wrong tone (too formal, too verbose). Decide: fine-tuning, few-shot prompting, or RAG? Justify your choice by listing what each approach would and would not fix.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production checklist: always evaluate on a task-specific held-out set; compare to a prompted baseline before deciding fine-tuning is worth the cost; version your fine-tuned checkpoints.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Fine-tuning does not teach the model new knowledge reliably. It adjusts behaviour, not factual recall. Use RAG for fresh facts.
  • A small fine-tuning dataset (100 examples) can be useful for format and style, but for complex capabilities, thousands of high-quality examples are needed.
  • Fine-tuned models still hallucinate. Fine-tuning does not solve the fundamental statistical pattern-matching nature of LLMs.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Fine tuning?

  • Correct answer: Fine-tuning does not teach the model new knowledge reliably. It adjusts behaviour, not factual recall. Use RAG for fresh facts.
  • Once Fine tuning is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Fine tuning into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: Production checklist: always evaluate on a task-specific held-out set; compare to a prompted baseline before deciding fine-tuning is worth the cost; version your fine-tuned checkpoints.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Fine tuning in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Fine-tuning vs training

Fine-tuning adapts an existing model to a narrower behavior or task, while training or pretraining builds broad model capability from large-scale data. They are different in data scale, cost, risk, and what changes in the system.

When it goes wrong

A team spent $40k fine-tuning a model on their product docs — and it still hallucinated current prices. Fine-tuning changes behavior, not knowledge freshness. RAG was the fix. This chapter is that $40k decision tree.

Why this matters

Fine-tuning vs training matters because teams often spend money changing weights when the real problem is retrieval, prompting, schema validation, or product design.

In plain terms

Training is like educating someone from childhood across many subjects. Fine-tuning is like onboarding an already skilled employee to your company's tone, forms, and procedures.

For non-technical readers

This is a genuine build-versus-buy decision worth understanding even if you're not technical: training a model from scratch costs millions of dollars and is realistically only done by a handful of large AI labs; fine-tuning an already-capable existing model to match your company's specific tone or task costs vastly less. Most of the time, when a business wants 'a custom AI,' what actually makes sense is either fine-tuning a small piece of an existing model, or simply writing better instructions for one, not training something from zero. If a vendor or team proposes 'training our own model,' it's worth asking whether they really mean training from scratch, which is rarely justified, or fine-tuning, which is a much smaller and more reasonable investment.

How it works

The decision flow is: identify the failure, check whether missing knowledge can be retrieved, check whether output format can be constrained, then consider fine-tuning only for repeated behavior that examples can teach.

  • Pretraining changes the base model by learning general language or multimodal patterns from huge corpora.
  • Fine-tuning starts from an existing model and updates weights on curated examples for a narrower behavior, format, domain, or policy.
  • Instruction tuning is a supervised fine-tuning style focused on following user instructions and response conventions.
  • LoRA and other adapter methods fine-tune a small set of parameters instead of every base-model weight.
  • RAG should be considered before fine-tuning when the main need is fresh, private, or citeable knowledge.

Topic diagram

Diagram: Adaptation decision tree for Fine-tuning vs training

A concrete example

If a support assistant answers in the wrong JSON format, fine-tuning or structured output may help. If it lacks the latest refund policy, RAG is usually better. If it cannot reason over a new modality or language family at all, that is closer to training or choosing a stronger base model.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Training gives broad capability but is expensive and risky; fine-tuning gives narrower behavioral control but can overfit; RAG keeps knowledge fresh but depends on retrieval quality.

Try it in code

const adaptationChoices = {
  pretraining: {
    changes: "base model weights",
    data: "trillions of broad tokens",
    cost: "very high",
    useWhen: "you need a new foundation model capability"
  },
  fineTuning: {
    changes: "model behavior for a narrower task",
    data: "thousands to millions of curated examples",
    cost: "medium",
    useWhen: "the model knows the domain but needs consistent format or style"
  },
  rag: {
    changes: "runtime context, not model weights",
    data: "documents and metadata",
    cost: "low to medium",
    useWhen: "knowledge changes often or must be cited"
  }
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take one LLM product failure and classify it as a prompting, structured-output, RAG, fine-tuning, or training problem. Write the cheapest experiment that would prove your choice.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-shaped slice should compare baseline prompting, RAG, structured output, and fine-tuning on the same eval set before changing model weights.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Fine-tuning is not the best way to add frequently changing facts; use retrieval when knowledge needs citations or freshness.
  • Training a foundation model is not a normal product-team task; most teams choose, prompt, retrieve, constrain, or fine-tune existing models.
  • Fine-tuning does not remove the need for evals, guardrails, monitoring, or rollback.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Fine-tuning vs training?

  • Once Fine-tuning vs training is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Fine-tuning is not the best way to add frequently changing facts; use retrieval when knowledge needs citations or freshness.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Fine-tuning vs training into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: A production-shaped slice should compare baseline prompting, RAG, structured output, and fine-tuning on the same eval set before changing model weights.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Fine-tuning vs training in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Training language models to follow instructions with human feedback
  • LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models

LoRA

LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that adds small trainable rank-decomposition matrices to each transformer layer instead of updating all model weights.

When it goes wrong

Full fine-tuning of a 70B model needs a GPU cluster; a LoRA adapter trains on one card overnight and ships as a file measured in megabytes. That difference decides whether per-customer behavior is economically possible.

Why this matters

LoRA matters because it makes fine-tuning accessible: you can adapt a large model to your domain on a single GPU without touching most of the weights.

In plain terms

Instead of repainting an entire building (full fine-tuning), LoRA adds thin decorative panels on top of the existing walls. The panels are small and cheap to add, and when removed, the original building is unchanged.

How it works

Internal flow: load base model (frozen) → add small LoRA matrices to Q, V, or all projection matrices → train only LoRA weights on task data → merge or keep separate at inference.

  • Full fine-tuning updates billions of parameters. LoRA adds tiny matrices — typically < 1% of model parameters — to each layer.
  • The weight update ΔW is decomposed as ΔW = AB, where A and B are low-rank matrices (e.g. rank 8 or 16). Only A and B are trained.
  • The original model weights are frozen. At inference, LoRA weights can be merged with the base weights (zero latency overhead) or kept separate (swappable per request).
  • Multiple LoRA adapters can be trained for different tasks and hot-swapped on the same base model — useful for serving many personalised variants.
  • QLoRA combines LoRA with 4-bit quantisation, enabling fine-tuning of 65B+ models on a single consumer GPU.

A concrete example

A 7B-parameter model requires ~14GB at float16. Full fine-tuning needs that plus optimiser states — ~56GB. LoRA at rank 8 adds only ~4M parameters, requiring < 100MB of additional storage and fitting in 8GB VRAM for training.

Trade-offs

  • Rank size vs adapter quality: Higher rank captures more task-specific change but costs more memory and training time.
  • Merged vs swappable adapters: Merged adapters reduce latency; swappable adapters support many customer-specific behaviours.
  • One adapter vs many adapters: One adapter is operationally simple; many adapters isolate domains and reduce interference.

Try it in code

const loraConfig = {
  rank: 8,
  alpha: 16,
  targetModules: ["q_proj", "v_proj"],
  trainBaseWeights: false
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: A team wants to fine-tune for three tasks: formal email writing, Python code style enforcement, and customer service tone. Should they train one LoRA for all three or three separate LoRAs? List the trade-offs of each approach.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production checklist: save LoRA weights separately from base model; version adapters alongside their base model checkpoint; evaluate merged vs unmerged latency.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • LoRA is not the same as prompt tuning. Prompt tuning adds trainable tokens to the input; LoRA modifies the attention weights directly.
  • Low rank does not mean low quality. In practice, most fine-tuning tasks require only low-rank updates to the pretrained weight space.
  • LoRA adapters cannot be safely shared between different base models. They are tied to the specific base model checkpoint they were trained on.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about LoRA?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: LoRA is not the same as prompt tuning. Prompt tuning adds trainable tokens to the input; LoRA modifies the attention weights directly.
  • Once LoRA is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing LoRA into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: Production checklist: save LoRA weights separately from base model; version adapters alongside their base model checkpoint; evaluate merged vs unmerged latency.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain LoRA in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Rank size vs adapter quality" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models

MoE

Mixture of Experts is an architecture where each token activates only a few specialized sub-networks (experts) out of many, letting a model carry enormous total parameters while spending only a fraction of them per token.

When it goes wrong

The model card says a trillion parameters, but inference is priced like a much smaller model. Mixture of Experts is why — only a slice of the network runs per token, and it changes the cost math you plan around.

Why this matters

MoE is why modern model economics look the way they do: capability scales with total parameters while your per-token bill scales with active parameters — understanding the split makes pricing, latency, and hosting requirements legible.

In plain terms

It is like a hospital with 200 specialists where each patient sees two: the institution's total expertise is vast, but the cost per visit is two consultations — and a good triage desk (the router) is what makes the whole thing work.

How it works

The flow per token: hidden state hits the MoE layer → router scores all experts → top-k experts process it → weighted combination continues through the network — repeated at every MoE layer, with different experts winning at each.

  • The mechanism: in MoE layers, a small router network scores which experts should process each token and sends it to the top-k (often 2 of 8–64+); expert outputs are combined and passed on — the routing is learned, per token, not hand-assigned by topic.
  • The headline math: a model can have, say, 700B total parameters but activate ~40B per token — which is why 'parameter count' stopped being one number, and why spec sheets now say 'total' and 'active' separately.
  • The trade it buys: near-frontier capability at mid-size compute cost per token — this is why many frontier and open-weight models (Mixtral, DeepSeek, and most current frontier systems) went MoE; dense scaling hit diminishing cost-effectiveness.
  • The costs it hides: all experts must sit in memory even though few run per token, so MoE trades compute for memory footprint — cheap to run per token, expensive to host; batching and expert-parallelism across GPUs get more complex.
  • Operationally MoE is mostly invisible behind an API but explains real behaviors: pricing that undercuts what total-parameter intuition suggests, and occasional sensitivity where routing shifts make behavior less smooth across similar inputs.

A concrete example

A dense 70B model and an MoE with 8×22B experts (total ~176B, active ~39B per token): the MoE scores meaningfully higher on benchmarks while its per-token inference FLOPs sit near the dense 40B class — but it needs the memory of a 176B model to host. For an API customer that's a better price/quality point; for a self-hosting team it's a 'do we have the VRAM?' conversation.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: MoE buys compute efficiency with memory footprint and system complexity (routing balance, expert parallelism) — nearly free through an API, a real infrastructure decision when self-hosting.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take two real model cards, one dense and one MoE. For each, write down total parameters, active parameters per token, and memory needed to host — then explain which is cheaper per token, which is harder to self-host, and why both statements can be true at once.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production judgment: read 'active vs total parameters' on model cards, expect MoE models to punch above their per-token cost, and when self-hosting, budget memory for total parameters — not the active number that sets the speed.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Experts are not human-legible specialists ('the coding expert'); routing is learned and mostly uninterpretable — patterns exist, but per-token, not per-topic.
  • A trillion total parameters does not mean a trillion parameters of runtime cost; that intuition breaks precisely at MoE — per-token cost tracks active parameters.
  • MoE is not a niche trick; it is the mainstream architecture at the frontier, and the total/active distinction is now baseline literacy for reading any model announcement.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about MoE?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once MoE is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Experts are not human-legible specialists ('the coding expert'); routing is learned and mostly uninterpretable — patterns exist, but per-token, not per-topic.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing MoE into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: Production judgment: read 'active vs total parameters' on model cards, expect MoE models to punch above their per-token cost, and when self-hosting, budget memory for total parameters — not the active number that sets the speed.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain MoE in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Context windows

The context window is the maximum number of tokens a model can process in a single forward pass — both prompt and response combined. It defines how much the model can 'see' and 'remember' within one interaction.

When it goes wrong

Everything works until a power user pastes a 300-page contract. The model 'reads' it — and misses the clause on page 200. Context is a budget, and what you spend it on decides answer quality.

Why this matters

Context windows matter because they constrain your product architecture: what can fit in a single call, what must be retrieved, and what must be summarised.

In plain terms

The context window is like a whiteboard. Everything you write on the whiteboard, the model can see and reference. When the whiteboard is full, you must erase something to write more. The model has no memory of what was erased.

For non-technical readers

This is why an AI chatbot sometimes seems to 'forget' something you told it earlier in a long conversation, it's not being careless, the earlier part has literally scrolled off its whiteboard. It's also why uploading a huge document doesn't always work well: if the document is longer than the model's context window, it simply can't see all of it at once. When a vendor claims their tool can 'read your entire knowledge base,' a fair follow-up question is whether it uses a bigger context window, a separate search step to find relevant sections first, or both.

How it works

Internal flow: system prompt + conversation history + retrieved documents + user message = total tokens. If total exceeds window, content must be trimmed, summarised, or retrieved selectively.

  • All current LLMs have fixed context windows set at training time (e.g. 8k, 32k, 128k, 1M tokens). Content outside the window is inaccessible.
  • Longer context windows increase cost: attention is O(n²) in sequence length, so 128k tokens costs ~256x more attention compute than 1k tokens.
  • Performance degrades in the middle of very long contexts — models attend better to the beginning and end ('lost in the middle' problem).
