  • There is not one universal generation pattern that works best for every content type; the right pattern depends on whether the content is inherently sequential like text, or spatially structured like an image, and different patterns have different quality, speed, and controllability trade-offs.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Generation patterns?

  • Once Generation patterns is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: There is not one universal generation pattern that works best for every content type; the right pattern depends on whether the content is inherently sequential like text, or spatially structured like an image, and different patterns have different quality, speed, and controllability trade-offs.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Generation patterns in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models

Text generation

Text generation is the process of producing coherent natural language output from a model, typically an autoregressive language model, one token at a time, conditioned on a prompt and the tokens already generated.

In plain terms

It is like a very well-read writer composing a response one word at a time, constantly considering everything written so far to decide what comes next.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a language model computing a probability distribution over the next possible token given all previous tokens, then a sampling strategy selecting the actual next token from that distribution, repeating this process token by token until a stopping condition like an end token or length limit is reached.

A concrete example

Generating a product description, the model predicts a probability distribution over likely next words at each step, such as favoring durable or lightweight after this backpack is, selects one, and repeats until the description is complete.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More deterministic sampling like low temperature or greedy decoding produces more predictable, repeatable text well-suited to factual tasks, while higher-temperature sampling produces more varied and creative text at the cost of consistency and sometimes coherence

Try it in code

async function generateText(prompt, temperature) {
  let tokens = [];
  while (!isStopToken(tokens.at(-1))) {
    const dist = await model.nextTokenDistribution(prompt, tokens);
    tokens.push(sample(dist, temperature));
  }
  return detokenize(tokens);
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Generate text from the same prompt at a low and a high sampling temperature, and compare the consistency and creativity of the outputs.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Text generation is not a lookup of pre-written responses; every output is constructed token by token from a learned probability distribution, which is why the same prompt can produce different outputs across runs unless sampling is made fully deterministic.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Text generation?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Text generation is not a lookup of pre-written responses; every output is constructed token by token from a learned probability distribution, which is why the same prompt can produce different outputs across runs unless sampling is made fully deterministic.
  • Once Text generation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Text generation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation

Image generation

Image generation produces novel images from a text description or other conditioning input, typically using a diffusion model that iteratively transforms random noise into a coherent image guided by the conditioning signal.

In plain terms

It is like an artist starting with a canvas of random static and gradually refining brushstrokes over many passes, using your description as a guide at every step, until a clear image emerges.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a diffusion model trained to predict and remove noise at each step of a gradual denoising process, conditioned on a text embedding or other input, repeated over many iterative steps starting from pure random noise until a coherent image results.

A concrete example

A prompt describing a red bicycle leaning against a brick wall guides a diffusion model's denoising process at every step, so the random noise it starts from gradually resolves into an image matching that description rather than an arbitrary scene.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More denoising steps generally produce higher-quality, more detailed images, but each additional step adds generation latency and compute cost, so production systems often use accelerated sampling methods that trade a small amount of quality for significantly faster generation

Try it in code

async function generateImage(prompt, steps = 30) {
  let x = randomNoise();
  for (let t = steps; t > 0; t--) {
    x = denoiseStep(x, t, encode(prompt));
  }
  return x; // fewer steps = faster but lower quality
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Generate an image from the same text prompt with a low and a high number of denoising steps, and compare the quality and generation time difference.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Image generation models do not retrieve or collage pieces of real training images to construct output; they generate genuinely new pixel patterns through the denoising process, which is why generated images can depict scenes that never existed in any training example.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Image generation?

  • Correct answer: Image generation models do not retrieve or collage pieces of real training images to construct output; they generate genuinely new pixel patterns through the denoising process, which is why generated images can depict scenes that never existed in any training example.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Image generation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Image generation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models

Audio generation

Audio generation produces novel speech, music, or sound from a model, typically conditioned on text, a reference voice, or a musical style, using architectures adapted from text or image generation to work on the specific structure of audio waveforms or spectrograms.

In plain terms

It is like a skilled voice actor or composer producing new audio in a specified style or voice, rather than playing back a pre-recorded clip.

How it works

In practice it comes down to converting audio into a more compact representation like a spectrogram or discrete audio tokens, generating that representation autoregressively or through diffusion conditioned on the input signal like text or a reference voice, then converting the generated representation back into a playable waveform.

A concrete example

A text-to-speech system converts input text into a sequence of audio tokens conditioned on a selected voice style, then decodes those tokens back into an audible waveform that sounds like natural speech in that voice.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Higher-fidelity audio generation, capturing more natural intonation and detail, generally requires more compute and higher latency, which matters a lot for real-time applications like live voice assistants versus offline content generation where latency is less critical

Try it in code

async function textToSpeech(text, voiceStyle) {
  const audioTokens = await ttsModel.generate(text, { style: voiceStyle });
  return decodeToWaveform(audioTokens);
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real audio generation use case, such as a customer service voice assistant, state whether latency or fidelity should be prioritized, and explain why given the specific context.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Generated speech or music is not simply a rearrangement of pre-recorded audio clips; modern audio generation models synthesize genuinely new waveforms, which is part of why they can produce natural-sounding speech for text that was never spoken by anyone in the training data.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Audio generation?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Audio generation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Generated speech or music is not simply a rearrangement of pre-recorded audio clips; modern audio generation models synthesize genuinely new waveforms, which is part of why they can produce natural-sounding speech for text that was never spoken by anyone in the training data.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Audio generation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models

Multimodal systems

Multimodal systems process or generate more than one type of content, such as text, images, and audio, within a single model or pipeline, allowing input in one modality to inform output in another.

In plain terms

It is like a person who can read a description, look at a photo, and listen to a voice message all at once, and synthesize an understanding that draws on all three rather than considering each in isolation.

For non-technical readers

In everyday terms: this is what lets you take a photo of a broken product part, type 'what's wrong with this,' and get a genuinely useful answer, because the AI is looking at the picture and reading your question together, not treating them as two separate requests. It's a capability worth specifically asking about when evaluating AI tools for support or field-service use cases, since not every 'AI that accepts images' actually reasons across image and text together in this deeper way.

How it works

In practice it comes down to encoding each input modality into a shared or aligned representation space, so that a model trained across modalities can relate a text description to an image region or an audio segment to a transcript, then generating output in whichever modality the task requires.

A concrete example

A multimodal assistant can take a photo of a broken appliance part along with a text question about it, and generate a text answer that correctly references specific visual details in the image, because the image and text are processed within a shared understanding rather than two disconnected pipelines.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Multimodal systems unlock capabilities no single-modality system can match, like grounding a text answer in an image's specific visual details, but they are more complex to build, evaluate, and debug since failures can originate in any modality's encoding or in how modalities are combined

Try it in code

async function answerAboutImage(image, question) {
  const imageEmbedding = await visionEncoder(image);
  const textEmbedding = await textEncoder(question);
  return await model.generate({ image: imageEmbedding, text: textEmbedding });
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Describe one real task where combining two modalities, such as an image and a text question about it, would produce a meaningfully better answer than either modality alone, and explain specifically why.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A system that can accept both text and image input is not automatically multimodal in a meaningful sense if it processes each modality with a completely separate, disconnected pipeline; genuine multimodal capability requires the model to relate information across modalities, not just accept multiple input types.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Multimodal systems?

  • Once Multimodal systems is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A system that can accept both text and image input is not automatically multimodal in a meaningful sense if it processes each modality with a completely separate, disconnected pipeline; genuine multimodal capability requires the model to relate information across modalities, not just accept multiple input types.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Multimodal systems in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GPT-4V system card
  • Gemini technical report

Multimodal GenAI and deployment

Multimodal GenAI combines text, images, audio, video, or documents in one workflow, then deploys the workflow with modality-specific validation, storage, latency, and safety controls.

Why this matters

Multimodal GenAI matters because many real workflows are not text-only: claims, medical intake, retail support, education, design, and field operations all combine media types.

In plain terms

It is like giving an assistant eyes, ears, and reading ability, then deciding which senses are allowed in production and how each one is checked.

How it works

The internal flow is upload, validate, preprocess, encode modality inputs, call a multimodal model, post-process output, run safety checks, and route low-confidence cases.

  • Multimodal inputs need preprocessing: resizing images, extracting frames, transcribing audio, parsing documents, and preserving metadata.
  • Each modality has different failure modes: OCR errors, blurry images, accents, missing frames, unsafe visual content, or private data in files.
  • Deployment requires asset storage, signed URLs, content-type checks, size limits, virus scanning, and retention policies.
  • Evaluation must include modality-specific cases, such as unreadable images, contradictory text and image evidence, or low-confidence transcriptions.
  • Human review is often required when multimodal outputs affect money, health, identity, safety, or compliance.

Topic diagram

Diagram: Multimodal deployment path for Multimodal GenAI and deployment

A concrete example

An insurance assistant receives accident photos and a text claim. It checks image readability, extracts visible damage, compares it with the user's description, flags low confidence for human review, and stores only approved evidence.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Richer context vs operational risk: images/audio can improve answers but add privacy, storage, latency, cost, and safety obligations.

Try it in code

const multimodalRequest = {
  inputs: [
    { type: "text", value: "Describe damage and estimate severity." },
    { type: "image", uri: "s3://claims/car-door.jpg" }
  ],
  checks: {
    imageReadable: true,
    noPiiInOutput: true,
    confidenceThreshold: 0.75,
    humanReviewIf: ["low_confidence", "medical_or_legal_claim", "high_value_claim"]
  }
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a multimodal deployment for product-return photo review. Include upload checks, model prompt, confidence threshold, human-review trigger, storage policy, and three eval cases.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-shaped slice should accept one text input and one image, validate file type and size, call a multimodal model, score confidence, and send uncertain cases to human review.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Multimodal does not mean the model sees everything reliably; poor image quality or missing frames can break the answer.
  • A text safety filter is not enough for image, audio, or document uploads.
  • Deployment is not only model choice; storage, permissions, preprocessing, and review queues are core parts of the system.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Multimodal GenAI and deployment?

  • Correct answer: Multimodal does not mean the model sees everything reliably; poor image quality or missing frames can break the answer.
  • Once Multimodal GenAI and deployment is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Multimodal GenAI and deployment into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: A production-shaped slice should accept one text input and one image, validate file type and size, call a multimodal model, score confidence, and send uncertain cases to human review.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Multimodal GenAI and deployment in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GPT-4V system card
  • Gemini technical report

Latent spaces

A latent space is the compressed, internal numeric representation a generative model uses to encode the essential structure of its content, from which it decodes a full output, distinct from the final generated pixels, tokens, or waveform samples themselves.

In plain terms

It is like a sculptor's compact mental model of a shape before carving it into full physical detail; the compact model captures the essential structure that gets expanded into the final, detailed piece.

How it works

In practice it comes down to an encoder compressing input content into a lower-dimensional latent representation capturing its essential structure, and a decoder expanding a point in that latent space back into full output, such that moving smoothly through the latent space produces smoothly varying, semantically meaningful changes in the decoded output.

A concrete example

In an image generation model's latent space, moving a point smoothly from one region to another can produce a smooth visual transition, like an image gradually shifting from a summer scene to a winter one, because nearby points in latent space decode to visually and semantically related images.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Working in a compressed latent space rather than raw pixel or token space makes generation dramatically more computationally efficient, but the quality of everything downstream depends entirely on how well the latent space actually captures the meaningful structure of the content

Try it in code

const z1 = encode(imageA), z2 = encode(imageB);
const zMid = interpolate(z1, z2, 0.5); // halfway point in latent space
const blended = decode(zMid); // semantically meaningful blend

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Explain in your own words why generating in a compressed latent space and then decoding to full resolution is more computationally efficient than generating directly in full pixel or waveform resolution.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A latent space's individual dimensions are not typically hand-designed to represent specific human concepts like color or size; useful, interpretable directions in latent space are usually discovered after training, not built in deliberately from the start.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Latent spaces?

  • Once Latent spaces is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A latent space's individual dimensions are not typically hand-designed to represent specific human concepts like color or size; useful, interpretable directions in latent space are usually discovered after training, not built in deliberately from the start.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Latent spaces in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models

Prompt-to-output flow

The prompt-to-output flow is the full pipeline a generative request travels through, from the user's raw prompt, through encoding, conditioning, generation, and post-processing, to the final delivered output, with each stage capable of introducing quality issues independent of the model's raw capability.

In plain terms

It is like tracing an order from a restaurant kitchen: even with a great chef, a mistake at order-taking, ingredient prep, or plating can ruin the final dish regardless of cooking skill.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a raw prompt being parsed and possibly rewritten or augmented, encoded into the conditioning representation the generative model expects, run through the generation process itself, and then post-processed, such as safety filtering, formatting, or upscaling, before being delivered as the final output.

A concrete example

A user's short image prompt might be automatically expanded with additional descriptive detail before being encoded and fed to the diffusion model, and the raw generated image might then pass through a safety filter and an upscaling step before being shown to the user.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Adding pipeline stages like prompt rewriting or post-generation filtering can meaningfully improve output quality and safety, but each added stage also adds latency and a new potential point of failure that needs its own monitoring

Try it in code

async function pipeline(rawPrompt) {
  const expanded = await rewritePrompt(rawPrompt);
  const conditioning = await encode(expanded);
  const raw = await generate(conditioning);
  return await postProcess(raw); // safety filter, upscaling, formatting
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Map out the full prompt-to-output pipeline for one real generative feature you use, and identify one stage besides the core model that could independently degrade output quality.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A disappointing generated output is not always evidence the underlying model is weak; the problem can just as easily originate in prompt preprocessing, conditioning encoding, or a post-processing step, so debugging generation quality requires checking the whole pipeline, not just the model.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Prompt-to-output flow?

  • Once Prompt-to-output flow is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A disappointing generated output is not always evidence the underlying model is weak; the problem can just as easily originate in prompt preprocessing, conditioning encoding, or a post-processing step, so debugging generation quality requires checking the whole pipeline, not just the model.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Prompt-to-output flow in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation

Controllability

Controllability is the degree to which a generative model's output can be reliably steered toward specific, intended properties, such as style, format, or content constraints, rather than producing plausible but uncontrolled variation.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between giving an assistant vague creative freedom versus giving them a specific brief with concrete constraints they reliably follow every time.

How it works

In practice it comes down to techniques such as detailed conditioning inputs, structured prompts, guidance parameters that weight how strongly the output follows the conditioning signal, or fine-tuning on examples with the desired specific property, used to narrow the space of likely outputs toward the intended result.

A concrete example

An image generation system exposes a guidance-strength parameter that lets a user dial up how strictly the output should adhere to the text prompt, trading some creative variation for stronger, more literal adherence to the requested details.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Higher controllability, forcing output to more strictly match specific constraints, often reduces the creative diversity or naturalness of results, so the right level of control depends on whether the use case values precision, like generating a specific product image, or creative variety, like ideation

Try it in code

await generateImage(prompt, {
  guidanceScale: 12 // higher = stricter adherence to prompt, less creative variation
});

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real generative feature, identify one control parameter, explicit or implicit, that could be exposed to users to give them more reliable steering over a specific output property.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A generative model's default output is not the only output it's capable of; low controllability in a specific product is often a design and interface choice, not an inherent limitation of the underlying model's capability to be steered more precisely.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Controllability?

  • Correct answer: A generative model's default output is not the only output it's capable of; low controllability in a specific product is often a design and interface choice, not an inherent limitation of the underlying model's capability to be steered more precisely.
  • Once Controllability is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Controllability in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models

Evaluation

Evaluation of generative AI systems measures output quality along multiple dimensions, such as coherence, factual accuracy, adherence to the prompt, and safety, since a single fluency or aesthetic quality score can hide serious failures in other dimensions.

In plain terms

It is like judging a dish on taste alone while ignoring whether it's actually safe to eat or matches what the customer ordered; a single quality axis misses what actually matters.

How it works

In practice it comes down to combining automated metrics for specific dimensions, human evaluation for subjective qualities like coherence or aesthetic appeal, and specific adversarial or edge-case testing for safety and prompt adherence, rather than relying on any single score to represent overall quality.

A concrete example

An image generation system's evaluation might separately score whether generated images match the requested content, whether they contain any unsafe elements, and how aesthetically pleasing human raters find them, since a model can excel at one dimension while failing another.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Comprehensive multi-dimensional evaluation catches failures that a single quality metric would miss entirely, but it takes significantly more effort to design and run than a single automated fluency or aesthetic score

Try it in code

const scores = {
  promptAdherence: scoreAdherence(output, prompt),
  safety: safetyClassifier(output),
  aesthetic: humanRating(output)
};
// A high score on one dimension doesn't imply the others.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real generative feature, define three separate evaluation dimensions it should be scored on beyond general output quality, and describe how you would measure each.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A generative model that produces fluent, polished-looking output is not automatically producing high-quality output in every sense that matters; polish and coherence say nothing about whether the output is factually accurate, safe, or faithful to what was actually requested.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Evaluation?

  • Once Evaluation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A generative model that produces fluent, polished-looking output is not automatically producing high-quality output in every sense that matters; polish and coherence say nothing about whether the output is factually accurate, safe, or faithful to what was actually requested.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Evaluation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena

Safety

Safety in generative AI covers preventing a system from producing harmful, offensive, or policy-violating content, whether through malicious prompting or unintentional generation, using both training-time alignment and runtime filtering.

In plain terms

It is like a publisher having both an editor who trains writers on standards, and a final review step before anything actually gets printed; relying on just one layer leaves gaps the other would catch.

For non-technical readers

The reputational-risk version of this for a non-technical stakeholder: a generative AI feature that occasionally produces something embarrassing or harmful, an inappropriate image, offensive text, is a real brand and legal risk, not just an engineering edge case. A responsible team should be able to describe two separate layers of protection, not just one, since relying on a single safety measure is how these incidents happen. If a team can only point to one safeguard, that's worth pushing back on before launch.

How it works

In practice it comes down to training-time techniques that align a model away from generating harmful content by default, combined with runtime input and output filters that catch attempts to bypass those defaults or edge cases the training didn't fully cover, layered together rather than relying on either alone.

A concrete example

An image generation system both trains its model to avoid generating certain categories of harmful content by default and runs a separate output classifier that blocks any generated image matching those categories before it's shown to the user, catching cases where the first layer alone might fail.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Layering training-time alignment with runtime filtering catches more harmful cases than either alone, but overly aggressive filtering increases false positives that block legitimate, benign requests, which is a real cost to product usefulness that has to be weighed against risk

Try it in code

function isSafe(output) {
  return trainingTimeAlignmentPassed(output) && runtimeSafetyFilter(output);
  // layered: neither alone is sufficient against adversarial prompting
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real generative feature, describe one training-time safety measure and one runtime filtering measure that would work together to catch a specific category of harmful output.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Training a model to avoid generating harmful content by default does not make runtime safety filtering unnecessary; determined adversarial prompting can often find ways around training-time alignment alone, which is why production systems layer both.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Safety?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Training a model to avoid generating harmful content by default does not make runtime safety filtering unnecessary; determined adversarial prompting can often find ways around training-time alignment alone, which is why production systems layer both.
  • Once Safety is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Safety in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications

Product UX

Product UX for generative AI features designs how uncertainty, variation, and occasional failure in generated output are surfaced to users, since generative outputs are probabilistic and imperfect in ways that deterministic software features usually aren't.

In plain terms

It is like designing a restaurant experience around a talented but occasionally inconsistent chef, giving guests visibility into what to expect and an easy way to send a dish back, rather than pretending every plate will be perfect.

For non-technical readers

For a product-minded reader, the key idea is that generative AI output is inherently a bit unpredictable, run the exact same request twice and you can get two different results, and a well-designed product embraces that honestly instead of pretending every result will be perfect. Offering a few options and an easy 'try again' button is usually a better customer experience than committing to a single, possibly disappointing result and hoping for the best.

How it works

In practice it comes down to interface patterns such as showing multiple generated variations for a user to choose from, providing easy regeneration or refinement controls, and setting clear expectations about output variability, so the product experience accounts for generation's inherent unpredictability rather than presenting it as a deterministic feature.

A concrete example

An image generation product shows four variations for a single prompt rather than one, letting the user pick the best result or regenerate, which accounts for the reality that any single generation might not be exactly what the user wanted.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Showing multiple variations and easy regeneration improves user satisfaction with imperfect generative output, but it also increases compute cost per user request compared to committing to a single generation attempt

Try it in code

<generation-result>
  <variation id="1" />
  <variation id="2" />
  <variation id="3" />
  <variation id="4" />
  <button>Regenerate</button>
</generation-result>

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Redesign one real generative feature's interface to better account for output variability, such as adding a regeneration option or showing multiple candidates, and explain what user problem it solves.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A generative feature's UX does not need to hide or minimize the fact that output is probabilistic; users tend to trust and enjoy generative features more when the interface honestly accounts for variability, like offering easy regeneration, rather than presenting a single output as a guaranteed, deterministic result.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Product UX?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A generative feature's UX does not need to hide or minimize the fact that output is probabilistic; users tend to trust and enjoy generative features more when the interface honestly accounts for variability, like offering easy regeneration, rather than presenting a single output as a guaranteed, deterministic result.
  • Once Product UX is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Product UX in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation

Cost control

Cost control for generative AI features means actively managing the compute cost per generation, since image, audio, and long-form text generation can be significantly more expensive per request than typical software operations, and usage can scale unpredictably.

In plain terms

It is like managing a restaurant's ingredient costs per dish, where a few expensive dishes on the menu can silently dominate the total food cost if ordered frequently without anyone tracking it.

For non-technical readers

The budget headline for any leader funding a generative AI feature: unlike most software costs, which barely move with usage, generating each image, voice clip, or long piece of text has a real, per-use cost that scales directly with how popular the feature becomes. A viral feature can turn into a surprisingly large bill overnight. Before launch, ask what the cost per use is at your default settings, and what happens to the budget if usage is ten times higher than expected.

How it works

In practice it comes down to tracking cost per generation type and per user or feature, applying rate limits or tiered access to expensive generation modes, and choosing cheaper generation settings, like fewer diffusion steps or a smaller model, where the product's quality bar allows it.

A concrete example

A product offers a fast, lower-cost generation mode by default and a slower, higher-quality mode as a premium option, so most users' typical usage doesn't drive disproportionate infrastructure cost while still offering higher quality when a user specifically needs it.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Aggressively minimizing generation cost through smaller models or fewer processing steps saves money per request, but it can measurably reduce output quality, so the right balance depends on how much quality a specific product tier or use case genuinely requires

Try it in code

const generationModes = {
  fast: { steps: 15, costPerImage: 0.002 },
  premium: { steps: 50, costPerImage: 0.01 }
};
// default = fast; premium is opt-in

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real generative feature, estimate the cost difference between its highest-quality and lowest-cost generation settings, and decide which one should be the product's default.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Generative AI feature cost is not primarily driven by the same factors as typical software cost, like request volume alone; the cost per single generation, driven by model size, output length, and processing steps, can vary enormously and dominate the total bill in ways a typical API endpoint never would.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Cost control?

  • Correct answer: Generative AI feature cost is not primarily driven by the same factors as typical software cost, like request volume alone; the cost per single generation, driven by model size, output length, and processing steps, can vary enormously and dominate the total bill in ways a typical API endpoint never would.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Cost control is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Cost control in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Governance

Governance for generative AI establishes organization-wide policy on what content types are permitted to be generated, how safety and quality are evaluated before launch, and who is accountable for a generative feature's real-world output.

In plain terms

It is like an editorial board's standing policy on what a publication will and won't print, applied consistently across every new piece rather than decided fresh by each individual writer.

For non-technical readers

For a leader overseeing multiple teams building AI features, governance is what prevents each team from independently reinventing, or skipping, safety and quality standards. A written policy, what content categories are off-limits, what testing is required before launch, who's accountable when something goes wrong, is the same kind of guardrail any regulated or reputation-sensitive business already applies to marketing or legal risk; generative AI content deserves the same discipline.

How it works

In practice it comes down to documented policy defining permitted and prohibited content categories for generation, required safety and evaluation testing before a generative feature launches, and clear accountability for reviewing and responding to generated content that violates policy after launch.

A concrete example

An organization's generative AI governance policy requires every new image generation feature to pass a documented safety evaluation covering specific prohibited content categories before launch, with a named team accountable for responding to any policy violations discovered afterward.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Clear organization-wide governance ensures consistent standards and accountability across every generative feature, but overly rigid policy that doesn't account for legitimate differences between use cases, like a creative tool versus a customer-facing assistant, can unnecessarily block legitimate functionality

Try it in code

const genAiPolicy = {
  prohibitedCategories: ["non-consensual likeness", "graphic violence"],
  requiredBeforeLaunch: ["safety_evaluation_report"],
  accountableTeam: "trust-and-safety"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write one governance rule that should apply consistently across every generative AI feature in a real organization, such as a required safety evaluation category or an accountability requirement.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Generative AI governance is not just a content-moderation afterthought bolted onto a finished feature; the permitted content categories, evaluation requirements, and accountability structure need to be decided during design, since retrofitting governance onto a launched feature is far harder than building it in from the start.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Governance?

  • Correct answer: Generative AI governance is not just a content-moderation afterthought bolted onto a finished feature; the permitted content categories, evaluation requirements, and accountability structure need to be decided during design, since retrofitting governance onto a launched feature is far harder than building it in from the start.
  • Once Governance is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Governance in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework

GenAI lab

The GenAI lab is a hands-on exercise to generate content with a real model across a few prompts, evaluate the outputs on at least two separate quality dimensions, and identify one concrete failure mode to fix.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to generating outputs for several real prompts using an available generative model, scoring each output on at least two distinct dimensions such as prompt adherence and safety, and identifying one specific failure pattern worth addressing.

It is like a test kitchen session where you don't just taste a dish, but also check its presentation and whether it matches the ordered recipe.

The brief

Generate outputs for five real prompts using an available generative tool, score each on prompt adherence and one other dimension, and identify one pattern in what causes lower scores.

A worked reference

A lab might generate images for five different prompts, score each for how well it matches the prompt and whether it contains any unwanted elements, and identify that longer or more detailed prompts consistently produce better adherence.

Starting point in code

const prompts = [/* 5 real prompts */];
const results = prompts.map(p => ({
  prompt: p,
  adherence: scoreAdherence(generate(p), p),
  safety: safetyClassifier(generate(p))
}));

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Testing multiple quality dimensions instead of just one takes more evaluation effort, but it reveals real trade-offs and failure patterns a single quality score would completely hide

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • The lab is not complete once generated outputs look good at a glance; without explicitly scoring separate quality dimensions, subtle failures in prompt adherence or safety can go completely unnoticed.

Going deeper

References

  • Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models

Mini project

The mini project ships a small, complete generative feature for one real use case, with a documented prompt-to-output pipeline, a basic multi-dimensional evaluation, and a cost estimate, that another engineer could extend.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a specific generation approach, a documented pipeline from prompt to final output, a small evaluation covering at least two quality dimensions, and a cost-per-generation estimate into one reusable, documented feature.

It is like building and validating one small but fully functioning creative tool, documented well enough that someone else could extend it to a related use case.

The brief

Build a small generative feature for one real use case, document its full prompt-to-output pipeline, evaluate it on two quality dimensions, and estimate its cost per generation.

A worked reference

A mini project might build a small product-description generator, with a documented prompt template, an evaluation checking both factual accuracy and tone, and a cost estimate per generated description.

Starting point in code

export const descriptionGenerator = {
  generate: (product) => llm.complete(promptTemplate(product)),
  evaluate: (output) => ({ factual: checkFacts(output), tone: checkTone(output) }),
  costPerCall: 0.001
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A narrowly scoped feature with real evaluation and cost analysis proves the approach genuinely works within known constraints, which is more valuable than a flashier feature with untested quality and unknown cost at scale

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A generative feature that produces impressive-looking outputs in a demo is not the same as a complete mini project; without a documented pipeline, a multi-dimensional evaluation, and a cost estimate, it's a proof of concept, not something ready to hand off.

Going deeper

References

  • OpenAI API documentation

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete generative AI product: generation approach and controllability design, a multi-dimensional evaluation and safety plan, a UX design accounting for output variability, and a cost-control and governance strategy, as one coherent system.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a justified generation approach, explicit controllability mechanisms, a multi-dimensional evaluation and layered safety plan, a UX design that surfaces variability honestly, and cost-control and governance policy into one documented product design.

It is like designing a complete creative studio's operations, not just one artist's individual technique, covering quality control, cost management, and standards all at once.

The brief

Write a one-page generative AI product design for a real use case: generation approach, one evaluation dimension, one UX decision for handling variability, and one cost-control measure.

A worked reference

A capstone for a marketing-copy generation product might specify an autoregressive text generation approach with adjustable tone controls, an evaluation covering factual accuracy and brand-voice adherence, a UX offering multiple variations per request, and a governance policy prohibiting generation of specific claim types without human review.

Starting point in code

const productDesign = {
  generation: "autoregressive text, adjustable tone",
  evaluation: ["factual_accuracy", "brand_voice"],
  ux: "show 3 variations per request",
  costControl: "fast mode default, premium opt-in"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A complete product design spanning generation, evaluation, UX, cost, and governance takes substantially more design effort than building generation capability alone, but it reflects everything actually required to ship a generative product responsibly and sustainably

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A generative AI product is not complete once the underlying generation quality is good; without a UX design that accounts for variability, a cost-control strategy, and a governance policy, a technically impressive generation capability can still become an unsustainable or risky product.

Going deeper

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework

Interview pack

The generative AI interview pack prepares you to discuss generation architecture trade-offs, controllability, multi-dimensional evaluation, and safety design credibly, backed by a concrete story about a real generative feature rather than a general description of what generative AI can do.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a creative studio's actual quality-control and safety process, not just describing that it produces creative work.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real generative feature you built, evaluated, or debugged, paired with technical answers distinguishing generation patterns, controllability techniques, and layered safety design.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how would you make sure this image generator doesn't produce something inappropriate describes a specific layered safety approach, training-time alignment plus runtime filtering, and proactively raises how you'd evaluate false-positive rate without being asked.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "Image generator occasionally produced flagged content",
  cause: "runtime filter alone, no training-time alignment layer",
  fix: "added alignment training + kept runtime filter as second layer",
  result: "false-negative rate dropped, monitored via weekly sampling"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a generative feature's quality, safety, or cost problem you encountered or would anticipate, its root cause, and the specific fix.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Being able to describe what a generative model can produce is not the same as understanding how to make it controllable, safe, and cost-effective in a real product; interviewers probe specifically for that applied engineering judgment.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • Correct answer: Being able to describe what a generative model can produce is not the same as understanding how to make it controllable, safe, and cost-effective in a real product; interviewers probe specifically for that applied engineering judgment.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications

Prompt Engineering

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: Prompt engineering is like writing a clear work order for a contractor. The better you define the job, constraints, examples, and output format, the better the result. Prompts are often the cheapest way to improve behavior before changing models or architecture.

Prompt anatomy

A prompt is the complete input sent to a language model. It typically consists of: a system prompt (who the model is and what it should do), conversation history, retrieved context, and the user's message.

Why this matters

Prompt anatomy matters because the structure of a prompt — not just its words — determines output quality, consistency, and safety.

In plain terms

A prompt is like a detailed job briefing before a task. The system prompt is the job description and company policies. The conversation history is the context of what has been discussed. The retrieved context is the reference materials. The user message is today's specific request.

For non-technical readers

For a non-technical reader, the practical insight is that an AI system's behavior is shaped far more by this hidden 'job briefing' than by the visible question a customer types. Two AI chatbots built on the identical underlying model can behave completely differently, one professional and cautious, one casual and expansive, purely because of how their instructions were written. If your company's AI assistant is behaving oddly, the fix is often rewriting this behind-the-scenes briefing, not switching to a 'better' model entirely.

How it works

Internal flow: all prompt components are concatenated into a single token sequence. The model attends over the entire sequence to generate each output token.

  • System prompt: set the model's role, behaviour, restrictions, and output format. Evaluated once per session but shapes all responses.
  • Context: retrieved documents, conversation history, or provided data the model should use. Keep it relevant — irrelevant context degrades performance.
  • Instruction: what you want the model to do. Be specific about format, length, tone, and required components.
  • Examples: few-shot examples of the desired input-output format. Highly effective for teaching novel formats or edge-case behaviour.
  • Output format: tell the model explicitly what format the output should take (JSON, markdown, list, a single sentence). Models follow explicit formatting instructions reliably.

A concrete example

System: 'You are a support agent for Acme SaaS. Answer using only the provided knowledge base. If you do not know, say so. Always include the relevant article title.' | Context: [retrieved KB articles] | User: 'How do I export my data?' The model now has role, constraint, evidence, and specific instruction.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Longer, more detailed prompts improve reliability but cost more tokens and introduce more surface area for contradictions or confusion.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take a prompt you currently use. Label each part: system prompt, context, instruction, examples, output format. Now identify the weakest part (where the model most often misses your intent) and rewrite just that part.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production checklist: version prompts like code, test changes with an eval set, separate system prompt from user input explicitly, and log prompts alongside outputs.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Adding more instructions does not always improve output. Contradictory or overly long prompts can degrade performance.
  • System prompts are not secure by default. Prompt injection can override them — do not rely on system prompts for security-critical restrictions.
  • The order of components matters. Placing the most important instruction at the beginning or end of the prompt tends to improve instruction following.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Prompt anatomy?

  • Correct answer: Adding more instructions does not always improve output. Contradictory or overly long prompts can degrade performance.
  • Once Prompt anatomy is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Prompt anatomy into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: Production checklist: version prompts like code, test changes with an eval set, separate system prompt from user input explicitly, and log prompts alongside outputs.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Prompt anatomy in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Instructions

Instructions are the explicit directives in a prompt that tell a model what task to perform, in what format, and under what constraints, distinct from the context or data the model is meant to act on.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between a work order that says exactly what to build and to what specification, versus just handing someone raw materials and hoping they infer the right project.

How it works

In practice it comes down to stating the task explicitly, specifying the desired output format, and naming any constraints or exclusions, separated clearly from the content the model should act on, so the model doesn't have to infer intent from ambiguous phrasing.

A concrete example

An instruction that says summarize the following article in exactly three bullet points, each under 15 words, focusing on financial impact produces a far more reliable result than summarize this.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Highly explicit instructions produce more consistent, predictable output, but writing them takes more upfront effort than a vague request, and overly rigid instructions can prevent the model from handling a case the instruction didn't anticipate

Try it in code

// Vague
"summarize this"

// Explicit: task, format, constraint
"Summarize the following article in exactly 3 bullet points,
each under 15 words, focusing on financial impact."

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take one vague instruction you've given a model, and rewrite it with an explicit task, format, and one constraint, then compare the outputs.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A vague instruction is not a shortcut that saves time; it typically produces an output that doesn't match what was actually wanted, requiring another round of clarification that costs more time than writing a specific instruction upfront.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Instructions?

  • Correct answer: A vague instruction is not a shortcut that saves time; it typically produces an output that doesn't match what was actually wanted, requiring another round of clarification that costs more time than writing a specific instruction upfront.
  • Once Instructions is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Instructions in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI function calling guide

Context

Context is the background information included in a prompt, such as retrieved documents, prior conversation, or relevant data, that the model needs to complete the task correctly, distinct from the instruction telling it what to do with that information.

In plain terms

It is like giving a consultant the actual case files before asking for a recommendation, rather than asking them to guess based on the question alone.

How it works

In practice it comes down to including only the information genuinely relevant to the specific task, structured clearly so the model can distinguish it from the instruction, since irrelevant or excessive context can dilute the model's attention on what actually matters.

A concrete example

A support-response prompt includes the specific customer's account history and the exact policy clause relevant to their question, rather than the entire policy manual or unrelated account details.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More context can help a model answer more accurately when the extra information is genuinely relevant, but including irrelevant or excessive context wastes budget and can distract the model from the actually important details

Try it in code

const prompt = {
  instruction: "Answer the customer's question using only the policy below.",
  context: retrievedPolicyClause, // relevant, not the whole manual
  question: customerMessage
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real prompt you use, identify one piece of included context that's unnecessary for the specific task, and one piece of relevant context that's currently missing.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • More context is not automatically better; context that's irrelevant to the specific task can dilute the model's attention and even introduce distracting or contradictory information rather than improving the answer.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Context?

  • Once Context is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: More context is not automatically better; context that's irrelevant to the specific task can dilute the model's attention and even introduce distracting or contradictory information rather than improving the answer.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Context in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

Few-shot examples

Few-shot prompting provides the model with 2–10 examples of the desired input-output behaviour directly in the prompt. The model learns the pattern from examples without any weight updates.

Why this matters

Few-shot examples matter because they are the cheapest way to teach the model a specific format or behaviour that it would otherwise guess inconsistently.

In plain terms

Few-shot examples are like showing an apprentice three finished products before asking them to make one. The model does not know your specific format from training, but seeing examples makes the pattern obvious.

How it works

Internal flow: examples are part of the prompt token sequence. The model attends to them and produces outputs matching the demonstrated pattern.

  • Zero-shot: just instructions, no examples. Works for simple tasks. Fails on novel formats or nuanced requirements.
  • One-shot: one example. Useful when the format is unusual and you cannot give more examples due to context limits.
  • Few-shot (3–10 examples): the most reliable way to teach format, style, and domain-specific rules without fine-tuning.
  • Example quality matters more than quantity. Three excellent, diverse examples outperform ten inconsistent ones.
  • Examples should cover edge cases: a normal case, a negative case (where the answer is 'none' or 'N/A'), and an ambiguous case.

A concrete example

For a sentiment classifier: instead of just 'classify as positive or negative', include: 'Review: Great product, fast shipping! → Positive. Review: Broke after two days, terrible quality. → Negative. Review: It is fine, nothing special. → Neutral. Now classify: Review: Surprised by how good this is for the price.' — the model now knows the exact format and range.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Each example costs tokens (context budget). Prioritise diversity over quantity. Test with and without examples using your eval set.

Hands-on lab

Task: Choose a classification task (support ticket routing, sentiment, intent detection). Write zero-shot, one-shot, and three-shot prompts. Test each on 10 real examples. How much does each additional example improve accuracy?

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production checklist: store canonical examples in a prompt library. Version them. When model output quality drops, check if examples are still representative.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • More examples are not always better. Past 5–10 examples, gains diminish quickly and context cost rises.
  • Examples from a different distribution than real inputs can hurt performance by setting wrong expectations.
  • Few-shot prompting is not fine-tuning. The model weights do not change. Patterns persist only within the context window.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Few-shot examples?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: More examples are not always better. Past 5–10 examples, gains diminish quickly and context cost rises.
  • Once Few-shot examples is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Few-shot examples into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: Production checklist: store canonical examples in a prompt library. Version them. When model output quality drops, check if examples are still representative.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Few-shot examples in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Chain-of-thought alternatives

Chain-of-thought alternatives are prompting or architectural techniques other than asking a model to write out step-by-step reasoning, such as structured decomposition into separate calls or using a model already trained to reason internally, used when explicit reasoning text isn't the right fit.

In plain terms

It is like choosing between asking someone to narrate their thinking out loud, breaking a problem into separate smaller questions you ask one at a time, or trusting an expert who reasons internally and just gives you the polished answer.

How it works
