Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Build a 10-query evaluation set with the correct answer labeled for each, run it against your current retrieval setup, and calculate recall at 5.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A model's general benchmark ranking on a public leaderboard does not guarantee it performs best on your specific domain and query patterns; only evaluation on your own realistic queries and documents tells you that.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Evaluation?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A model's general benchmark ranking on a public leaderboard does not guarantee it performs best on your specific domain and query patterns; only evaluation on your own realistic queries and documents tells you that.
  • Once Evaluation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Evaluation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • MTEB: Massive Text Embedding Benchmark

Drift

Drift in an embedding system happens when the meaning of content or queries changes over time relative to when the embeddings were generated, or when the embedding model itself is updated, causing previously good retrieval to gradually degrade without any code changing.

In plain terms

It is like a map slowly going out of date as new roads are built and old ones close, even though the map itself never changed; what changed is the territory it's supposed to represent.

How it works

In practice it comes down to either the underlying content's vocabulary and topics shifting over time relative to a static embedding index, or an embedding model provider updating or deprecating a model version, both of which can shift the vector space in ways that make old embeddings inconsistent with new ones.

A concrete example

A support knowledge base embedded a year ago retrieves noticeably worse results today because new product terminology introduced since then wasn't part of the original document set the embeddings were built from.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Re-embedding the entire corpus regularly keeps the vector space current with new terminology and an updated model, but re-embedding a large corpus has real compute cost and requires careful migration so old and new vectors aren't mixed inconsistently in the same index

Try it in code

const driftPolicy = {
  trigger: "recall@5 on the eval set drops below 0.75, OR embedding model has a new major version",
  action: "re-embed the full corpus, run the eval set again before cutover"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Define a re-embedding trigger policy for one real system: what signal, such as a new model release or a measured drop in evaluation recall, would prompt a full re-embedding of the corpus.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • An embedding index is not a set-once asset; without periodic re-evaluation and re-embedding, retrieval quality can quietly decay as content and language evolve, with no error to signal the degradation.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Drift?

  • Once Drift is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: An embedding index is not a set-once asset; without periodic re-evaluation and re-embedding, retrieval quality can quietly decay as content and language evolve, with no error to signal the degradation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Drift in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • MTEB: Massive Text Embedding Benchmark

Storage

Storage for embeddings covers how vectors are persisted and organized, including choice of vector database or index, whether to compress vectors with quantization, and how to keep vectors consistent with their source content as it's updated or deleted.

In plain terms

It is like deciding how to organize and shelve an ever-growing library so that adding, removing, or updating a book doesn't require reorganizing the whole building.

How it works

In practice it comes down to persisting each vector with an identifier linking it back to its source content and metadata, choosing an index structure suited to the expected query volume, and applying quantization to compress vectors when storage cost at scale outweighs the small accuracy loss it introduces.

A concrete example

A system storing 10 million document-chunk embeddings uses int8 quantization to cut storage by roughly four times, accepting a small, measured reduction in retrieval precision in exchange for substantially lower infrastructure cost.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Storing full-precision vectors preserves maximum retrieval accuracy, but quantized or compressed vectors cost significantly less to store and search at large scale, with a measurable but often acceptable accuracy trade-off

Try it in code

async function onDocumentUpdated(doc) {
  const newEmbedding = await embed(doc.text);
  await vectorStore.upsert(doc.id, newEmbedding, { updatedAt: Date.now() });
  // If this step silently fails, retrieval serves a stale vector forever.
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design the update path for one real system: when a source document changes, what specifically needs to happen to its stored embedding, and how would you detect if that update step silently failed.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Vector storage is not set-and-forget once vectors are inserted; when source content is updated or deleted, the corresponding vectors must be updated or removed too, or retrieval will return stale or orphaned results that no longer match reality.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Storage?

  • Once Storage is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Vector storage is not set-and-forget once vectors are inserted; when source content is updated or deleted, the corresponding vectors must be updated or removed too, or retrieval will return stale or orphaned results that no longer match reality.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Storage in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • FAISS documentation

Serving

Serving embeddings in production means generating vectors for new content or incoming queries in real time, with attention to latency, batching, and caching, since embedding generation sits directly in the critical path of a live retrieval request.

In plain terms

It is like a translation booth that needs to keep up with a live conversation in real time, not just produce an accurate translation eventually.

How it works

In practice it comes down to an embedding service that batches multiple simultaneous embedding requests together to use compute more efficiently, caches embeddings for frequently repeated queries, and is deployed close to the retrieval system to minimize network latency added to every search request.

A concrete example

A search API caches the embedding for common repeated queries like reset password so it doesn't recompute the same vector on every request, cutting p95 latency for the most frequent query patterns.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Batching embedding requests improves throughput and reduces cost per request, but it adds a small latency delay while waiting to fill a batch, which can hurt single-request latency if the batch window is tuned too aggressively for throughput

Try it in code

const cache = new Map();
async function embedQuery(text) {
  if (cache.has(text)) return cache.get(text); // skip recompute for repeats
  const vec = await embeddingModel.embed(text);
  cache.set(text, vec);
  return vec;
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Measure or estimate the latency added by embedding generation for a single live query in a real or hypothetical system, and identify one change, such as caching or a smaller model, that would reduce it.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Embedding generation is not a one-time offline cost that disappears after initial indexing; every live user query also needs an embedding generated in real time, and that step's latency directly adds to the user's perceived response time.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Serving?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Embedding generation is not a one-time offline cost that disappears after initial indexing; every live user query also needs an embedding generated in real time, and that step's latency directly adds to the user's perceived response time.
  • Once Serving is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Serving in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI embeddings guide

Embedding lab

The embedding lab is a hands-on exercise to embed a small real document set, run retrieval against a handful of realistic queries, and measure whether the correct chunk is actually retrieved, rather than trusting embeddings work from theory alone.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to chunking a small real document, generating embeddings for each chunk, writing three to five realistic queries with a known correct chunk for each, running retrieval, and checking whether the correct chunk appears in the top results.

It is like test-driving a car around the block before trusting it for a long trip.

The brief

Chunk and embed one real short document, write five queries with the correct chunk labeled in advance, and measure how many are correctly retrieved in the top 3 results.

A worked reference

A lab might embed a 10-page FAQ document in chunks, write five real user-style questions with the correct chunk labeled for each, and measure how many of the five correctly retrieve their labeled chunk in the top 3 results.

Starting point in code

// Lab: label the correct chunk BEFORE running retrieval
const labeledQueries = [
  { query: "what is the refund window?", correctChunkId: "policy-chunk-4" },
  // ...4 more
];
// Run retrieval, then check: did the correct chunk land in the top 3?

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Testing on a small, real, labeled set surfaces genuine retrieval problems fast, but it won't catch issues that only appear at larger scale, like index performance degradation or rare query patterns not represented in the small test set

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • The lab is not complete when retrieval returns something plausible-looking for each query; it's only meaningful once you've labeled the actual correct answer in advance and checked whether retrieval found it.

Going deeper

References

  • MTEB: Massive Text Embedding Benchmark

Mini project

The mini project ships a small, complete embedding-backed search feature for one real document set, with chunking, storage, retrieval, and a basic evaluation set, that another engineer could extend to a different document set.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a chunking strategy, an embedding model choice with a stated reason, a storage or index choice, and a small labeled evaluation set into one documented, reusable search feature.

It is like building a small working search engine for one shelf of a library, in a way that could be extended to the rest of the building.

The brief

Build semantic search over one real small document set you have access to, including a 10-query evaluation set, and report the recall at 5 you measured.

A worked reference

A mini project might build semantic search over a team's internal wiki: chunking by section, embedding each chunk, storing vectors with source metadata, and a 10-query evaluation set showing retrieval quality.

Starting point in code

export const wikiSearch = {
  chunk: (doc) => chunkBySection(doc),
  embed: (chunk) => embeddingModel.embed(chunk.text),
  retrieve: (query, topK = 5) => index.query(embedQuery(query), { topK }),
  evalSet: require("./eval-queries.json") // 10 labeled queries
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A narrowly scoped project with a real evaluation set proves the approach actually works, which is more valuable than a broader but unverified search feature across many document types

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A search feature that returns plausible-looking results in a demo is not the same as a working mini project; without a labeled evaluation set proving retrieval quality, there's no evidence it actually works beyond the few examples you happened to try.

Going deeper

References

  • Sentence Transformers documentation

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete embedding architecture for a real product: chunking strategy, embedding model choice with justification, storage and indexing plan, drift monitoring, and an evaluation plan, as one coherent system.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a chosen chunking approach, an embedding model selected and justified against real evaluation data, a storage and metadata design, a re-embedding or drift-monitoring policy, and an ongoing evaluation cadence into one documented architecture.

It is like designing the complete cataloging and shelving system for a growing library, including a plan for what happens as the collection ages and changes.

The brief

Write a one-page embedding architecture for a real product: chunking strategy, embedding model choice with a justification, storage plan, and one drift-monitoring trigger.

A worked reference

A capstone for a legal document search product might specify section-based chunking, an embedding model chosen after evaluation on real legal queries, metadata for jurisdiction and date, a quarterly re-embedding trigger, and a monthly recall-at-5 evaluation report.

Starting point in code

const embeddingArchitecture = {
  chunking: "section-based, ~400 tokens",
  model: "chosen after evaluation on 50 real legal queries",
  metadata: ["jurisdiction", "effective_date"],
  driftTrigger: "quarterly re-embed OR recall@5 < 0.75",
  evalCadence: "monthly recall@5 report"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A complete embedding architecture takes real evaluation work to justify each choice, but it prevents the common failure of picking a popular embedding model and chunking strategy without verifying they actually fit the product's real content and queries

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • An embedding architecture is not complete once search returns reasonable-looking results in early testing; without a drift-monitoring plan and an ongoing evaluation cadence, retrieval quality will degrade silently as content and language evolve.

Going deeper

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
  • MTEB: Massive Text Embedding Benchmark

Interview pack

The embeddings interview pack prepares you to discuss vector search design credibly: chunking trade-offs, embedding model selection, distance metric choice, and how you'd detect and handle drift, backed by a concrete story rather than a definition of what an embedding is.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a search engine's actual ranking mechanics, not just that it finds relevant results.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real embedding or retrieval system you built or debugged, paired with technical answers distinguishing chunking strategies, embedding model trade-offs, and how evaluation and drift monitoring fit together.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how you would build semantic search for this product names a specific chunking approach with a reason, a specific embedding model with a reason, and proactively raises how you'd evaluate and monitor it over time without being asked.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "Search recall silently dropped over 6 months",
  cause: "new product terminology wasn't in the original embedded corpus",
  fix: "added a recall@5 monitor + quarterly re-embed trigger",
  result: "regressions now caught within a week instead of a customer complaint"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a retrieval quality problem you encountered or would anticipate, such as bad chunking or model drift, its root cause, and the specific fix.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that embeddings capture semantic similarity is not the same as being able to justify a chunking strategy, distance metric, or drift-monitoring plan for a specific real product; interviewers probe for that applied judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Knowing that embeddings capture semantic similarity is not the same as being able to justify a chunking strategy, distance metric, or drift-monitoring plan for a specific real product; interviewers probe for that applied judgment specifically.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • MTEB: Massive Text Embedding Benchmark

Vector Databases

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: A vector database is like a library organized by meaning instead of alphabetical order. You ask for a concept and it finds nearby concepts quickly. It stores and searches embeddings at product scale, often with metadata and filtering.

Vector DB role

A vector database stores embeddings and metadata so applications can retrieve semantically similar chunks, items, or memories at low latency.

In plain terms

It is like a similarity index for meaning rather than alphabetical order.

For non-technical readers

In plain terms, a vector database is the piece of infrastructure that makes 'ask questions about our documents' AI features possible at a reasonable cost and speed, it's a specialized search index for meaning rather than exact words. It's an added infrastructure cost and something your team needs to operate, so it's a fair question to ask whether a smaller-scale feature genuinely needs one, versus a simpler search approach that might suffice at lower cost and complexity.

How it works

In practice it comes down to embedding storage, nearest-neighbor indexes, metadata filters, namespaces, upserts, deletes, and recall/latency tuning.

A concrete example

A RAG system stores policy chunks as vectors with source ids and ACL metadata, then queries nearest neighbors for each user question.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Vector DBs improve semantic retrieval but add index tuning, freshness, and permission complexity.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Map the role of source store, embedding job, vector DB, and application in one RAG workflow.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not treat a vector database as the source of truth; keep source documents and provenance elsewhere.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Vector DB role?

  • Correct answer: Do not treat a vector database as the source of truth; keep source documents and provenance elsewhere.
  • Once Vector DB role is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Vector DB role in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Indexing

Indexing organizes chunks, vectors, metadata, and lexical terms so retrieval can answer queries quickly and repeatably.

In plain terms

It is like a library catalog plus a semantic map: one helps find exact labels, the other finds similar meaning.

How it works

In practice it comes down to vector indexes, BM25 indexes, metadata filters, namespaces, rebuilds, compaction, and freshness checks.

A concrete example

A product docs index may store vector embeddings, title keywords, version metadata, and customer visibility rules.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Fast indexes trade recall for latency; exact search is expensive at scale.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create an index plan for 1,000 docs: fields, filters, rebuild cadence, and one freshness metric.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not update content without a strategy for stale vectors and deleted documents.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Indexing?

  • Once Indexing is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not update content without a strategy for stale vectors and deleted documents.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Indexing in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

HNSW

HNSW is an approximate nearest-neighbor index that builds layered graph links between vectors so search can navigate quickly to nearby points.

In plain terms

It is like a city map with highways and local roads: long links get you near the area, short links find the exact street.

How it works

In practice it comes down to graph layers, entry point, efConstruction, efSearch, M parameter, recall, latency, and memory overhead.

Topic diagram

Diagram: HNSW graph search for HNSW

A concrete example

Increasing efSearch often improves recall but makes each query slower.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Higher recall costs memory and latency; lower settings are faster but can miss relevant chunks.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Run two HNSW settings and compare recall@5, p95 latency, and memory use.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not tune HNSW without a labeled retrieval eval set.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about HNSW?

  • Once HNSW is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not tune HNSW without a labeled retrieval eval set.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain HNSW in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Efficient and robust approximate nearest neighbor search using Hierarchical Navigable Small World graphs

IVF

IVF, or inverted file indexing, clusters vectors into partitions and searches only selected partitions to reduce nearest-neighbor search cost.

In plain terms

It is like sorting books into rooms first, then searching only the most relevant rooms.

How it works

In practice it comes down to centroids, partitions, nlist, nprobe, recall, latency, and training the index on representative vectors.

A concrete example

A million vectors can be clustered into partitions; at query time the system probes the nearest partitions instead of scanning all vectors.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Searching fewer partitions is faster but may miss relevant vectors in nearby clusters.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Choose nprobe values for a small eval set and graph recall vs latency.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not train IVF on unrepresentative data and expect stable recall.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about IVF?

  • Once IVF is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not train IVF on unrepresentative data and expect stable recall.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain IVF in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • FAISS documentation

Hybrid search

Hybrid search combines lexical matching such as BM25 with vector similarity so retrieval can handle exact terms and semantic intent.

In plain terms

It is like asking both a librarian who knows exact titles and an expert who understands the topic.

How it works

In practice it comes down to keyword scoring, vector scoring, score normalization, rank fusion, metadata filters, and re-ranking.

A concrete example

A query for 'SOC2 retention' needs exact compliance terms and semantically related policy text.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Hybrid search improves recall but adds scoring complexity and tuning burden.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Compare BM25-only, vector-only, and hybrid results for five queries with acronyms or product names.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not add vector search and assume exact identifiers, error codes, or legal terms will still rank well.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Hybrid search?

  • Once Hybrid search is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not add vector search and assume exact identifiers, error codes, or legal terms will still rank well.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Hybrid search in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

BM25

BM25 is a lexical ranking function that scores documents based on exact term overlap, term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length normalization.

In plain terms

It is like a librarian who is excellent at finding exact words, acronyms, product names, and error codes.

How it works

In practice it comes down to tokenization, term frequency, inverse document frequency, length normalization, analyzers, and query terms.

A concrete example

A search for 'ERR_CONN_RESET' should use BM25-style lexical matching because semantic embeddings may blur the exact error code.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: BM25 is precise for exact terms but misses semantic matches with different wording.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Compare BM25 and vector results for five queries containing acronyms, error codes, and product names.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not replace keyword search entirely with vectors when users search for ids, codes, names, or legal terms.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about BM25?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once BM25 is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not replace keyword search entirely with vectors when users search for ids, codes, names, or legal terms.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain BM25 in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • The Probabilistic Relevance Framework: BM25 and Beyond

Metadata filtering

Metadata filtering restricts retrieval by structured fields such as tenant, permissions, document type, language, region, freshness, or product version.

In plain terms

It is like asking a librarian to search only books you are allowed to read and only from the current edition.

How it works

In practice it comes down to filterable fields, ACLs, tenant ids, timestamps, product versions, query planning, and post-filter vs pre-filter behavior.

A concrete example

A support assistant retrieves only documents for the user's product tier, region, and permission group before ranking chunks.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Strict filters improve security and relevance but can reduce recall if metadata is incomplete or stale.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design metadata fields for private product documentation and write three queries that must be filtered.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not apply permission filters after generation; retrieval must enforce access before evidence reaches the model.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Metadata filtering?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Metadata filtering is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not apply permission filters after generation; retrieval must enforce access before evidence reaches the model.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Metadata filtering in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Re-ranking

Re-ranking is a second-stage retrieval step that takes the top K retrieved chunks and re-scores them with a more expensive, more accurate relevance model before passing the top N to the LLM.

Why this matters

Re-ranking matters because it improves the signal-to-noise ratio of the context passed to the LLM, which directly improves answer quality.

In plain terms

Retrieval is a fast, rough filter — like a keyword search that returns 20 results. Re-ranking is a careful expert review of those 20 results to order them by true relevance. You do the rough pass cheaply, then spend more effort on the shortlist.

How it works

Internal flow: retrieval top K → cross-encoder scores (query, chunk₁), (query, chunk₂), … (query, chunkK) → sort by score → take top N → pass to LLM.

  • Cross-encoders take the (query, document) pair as joint input and produce a relevance score. More accurate than embedding similarity but too slow for full-corpus search.
  • Bi-encoders (embedding models) encode query and document separately — fast enough for full-corpus search but less accurate for precise relevance judgement.
  • Re-ranking pipeline: fast bi-encoder retrieves top 50–100 candidates → cross-encoder re-ranks to top 3–5 → top 3–5 passed to LLM.
  • Re-ranking significantly improves precision (sending the right chunks to the LLM) without changing recall (the initial retrieval covers the same documents).
  • Models like Cohere Rerank, BGE-Reranker, and Jina Reranker are specialised for this step.

A concrete example

A legal document search retrieves 20 potentially relevant clauses. Without re-ranking, the top 3 by embedding similarity might be thematically related but not the most precise match. A cross-encoder re-ranker reads each clause paired with the query and correctly identifies that clause 7 and clause 14 are the most directly relevant.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Re-ranking adds 50–200ms latency and API/compute cost. The improvement in answer quality often justifies it for high-stakes use cases.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take 10 queries and their retrieved top 10 chunks. Manually re-rank each query by relevance. Compare your ranking to the original retrieval order. How often does the correct chunk move up? This is the potential improvement a re-ranker could provide.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Eval: measure precision@3 with and without re-ranking. If the correct chunk moves from position 4–6 to position 1–3, re-ranking is working.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Re-ranking does not improve recall — if the correct chunk was not in the initial top K, re-ranking cannot find it. Fix recall first.
  • Re-ranking is not always necessary. For simple, clear queries with good chunking, basic retrieval may already produce the correct top 3.
  • Adding a re-ranker does not replace evaluation. Measure whether it actually improves answer quality on your specific corpus and query distribution.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Re-ranking?

  • Once Re-ranking is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Re-ranking does not improve recall — if the correct chunk was not in the initial top K, re-ranking cannot find it. Fix recall first.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Re-ranking into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: Eval: measure precision@3 with and without re-ranking. If the correct chunk moves from position 4–6 to position 1–3, re-ranking is working.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Re-ranking in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Schema design

Schema design in a vector database defines what fields accompany each vector, such as source ID, metadata for filtering, and namespace, structured so that retrieval queries can combine similarity search with precise filtering efficiently.

In plain terms

It is like designing a library card catalog: the book's shelf position is one thing, but the catalog also needs author, genre, and date fields to let people search by more than just similarity of content.

How it works

In practice it comes down to defining a vector field alongside indexed metadata fields such as source document ID, category, timestamp, and access permissions, choosing which metadata fields need their own index for fast filtering versus which can be scanned linearly on a small result set.

A concrete example

A support-document schema stores the embedding vector, a source document ID, a department tag, and a last-updated timestamp, so queries can filter to one department's fresh documents before ranking by similarity.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Indexing more metadata fields makes more filter combinations fast, but each additional indexed field adds storage and write overhead, so schema design should prioritize the filters queries will actually use

Try it in code

{
  "vector": [0.012, -0.44, /* ...1536 dims */],
  "id": "doc_4821_chunk_3",
  "metadata": {
    "sourceDocId": "doc_4821",
    "department": "billing",
    "updatedAt": "2025-01-14",
    "aclGroup": "internal"
  }
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a schema for one real document retrieval system, listing which metadata fields need their own index and which don't.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A vector database schema is not just the vector plus an ID; without deliberately chosen metadata fields, there is no way to scope or filter a similarity search, which most real production queries require.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Schema design?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A vector database schema is not just the vector plus an ID; without deliberately chosen metadata fields, there is no way to scope or filter a similarity search, which most real production queries require.
  • Once Schema design is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Schema design in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Pinecone chunking strategies

Multi-tenancy

Multi-tenancy in a vector database means isolating one customer or team's vectors from another's, ensuring a similarity search never returns or is influenced by data belonging to a different tenant.

In plain terms

It is like separate locked storage units in the same facility: everyone shares the building, but no unit's contents are visible or accessible from another.

For non-technical readers

If your company sells software to multiple customers, this is the specific technical guarantee that keeps one customer's data from ever leaking into another customer's AI search results. It's exactly the kind of thing worth asking a vendor about directly: 'how do you guarantee my company's data never shows up in another customer's results,' since a vague answer here is a real red flag for a multi-customer product handling any sensitive data.

How it works

In practice it comes down to partitioning vectors by tenant through separate indexes, separate namespaces within a shared index, or a mandatory tenant-ID metadata filter enforced on every single query, chosen based on tenant count, isolation requirements, and query performance needs.

A concrete example

A SaaS product serving thousands of customers uses a shared index with a mandatory tenant-ID filter applied to every query at the infrastructure level, rather than trusting application code to always remember to add the filter.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Separate indexes per tenant give the strongest isolation guarantee and simplest security reasoning, but they don't scale well to thousands of small tenants, while a shared index with enforced filtering scales better but requires the filter enforcement to be bulletproof, not just a convention

Try it in code

async function search(tenantId, queryVector, topK = 5) {
  return index.query({
    vector: queryVector,
    topK,
    filter: { tenantId: { "$eq": tenantId } } // enforced, not optional
  });
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real multi-tenant system, decide between separate indexes per tenant and a shared index with enforced filtering, and justify the choice based on expected tenant count.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Relying on application code to remember to filter by tenant ID on every query is not real multi-tenant isolation; a single missed filter in one code path can leak one tenant's data into another's search results, so isolation needs to be enforced at the infrastructure level, not just by convention.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Multi-tenancy?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Multi-tenancy is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Relying on application code to remember to filter by tenant ID on every query is not real multi-tenant isolation; a single missed filter in one code path can leak one tenant's data into another's search results, so isolation needs to be enforced at the infrastructure level, not just by convention.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Multi-tenancy in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • FAISS documentation

Performance tuning

Performance tuning for a vector database means adjusting index parameters, such as the number of graph connections in an HNSW index or the number of clusters in an IVF index, to balance search speed, recall accuracy, and memory usage for a specific workload.

In plain terms

It is like tuning a car's suspension: a stiffer setting is faster on a smooth track but rougher on bumpy roads, and the right setting depends on the specific driving conditions.

How it works

In practice it comes down to adjusting index-specific parameters, such as HNSW's connectivity and search-depth parameters or IVF's cluster count and number of clusters probed per query, and measuring the resulting trade-off between query latency, recall, and memory footprint on representative data and query patterns.

A concrete example

Increasing an HNSW index's search-depth parameter improves recall but increases query latency, so a team tunes it by testing several values against their own evaluation set until they find the point where recall is good enough without exceeding their latency budget.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More aggressive tuning for higher recall almost always costs more latency or memory, so performance tuning is fundamentally about finding the acceptable trade-off point for a specific product's requirements, not chasing the single best setting in the abstract

Try it in code

const configs = [
  { efSearch: 50 },  { efSearch: 100 }, { efSearch: 200 }
];
for (const cfg of configs) {
  const { recallAt5, p95LatencyMs } = await benchmark(cfg);
  console.log(cfg, { recallAt5, p95LatencyMs });
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real vector index, identify one tunable parameter, and describe the experiment you'd run to find its right setting for your latency and recall requirements.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Default index parameters are not necessarily right for every workload; the optimal settings depend heavily on vector count, dimensionality, and query patterns, so performance tuning requires testing against your own actual data, not copying another team's configuration.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Performance tuning?

  • Once Performance tuning is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Default index parameters are not necessarily right for every workload; the optimal settings depend heavily on vector count, dimensionality, and query patterns, so performance tuning requires testing against your own actual data, not copying another team's configuration.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Performance tuning in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Efficient and robust approximate nearest neighbor search using HNSW

Backup

Backup for a vector database means having a reliable, tested way to restore both the vectors and their associated metadata after data loss, corruption, or an accidental deletion, since a vector index alone is often expensive and slow to fully regenerate from scratch.

In plain terms

It is like keeping a copy of a hand-built model, not just the raw materials; regenerating the model from scratch after a loss can take far longer than restoring a backup.

How it works

In practice it comes down to periodic snapshots of the vector index and its metadata, stored separately from the live system, with a documented and tested restore procedure, distinct from simply having the ability to re-run the embedding pipeline from source documents.

A concrete example

A team backs up their vector index nightly to object storage, and periodically tests a full restore into a staging environment to confirm the backup is actually usable, not just that the backup job reports success.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Frequent backups reduce the amount of data lost in an incident, but they add storage cost and operational overhead, so backup frequency should match how quickly and expensively the underlying data actually changes

Try it in code

# Nightly snapshot to object storage
vectordb-cli snapshot create --index prod-docs --dest s3://backups/vectordb/

# Monthly restore drill (into staging, not prod)
vectordb-cli snapshot restore --file latest.snapshot --index staging-restore-test

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real vector database deployment, define a backup frequency and describe the specific test you'd run to confirm a backup can actually be restored, not just that it exists.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Being able to regenerate embeddings from the original source documents is not the same as having a tested backup; regenerating a large corpus of embeddings from scratch can take significant time and compute cost, which may be unacceptable during an actual incident.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Backup?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Backup is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Being able to regenerate embeddings from the original source documents is not the same as having a tested backup; regenerating a large corpus of embeddings from scratch can take significant time and compute cost, which may be unacceptable during an actual incident.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Backup in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • FAISS documentation

Operations

Operations for a vector database means monitoring query latency, recall quality, index size growth, and write throughput in production, so degradation is caught through metrics rather than discovered when users notice worse search results.

In plain terms

It is like a warehouse manager tracking how long it takes to find an item and whether the found item is actually correct, not just whether the warehouse is still standing.

For non-technical readers

The non-technical version: an AI search feature that worked great at launch can quietly get slower or less accurate over time as more data piles in, with no obvious error to alert anyone. This is why 'we launched it and it worked' isn't the same as 'it's still working well six months later'; ask whether your team is actually watching search quality and speed on an ongoing basis, not just at launch.

How it works

In practice it comes down to tracking p50 and p95 query latency, periodic recall sampling against a labeled evaluation set, index size and memory usage growth over time, and write or upsert throughput, with alerts when any metric drifts from its established baseline.

A concrete example

A dashboard tracks that p95 query latency has crept up 40 percent over a month as the index grew, prompting a decision to either re-tune index parameters or shard the index across more nodes.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Comprehensive operational monitoring catches performance and quality degradation early, but building recall-sampling pipelines in particular takes real ongoing effort compared to just monitoring latency and uptime

Try it in code

const dashboard = {
  p95QueryLatencyMs: "alert if > 2x 30-day baseline",
  recallAt5Sample: "alert if < 90% on weekly labeled sample",
  indexSizeGrowth: "alert if > 20% week-over-week unexpectedly"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Choose three operational metrics for a real vector database deployment, and define an alert threshold for each based on a specific, actionable degradation.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A vector database that is up and responding is not necessarily performing well; latency can degrade gradually as the index grows, and recall can silently drop due to configuration drift, neither of which shows up as a simple uptime or error-rate metric.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Operations?

  • Once Operations is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A vector database that is up and responding is not necessarily performing well; latency can degrade gradually as the index grows, and recall can silently drop due to configuration drift, neither of which shows up as a simple uptime or error-rate metric.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Operations in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Efficient and robust approximate nearest neighbor search using HNSW

Vector DB lab

The vector DB lab is a hands-on exercise to load a small real dataset into a vector index, measure baseline recall and latency, then adjust one index parameter and measure how both change.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to indexing a small labeled dataset, running a baseline set of queries to measure recall and latency, then changing one specific index parameter and re-measuring both to observe the actual trade-off rather than assuming it.

It is like tuning one dial on a machine and observing exactly what changes, instead of guessing at the effect.

The brief

Index a small labeled dataset, measure baseline recall and latency, then change one index parameter and report the measured change in both.

A worked reference

A lab might index 10,000 sample documents, measure recall at 5 and p95 latency at a baseline HNSW setting, then increase the search-depth parameter and measure both again to see the concrete trade-off.

Starting point in code

const baseline = await benchmark({ efSearch: 50 });
const tuned = await benchmark({ efSearch: 150 });
console.log({ baseline, tuned }); // compare recall@5 and p95 latency

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Measuring the actual before-and-after trade-off on real data takes more setup than reading about parameter effects in the abstract, but it builds real calibration for how a specific parameter behaves on your own data shape

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • The lab is not complete once the index returns results; without measuring recall against a labeled set and latency under the parameter change, you have no evidence of what actually improved or got worse.

Going deeper

References

  • Efficient and robust approximate nearest neighbor search using HNSW

Mini project

The mini project ships a small, complete vector database setup for one real use case, with a documented schema, tuned index parameters, and a basic operational dashboard, that another engineer could extend.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a documented schema with metadata fields, index parameters chosen and justified from measured trade-offs, and at least one operational metric being tracked into one reusable, documented setup.

It is like setting up and documenting one well-organized storage system that could serve as the template for organizing a related one.

The brief

Set up a vector index for one real use case, document the schema and index parameter choices with measurements, and add one operational dashboard metric.

A worked reference

A mini project might set up a vector index for a product-search feature, with a documented schema including category and price metadata, tuned HNSW parameters based on measured recall, and a latency dashboard.

Starting point in code

export const productSearchIndex = {
  schema: { vector: "embedding", metadata: ["category", "price"] },
  indexParams: { type: "hnsw", efSearch: 100 }, // chosen from measured recall
  dashboardMetric: "p95QueryLatencyMs"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A narrowly scoped, well-documented setup with real measurements is more valuable than a larger deployment with unverified performance and no operational visibility

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A vector index that returns results in testing is not the same as a complete mini project; without documented schema decisions, measured parameter tuning, and at least basic monitoring, it's not ready to hand off.

Going deeper

References

  • FAISS documentation

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete vector database architecture for a real product: schema design, index choice and tuning, multi-tenancy approach, backup strategy, and an operational monitoring plan, as one coherent system.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a documented schema, a justified index type and tuned parameters, a multi-tenancy isolation approach appropriate to tenant count, a tested backup strategy, and operational monitoring into one complete, documented architecture.

It is like designing the complete operating plan for a large archive: how it's organized, how it's kept fast to search, how different departments' materials stay separate, and how it recovers from damage.

The brief

Write a one-page vector database architecture for a real product: schema, index choice with tuning rationale, multi-tenancy approach, and backup strategy.

A worked reference

A capstone for a multi-customer document search product might specify a shared index with enforced tenant-ID filtering, HNSW tuned for a measured latency budget, nightly tested backups, and a dashboard tracking recall, latency, and index growth.

Starting point in code

const architecture = {
  schema: "vector + sourceId + department + updatedAt",
  index: "HNSW, efSearch=100 (tuned for 50ms p95 budget)",
  multiTenancy: "shared index, enforced tenantId filter",
  backup: "nightly snapshot, monthly restore drill",
  monitoring: "recall@5 weekly sample, p95 latency alarm"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A complete architecture spanning schema, tuning, isolation, backup, and monitoring takes substantially more design work than getting a vector search feature functionally working, but it reflects what's actually needed to run one reliably at scale

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A vector database architecture is not complete once search returns good results in testing; without a tested backup strategy and multi-tenant isolation guarantees, it is not ready for production use with real customer data.

Going deeper

References

  • Efficient and robust approximate nearest neighbor search using HNSW
  • FAISS documentation

Interview pack

The vector databases interview pack prepares you to discuss index selection, tuning trade-offs, multi-tenancy, and operational concerns credibly, backed by a concrete story rather than reciting the definition of a vector database.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a library's actual cataloging and access-control system, not just that it can be searched.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real vector database system you built, tuned, or debugged, paired with technical answers distinguishing index types, tuning trade-offs, and multi-tenancy isolation approaches.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how would you scale this vector search to millions of documents names a specific index type and tuning rationale, describes a multi-tenancy isolation approach, and proactively raises backup and recall-monitoring plans without being asked.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "Search latency crept up 40% as the index grew",
  cause: "efSearch never re-tuned after 10x data growth",
  fix: "re-benchmarked and re-tuned index parameters, added a growth alert",
  result: "p95 latency back within budget, caught earlier next time"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a vector search performance or recall problem you encountered or would anticipate, its root cause, and the specific fix.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that HNSW and IVF are index types is not the same as being able to justify which one and what parameters fit a specific real workload; interviewers probe for that applied tuning judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • Correct answer: Knowing that HNSW and IVF are index types is not the same as being able to justify which one and what parameters fit a specific real workload; interviewers probe for that applied tuning judgment specifically.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Efficient and robust approximate nearest neighbor search using HNSW

RAG

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: RAG is like asking a skilled analyst to answer with a stack of source documents open on the desk. The analyst is still doing the writing, but the answer must be grounded in the documents that were retrieved. RAG helps when the model needs private, fresh, or domain-specific knowledge that was not reliable in pretraining.

The running example

Throughout this track, imagine you own HelpDesk: an internal assistant that answers employee questions from the company wiki, HR policies, and engineering runbooks. Every chapter is a decision you will make — and a way HelpDesk can fail — on the road to shipping it.

RAG overview

RAG, or Retrieval-Augmented Generation, is a production pattern that retrieves trusted context at request time, gives that context to a model, and requires the answer to stay grounded in the retrieved evidence.

When it goes wrong

Your company chatbot confidently tells a customer the refund window is 60 days. It's 30. The model wasn't lying — it answered from its training data instead of your current policy. RAG exists to fix exactly this.

Why this matters

RAG matters because it lets LLM products use fresh, private, permissioned, and citeable knowledge without retraining the model.

In plain terms

RAG is like asking a skilled analyst to answer with source documents open on the desk. The analyst still writes the answer, but the answer must be supported by retrieved evidence.

For non-technical readers

This is the standard approach for letting an AI assistant answer questions about your company's own documents, policies, or product catalog, information it was never trained on, without retraining anything. It's why a support chatbot can correctly cite your actual return policy instead of guessing, and it's also why the quality of your company's documents matters directly: RAG can only be as accurate as the source material it retrieves from. If your internal documentation is outdated or contradictory, a RAG-based assistant will confidently repeat those same problems back to your customers.

How it works

The internal flow is source ingestion, permission filtering, chunking, embedding, indexing, retrieval, re-ranking, prompt assembly, grounded generation, citation validation, and eval logging.

  • Ingestion converts source documents into clean records with provenance, permissions, version, and metadata.
  • Chunking splits records into retrievable units; chunk size and overlap control the precision vs context trade-off.
  • Embedding and indexing make chunks searchable by meaning, keywords, metadata, or hybrid ranking.
  • Retrieval and re-ranking select the evidence that should enter the model context.
  • Generation must cite, abstain, or escalate when evidence is missing, stale, contradictory, or insufficient.

Topic diagram

Diagram: RAG evidence path for RAG overview

A concrete example

A benefits assistant answers 'Can I carry over unused PTO?' by retrieving the current policy chunk, checking employee region metadata, generating a cited answer, and refusing to answer if no current policy source is found.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Recall vs precision: retrieving more evidence reduces misses but can add noise, increase cost, and make hallucinations harder to detect.

Try it in code

async function answerWithRag(question, corpus) {
  const chunks = chunkDocuments(corpus, { size: 500, overlap: 80 });
  const index = await buildVectorIndex(chunks);
  const candidates = await index.search(await embed(question), { topK: 8 });
  const evidence = rerank(question, candidates).slice(0, 3);
  if (!evidence.length) return { answer: "I do not know from the available sources.", citations: [] };
  return generateGroundedAnswer({ question, evidence, requireCitations: true });
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Build a five-document RAG test: create chunks with source ids, write ten questions with expected source ids, include two unanswerable questions, and score retrieval recall plus citation support.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-shaped slice should include a tiny corpus, expected-source evals, one unanswerable query, citation checks, permission filtering, and fallback behavior when evidence is missing.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • RAG does not guarantee truth; it only improves answers when retrieval finds the right evidence and generation uses it faithfully.
  • More chunks are not always better because irrelevant context can distract the model.
  • A citation is not enough; the cited passage must actually support the generated claim.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about RAG overview?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: RAG does not guarantee truth; it only improves answers when retrieval finds the right evidence and generation uses it faithfully.
  • Once RAG overview is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing RAG overview into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
