  • Correct answer: A production-shaped slice should include a tiny corpus, expected-source evals, one unanswerable query, citation checks, permission filtering, and fallback behavior when evidence is missing.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain RAG overview in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

Document ingestion

Document ingestion turns source files into clean, traceable records that a RAG system can chunk, index, retrieve, and cite — and keeps them current as sources change.

When it goes wrong

The answer bot keeps citing a policy that was deleted three months ago. Nobody wired the pipeline to remove stale files — ingestion isn't a one-time import, it's a live feed that has to handle updates and deletes.

Why this matters

Ingestion quality is an upper bound on everything downstream: no chunking, embedding, or prompt engineering can recover information that was mangled at parse time or lost when a source changed without the index noticing.

In plain terms

It is like preparing evidence for a courtroom: collect it, label it, remove noise, preserve provenance, and keep a chain of custody. Evidence with no chain of custody gets thrown out; a chunk with no source metadata can't be cited or deleted.

How it works

The pipeline: connectors pull sources → parse and OCR → clean and normalize → extract metadata and ACLs → deduplicate → assign stable IDs and versions → hand to chunking, on a schedule that matches how fast sources change.

  • Parsing is where most silent quality loss happens: PDFs with two-column layouts, tables, headers repeated on every page, and scanned pages needing OCR. A parser that reads a table row-by-row across columns produces text no chunker can save.
  • Every record needs provenance metadata: source URI, document version or checksum, owner, timestamp, and access-control labels. Without a stable document ID you cannot update or delete its chunks later — you can only rebuild the whole index.
  • Ingestion is a sync process, not an import: it must handle new documents, changed documents (re-parse, re-chunk, re-embed only what changed), and deleted documents (remove their chunks, or the bot keeps citing them).
  • Deduplication matters more than it looks: wikis and drives are full of near-copies, and retrieval will happily fill all top-5 slots with five versions of the same stale page.
  • Permissions must be captured at ingestion time and enforced at query time. If ACLs aren't attached to chunks, your assistant becomes a search engine over documents users were never allowed to read.

A concrete example

A 60-page PDF policy manual becomes ~180 records: text blocks with page numbers, section headings, an ACL label ('employees-all' vs 'managers-only'), a version checksum, and a source ID. When HR uploads v2, the pipeline diffs checksums, re-processes only the 12 changed pages, and tombstones chunks from the deleted appendix.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Aggressive cleaning improves retrieval but can destroy layout cues (tables, headings, page numbers) that citations and answer quality depend on.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design an ingestion record for one real PDF: source URI, owner, ACL, version checksum, parser warnings, and per-page text with section headings. Then write down what happens to its chunks when the document is updated and when it is deleted.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production ingestion pipeline logs per-document parse warnings, tracks index freshness as a metric (age of oldest un-synced change), and can answer 'which chunks came from this document?' so updates and deletions are surgical.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Ingestion is not a one-time import; a corpus without update and delete handling starts rotting the day it ships.
  • PDF-to-text is not a solved problem — tables, columns, and scans fail quietly, and the failure only shows up later as 'retrieval misses'.
  • Skipping ACL capture at ingestion cannot be fixed at query time; by then nobody knows who was allowed to see what.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Document ingestion?

  • Correct answer: Ingestion is not a one-time import; a corpus without update and delete handling starts rotting the day it ships.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Document ingestion is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Document ingestion into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: A production ingestion pipeline logs per-document parse warnings, tracks index freshness as a metric (age of oldest un-synced change), and can answer 'which chunks came from this document?' so updates and deletions are surgical.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Document ingestion in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

Chunking

Chunking is the process of splitting documents into smaller segments before embedding and storing them, so relevant pieces can be retrieved individually rather than retrieving entire documents.

When it goes wrong

A user asks about parental leave and the bot says no policy exists. The policy is in the corpus — but split mid-table across two chunks, so neither chunk looks relevant to the query.

Why this matters

Chunking matters because poor chunking is often the root cause of RAG retrieval failures — the right information exists in the corpus but cannot be found because it is split badly.

In plain terms

Chunking a document is like cutting a long article into index cards. Each card covers one topic. When you need information, you look up relevant cards — not the whole stack of magazines.

How it works

Internal flow: raw documents → parse and clean → split into chunks → embed each chunk → store chunk + embedding + metadata in vector database.

  • Fixed-size chunking splits by token or character count (e.g. every 512 tokens). Simple and predictable but may split mid-sentence or mid-paragraph.
  • Semantic chunking splits at natural boundaries: paragraphs, sections, or topic changes. Better quality but harder to implement uniformly.
  • Chunk overlap (e.g. 50-token overlap between adjacent chunks) ensures context near boundaries is not lost when a chunk is retrieved in isolation.
  • Chunk size trade-off: small chunks are more precise but may lack context. Large chunks carry more context but match queries less precisely and cost more tokens per retrieval.
  • Metadata per chunk (document title, section, author, date) enables filtering during retrieval and improves citation quality.

Topic diagram

Diagram: Chunking pipeline for Chunking

A concrete example

A 40-page employee handbook chunked at 512 tokens with 50-token overlap produces ~200 chunks. A question about parental leave retrieves the 3–5 most relevant chunks about that topic, not all 200. The answer can cite the specific section retrieved.

Trade-offs

  • Fixed-size vs semantic chunks: Fixed chunks are simple and predictable; semantic chunks preserve meaning but are harder to automate.
  • Small chunks vs large chunks: Small chunks improve precision; large chunks preserve context but add noise and token cost.
  • Overlap vs storage cost: Overlap improves recall across boundaries but increases index size and duplicate evidence.

Try it in code

function chunkText(text, size = 500, overlap = 80) {
  const chunks = [];
  for (let start = 0; start < text.length; start += size - overlap) {
    chunks.push(text.slice(start, start + size));
  }
  return chunks;
}

console.log(chunkText(policyDocument, 500, 80).length);

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take a 5-page technical document. Chunk it three ways: (1) every 200 words, (2) at every paragraph break, (3) at every section heading. For each, write one question that would be answered well and one that would fail. Explain why.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production checklist: log which chunks are retrieved for each query; inspect retrieved chunks that led to wrong answers; tune chunk size and overlap based on evidence.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • There is no universally optimal chunk size. It depends on document structure, query type, and the embedding model.
  • Larger chunks are not always better. Large chunks embed poorly because a single vector must summarise too much content.
  • Chunking strategy is a hyperparameter that needs evaluation, not a one-time decision.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Chunking?

  • Correct answer: There is no universally optimal chunk size. It depends on document structure, query type, and the embedding model.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Chunking is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Chunking into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: Production checklist: log which chunks are retrieved for each query; inspect retrieved chunks that led to wrong answers; tune chunk size and overlap based on evidence.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Chunking in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Fixed-size vs semantic chunks" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
  • Pinecone chunking strategies

Embedding

Embedding in a RAG pipeline converts each chunk and each query into vectors so retrieval can match meaning rather than exact words.

When it goes wrong

Searching 'PTO' returns nothing, but 'paid time off' returns the right document. Your embedding model treats them as unrelated strings — model choice quietly decides which synonyms your search understands.

Why this matters

Embedding choice quietly decides which paraphrases, synonyms, and domain terms your search understands — it is the vocabulary of your retrieval system.

In plain terms

It is like translating documents and questions into coordinates on the same map of meaning — questions land near the passages that answer them, even when they share no words.

How it works

The flow: normalize text → batch chunks through the embedding model at index time → embed each query at request time → nearest-neighbor search compares the two in the same vector space.

  • The embedding model defines what 'similar' means. 'PTO' and 'paid time off' are close in a good model and unrelated in a bad one; domain jargon, product names, and error codes are where general-purpose models are weakest.
  • Query and document embeddings must come from the same model and version. Mixing models in one index silently breaks similarity — nothing errors, results are just quietly wrong.
  • Dimensionality is a cost dial: 1536-dim vectors cost roughly 4x the storage and search compute of 384-dim ones. Many products get identical answer quality from smaller, cheaper embeddings — measure before paying.
  • Embeddings are computed once per chunk but per query at runtime, so query embedding latency and cost sit on every request path.
  • Changing the embedding model means re-embedding the entire corpus. Version your index by model so you can run old and new side by side and cut over after evaluation.

A concrete example

A support corpus of 200k chunks embedded at 768 dimensions is ~600 MB of vectors. A query about 'annual leave carry-over' retrieves the right policy chunk even though the document says 'vacation rollover' — that is embeddings earning their keep. The same index misses 'error E-4012' queries entirely, which is why hybrid search exists.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Larger, newer embedding models improve nuance but raise storage, latency, and re-embedding cost — and every model change is a full-corpus migration.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take ten real user questions and embed them alongside your corpus using two different embedding models. For each question, compare the top-3 retrieved chunks. Find one query where the models disagree and explain which vocabulary gap caused it.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production embedding setup pins the model version in index metadata, evaluates candidate models on your own queries (not just public benchmarks), and has a tested re-embedding migration path.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not mix embeddings from different models or versions in the same index; similarity scores across them are meaningless.
  • The top embedding model on a public leaderboard is not automatically the best on your corpus — domain vocabulary dominates.
  • Embeddings do not understand negation or numbers well; 'refunds allowed' and 'refunds not allowed' can sit uncomfortably close together.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Embedding?

  • Once Embedding is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not mix embeddings from different models or versions in the same index; similarity scores across them are meaningless.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Embedding into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: A production embedding setup pins the model version in index metadata, evaluates candidate models on your own queries (not just public benchmarks), and has a tested re-embedding migration path.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Embedding in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

Indexing

Indexing organizes chunks, vectors, lexical terms, and metadata so retrieval can answer queries in milliseconds, stay current as content changes, and respect permissions.

When it goes wrong

The demo searched ten thousand chunks instantly. At ten million, every query takes four seconds and the vector database bill has tripled. Exact search doesn't scale; approximate indexes are the trade you now have to understand.

Why this matters

The index is where scale, freshness, latency, and permissions all collide — most 'RAG is slow' and 'RAG cites deleted docs' complaints are index problems, not model problems.

In plain terms

It is like a library catalog plus a semantic map: the catalog finds exact labels fast, the map finds similar meaning — and both are useless if nobody removes cards for books that left the building.

How it works

The lifecycle: build (vectors + terms + metadata) → serve queries with filters → apply updates and deletes → compact/rebuild → monitor freshness, recall, and latency.

  • Exact (brute-force) vector search is fine up to roughly a million vectors; beyond that you need approximate indexes (HNSW, IVF) that trade a little recall for large speedups. That trade is tunable and should be measured, not defaulted.
  • A useful index stores more than vectors: lexical terms for keyword search, metadata fields for filtering (tenant, ACL, date, product version), and the source ID that links every chunk back to its document.
  • Metadata filtering interacts badly with approximate search if applied after: filter-first vs filter-after can change both latency and recall. Know which your database does.
  • Updates are the hard part: deleted documents must tombstone their chunks, updated documents must replace them, and some index types degrade with heavy churn until rebuilt or compacted.
  • Namespaces or per-tenant indexes are the blunt but reliable isolation tool; a metadata filter bug is a data leak, a namespace boundary usually isn't.

A concrete example

A product-docs index holds 3M chunks: HNSW for vectors, BM25 terms, and metadata {version, plan_tier, visibility}. Free-tier queries filter to public docs before search. A nightly job compacts tombstones and reports freshness: 'oldest un-indexed change: 4h'. When p95 latency crept past 300ms, tuning efSearch down bought back 80ms for a measured 1% recall cost.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Fast approximate indexes trade recall for latency, and heavily filtered or heavily churned indexes quietly lose both until measured.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write an index plan for 1,000 documents: fields to store per chunk, three metadata filters you will support, how updates and deletes flow through, rebuild/compaction cadence, and the single freshness metric you would alert on.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production index has a freshness metric with an alert, a documented update/delete path, per-tenant isolation strategy, and recorded recall@k measured on your own queries at the current index settings.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • An index is not write-once; without a delete and update strategy it becomes a museum of stale content that retrieval keeps surfacing.
  • Approximate search recall is a setting, not a fact — the same database gives different recall at different tuning, so benchmark on your data.
  • Metadata filters are not free; aggressive filtering over an approximate index can quietly collapse the candidate pool and miss obvious results.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Indexing?

  • Correct answer: An index is not write-once; without a delete and update strategy it becomes a museum of stale content that retrieval keeps surfacing.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Indexing is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Indexing into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: A production index has a freshness metric with an alert, a documented update/delete path, per-tenant isolation strategy, and recorded recall@k measured on your own queries at the current index settings.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Indexing in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

Retrieval

Retrieval is the step in RAG that finds the most relevant chunks for a given query by comparing the query's embedding to stored chunk embeddings and returning the top K nearest neighbours.

When it goes wrong

The right document is in the index — at position 27 of the ranked results. Your prompt only includes the top 5. Retrieval quality, not the LLM, is why the answer is wrong.

Why this matters

Retrieval quality is the single most important factor in RAG answer quality. A perfect generator with bad retrieval produces wrong answers.

In plain terms

Retrieval is like a librarian who has read all the index cards and, the moment you ask a question, instantly hands you the most relevant ones without reading every card each time.

How it works

Internal flow: user query → embed query → vector similarity search → top K chunks → (re-rank) → pass to LLM with original query.

  • Dense retrieval: embed the query with the same model used to embed chunks. Find nearest neighbours in vector space using cosine similarity or dot product.
  • Sparse retrieval (BM25): match query terms directly against document terms with TF-IDF weighting. Better for exact keyword matches and rare terms.
  • Hybrid retrieval: combine dense and sparse scores. Usually outperforms either alone, especially for queries mixing semantic intent and specific keywords.
  • Top-K: retrieve the K most similar chunks. K is a hyperparameter — too small misses relevant content, too large floods the context with noise.
  • Query rewriting: paraphrase or expand the user's query before retrieval. Improves recall when user queries are ambiguous or short.

Topic diagram

Diagram: Retrieval funnel for Retrieval

A concrete example

User asks: 'What is our Q3 refund policy for enterprise customers?' Dense retrieval finds chunks about refunds semantically. BM25 finds chunks containing 'Q3' and 'enterprise'. Hybrid retrieval combines both signals and returns the specific policy section more reliably than either alone.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Dense retrieval is better for semantic similarity; BM25 is better for exact terms; hybrid is better overall but adds complexity and latency.

Try it in code

async function retrieve(query, embed, vectorDb) {
  const queryVector = await embed(query);
  const matches = await vectorDb.search(queryVector, { topK: 5 });
  return matches.filter((match) => match.score >= 0.78);
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take 10 questions for your use case and manually find the correct chunk in your document corpus. Then run your retrieval system. For each question, did the correct chunk appear in the top 3, top 5, or not at all? This is your recall@3 and recall@5.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Eval: measure retrieval recall@K — what fraction of questions have the correct chunk in the top K results. Fix retrieval before tuning the generator.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Vector similarity is not the same as relevance. High cosine similarity does not guarantee the chunk actually answers the query.
  • More retrieved chunks are not always better. Irrelevant chunks in the context confuse the generator and increase cost.
  • Retrieval and generation failures have different signatures — wrong answer vs no answer. Diagnose which is failing before tuning.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Retrieval?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Vector similarity is not the same as relevance. High cosine similarity does not guarantee the chunk actually answers the query.
  • Once Retrieval is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Retrieval into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: Eval: measure retrieval recall@K — what fraction of questions have the correct chunk in the top K results. Fix retrieval before tuning the generator.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Retrieval in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
  • BEIR benchmark

Hybrid search

Hybrid search runs lexical matching (BM25) and vector similarity together and fuses the results, so retrieval handles both exact terms and semantic intent.

When it goes wrong

A user searches for error code 'E-4012'. Semantic search returns documents about error handling in general — the exact code appears nowhere in the results. Keyword search would have nailed it. You need both.

Why this matters

Hybrid search matters because real users mix identifiers and intent in the same query stream, and either retrieval mode alone has a blind spot the other covers.

In plain terms

It is like asking both a librarian who knows exact titles and an expert who understands the topic — then merging their two reading lists into one.

How it works

The flow: run BM25 and vector search in parallel → normalize or rank-fuse the two candidate lists → apply metadata filters → hand the fused top-K to re-ranking.

  • Vector search is blind to exact strings it never learned: error codes, SKUs, legal clause numbers, acronyms, and people's names. BM25 nails those. BM25 is blind to paraphrase; vectors nail that. Real query streams contain both kinds.
  • The two scorers produce incomparable numbers (BM25 scores are unbounded, cosine similarity is [-1,1]), so fusion is its own step: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) merges by rank and is the robust default; weighted score blending needs normalization and tuning.
  • The lexical/semantic weight is corpus-dependent: technical docs with lots of identifiers lean lexical; conversational knowledge bases lean semantic. There is no universal split — evaluate on your own queries.
  • Hybrid retrieval usually feeds a re-ranker: cast a wide net with both methods (say top-50 fused), then let a cross-encoder pick the best 5.
  • Operationally you are now running two indexes that must stay in sync — a document updated in one but not the other produces confusing, hard-to-debug rankings.

A concrete example

Query: 'SOC2 retention'. BM25 finds the compliance doc containing the literal token 'SOC2'; vector search finds 'how long we keep customer data' passages that never say SOC2. RRF fusion puts both in the top five. Vector-only missed the compliance doc at rank 23; BM25-only missed the data-retention policy entirely.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Hybrid improves recall on identifier-heavy and paraphrase-heavy queries alike, but doubles the moving parts — two indexes, a fusion step, and more tuning surface.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Pick five queries containing acronyms, product names, or error codes and five phrased as natural questions. Run BM25-only, vector-only, and hybrid retrieval on all ten, record where the correct chunk ranks in each, and write one sentence on which query class each method lost.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production hybrid setup keeps an eval set split by query type (identifiers, paraphrases, mixed) and reports recall per type, so a tuning change that helps one class can't silently gut the other.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Adding vector search does not retire keyword search; exact identifiers, error codes, and legal terms still need lexical matching to rank reliably.
  • Fusing scores is not as simple as adding them — BM25 and cosine similarity live on different scales, which is why rank-based fusion like RRF is the default.
  • Hybrid is not automatically better; on a corpus of purely conversational text, a well-tuned vector index plus re-ranker can match it with half the operational load. Measure.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Hybrid search?

  • Correct answer: Adding vector search does not retire keyword search; exact identifiers, error codes, and legal terms still need lexical matching to rank reliably.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Hybrid search is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Hybrid search into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: A production hybrid setup keeps an eval set split by query type (identifiers, paraphrases, mixed) and reports recall per type, so a tuning change that helps one class can't silently gut the other.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Hybrid search in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

Re-ranking

Re-ranking is a second-stage retrieval step that takes the top K retrieved chunks and re-scores them with a more expensive, more accurate relevance model before passing the top N to the LLM.

When it goes wrong

Top-10 retrieval recall is 95%, but answers are still wrong: the best chunk sits at rank 8 and the model anchors on rank 1. A re-ranker exists to fix the order, not the pool.

Why this matters

Re-ranking matters because it improves the signal-to-noise ratio of the context passed to the LLM, which directly improves answer quality.

In plain terms

Retrieval is a fast, rough filter — like a keyword search that returns 20 results. Re-ranking is a careful expert review of those 20 results to order them by true relevance. You do the rough pass cheaply, then spend more effort on the shortlist.

How it works

Internal flow: retrieval top K → cross-encoder scores (query, chunk₁), (query, chunk₂), … (query, chunkK) → sort by score → take top N → pass to LLM.

  • Cross-encoders take the (query, document) pair as joint input and produce a relevance score. More accurate than embedding similarity but too slow for full-corpus search.
  • Bi-encoders (embedding models) encode query and document separately — fast enough for full-corpus search but less accurate for precise relevance judgement.
  • Re-ranking pipeline: fast bi-encoder retrieves top 50–100 candidates → cross-encoder re-ranks to top 3–5 → top 3–5 passed to LLM.
  • Re-ranking significantly improves precision (sending the right chunks to the LLM) without changing recall (the initial retrieval covers the same documents).
  • Models like Cohere Rerank, BGE-Reranker, and Jina Reranker are specialised for this step.

A concrete example

A legal document search retrieves 20 potentially relevant clauses. Without re-ranking, the top 3 by embedding similarity might be thematically related but not the most precise match. A cross-encoder re-ranker reads each clause paired with the query and correctly identifies that clause 7 and clause 14 are the most directly relevant.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Re-ranking adds 50–200ms latency and API/compute cost. The improvement in answer quality often justifies it for high-stakes use cases.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take 10 queries and their retrieved top 10 chunks. Manually re-rank each query by relevance. Compare your ranking to the original retrieval order. How often does the correct chunk move up? This is the potential improvement a re-ranker could provide.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Eval: measure precision@3 with and without re-ranking. If the correct chunk moves from position 4–6 to position 1–3, re-ranking is working.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Re-ranking does not improve recall — if the correct chunk was not in the initial top K, re-ranking cannot find it. Fix recall first.
  • Re-ranking is not always necessary. For simple, clear queries with good chunking, basic retrieval may already produce the correct top 3.
  • Adding a re-ranker does not replace evaluation. Measure whether it actually improves answer quality on your specific corpus and query distribution.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Re-ranking?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Re-ranking is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Re-ranking does not improve recall — if the correct chunk was not in the initial top K, re-ranking cannot find it. Fix recall first.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Re-ranking into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: Eval: measure precision@3 with and without re-ranking. If the correct chunk moves from position 4–6 to position 1–3, re-ranking is working.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Re-ranking in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

Grounding

Grounding constrains an AI answer to explicit evidence — retrieved documents, tool results, database rows, or policy rules — so every claim can be traced to a source.

When it goes wrong

The bot cites the right document and still invents a number that isn't in it. Retrieval succeeded; generation drifted. Grounding is the discipline of keeping every claim tied to evidence.

Why this matters

Grounding is the difference between 'the answer sounds right' and 'the answer is backed by something we can show a user, an auditor, or a court'.

In plain terms

It is like requiring every claim in a report to cite the page or data row that supports it: the author can still write badly, but now you can check.

How it works

The flow: select evidence → generate with constraints and per-claim citations → verify claims against cited passages → abstain or escalate when support is missing → log the evidence trail.

  • Retrieval success does not guarantee grounding: the model can hold the right passage and still 'improve' it — rounding numbers, merging conditions, adding plausible details that aren't there. Grounding failures with correct retrieval are the most dangerous kind because the citation looks legitimate.
  • Grounding has three levers: prompt constraints ('answer only from the provided sources'), citation requirements per claim, and post-generation verification that checks each claim against its cited passage.
  • Abstention is half of grounding: when evidence is missing or contradictory, the grounded behavior is 'I can't answer from the available sources', not a best guess. Products need an explicit abstention path or the model will fill silence with fiction.
  • Verification can be automated: an entailment check or LLM judge asks 'does the cited passage support this sentence?' — expensive on every request, cheap on a sampled slice, and invaluable in evals.
  • Conflicting sources need a policy: prefer newer versions, prefer higher-authority sources, or surface the conflict — silently picking one is how bots quote superseded policies.

A concrete example

A benefits assistant retrieves the parental-leave policy correctly, then answers '16 weeks fully paid'. The document says 16 weeks at 80% pay. Retrieval scored 100%; grounding failed. A per-claim entailment check would flag the sentence as unsupported by its own citation before a user files a complaint based on it.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Strict grounding reduces hallucination but increases refusals when retrieval is weak — teams tune the threshold based on whether a wrong answer or a non-answer costs more in their domain.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take one real generated answer from any RAG system. Split it into individual claims and mark each one supported, unsupported, or contradicted by the retrieved evidence. Count how many unsupported claims hid inside an answer that 'looked cited'.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production grounding setup measures faithfulness separately from retrieval recall (they fail independently), samples live answers for claim-level verification, and defines the abstention message users see when evidence is missing.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A citation is not proof: the cited passage must actually support the specific sentence, and models routinely cite correctly while paraphrasing incorrectly.
  • Grounding is not solved by better retrieval; generation can drift from perfect evidence, which is why faithfulness needs its own metric.
  • Refusing to answer is not a failure state — for a grounded system with missing evidence, it is the correct output.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Grounding?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A citation is not proof: the cited passage must actually support the specific sentence, and models routinely cite correctly while paraphrasing incorrectly.
  • Once Grounding is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Grounding into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: A production grounding setup measures faithfulness separately from retrieval recall (they fail independently), samples live answers for claim-level verification, and defines the abstention message users see when evidence is missing.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Grounding in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

Citation UX

Citation UX designs how users see, inspect, and trust the sources behind AI-generated claims — and what the product shows when evidence is weak or missing.

When it goes wrong

Users stopped trusting correct answers because a citation once pointed at the wrong paragraph. Cite too little and users can't verify; cite wrong once and they stop believing you.

Why this matters

Citation UX is where grounding becomes visible: the pipeline can be perfectly faithful, but if users can't verify claims quickly, the product earns no trust from it.

In plain terms

It is like footnotes in a research paper, but optimized for a reader with eight seconds: the goal is fast verification, not academic completeness.

How it works

The flow: map claims to sources during generation → render markers inline → show snippet on inspection → deep-link to the exact location → design explicit states for missing or conflicting evidence.

  • Citation precision drives trust more than citation volume: one link to the exact paragraph beats five links to 40-page PDFs. A citation the user can't verify in a few seconds is decoration.
  • Granularity is a design decision: per-answer citations are cheap but vague; per-claim citations are what actually lets a user check the sentence they care about.
  • Show the quoted snippet, not just the link: displaying the supporting passage inline (with a link to the full source at the right page/anchor) is the single highest-trust pattern.
  • The empty state is part of the design: when the system abstains or evidence is partial, the UX must say so plainly — 'no policy found covering this' builds more trust than a hedged paragraph.
  • One wrong citation costs more trust than ten right ones earn; users who catch a citation pointing at the wrong paragraph stop clicking citations at all — which quietly removes your verification layer.

A concrete example

A policy answer renders three sentences, each with a superscript marker. Hovering shows the quoted snippet ('…16 weeks at 80% of base salary…', Parental Leave Policy v4, p.2) and clicking opens the PDF at that page. When a user asks about sabbaticals — not covered by any document — the assistant says exactly that and offers the HR contact instead of an unsourced guess.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More citation detail improves verifiability but clutters the answer — the escape hatch is progressive disclosure: clean answer by default, evidence on demand.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Redesign one RAG answer so each factual sentence links to a specific supporting snippet with a page or anchor. Then design the same answer's empty state: what exactly does the user see when no source covers the question?

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production citation UX tracks whether citations get clicked, samples whether cited passages actually support their sentences, and treats a broken or wrong citation link as a bug with user-visible impact, not a cosmetic issue.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Citing a whole document is not really citing; if the user can't find the supporting passage in seconds, the citation fails its purpose.
  • More citations are not more trustworthy — three precise ones beat eight vague ones, and padding answers with weak sources reads as hand-waving.
  • Citations are not only for users: the same claim-to-source mapping powers your faithfulness evals and audits, so building it properly pays twice.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Citation UX?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Citing a whole document is not really citing; if the user can't find the supporting passage in seconds, the citation fails its purpose.
  • Once Citation UX is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Citation UX into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: A production citation UX tracks whether citations get clicked, samples whether cited passages actually support their sentences, and treats a broken or wrong citation link as a bug with user-visible impact, not a cosmetic issue.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Citation UX in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

RAG evaluation

RAG evaluation measures the pipeline in two separately-failing halves: did retrieval find the right evidence, and did generation use that evidence faithfully?

When it goes wrong

After 'improving' chunking, support tickets doubled. Nothing in the deploy pipeline measured retrieval or answer quality, so the regression shipped silently. Evals are how RAG changes stop being guesses.

Why this matters

Without evals, every RAG change is a guess, and the feedback loop is user complaints arriving weeks late and aimed at the wrong stage.

In plain terms

It is like grading both the research process and the final essay — a great essay built on the wrong sources fails, and perfect sources summarized dishonestly fail too.

How it works

The flow: build a golden set with expected sources → measure retrieval (recall@k) and generation (faithfulness, citation precision) separately → include abstention tests → run on every change → sample production traffic to keep the set honest.

  • Retrieval metrics come first: recall@k ('was the right chunk in the top k?') and precision against a golden set of questions with known source IDs. If recall@5 is 60%, no prompt work will fix your answer quality ceiling.
  • Generation metrics sit on top: faithfulness (claims supported by retrieved evidence), citation precision (citations point at genuinely supporting passages), and answer quality. LLM-as-judge scales these; humans calibrate the judge.
  • The golden set must include unanswerable questions — the correct behavior is abstention, and a system that never says 'I don't know' will fail exactly when it matters.
  • End-to-end scores hide root causes: a wrong answer might be a chunking bug, a retrieval miss, a re-ranking mistake, or generation drift. Per-stage metrics tell you which fix to make.
  • Evals are regression tests: every chunking change, embedding swap, prompt edit, or model upgrade runs the same set, or improvements in one place silently break another.

A concrete example

A 60-question golden set: each question lists expected source chunk IDs, a reference answer, and 8 are unanswerable. A chunking 'improvement' ships only after the eval shows recall@5 went from 78% to 84% with faithfulness flat. Two weeks later a model upgrade drops faithfulness from 96% to 88% on the same set — caught in CI, not in support tickets.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Optimizing the end-to-end answer score alone hides which stage failed — separated metrics cost more setup but make failures actionable.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Build a ten-question eval set for any corpus you know: each question gets expected source IDs and a reference answer, and two questions must be unanswerable. Run it once and separate the failures into retrieval misses vs generation drift.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production eval setup runs in CI against every pipeline change, tracks per-stage metrics over time, and refreshes the golden set from real production queries so it measures the traffic you actually get.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Answer ratings alone cannot tell you whether retrieval or generation failed; inspect source recall and citation support separately.
  • A static golden set goes stale — production queries drift, and an eval set that no longer resembles real traffic gives confident, irrelevant scores.
  • High faithfulness with low recall is still a bad system: it faithfully reports from the wrong or incomplete evidence.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about RAG evaluation?

  • Correct answer: Answer ratings alone cannot tell you whether retrieval or generation failed; inspect source recall and citation support separately.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once RAG evaluation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing RAG evaluation into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: A production eval setup runs in CI against every pipeline change, tracks per-stage metrics over time, and refreshes the golden set from real production queries so it measures the traffic you actually get.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain RAG evaluation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

Production architecture

Production architecture for RAG connects ingestion, chunking, embedding, indexing, retrieval, re-ranking, generation, and citation rendering into one deployable system with a latency budget, cost model, and failure behavior defined at every stage.

When it goes wrong

The prototype answered in two seconds. In production it takes nine: ACL checks, re-ranking, grounding validation, and a cold vector index all landed on the request path. Architecture is where those costs get planned instead of discovered.

Why this matters

The gap between a RAG demo and a RAG product is architecture: freshness pipelines, latency budgets, permission enforcement, fallbacks, and traces — none of which show up in a notebook.

In plain terms

It is like the full assembly line for a product, not the design of one station: the demo proves a station works once; production means the whole line runs all day, every day, with alarms.

How it works

The flow: offline content pipeline keeps the index current; online request pipeline retrieves, re-ranks, generates, and cites within budget; monitoring watches freshness, recall, faithfulness, latency, and cost across both.

  • Draw the two paths separately: the offline path (ingest → chunk → embed → index) runs on content-change schedules; the online path (query → retrieve → re-rank → generate → cite) runs per-request under a latency budget. Different SLOs, different failure modes, different owners.
  • Budget the online path explicitly: e.g. 900ms p95 total = 50ms query embedding + 150ms hybrid retrieval + 200ms re-ranking + 450ms generation + 50ms validation. Unbudgeted stages are where the mystery seconds hide.
  • ACL enforcement is an online-path stage, not an afterthought: permission filters run at retrieval, and every skipped filter is a data leak with a friendly chat interface.
  • Every stage needs a failure behavior: retrieval timeout → say sources are unavailable (never silently fall back to ungrounded generation); empty results → abstain; re-ranker down → serve fused retrieval order with a logged degradation flag.
  • The trace is the debugging tool: one request ID should link the query, retrieved chunk IDs and scores, re-rank order, prompt, output, citations, latencies, and token costs. Without it, 'the bot gave a weird answer yesterday' is unanswerable.

A concrete example

A support assistant in production: nightly ingestion re-embeds only changed docs (about 2% daily); the online path holds a 900ms p95 budget; retrieval filters by customer tier; if the vector store times out, users see 'source lookup is unavailable right now' instead of a confident guess from model memory; and every answer stores a full trace, which is how the team found that one 'hallucination' report was actually a stale-index problem.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Each production concern (ACLs, re-ranking, validation, tracing) adds latency and engineering cost to a pipeline that worked 'fine' in the demo — the architecture is where you spend that budget deliberately instead of discovering it in incident reviews.

Try it in code

const pipeline = {
  ingestion: "nightly, re-embed only changed docs",
  retrieval: "hybrid search + re-rank, 200ms budget",
  generation: "constrained to retrieved evidence",
  fallback: "no eligible sources -> escalate to human"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Sketch the architecture for one real RAG use case: both paths, a latency budget per online stage, how a changed document reaches the index, the ACL enforcement point, and the exact user-facing behavior for retrieval timeout, empty results, and re-ranker failure.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-shaped slice defines the stage-by-stage latency budget, a fallback per stage, index freshness monitoring with an alert, and a per-request trace linking evidence to answer — before scaling traffic.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Failure recovery

Failure recovery defines what a RAG system does when retrieval, model calls, validation, or dependencies fail — detecting the problem, degrading safely, and telling the user the truth.

When it goes wrong

The vector database went down, and the bot answered every question from the model's stale memory — confidently, with no disclosure. What it should have done instead is a design decision, not an accident.

Why this matters

RAG has more moving dependencies than most features — vector store, embedding service, model API, document pipeline — so partial failure is the normal operating condition, not the exception.

In plain terms

It is like an emergency procedure on an aircraft: the point is not to prevent every failure but to make sure a failure leads to a checklist, not a surprise.

How it works

The flow: detect (timeouts, error rates, empty-result rates) → contain (circuit breakers, retry budgets) → degrade along pre-decided paths → communicate honestly to the user → trace and alert so recovery is measured, not hoped for.

  • Enumerate the failure modes first — vector store down or slow, empty retrieval, model API errors and rate limits, malformed model output, grounding-check failures — because each needs a different response, and 'retry everything' handles none of them well.
  • The cardinal rule: never silently fall back from grounded answers to ungrounded model memory. An outage that makes the bot honest-but-limited is an incident; one that makes it confidently wrong is a trust event.
  • Retries need budgets and backoff: retrying a timing-out vector store amplifies the outage, and retrying a generation call doubles cost. Circuit breakers stop a failing dependency from being hammered by every request.
  • Degraded modes are product decisions made in advance: serve cached answers for popular questions (marked as possibly stale), skip the re-ranker with a logged flag, or abstain with escalation to a human channel.
  • Every degradation must be visible in traces and metrics; a fallback that fires silently for three weeks is a slow-motion incident nobody is watching.

A concrete example

The vector store starts timing out at 2am. The circuit breaker opens after 5 failures; the assistant switches to 'I can't search the knowledge base right now — here's the support contact' for uncached questions and serves cached answers (labeled as such) for the top 50. The on-call gets paged by the fallback-rate alert, not by morning complaints. Total confidently-wrong answers served: zero.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Retries and fallbacks improve availability but can amplify latency and cost during outages, and the safest fallback (abstention) has the worst user experience — these trades get made calmly in design or badly at 3am.

Try it in code

async function evaluateRetrieval(query, expectedSourceId) {
  const results = await retrieve(query, { topK: 5 });
  return {
    foundExpectedSource: results.some((r) => r.sourceId === expectedSourceId),
    topScore: results[0]?.score ?? 0,
    returnedSources: results.map((r) => r.sourceId)
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For a RAG assistant you can imagine concretely, write the exact user-facing behavior for four failures: retrieval timeout, empty results, model API rate-limit, and a grounding check that fails on the generated answer. Then name the metric that tells you each fallback is firing.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production failure-recovery setup has a written fallback per failure mode, circuit breakers on external dependencies, an alert on fallback-rate, and a chaos test that proves the degraded modes actually work.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not silently fall back from grounded answers to ungrounded model guesses; users cannot tell the difference, which is exactly the problem.
  • Retries are not resilience — without backoff, budgets, and circuit breakers they turn a dependency blip into a self-inflicted outage.
  • A fallback that has never been exercised in a test does not exist; outage day is the wrong time to discover the cached-answer path 404s.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Failure recovery?

  • Once Failure recovery is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not silently fall back from grounded answers to ungrounded model guesses; users cannot tell the difference, which is exactly the problem.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Failure recovery into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: A production failure-recovery setup has a written fallback per failure mode, circuit breakers on external dependencies, an alert on fallback-rate, and a chaos test that proves the degraded modes actually work.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Failure recovery in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

RAG lab

The RAG lab is a hands-on exercise to build a small end-to-end RAG pipeline over a handful of real documents, and evaluate it on both retrieval recall and answer faithfulness to the retrieved evidence.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to chunking and embedding a small real document set, building retrieval and generation into one working pipeline, then evaluating it on a labeled query set for both whether the correct chunk was retrieved and whether the generated answer is actually faithful to that chunk.

It is like building and taste-testing a complete small-batch recipe before scaling up the kitchen.

The brief

Build a small RAG pipeline over 10 real documents, run 5 test queries, and separately score retrieval accuracy and answer faithfulness for each.

A worked reference

A lab might build a RAG pipeline over 10 real FAQ documents, run 5 test queries, and separately score whether the correct document was retrieved and whether the generated answer accurately reflects it, since a system can retrieve correctly but still generate an unfaithful answer.

Starting point in code

for (const { query, correctChunkId } of testQueries) {
  const retrieved = await retrieve(query);
  const answer = await generate(query, retrieved);
  console.log({
    retrievalCorrect: retrieved.some(r => r.id === correctChunkId),
    faithful: await checkFaithful(answer, retrieved)
  });
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Mini project

The mini project ships a small, complete RAG feature for one real document set, with a documented pipeline, citations, and a basic evaluation set, that another engineer could extend to a different document set.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a documented ingestion and chunking approach, retrieval and generation wired together, visible citations, and a small labeled evaluation set into one reusable, documented RAG feature.

It is like building one small but fully functioning reference desk for one topic area, documented well enough to extend to another topic.

The brief

Build a RAG feature over one real small document set, including citations and a 10-query evaluation set measuring both retrieval accuracy and faithfulness.

A worked reference

A mini project might build a RAG feature over a team's internal wiki, with a documented chunking strategy, citations on every answer, and a 10-query evaluation set showing retrieval and faithfulness scores.

Starting point in code

export const wikiRagFeature = {
  chunking: "by section",
  citations: true,
  evalSet: require("./eval-10.json") // retrieval + faithfulness scored
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete RAG architecture for a real product: ingestion and chunking strategy, retrieval and re-ranking design, generation and citation approach, evaluation plan, and production monitoring, as one coherent system.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a documented ingestion pipeline, a chunking and embedding strategy, retrieval and re-ranking tuned for the product's latency budget, a citation and grounding-check design, an evaluation plan covering retrieval and faithfulness, and production monitoring into one complete architecture.

It is like designing a complete research and fact-checking department, not just one researcher's individual process.

The brief

Write a one-page RAG architecture for a real product: ingestion and chunking strategy, retrieval and re-ranking approach, citation design, and an evaluation plan.

A worked reference

A capstone for a legal research RAG product might specify section-based chunking, hybrid keyword and semantic retrieval with re-ranking, mandatory citations with a grounding check, an evaluation set built from real legal queries, and a weekly faithfulness audit.

Starting point in code

const ragArchitecture = {
  chunking: "section-based",
  retrieval: "hybrid keyword + semantic, re-ranked",
  citations: "mandatory, with grounding check",
  evalPlan: "retrieval recall + faithfulness, weekly",
  monitoring: "citation-accuracy audit"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Interview pack

The RAG interview pack prepares you to discuss retrieval-augmented generation system design credibly: chunking and retrieval trade-offs, citation and grounding design, and evaluation methodology, backed by a concrete story rather than a general description of what RAG is.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a research department's actual sourcing and fact-checking standards, not just that it cites sources.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real RAG system you built, evaluated, or debugged, paired with technical answers distinguishing chunking strategies, retrieval and re-ranking design, and faithfulness evaluation.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how would you build a RAG system for this product names a specific chunking and retrieval approach, describes a citation and grounding-check design, and proactively raises how you'd evaluate faithfulness without being asked.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "RAG answers cited sources but sometimes misrepresented them",
  cause: "no faithfulness check between claim and cited passage",
  fix: "added a grounding check before showing the answer",
  result: "faithfulness score in eval went from 85% to 97%"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a RAG quality problem you encountered or would anticipate, such as bad chunking or an unfaithful generation, its root cause, and the specific fix.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that RAG retrieves documents before generating an answer is not the same as understanding how to design chunking, ranking, and faithfulness evaluation for a specific real product; interviewers probe for that applied judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • Correct answer: Knowing that RAG retrieves documents before generating an answer is not the same as understanding how to design chunking, ranking, and faithfulness evaluation for a specific real product; interviewers probe for that applied judgment specifically.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks

MCP

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: MCP is like a USB-C port for AI applications: one standard way for a model client to discover tools, resources, and prompts from many servers. A protocol boundary keeps tool access governable. Teams can add capabilities without hard-wiring every integration into every assistant.

The running example

Throughout this track, imagine you own 'tickets-mcp': your company's internal ticket-system server, used by the IDE assistant, the support bot, and the agent platform. Every chapter is a decision you'll make taking it from a laptop demo to a governed, versioned, observable piece of company infrastructure.

Protocol overview

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard for connecting AI applications to tools, data, and prompts through servers — one protocol contract instead of a custom integration per app-tool pair.

When it goes wrong

Your team built the ticket-system integration three times: once for the IDE assistant, once for the support bot, once for the agent platform. All three drifted apart; two have auth bugs. MCP exists so the fourth time is the last time.

Why this matters

MCP matters because integrations are where AI products spend most of their engineering: a standard protocol turns each integration from a per-app project into a reusable asset.

In plain terms

It is like USB-C for AI integrations: before it, every device needed its own cable for every laptop; after it, anything that speaks the standard plugs into anything else — and the interesting engineering moves from cables to what you plug in.

How it works

The flow: host starts a client per server → initialize handshake negotiates versions and capabilities → client discovers tools/resources/prompts → model or user invokes them → server executes and returns structured results → host feeds results back into context.

  • The problem it solves is M×N integration sprawl: M AI applications times N tools previously meant M×N custom connectors; with MCP it is M clients + N servers, each written once against the same contract.
  • Three roles: the host (the AI application — an IDE, a chat app, an agent runtime), the client (the protocol connection the host manages, one per server), and the server (the program exposing capabilities). One host can hold many clients; one server can serve many hosts.
