  • Separate the planes: the control plane (budgets, permissions, approval policies, kill switches) is configuration owned by humans; the data plane (loops, tool calls, state) is execution. Emergencies are handled by changing config, not deploying code.
  • State and traces are infrastructure, not logs: run state enables resume; traces enable debugging, audits, and evals. Both need schemas, retention, and access control from day one.
  • The kill switch is a requirement: per-run cancel, per-tool disable, and global pause — tested, not theoretical. The day you need it is the wrong day to build it.

A concrete example

An ops-agent platform: runs enqueue with a tenant budget check; workers execute loops, checkpointing to Postgres; every tool call passes a gateway that scopes credentials per task and logs to the trace store; refunds and deletes pend in an approval service; a dashboard shows live runs with cost meters; and one config flag pauses all runs — used once during a provider incident, worth the build that afternoon.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Platform components add real engineering cost before the first agent ships, but every skipped component reappears as an incident class — no state means lost work, no gateway means no audit, no kill switch means a very long night.

Try it in code

const architecture = {
  intake: "task queue",
  loop: "plan -> act -> observe -> reflect",
  tools: "scoped, least-privilege",
  budgets: "step + cost caps per task",
  monitoring: "completion rate, cost/task, checkpoint rejection rate"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Draw the production path for one agent from 'user submits task' to 'result delivered': where the queue, state store, tool gateway, approval service, and trace store sit, plus the three things the control plane can change without a deploy — including the kill switch.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-shaped slice runs one real agent through the full path — queued, checkpointed, gateway-mediated, approval-gated, traced, budget-capped — and proves the kill switch works with a test.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Agent lab

The agent lab is a hands-on exercise to build a small agent that completes a multi-step task using at least two tools, with a step budget and a failure-handling path, then test it against both a normal case and a deliberately injected tool failure.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to building an agent with a defined task, at least two distinct tool integrations, an explicit step or cost budget, and a failure-handling path, then running it on a normal case and a case with a deliberately injected tool failure to confirm both work as intended.

It is like a supervised trial run for a new employee, watching both a normal day and a day where something goes wrong.

The brief

Build an agent using two tools with a step budget, run it on a normal task, then inject a tool failure and confirm the agent detects and handles it rather than claiming false success.

A worked reference

A lab might build an agent that reads a file, transforms its contents, and writes the result, with a 10-step budget, then inject a failure in the write step to confirm the agent detects it and reports the failure instead of claiming success.

Starting point in code

const result = await agent.run(task, { injectFailureAt: "writeStep" });
assert(result.status !== "false_success");
assert(result.status === "detected_and_reported");

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Mini project

The mini project ships a small, complete agent for one real task, with scoped tool access, a cost budget, failure handling, and basic logging, that another engineer could run and extend.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a defined task scope, least-privilege tool access, an explicit cost or step budget, a failure-handling path, and logging into one documented, working agent.

It is like building and documenting one fully functioning automated assistant for a specific job, in a way that could be adapted for a related job.

The brief

Build a small agent for one real task with scoped tool access, a cost budget, and a tested failure-handling path, and hand it to a teammate to run.

A worked reference

A mini project might build an agent that triages incoming support tickets by category and urgency, with read-only access to the ticket system, a 5-step budget per ticket, a fallback to manual triage on failure, and a log of every decision.

Starting point in code

export const ticketTriageAgent = {
  tools: ["readTicket"], // read-only
  budget: { maxSteps: 5 },
  onFailure: "escalate_to_manual_triage",
  log: (decision) => auditLog.write(decision)
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete production agent system for a real use case: task decomposition, tool scoping and security, cost budgets, failure handling, and a full production architecture with monitoring, as one coherent system.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a documented task decomposition, least-privilege tool scoping, explicit cost and step budgets, a tested failure-handling and escalation path, and a production architecture with queuing, logging, and monitoring into one complete, documented agent system.

It is like designing the complete operating plan for a new automated department, not just training one employee to do one task well.

The brief

Write a one-page production agent system design for a real use case: task decomposition, tool scoping, a cost budget, and a failure-handling path.

A worked reference

A capstone for an automated code-review agent might specify task decomposition into diff analysis and comment generation, read-only scoped repository access, a per-review cost budget, an escalation path for low-confidence reviews, and a dashboard tracking completion rate and cost.

Starting point in code

const agentSystem = {
  decomposition: ["diff analysis", "comment generation"],
  toolScope: "read-only repo access",
  costBudget: "$0.10 per review",
  escalation: "low-confidence review -> human",
  monitoring: "completion rate, cost/review dashboard"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Interview pack

The AI agents interview pack prepares you to discuss agent system design credibly: tool scoping for security, cost budgeting, and failure handling, backed by a concrete story about a real agent rather than a general description of what agents can do.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a real automated system's actual safety and cost controls, not just what tasks it can perform.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real agent you built, scoped, or debugged, paired with technical answers distinguishing tool security scoping, cost budgeting, and failure-recovery design.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how would you make sure this agent doesn't run away with cost or cause damage describes a specific step budget, least-privilege tool scoping, and a tested failure-handling path, not just enthusiasm for what the agent can accomplish.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "Agent looped 40 times on a failing approach before anyone noticed",
  cause: "no step or cost budget was configured",
  fix: "added a 15-step cap with escalate-on-exceed",
  result: "stuck runs now surface in minutes, not after a large bill"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about an agent that failed, ran costly, or took a risky action you didn't anticipate, its root cause, and the specific safeguard you added afterward.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Building an agent that completes tasks successfully in a demo is not the same as understanding how to make it safe and cost-bounded in production; interviewers probe specifically for that applied engineering judgment.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Building an agent that completes tasks successfully in a demo is not the same as understanding how to make it safe and cost-bounded in production; interviewers probe specifically for that applied engineering judgment.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

Agentic AI

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: Agentic systems are like an operations room: different specialists coordinate, hand off tasks, escalate blockers, and keep a shared view of state. Coordination becomes the product risk. Reliability depends on state, ownership, retries, and human checkpoints.

Agentic AI and orchestration

Agentic AI orchestration coordinates multiple model calls, tools, agents, memory, approvals, and state transitions so an AI system can complete multi-step work safely.

Why this matters

Agentic AI orchestration matters because useful AI products often require coordinated actions, not just one model response.

In plain terms

It is like an air-traffic controller for AI work: each agent or tool may be capable, but orchestration decides order, handoffs, safety checks, and when to stop.

How it works

The internal flow is goal intake, plan selection, agent/tool routing, evidence handoff, validation, human approval when needed, and final response.

  • Orchestration owns the workflow state: goal, plan, active step, tool outputs, approvals, errors, and stop conditions.
  • An orchestrator can route work to specialized agents such as researcher, planner, executor, critic, or writer.
  • Good orchestration defines contracts between agents: input schema, output schema, evidence requirements, timeout, and owner.
  • The orchestrator must enforce budgets for cost, time, retries, and tool permissions.
  • Production orchestration needs observability: traces for model calls, tool calls, handoffs, decisions, and human approvals.

Topic diagram

Diagram: Agentic orchestration loop for Agentic AI and orchestration

A concrete example

A refund-resolution system can orchestrate a policy reader, order checker, fraud-risk agent, and response writer. The orchestrator stops if policy evidence is missing or if a refund exceeds the approval threshold.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Autonomy vs control: more orchestration can solve richer tasks, but every handoff adds failure modes, latency, and audit requirements.

Try it in code

const orchestrator = {
  goal: "resolve customer refund request",
  steps: [
    { agent: "policy_reader", input: "refund policy", output: "eligible rules" },
    { agent: "order_checker", input: "order id", output: "purchase and shipment facts" },
    { agent: "decision_agent", input: "rules + facts", output: "approve, deny, or escalate" }
  ],
  stopWhen: "decision is validated or human approval is required"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design an orchestrated two-agent workflow for incident triage. Include agent roles, message schema, tool permissions, stop conditions, and one approval gate.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-shaped slice should include two agents, a typed handoff, one tool call, one approval gate, trace logging, and a max-step stop condition.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Agentic AI is not just a longer prompt; it needs state, tools, observations, and stop rules.
  • More agents do not automatically improve quality; they can create coordination overhead and inconsistent decisions.
  • An orchestrator should not trust agent outputs without contracts, evidence, and validation.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Agentic AI and orchestration?

  • Correct answer: Agentic AI is not just a longer prompt; it needs state, tools, observations, and stop rules.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Agentic AI and orchestration is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Agentic AI and orchestration into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: A production-shaped slice should include two agents, a typed handoff, one tool call, one approval gate, trace logging, and a max-step stop condition.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Agentic AI and orchestration in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

Agentic workflows

Agentic workflows are structured, multi-step processes an AI system follows to complete a task: planning, taking actions, observing results, and adjusting, repeated until the goal is met or a stopping condition is hit.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between answering a question from memory and actually going through the steps of solving a problem, checking your work along the way.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a defined sequence or graph of steps such as plan, act, observe, and reflect that an agent or set of agents follows to complete a task, as opposed to a single prompt-response exchange.

A concrete example

A deployment agent workflow might read the change request, check CI status, run a smoke test, and only then trigger deployment, pausing for human approval if any step fails.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More workflow structure with explicit steps and checkpoints makes behavior predictable and debuggable, but rigid workflows handle novel situations worse than a more flexible, general-purpose agent loop

Try it in code

const workflow = [
  { step: "plan", output: "ordered subtasks" },
  { step: "act", output: "tool call result" },
  { step: "observe", output: "did it match expectation?" },
  { step: "reflect", output: "retry, continue, or escalate" }
];
// Stop condition and escalation path must be explicit, not implied.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a four-step agentic workflow for one real task, naming the stopping condition and what triggers a retry versus an escalation.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • An agentic workflow is not the same as a single long prompt; the value comes from the loop of acting and observing real results, not from asking a model to imagine the whole process in one shot.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Agentic workflows?

  • Correct answer: An agentic workflow is not the same as a single long prompt; the value comes from the loop of acting and observing real results, not from asking a model to imagine the whole process in one shot.
  • Once Agentic workflows is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Agentic workflows in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

Single vs multi-agent

Single vs multi-agent design decides whether one agent handles an entire task with full context, or whether the task is split across multiple specialized agents that collaborate, each with narrower scope and context.

In plain terms

It is like deciding whether one generalist handles a whole project or whether you assemble a small team of specialists who hand work to each other.

How it works

In practice it comes down to choosing between one agent handling a task end to end with a large context and broad tool access, versus splitting the task across specialized agents that each handle a narrower slice and pass results to each other or a coordinator.

A concrete example

A single agent can handle a small bug fix end to end; a multi-agent setup might use a research agent, a coding agent, and a review agent for a larger feature, so no one agent's context gets overloaded.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A single agent is simpler to reason about and debug, while multi-agent setups can parallelize and specialize but add coordination overhead and new failure modes like miscommunication or inconsistent shared state

Try it in code

// Single agent: simplest, use when context fits and tasks don't parallelize.
await agent.run(task);

// Multi-agent: use when subtasks are independent and context-heavy.
const [research, code, review] = await Promise.all([
  researchAgent.run(subtaskA),
  codeAgent.run(subtaskB),
  reviewAgent.run(subtaskC)
]);

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take one task you'd normally give to a single agent, design how you would split it across two specialized agents, then judge whether the split actually reduces complexity or adds it.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • More agents is not automatically better performance; splitting a task that didn't need splitting adds coordination cost and new failure surfaces without a corresponding benefit.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Single vs multi-agent?

  • Correct answer: More agents is not automatically better performance; splitting a task that didn't need splitting adds coordination cost and new failure surfaces without a corresponding benefit.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Single vs multi-agent is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Single vs multi-agent in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Model Context Protocol documentation

Agent-to-agent communication

Agent-to-agent communication is the structured exchange of tasks, evidence, decisions, and constraints between specialized agents in a multi-agent workflow.

Why this matters

Agent-to-agent communication matters because multi-agent systems fail at handoff boundaries more often than inside individual model calls.

In plain terms

It is like a hospital handoff between specialists: the next doctor needs the patient facts, evidence, open questions, urgency, and what has already been ruled out.

How it works

The internal flow is task assignment, typed message, evidence transfer, receiver validation, response or escalation, and trace logging.

  • Messages need explicit sender, receiver, task id, intent, payload, evidence, timestamp, and required response shape.
  • Agents should exchange claims with evidence, not vague summaries that cannot be verified.
  • The receiving agent must know whether it can act, must ask for clarification, or must escalate.
  • Communication protocols need budgets, retries, timeouts, deduplication, and versioned schemas.
  • Unsafe handoffs happen when one agent passes untrusted instructions to another as if they were policy.

Topic diagram

Diagram: Agent handoff contract for Agent-to-agent communication

A concrete example

A research agent sends a writer agent three source-backed claims plus open questions. The writer is allowed to draft, but not invent missing evidence. If evidence is missing, it must return a clarification request.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Richer context vs tighter contracts: richer messages improve collaboration but can carry noise, stale data, or injected instructions.

Try it in code

const message = {
  from: "research_agent",
  to: "writer_agent",
  taskId: "brief-42",
  intent: "handoff_findings",
  payload: {
    claims: ["Latency rose after vector index rebuild."],
    evidence: [{ sourceId: "trace-17", quote: "p95 latency 8.2s" }],
    openQuestions: ["Was cache warming disabled?"]
  },
  contract: { requiresEvidence: true, maxAgeMinutes: 10 }
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a JSON message from a research agent to a writer agent. Include two claims, two evidence records, one open question, and a rule that the writer must not invent unsupported facts.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-shaped slice should implement a typed message envelope, evidence-required handoff, receiver validation, timeout, retry, and trace id.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Agent-to-agent communication is not the same as concatenating chat transcripts.
  • Agents should not treat another agent's conclusion as ground truth without evidence.
  • A natural-language handoff without schema, ownership, and stop rules is hard to debug.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Agent-to-agent communication?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Agent-to-agent communication is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Agent-to-agent communication is not the same as concatenating chat transcripts.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Agent-to-agent communication into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: A production-shaped slice should implement a typed message envelope, evidence-required handoff, receiver validation, timeout, retry, and trace id.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Agent-to-agent communication in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Model Context Protocol documentation

Task decomposition

Task decomposition breaks a large or ambiguous goal into smaller subtasks, each with a clear input, output, and success check, so an agent's progress can be verified incrementally rather than trusted only at the final result.

In plain terms

It is like breaking a renovation project into permits, demolition, framing, and inspection instead of just saying the house should be renovated.

How it works

In practice it comes down to breaking a large, ambiguous goal into smaller, independently verifiable subtasks with clear inputs, outputs, and success criteria, so progress and failure can be checked at each step rather than only at the end.

A concrete example

Migrating a billing system decomposes into auditing the current schema, designing the new schema, writing a migration script, dry-running it against a copy, then applying it, each with a distinct pass or fail check.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Fine-grained decomposition makes failures easier to isolate and retry, but excessive decomposition adds coordination overhead and can lose sight of the overall goal if subtasks are checked in isolation

Try it in code

const subtasks = [
  { name: "audit schema", success: r => r.tablesListed > 0 },
  { name: "design new schema", success: r => r.reviewedByOwner },
  { name: "write migration", success: r => r.dryRunPassed },
  { name: "apply migration", success: r => r.appliedWithoutError }
];
// Each subtask has its own explicit, checkable success condition.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take one large, vague task and decompose it into three subtasks, each with an explicit, checkable success condition.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A subtask is not properly decomposed just because it's smaller; if it lacks its own clear success criteria, it is still an unverifiable black box, just a shorter one.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Task decomposition?

  • Once Task decomposition is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A subtask is not properly decomposed just because it's smaller; if it lacks its own clear success criteria, it is still an unverifiable black box, just a shorter one.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Task decomposition in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

Coordination

Coordination is how multiple agents stay consistent with each other: sharing state, avoiding duplicated or conflicting work, and handing off results cleanly through a coordinator, shared queue, or direct messaging.

In plain terms

It is like a construction site where the electrician and plumber need to know who's working in which wall and when, or they'll damage each other's work.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a shared state or message-passing mechanism, such as a coordinator agent, a shared task queue, or direct agent-to-agent messages, that keeps multiple agents working toward a consistent goal without duplicating work or acting on stale information from each other.

A concrete example

Two agents working on the same codebase coordinate through a shared task list so one doesn't start refactoring a file the other has already claimed and is mid-edit on.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A central coordinator simplifies reasoning about who does what but becomes a bottleneck and single point of failure, while peer-to-peer coordination scales better but is harder to debug when agents disagree

Try it in code

const taskQueue = new SharedTaskQueue();

async function claimAndWork(agentId) {
  const task = await taskQueue.claim(agentId); // locks the task
  if (!task) return;
  const result = await performTask(task);
  await taskQueue.complete(task.id, result);
}
// Locking prevents two agents from claiming the same file/task.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a coordination mechanism for two agents working on the same repository so they cannot both edit the same file simultaneously.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Running agents in parallel is not automatically faster or safer; without real coordination, parallel agents can duplicate work or act on contradictory assumptions about shared state.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Coordination?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Coordination is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Running agents in parallel is not automatically faster or safer; without real coordination, parallel agents can duplicate work or act on contradictory assumptions about shared state.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Coordination in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Model Context Protocol documentation

State machines

State machines give agentic systems explicit, named stages and transition rules, so the system always knows precisely what stage a task is in and what actions are valid next, rather than inferring progress from unstructured conversation history.

In plain terms

It is like a traffic light system where only specific transitions are allowed, instead of drivers guessing when it's safe to go based on vibes.

How it works

In practice it comes down to modeling an agent's task progress as explicit states and transitions, such as planning, executing, verifying, and done or failed, so the system always knows exactly what stage it is in and what actions are valid from there, instead of inferring progress from free-text history.

A concrete example

An approval workflow agent models states as drafting, pending review, approved, executed, and verified, and can only move to executed after an explicit approved transition, closing off invalid shortcuts.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Explicit state machines make invalid transitions impossible and debugging straightforward, but they require upfront design and don't flexibly handle situations the designer didn't anticipate

Try it in code

const transitions = {
  planning:        ["executing"],
  executing:       ["verifying", "failed"],
  verifying:       ["pending_approval", "failed"],
  pending_approval: ["executed"],   // requires human action
  executed:        ["done"],
  failed:          ["planning"]     // retry from the top
};
// Any transition not listed here is invalid and must be rejected.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Draw a state machine for one agentic task with at least four states, and identify one transition that must require human approval before it can happen.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Letting an agent figure out what step it's on from free-text history is not the same as a state machine; without explicit states, the system can silently skip a required stage like human approval.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about State machines?

  • Once State machines is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Letting an agent figure out what step it's on from free-text history is not the same as a state machine; without explicit states, the system can silently skip a required stage like human approval.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain State machines in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

Retries

Retries handle recoverable failures in an agentic workflow by detecting a failed step, deciding if retrying makes sense, and re-attempting with a bounded limit, instead of either giving up immediately or looping forever.

In plain terms

It is like knowing when to redial a dropped call versus when to stop calling a number that was simply wrong.

How it works

In practice it comes down to detecting a failed step such as a tool error, a failed validation, or an unexpected output shape, deciding whether the failure is retryable, and re-attempting with backoff or a modified approach, bounded by a maximum attempt count to avoid infinite loops.

A concrete example

A tool call that times out due to a transient network issue is retried once with backoff; a tool call that fails because of invalid input is not blindly retried, since retrying identical invalid input will fail identically.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Retrying aggressively recovers from transient failures automatically, but retrying a fundamentally wrong action just repeats the same mistake and wastes cost and time without a plan change

Try it in code

async function withRetry(fn, { maxAttempts = 3, isRetryable }) {
  for (let attempt = 1; attempt <= maxAttempts; attempt++) {
    try { return await fn(); }
    catch (err) {
      if (!isRetryable(err) || attempt === maxAttempts) throw err;
      await sleep(2 ** attempt * 100); // backoff
    }
  }
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one agentic tool call, define what counts as a retryable failure, what counts as a non-retryable failure, and the maximum retry count before escalating to a human.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Not every failure should be retried the same way; a retry strategy needs to distinguish transient failures worth retrying as-is from logical failures that need a different approach, not repetition.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Retries?

  • Once Retries is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Not every failure should be retried the same way; a retry strategy needs to distinguish transient failures worth retrying as-is from logical failures that need a different approach, not repetition.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Retries in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Claude Code security (network request approval)

Human checkpoints

Human checkpoints are deliberate pause points in an agentic workflow where the system stops and requires explicit human approval before continuing, placed specifically before irreversible or high-risk actions.

In plain terms

It is like requiring a supervisor's sign-off before pulling the lever on heavy machinery, even though the operator could technically do it alone.

How it works

In practice it comes down to explicit pause points built into the workflow where the agent must get human approval before proceeding, typically placed before irreversible or high-risk actions rather than scattered arbitrarily.

A concrete example

An agent can draft a database migration and a rollback plan automatically, but must wait for a named human's approval before actually applying it to production.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More checkpoints reduce the risk of costly autonomous mistakes but slow down the workflow and require available humans, so checkpoints should be placed only where the cost of a wrong autonomous action is genuinely high

Try it in code

async function applyMigration(plan) {
  const approval = await requestHumanApproval({
    action: "apply migration",
    plan,
    reversible: false
  });
  if (!approval.granted) return { status: "blocked", reason: approval.reason };
  return execute(plan);
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one agentic workflow you're familiar with, identify the single highest-risk, least-reversible action and design a human checkpoint that blocks it until approved.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A human checkpoint is not the same as a human glancing at a log after the fact; it must actually block the next action until approval is given, or it is just an audit trail, not a control.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Human checkpoints?

  • Once Human checkpoints is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A human checkpoint is not the same as a human glancing at a log after the fact; it must actually block the next action until approval is given, or it is just an audit trail, not a control.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Human checkpoints in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Claude Code permissions

Failure scenarios

Failure scenario analysis enumerates the concrete ways an agentic system can fail, such as a wrong plan, a tool error, agent miscommunication, or silent partial completion, and defines detection and recovery for each in advance, rather than discovering them live in production.

In plain terms

It is like a fire drill: you plan for specific failure modes before the building is actually on fire.

How it works

In practice it comes down to deliberately enumerating how an agentic system can fail, including wrong plans, tool errors, misinterpreted instructions, cascading multi-agent miscommunication, or silent partial completion, and defining the detection and recovery behavior for each before it happens in production.

A concrete example

A failure scenario for a multi-agent research task considers what happens if one agent returns a plausible but factually wrong summary that a second agent then builds on without verification, and designs a cross-check step to catch it.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Thorough failure scenario planning catches costly mistakes before they reach users, but it takes real effort to enumerate and test, so it should be prioritized for the highest-impact and least-reversible actions first

Try it in code

const failureScenarios = [
  { name: "wrong plan", detect: "output contradicts stated goal" },
  { name: "tool error", detect: "non-zero exit code or exception" },
  { name: "silent partial completion", detect: "fewer files changed than plan listed" },
  { name: "compounding agent error", detect: "downstream agent builds on unverified upstream output" }
];

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: List three specific ways one real agentic workflow could fail silently without crashing, and define one detection signal for each.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • The scariest agentic failures are rarely a single dramatic error; they are often silent partial completions or subtly wrong intermediate results that get compounded by the next step without anyone noticing.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Failure scenarios?

  • Once Failure scenarios is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: The scariest agentic failures are rarely a single dramatic error; they are often silent partial completions or subtly wrong intermediate results that get compounded by the next step without anyone noticing.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Failure scenarios in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

Careful deployment

Careful deployment introduces agentic autonomy gradually: starting in shadow mode where the agent proposes actions without executing them, then limited scope with required approval, then wider autonomy only once outcomes are measured and trusted.

In plain terms

It is like a new pilot logging simulator hours, then supervised flights, before ever flying solo with passengers.

How it works

In practice it comes down to rolling out agentic capability gradually through shadow mode observing without acting, limited scope with human approval, then expanded autonomy, based on measured outcomes at each stage rather than granting full autonomy immediately.

A concrete example

A support-ticket triage agent first runs in shadow mode, logging what it would have done without acting, then is allowed to act only on low-risk ticket categories, expanding scope only after weeks of measured accuracy.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Gradual deployment delays reaching full autonomy and its efficiency gains, but it catches systemic problems while the blast radius is still small, which is far cheaper than discovering them after full rollout

Try it in code

const rolloutStages = [
  { stage: "shadow", acts: false, logsProposedActions: true },
  { stage: "limited", acts: true, scope: ["low_risk_categories"], requiresApproval: true },
  { stage: "expanded", acts: true, scope: ["most_categories"], requiresApproval: false }
];
// Advance a stage only after the previous stage's metrics hit target.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a three-stage careful deployment plan of shadow, limited, and expanded for one agentic capability, with the specific measurement that would justify moving to the next stage.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A successful pilot on a narrow task does not automatically justify expanding scope broadly; each expansion in autonomy or task breadth should be treated as a new deployment stage with its own evaluation.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Careful deployment?

  • Once Careful deployment is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A successful pilot on a narrow task does not automatically justify expanding scope broadly; each expansion in autonomy or task breadth should be treated as a new deployment stage with its own evaluation.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Careful deployment in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Claude Code security

Governance

Governance for agentic systems is the set of policies controlling what autonomous actions are allowed, who is accountable for agent decisions, how behavior is audited, and how the policy itself gets updated as new risks are discovered.

In plain terms

It is like the operating charter for a team with real authority to act: it names who is accountable and what gets reviewed.

How it works

In practice it comes down to policies defining which agentic actions require approval, who owns escalations, how agent behavior is audited, and how policy is updated as new failure modes are discovered, specifically for multi-agent and autonomous workflows.

A concrete example

A governance policy names a specific owner accountable for an agent's autonomous actions in production, requires an audit log of every action taken, and defines a monthly review of near-miss incidents.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Strong governance creates accountability and a paper trail for autonomous decisions, but policy that is too rigid or slow to update can't keep pace with how quickly agentic capabilities and failure modes evolve

Try it in code

const governancePolicy = {
  owner: "platform-team@company.com",
  auditLog: "every autonomous action, with trace id",
  reviewCadence: "monthly near-miss review",
  escalationPath: "pause agent -> notify owner -> incident review"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Name one agentic workflow you're familiar with, and write down who is accountable if it takes a wrong autonomous action, and how you would find out that it did.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Agentic governance is not the same as writing a values statement about responsible AI; it needs concrete ownership, audit logging, and an actual review cadence, or it has no enforcement mechanism.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Governance?

  • Once Governance is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Agentic governance is not the same as writing a values statement about responsible AI; it needs concrete ownership, audit logging, and an actual review cadence, or it has no enforcement mechanism.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Governance in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework

Evaluation

Evaluation for agentic systems tests whole task completions end to end, not just individual tool calls: does the agent reach the correct final state, does it recover from injected failures, and does it stay within its allowed scope.

In plain terms

It is like grading a road trip by whether the destination was reached safely, not just whether each individual turn signal worked.

How it works

In practice it comes down to test scenarios covering the full multi-step task rather than just one isolated tool call, checking whether the agent reaches a correct final state, recovers from injected failures, and stays within its defined scope, run repeatedly as the workflow changes.

A concrete example

An eval suite for a coding agent includes a task with an intentionally broken test to check whether the agent correctly identifies and fixes the root cause rather than just declaring success early.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: End-to-end evals are more expensive to build and run than single-step checks, but they are the only way to catch failures that only appear from compounding errors across multiple steps

Try it in code

const endToEndEvalCase = {
  name: "recovers from injected failure",
  setup: () => breakTestFileOnPurpose(),
  run: task => agent.run(task),
  assert: result => result.rootCauseIdentified && result.testsPassAfterFix
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write one end-to-end eval case for an agentic workflow that includes at least one injected failure partway through, and define what a correct recovery looks like.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • An agent passing every individual tool call correctly does not guarantee the overall task succeeds; errors can compound across steps in ways single-step tests never surface.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Evaluation?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Evaluation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: An agent passing every individual tool call correctly does not guarantee the overall task succeeds; errors can compound across steps in ways single-step tests never surface.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Evaluation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

Operations

Operations for agentic systems means monitoring live behavior in production: task completion rate, how often human checkpoints actually block or reject an action, cost per task, and alerting when behavior drifts from its established baseline.

In plain terms

It is like monitoring a fleet of delivery vehicles for on-time rate and driver overrides, not just whether the engines are running.

How it works

In practice it comes down to monitoring agent action logs, task completion rate, human-checkpoint intervention rate, and cost per task in production, with alerts when an agent's behavior or failure rate shifts from its established baseline.

A concrete example

A dashboard tracks how often a deployment agent's proposed actions are rejected at the human checkpoint; a sudden rise signals either a model regression or a workflow change the agent hasn't adapted to.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Deep operational visibility into agent behavior catches drift and silent failure early, but instrumenting every action and decision point adds real engineering cost that must scale with the workflow's actual risk

Try it in code

const agentDashboard = {
  taskCompletionRate: "alert if drops below 90% of 7-day baseline",
  checkpointRejectionRate: "alert if rises above 2x 7-day baseline",
  costPerTask: "alert if p95 exceeds budget"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Choose three operational metrics for one agentic workflow, such as completion rate, checkpoint rejection rate, and cost per task, and define an alert threshold for each.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • An agentic system running without visible errors is not the same as running correctly; without monitoring completion quality and checkpoint rejection rates, silent degradation can persist for a long time.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Operations?

  • Correct answer: An agentic system running without visible errors is not the same as running correctly; without monitoring completion quality and checkpoint rejection rates, silent degradation can persist for a long time.
  • Once Operations is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Operations in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Claude Code monitoring usage

Agentic lab

The agentic lab is a hands-on exercise to build a small but complete agentic workflow: task decomposition, at least one human checkpoint, and a retry path, then verify it against a deliberately injected failure.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to building a small multi-step agentic workflow end to end with at least one human checkpoint and one injected failure, then observing whether the retry, checkpoint, and recovery logic actually behave as designed.

It is like a controlled fire drill for a small system you built yourself, so you can see the safety mechanisms actually engage.

The brief

Build a three-step agentic workflow with one human checkpoint, inject a failure at the middle step, and verify the workflow escalates rather than silently continuing.

A worked reference

A lab implementation might build a three-step file-processing agent with a checkpoint before a destructive action, then inject a failure at step two to confirm the retry and escalation logic actually triggers.

Starting point in code

// Lab: 3-step workflow with one checkpoint and one injected failure.
const steps = ["fetch", "transform", "write"];
// inject a failure at "transform" and confirm escalation, not silent continue
const result = await runWorkflow(steps, { injectFailureAt: "transform" });
assert(result.status === "escalated_to_human");

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Mini project

The mini project ships a small, complete agentic workflow for one real task, including explicit scope, at least one human checkpoint, and documented failure handling, usable by another engineer without further explanation.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to shipping one small but complete multi-agent or checkpointed workflow for a real task, with documented scope, checkpoints, and failure handling, that another engineer could run and extend.

It is like shipping one well-labeled, working tool for the team instead of an ambitious framework nobody else can operate.

The brief

Build one small agentic workflow with a documented checkpoint and failure path, then hand it to a teammate and see if they can extend it without asking you questions.

A worked reference

A mini project might implement a pull-request triage workflow that labels and summarizes incoming pull requests automatically but requires human approval before assigning a reviewer, with a documented failure path if labeling confidence is low.

Starting point in code

const prTriageWorkflow = {
  autonomous: ["label pr", "summarize diff"],
  checkpoint: "assign reviewer",   // requires human approval
  onLowConfidence: "escalate to human triage queue"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete agentic system for a real use case: how the task is decomposed, whether it uses single or multiple agents, how they coordinate, where checkpoints and retries live, and the staged deployment plan used to roll it out safely.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to designing a complete agentic system for a real use case, including task decomposition, single- versus multi-agent structure, coordination mechanism, checkpoints, failure handling, governance, and the deployment stages used to roll it out safely.

It is like designing a full operations manual for a new automated system before it ever touches production.

The brief

Write a one-page agentic system design for a real use case: task decomposition, single versus multi-agent choice, one checkpoint, and a three-stage deployment plan.

A worked reference

A capstone for an automated incident-response assistant might specify a coordinator agent dispatching to a diagnosis agent and a remediation agent, with a mandatory human checkpoint before any remediation action, deployed first in shadow mode.

Starting point in code

const incidentResponseAgent = {
  coordinator: "dispatches to diagnosis and remediation agents",
  checkpoint: "human approval required before any remediation action",
  rollout: ["shadow", "limited (read-only diagnosis)", "expanded (with approval gate)"]
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Interview pack

The agentic AI interview pack prepares you to discuss multi-step and multi-agent system design credibly: how you decompose tasks, coordinate agents, place checkpoints, and roll out autonomy safely, backed by a real story about a failure and its recovery.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a safety system in detail, not just describing the exciting things the system can do.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real agentic workflow you designed, including a failure it encountered and how checkpoints or retries handled it, paired with technical answers on coordination, state, and safe deployment.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how you would deploy an autonomous agent safely describes a staged rollout of shadow, limited, and expanded phases, a specific checkpoint before irreversible actions, and a concrete metric that would justify each stage transition.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "Multi-agent research task compounded a wrong summary",
  cause: "downstream agent trusted upstream output with no cross-check",
  fix: "added a verification step before any agent builds on another's output",
  result: "caught 2 similar errors in eval before they reached production"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a time an agentic or automated workflow failed in an interesting way, what checkpoint or safeguard was missing, and what you changed afterward.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Describing what an agent can do is not the same as showing you understand how it fails and recovers; interviewers probe specifically for checkpoint, retry, and failure-handling reasoning.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • Correct answer: Describing what an agent can do is not the same as showing you understand how it fails and recovers; interviewers probe specifically for checkpoint, retry, and failure-handling reasoning.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models

Memory

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: AI memory is like a notebook for an assistant. Short-term notes help with the current conversation; long-term notes help future sessions remember stable preferences or facts. Memory improves continuity, but it also creates privacy, retention, and correctness risks.

Memory types

Memory types is the taxonomy engineers use to decide what an AI system should remember and for how long: transient task state, recent conversation, specific past episodes, and long-lived facts about a user or domain.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between a sticky note, a meeting's minutes, a diary entry, and a person's résumé: each holds information at a different lifespan and level of generality.

How it works

In practice it comes down to categorizing what an agent needs to remember into working memory for current task state, conversation memory for recent turns, episodic memory for specific past interactions, and semantic memory for durable facts and preferences, each with different retention and retrieval needs.

A concrete example

A coding assistant needs working memory for the current file being edited, conversation memory for the last few exchanges, and semantic memory for a user's stated preference to avoid a specific library, but rarely needs episodic memory of every past session.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Remembering more increases personalization and continuity but increases storage cost, privacy exposure, and the risk of stale or contradictory facts leaking into new conversations

Try it in code

const memory = {
  working: { scope: "current task", ttl: "discarded at task end" },
  conversation: { scope: "current session", ttl: "session length" },
  episodic: { scope: "one past event", ttl: "policy-defined" },
  semantic: { scope: "durable fact", ttl: "until corrected or revoked" }
};
// Route each new piece of information to exactly one bucket.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For an assistant you use regularly, list what it should remember as working memory, conversation memory, and semantic memory, and note what it currently gets wrong.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Memory is not one undifferentiated blob of everything said before; treating all memory types the same way causes systems to either forget what matters or retain what should have expired.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Memory types?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Memory types is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Memory is not one undifferentiated blob of everything said before; treating all memory types the same way causes systems to either forget what matters or retain what should have expired.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Memory types in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems

Conversation memory

Conversation memory is the short-term record of the current session's back-and-forth, used so an assistant doesn't ask a user to repeat information they already gave a few turns earlier.

In plain terms

It is like remembering what was just said earlier in the same phone call, without necessarily remembering it after you hang up.

How it works

In practice it comes down to keeping the recent turns of a single session in context, with a strategy such as truncation, a sliding window, or summarization for what happens once the conversation exceeds the context window.

A concrete example

A support bot references a customer's order number given three messages ago without asking again, because that turn is still inside the active context window.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Keeping full raw history preserves nuance but consumes context budget quickly, while sliding-window truncation is cheap but silently drops early details a later turn might reference

Try it in code

function truncateConversation(turns, maxTokens) {
  let kept = [];
  let used = 0;
  for (const turn of [...turns].reverse()) {
    const cost = estimateTokens(turn);
    if (used + cost > maxTokens) break;
    kept.unshift(turn);
    used += cost;
  }
  return kept; // oldest turns drop first
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a truncation rule for a chat session: what gets dropped first when the window fills, and what one piece of information should never be dropped.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Conversation memory is not persistent across sessions by default; without an explicit save-to-long-term-store step, it disappears when the session ends.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Conversation memory?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Conversation memory is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Conversation memory is not persistent across sessions by default; without an explicit save-to-long-term-store step, it disappears when the session ends.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Conversation memory in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems

Semantic memory

Semantic memory stores durable, reusable facts about a user, project, or domain, independent of any single conversation, so the system can recall a stable preference months later without replaying every past chat.

In plain terms

It is like a person's long-term general knowledge, as opposed to their memory of any one specific conversation.

How it works

In practice it comes down to extracting durable facts such as preferences, stable attributes, and decisions from conversations and storing them separately from raw transcripts, then retrieving only the relevant facts into context for a new session.

A concrete example

An assistant remembers that a user's team uses a specific deployment process, extracted once and reused across unrelated future conversations, rather than re-deriving it from scratch each time.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Extracting and storing facts explicitly is more reliable than re-deriving them from raw history each time, but extraction can be wrong, and wrong facts persist and compound unless there is a correction mechanism

Try it in code

async function extractFact(turn) {
  // Extraction should produce a structured, correctable record,
  // not a raw quote.
  return {
    subject: "user",
    predicate: "prefers_language",
    value: "TypeScript",
    sourceTurnId: turn.id,
    confidence: 0.9
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Pick one fact an assistant should remember about you long-term, write it as a structured fact rather than a quote, and define how you would let yourself correct it if it becomes wrong.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Semantic memory is not the same as just keeping old conversation logs around; a fact needs to be extracted, validated, and structured to be useful and correctable later.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Semantic memory?

  • Once Semantic memory is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Semantic memory is not the same as just keeping old conversation logs around; a fact needs to be extracted, validated, and structured to be useful and correctable later.
