  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A tool that works reliably in testing is not guaranteed to behave the same under real production conditions; production tool calling needs its own safeguards for slowness, partial outages, and volume that a development environment rarely exposes.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Production patterns?

  • Once Production patterns is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A tool that works reliably in testing is not guaranteed to behave the same under real production conditions; production tool calling needs its own safeguards for slowness, partial outages, and volume that a development environment rarely exposes.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Production patterns in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Google SRE book

Tool lab

The tool lab is a hands-on exercise to implement one real tool with a schema, argument validation, and structured error handling, then test it against both a valid call and a deliberately invalid one.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to defining a tool schema, implementing argument validation and structured error responses, and testing the tool against a valid call and an invalid call to confirm both the happy path and the error path behave as designed.

It is like building and testing one real appliance's safety features, not just plugging it in and hoping it works.

The brief

Implement one real tool with a schema and validation, then test it with both a valid call and a deliberately invalid one, confirming the error response is helpful.

A worked reference

A lab might implement a book_meeting tool with a schema requiring a time and attendee list, validate that the time isn't in the past, and test both a valid booking and an attempt to book a meeting in the past to confirm the structured error fires correctly.

Starting point in code

// Valid call
await bookMeeting({ time: "2025-06-01T14:00:00Z", attendees: ["a@co.com"] });
// Invalid call — time in the past
await bookMeeting({ time: "2020-01-01T14:00:00Z", attendees: ["a@co.com"] });
// Expect: structured error, not a silent failure or a booked meeting

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Testing both valid and invalid calls takes more setup than just confirming the tool works on a clean example, but it's the only way to know the validation and error handling actually function as designed

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • The lab is not complete once a tool works on a valid call; without testing the invalid case and confirming a helpful structured error, half the tool's real design isn't actually verified.

Going deeper

References

  • OpenAI function calling guide

Mini project

The mini project ships one small, complete tool for a real agent, with a schema, validation, idempotency handling if relevant, structured errors, and an audit log, that another engineer could plug into a different agent.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a tool schema, argument validation, idempotency handling where the tool has side effects, structured error responses, and audit logging into one documented, reusable tool.

It is like building one fully production-ready appliance, documented well enough that someone else could install it into a different system.

The brief

Build one real tool with a schema, validation, structured errors, and an audit log, and hand it to a teammate to integrate into a different agent.

A worked reference

A mini project might build a create_support_ticket tool with a validated schema, an idempotency key tied to the originating message, structured errors for missing fields, and an audit log of every call.

Starting point in code

export const createSupportTicket = {
  schema: { subject: "string", body: "string", priority: "enum" },
  idempotencyKey: (args) => hash(args.subject + args.body),
  validate: (args) => args.subject.length > 0,
  onError: (err) => ({ error: { type: "invalid_argument", message: err.message } })
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A narrowly scoped, fully hardened tool is more valuable and more trustworthy to reuse than several tools built quickly without validation, idempotency, or error handling

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A tool that works when called correctly once is not the same as a complete mini project; without validation, error handling, and audit logging, it isn't ready for a real agent to rely on unsupervised.

Going deeper

References

  • OpenAI function calling guide

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete tool-calling architecture for a real agent: schema and validation standards, idempotency and retry policy, error-handling conventions, audit logging, and production safeguards, as one coherent system.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining schema and validation standards applied consistently across every tool, an idempotency and retry policy matched to each tool's side effects, structured error conventions, comprehensive audit logging, and production safeguards like timeouts and circuit breakers into one documented architecture.

It is like designing the complete electrical and safety code for a building's appliances, not just wiring one lamp correctly.

The brief

Write a one-page tool-calling architecture for a real agent: schema and validation standard, idempotency policy, error format, and one production safeguard applied to every tool.

A worked reference

A capstone for a customer-support agent might specify a shared JSON-schema validation library for every tool, idempotency keys for any tool with a side effect, a standard error-response format, full audit logging, and a 5-second timeout with circuit breakers on every external tool call.

Starting point in code

const toolArchitecture = {
  schemaStandard: "shared JSON Schema validation library",
  idempotency: "required for any tool with a side effect",
  errorFormat: "{ type, field, message, suggestion }",
  auditLogging: "every call, every tool, durable storage",
  productionSafeguards: "5s timeout + circuit breaker on every external tool"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A complete tool-calling architecture with consistent standards takes real design effort across every tool, but it prevents the common failure of each tool being built inconsistently with different validation, error, and safety conventions

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A tool-calling system is not complete once individual tools work correctly in isolation; without consistent standards for validation, idempotency, errors, and production safeguards across every tool, the system as a whole remains unpredictable and hard to operate.

Going deeper

References

  • OpenAI function calling guide
  • Google SRE book

Interview pack

The tool calling interview pack prepares you to discuss tool schema design, validation, idempotency, and production safeguards credibly, backed by a concrete story rather than a definition of function calling.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a real appliance's safety engineering, not just that it has an on switch.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real tool-calling system you built or debugged, paired with technical answers distinguishing schema validation, idempotency design, error handling, and production safeguards.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how would you design tool calling for this agent names a specific validation approach, an idempotency strategy for any side-effecting tool, and proactively raises timeout and circuit-breaker behavior without being asked.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "Agent double-charged a customer after a network retry",
  cause: "charge_customer tool had no idempotency key",
  fix: "added an idempotency key tied to the order ID",
  result: "verified via a repeated-call test that it charges exactly once"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a tool-calling failure you encountered or would anticipate, such as a duplicated side effect or an unhandled error, its root cause, and the specific fix.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that models can call tools is not the same as understanding how to make those tools safe, idempotent, and resilient in production; interviewers probe for that applied engineering judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Knowing that models can call tools is not the same as understanding how to make those tools safe, idempotent, and resilient in production; interviewers probe for that applied engineering judgment specifically.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI function calling guide

Structured Output

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: Structured output is like asking someone to fill a form instead of writing a paragraph. Software can reliably read fields from a form. Production systems need predictable output shapes for APIs, workflows, validations, and storage.

Why structure matters

Structured output makes AI responses usable by software because downstream code can parse, validate, route, and test them deterministically.

In plain terms

It is the difference between a paragraph saying 'urgent bug' and a ticket object with priority='P1'.

How it works

In practice it comes down to schemas, parsers, validators, retries, typed clients, and downstream contracts.

A concrete example

A triage bot returns a typed incident record that automation can route to the right on-call team.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Structure improves automation but reduces expressive freedom.

Hands-on lab

Task: Convert one free-form AI answer into a typed object with required fields and validation rules.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not build workflows by regex-parsing free-form prose.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Why structure matters?

  • Once Why structure matters is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not build workflows by regex-parsing free-form prose.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Why structure matters in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

JSON schema

JSON Schema describes the shape, required fields, data types, and constraints of JSON so model output or tool input can be validated.

In plain terms

It is like a contract that says exactly which boxes a response must fill and what each box may contain.

How it works

In practice it comes down to types, required fields, enums, arrays, nested objects, patterns, additionalProperties, and versioning.

A concrete example

A support classifier returns {category, priority, rationale} where category must be one of five allowed strings.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Strict schemas improve reliability but can reject useful partial answers.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a schema for extracting invoice number, vendor, amount, currency, and due date.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not trust JSON-looking text unless it has been parsed and validated.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about JSON schema?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once JSON schema is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not trust JSON-looking text unless it has been parsed and validated.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain JSON schema in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Constrained decoding

Constrained decoding restricts token generation so the model can only produce outputs that match a grammar, schema, or allowed token set.

In plain terms

It is like rails on a train track: the model still moves forward, but invalid paths are blocked.

How it works

In practice it comes down to token masks, grammar states, schema automata, valid continuations, and parser feedback.

A concrete example

A JSON generator prevents the model from emitting a string where the schema requires a number.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Constraints improve parseability but can reduce naturalness and sometimes force awkward outputs.

Hands-on lab

Task: List three constraints that guarantee JSON validity but not factual correctness.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not confuse valid syntax with correct semantics.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Constrained decoding?

  • Once Constrained decoding is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not confuse valid syntax with correct semantics.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Constrained decoding in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Validation

Validation checks whether AI inputs, outputs, or tool calls satisfy explicit rules before the system trusts or acts on them.

In plain terms

It is like a compiler and safety inspector for model behavior.

How it works

In practice it comes down to schema checks, policy checks, semantic checks, source checks, confidence thresholds, and rejection handling.

A concrete example

An extraction workflow validates date format, total amount range, and source evidence before writing to billing.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Strict validation catches errors but can increase false rejects.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write validation rules for a refund approval response before it reaches payment code.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not validate only after side effects have already happened.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Validation?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not validate only after side effects have already happened.
  • Once Validation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Validation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Repair loops

Repair loops ask the model or a deterministic fixer to correct invalid structured output using validation errors as feedback.

In plain terms

It is like returning a form with highlighted fields that must be fixed before submission.

How it works

In practice it comes down to parse errors, schema errors, retry prompts, bounded attempts, deterministic repair, and fallback escalation.

A concrete example

If the model omits a required 'currency' field, the repair loop asks for the same object with that field filled.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Repair improves success rate but adds latency and can hide flaky prompts.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a two-attempt repair loop for invalid JSON extraction.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not retry forever; repair loops need attempt limits and telemetry.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Repair loops?

  • Correct answer: Do not retry forever; repair loops need attempt limits and telemetry.
  • Once Repair loops is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Repair loops in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Typed clients

Typed clients wrap AI outputs and tool calls in language-level types so application code catches contract mismatches early.

In plain terms

It is like replacing sticky-note instructions with a compiled interface.

How it works

In practice it comes down to generated types, schema-to-code tools, parsing boundaries, version compatibility, and runtime validation.

A concrete example

A TypeScript client exposes SupportTicketClassification rather than a raw string from the model.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Typed clients improve maintainability but require schema version discipline.

Hands-on lab

Task: Generate an interface for a structured answer and list the runtime checks still required.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not skip runtime validation just because compile-time types exist.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Typed clients?

  • Correct answer: Do not skip runtime validation just because compile-time types exist.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Typed clients is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Typed clients in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Extraction

Extraction is using a model to pull specific structured fields out of unstructured text, such as pulling a date, amount, and vendor name from an invoice, constrained to a defined schema so the output is directly usable by downstream code.

In plain terms

It is like a skilled clerk reading a messy handwritten form and filling out a clean, standardized digital form with exactly the fields that matter.

How it works

In practice it comes down to prompting a model with the source text and a target schema, constraining its output to that schema, and validating the extracted fields against expected types and formats before passing them to downstream systems.

A concrete example

An extraction system pulls invoice number, due date, and total amount from scanned invoices with varying layouts, producing consistent structured records regardless of each invoice's specific visual format.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Extraction handles varied, messy input formats far more flexibly than a rules-based parser, but it can occasionally misextract a field on an unusual layout, so validation and a human-review path for low-confidence extractions still matter

Try it in code

const schema = { invoiceNumber: "string", dueDate: "string", totalAmount: "number" };
const extracted = await extractStructured(invoiceText, schema);
validateAgainstSchema(extracted, schema); // don't trust it just because it parsed

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real unstructured document type, design an extraction schema listing the specific fields you'd want pulled out.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A model successfully extracting fields on most documents does not guarantee it will extract correctly on a genuinely novel layout; extraction systems still need validation and a fallback for low-confidence or malformed results.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Extraction?

  • Correct answer: A model successfully extracting fields on most documents does not guarantee it will extract correctly on a genuinely novel layout; extraction systems still need validation and a fallback for low-confidence or malformed results.
  • Once Extraction is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Extraction in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI function calling guide

Classification

Classification constrains a model's output to one of a fixed set of predefined categories, distinct from open-ended generation, so downstream code can reliably branch logic based on the result.

In plain terms

It is like sorting incoming mail into a fixed set of labeled bins, rather than writing a free-form description of each piece of mail.

How it works

In practice it comes down to constraining the model's output to a defined enum of valid categories, either through the prompt, a schema, or constrained decoding, so the result is always one of the expected values rather than a free-text description that has to be parsed and matched.

A concrete example

A support-ticket classifier constrains its output to exactly one of billing, technical, or account categories, rather than generating a free-text description like this seems like a billing issue that then has to be parsed to determine the category.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Constraining output to a fixed category set makes downstream logic simple and reliable, but it requires the category set to genuinely cover the real cases; an input that doesn't fit any category well will still be forced into the closest one unless an explicit other or unknown option exists

Try it in code

const categories = ["billing", "technical", "account", "other"]; // explicit "other" for edge cases
const result = await classify(ticketText, { enum: categories });

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real classification task, define the category set, including whether an explicit unknown or other category is needed for edge cases.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A classification model choosing one of the valid categories is not the same as choosing the correct one; classification accuracy still needs to be measured and validated, constraining the output format doesn't guarantee the content is right.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Classification?

  • Once Classification is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A classification model choosing one of the valid categories is not the same as choosing the correct one; classification accuracy still needs to be measured and validated, constraining the output format doesn't guarantee the content is right.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Classification in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI function calling guide

Workflows

Workflows for structured output chain multiple structured-output steps together, where one step's validated output becomes the next step's input, letting complex multi-stage processes stay reliable end to end.

In plain terms

It is like an assembly line where each station's output must meet a specification before it's passed to the next station, rather than passing along whatever came out regardless of whether it's correct.

How it works

In practice it comes down to designing each stage of a multi-step process to produce schema-validated output, checking that output before passing it to the next stage, so an error at one stage is caught early rather than silently propagating and compounding through the rest of the workflow.

A concrete example

A document-processing workflow extracts structured fields in one step, validates them, then uses only the validated fields as input to a second step that generates a structured summary, rather than passing raw, unvalidated extraction results forward.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Validating structured output at each stage of a workflow catches errors early and prevents them from compounding, but it adds a validation step at every stage, which takes more design effort than a single end-to-end unstructured pass

Try it in code

const fields = await extractStructured(doc, invoiceSchema);
if (!isValid(fields, invoiceSchema)) throw new StageError("extraction");
const summary = await generateStructured(fields, summarySchema); // only validated input passed forward

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real multi-step process, identify one intermediate stage where validating structured output before passing it forward would prevent an error from compounding.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A multi-step workflow doesn't need validation only at the final output; an error introduced early in a chain of structured steps can compound and become harder to trace the further it propagates, so each stage's output should be validated before it feeds the next.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Workflows?

  • Once Workflows is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A multi-step workflow doesn't need validation only at the final output; an error introduced early in a chain of structured steps can compound and become harder to trace the further it propagates, so each stage's output should be validated before it feeds the next.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Workflows in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI function calling guide

Testing

Testing structured output means verifying that a model reliably produces schema-valid output across representative and edge-case inputs, not just confirming it worked correctly on one example during development.

In plain terms

It is like testing a form-filling assistant against many different messy input documents, not just the one clean example you used while building it.

How it works

In practice it comes down to building a test set of representative and edge-case inputs, running the structured-output generation against each, and checking both schema validity and correctness of the extracted or classified content, repeating whenever the prompt or model changes.

A concrete example

A structured-extraction system is tested against 20 real invoices with varying layouts, revealing that it fails to extract the total amount correctly on invoices with a specific unusual formatting pattern the original single test example never covered.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Building a proper test set for structured output takes more upfront effort than testing on one clean example, but it's the only way to catch schema violations or extraction errors on the messy, varied inputs a system will actually encounter

Try it in code

const testCases = [
  { input: cleanInvoice, expectValid: true },
  { input: messyScannedInvoice, expectValid: true }, // edge case
];
testCases.forEach(t => assert(validateAgainstSchema(extract(t.input)) === t.expectValid));

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Build a five-example test set for one real structured-output task, including one deliberately messy edge case, and check whether the output is schema-valid and correct for all five.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A structured-output prompt working correctly on one clean example does not mean it reliably produces valid output across the messy range of real inputs it will actually see; edge cases and unusual formats need their own test coverage.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Testing?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A structured-output prompt working correctly on one clean example does not mean it reliably produces valid output across the messy range of real inputs it will actually see; edge cases and unusual formats need their own test coverage.
  • Once Testing is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Testing in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena

Versioning

Versioning for structured output means tracking changes to an output schema over time, so downstream consumers of that structured data aren't unexpectedly broken when a field is added, removed, or changed.

In plain terms

It is like a database schema migration: existing code that reads the old structure needs a compatible transition, not a sudden silent change that breaks every consumer.

How it works

In practice it comes down to tracking schema versions explicitly, communicating breaking changes such as a renamed or removed field, and where possible supporting a transition period where downstream consumers can migrate before the old schema version is retired.

A concrete example

A structured-extraction schema adds a new required field; rather than breaking every existing downstream consumer immediately, the field is introduced as optional first, with a deprecation notice before it becomes required in a later version.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Maintaining a transition period for schema changes reduces the risk of breaking downstream consumers, but it means supporting both old and new schema shapes simultaneously for a time, adding real complexity

Try it in code

// v1: { name, amount }  ->  v2: { name, amount, currency }
// currency introduced as optional first, required only in a later version
const schemaV2 = { name: "string", amount: "number", currency: { type: "string", optional: true } };

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real structured-output schema, describe a change you might need to make, and how you would roll it out without breaking existing downstream consumers.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Changing a structured output's schema is not a purely internal implementation detail; any downstream code that parses that structure can break immediately if a field is renamed or removed without versioning or a migration path.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Versioning?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Changing a structured output's schema is not a purely internal implementation detail; any downstream code that parses that structure can break immediately if a field is renamed or removed without versioning or a migration path.
  • Once Versioning is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Versioning in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI function calling guide

Monitoring

Monitoring tracks live AI behavior so teams notice quality, safety, latency, cost, and reliability changes after deployment.

In plain terms

It is like a dashboard for a system whose behavior can drift without code changes.

How it works

In practice it comes down to metrics, alerts, traces, feedback, drift signals, eval sampling, and dashboards.

A concrete example

A dashboard shows answer thumbs-down rate, citation failures, p95 latency, token cost, and guardrail block rate.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More monitoring creates noise unless alerts map to action.

Hands-on lab

Task: Choose five production metrics and one alert threshold for a RAG product.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not monitor only uptime for an AI feature.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Monitoring?

  • Once Monitoring is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not monitor only uptime for an AI feature.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Monitoring in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Failure handling

Failure handling for structured output means detecting when a model's response doesn't match the expected schema and applying a defined recovery, such as a repair attempt or a fallback, rather than passing invalid data downstream or crashing.

In plain terms

It is like a form-processing system that flags an incomplete or malformed form for correction, instead of filing it as if it were valid and letting the error propagate.

How it works

In practice it comes down to validating every structured output against its schema immediately after generation, and on failure, attempting a repair, such as asking the model to fix the specific validation error, or falling back to a default or an escalation path if repair also fails, rather than passing invalid data to downstream systems.

A concrete example

A structured-extraction call returns a response missing a required field; the system detects the schema violation, asks the model to specifically fill in the missing field, and only escalates to a human review queue if the repair attempt also fails.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Building repair and fallback logic for structured-output failures adds real engineering effort, but without it, a single malformed response can silently corrupt downstream data or crash a pipeline that assumed the output would always be valid

Try it in code

let result = await extractStructured(doc, schema);
if (!isValid(result, schema)) {
  result = await repair(doc, schema, validationErrors(result, schema));
}
if (!isValid(result, schema)) escalateToHumanReview(doc);

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real structured-output integration, design what should happen when the model's response fails schema validation, including one repair attempt and one fallback if repair also fails.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Structured output being usually valid is not a reason to skip failure handling; the specific cases where it fails are often the most important to catch, since silently accepting invalid data can be worse than an explicit, visible failure.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Failure handling?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Structured output being usually valid is not a reason to skip failure handling; the specific cases where it fails are often the most important to catch, since silently accepting invalid data can be worse than an explicit, visible failure.
  • Once Failure handling is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Failure handling in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI function calling guide

Output lab

The output lab is a hands-on exercise to build a small structured-output pipeline with schema validation and a repair step, then test it against a deliberately malformed response to confirm the repair and fallback logic actually works.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to implementing a structured-output call with schema validation, a repair attempt on failure, and a fallback if repair also fails, then deliberately feeding it a malformed response to confirm each failure-handling step triggers correctly.

It is like testing a safety mechanism by deliberately triggering the failure condition, not just assuming it works because you built it.

The brief

Build a structured-output pipeline with validation and a repair step, then deliberately feed it a malformed response and confirm the repair and fallback logic trigger correctly.

A worked reference

A lab might build an invoice-extraction pipeline, deliberately truncate a model response to simulate a malformed result, and confirm the validation catches it, the repair attempt succeeds or fails as expected, and the fallback path triggers correctly if needed.

Starting point in code

const malformed = '{ "name": "Acme"'; // deliberately truncated
const parsed = tryParse(malformed);
assert(!isValid(parsed, schema)); // validation should catch this
const repaired = await repair(malformed, schema);
assert(isValid(repaired, schema)); // repair should fix it

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Deliberately testing the failure path takes more setup than only testing the happy path, but it's the only way to know the repair and fallback logic actually functions when it's needed

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • The lab is not complete once structured output works on a clean input; without testing a deliberately malformed response, you have no evidence the repair and fallback logic you built actually functions.

Going deeper

References

  • OpenAI function calling guide

Mini project

The mini project ships a small, complete structured-output feature for one real task, with a versioned schema, validation, repair logic, and a test set, that another engineer could extend.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a versioned output schema, validation and repair logic, and a labeled test set covering representative and edge cases into one documented, reusable structured-output feature.

It is like shipping one small but fully functioning, tested form-processing system, not just a prompt that happens to work on one example.

The brief

Build a structured-output feature for one real task with a versioned schema, validation and repair logic, and a test set, and hand it to a teammate to extend.

A worked reference

A mini project might build a structured invoice-extraction feature with a versioned schema, validation with a repair step, and a 10-invoice test set covering varied layouts.

Starting point in code

export const invoiceExtractor = {
  schemaVersion: "v2",
  validate: (result) => validateAgainstSchema(result, invoiceSchemaV2),
  repair: (result, errors) => repairExtraction(result, errors),
  testSet: require("./eval-10-invoices.json")
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A narrowly scoped, fully tested structured-output feature is more valuable than a broader one that was never tested against edge cases or given failure handling

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • Structured output that parses correctly on the examples you tried is not the same as a complete mini project; without versioning, failure handling, and a real test set, it's not ready for another engineer to extend or trust.

Going deeper

References

  • OpenAI function calling guide

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete structured-output system for a real product: schema design and versioning, validation and repair strategy, testing standard, and workflow chaining, as one coherent system.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a documented, versioned schema design, a validation and repair strategy applied consistently, a testing standard covering representative and edge cases, and a workflow design chaining validated structured outputs across multiple stages into one complete system.

It is like designing a complete data-processing pipeline's quality-control system, not just one well-formed output in isolation.

The brief

Write a one-page structured-output system design for a real product: schema versioning approach, failure-handling strategy, and one workflow chaining decision.

A worked reference

A capstone for a document-processing product might specify a versioned extraction schema, a repair-then-escalate failure strategy, a 30-case test set per schema, and a multi-stage workflow where each stage validates before passing output to the next.

Starting point in code

const structuredOutputSystem = {
  schema: "versioned, v2 adds optional currency field",
  failureStrategy: "repair once, then escalate to human review",
  testing: "30-case set covering messy real-world layouts",
  workflow: "extract -> validate -> summarize, each stage gated"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A complete structured-output system spanning schema design, validation, testing, and workflow chaining takes substantially more design work than one working extraction prompt, but it reflects what's actually needed to run structured output reliably at scale

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A structured-output system is not complete once one extraction task works correctly in testing; without versioning, failure handling, and a workflow design that validates at every stage, errors can compound silently across a multi-step process.

Going deeper

References

  • OpenAI function calling guide

Interview pack

The structured output interview pack prepares you to discuss schema design, validation, repair strategies, and versioning credibly, backed by a concrete story rather than a general statement that structured output matters.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a real data-processing pipeline's quality-control decisions, not just that you use JSON schemas.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real structured-output system you built or debugged, paired with technical answers distinguishing schema validation, repair logic, and versioning strategy.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how would you make sure this extraction pipeline doesn't silently corrupt data describes a specific validation and repair strategy, a versioning plan for schema changes, and proactively raises how a multi-stage workflow validates at each step.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "A schema change silently broke a downstream consumer",
  cause: "no versioning — a field was renamed without a transition period",
  fix: "introduced the new field as optional, deprecated the old one gradually",
  result: "next schema change shipped with zero downstream breakage"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a structured-output failure you encountered or would anticipate, such as a schema violation or a silent data-corruption issue, its root cause, and the specific fix.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that models can produce JSON output is not the same as understanding how to validate, repair, and version that output reliably in a real pipeline; interviewers probe for that applied engineering judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • Correct answer: Knowing that models can produce JSON output is not the same as understanding how to validate, repair, and version that output reliably in a real pipeline; interviewers probe for that applied engineering judgment specifically.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI function calling guide

Evaluation

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: Evaluation is like a driving test for an AI system. One good trip around the block is not enough; you need repeatable routes, edge cases, and scoring. Evals tell you whether a prompt, model, retrieval change, or guardrail made the product better or worse.

The running example

Throughout this track, imagine you own quality for 'Reply', a support assistant answering from your company's help center. Every chapter builds the measurement system around it — golden sets, gates, judges, dashboards — until 'is Reply getting better or worse?' has an answer that isn't an opinion.

Eval strategy

An evaluation strategy defines how you measure whether your AI system is producing good outputs — what data to test on, what metrics to compute, how often to run evals, and what threshold gates a release.

Why this matters

Eval strategy matters because without it, you cannot tell whether a change improved or degraded the system. You are flying blind.

In plain terms

An eval strategy is a testing plan for AI behaviour. Just as you would not ship software without tests, you should not ship an AI product without a defined eval suite that runs before every significant change.

How it works

Internal flow: change (prompt, model, retrieval) → run eval suite → compare metrics → gate release decision → log results for trend analysis.

  • Golden datasets: a fixed set of inputs with known correct or preferred outputs, used consistently across model/prompt/retrieval changes to compare performance.
  • Unit evals: test specific capabilities in isolation — does the model correctly extract dates? Does it refuse harmful requests? Does it follow the output format?
  • End-to-end evals: test the full pipeline — does the RAG system answer the 100 canonical support questions correctly?
  • LLM-as-judge: use a stronger model (often with a rubric) to score outputs at scale. Cheaper than human review, useful for quality dimensions like helpfulness or coherence.
  • Human review: essential for high-stakes outputs, edge cases, and calibrating automated eval metrics. Cannot be replaced entirely but should be focused on high-value cases.

A concrete example

Before shipping a new prompt for a support bot: run 100 golden questions through old and new prompts; compute answer quality (LLM-as-judge), citation accuracy (exact match), refusal rate, latency, and cost. Require that new prompt scores ≥ old on quality and ≤ old on cost before merging.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More evals = more confidence but more cost and time. Start with a small golden set (50–100 examples), add over time as you discover failure modes.

Hands-on lab

Task: For an AI feature you are building, write: (1) 10 golden test cases with expected outputs, (2) the 3 most important metrics, (3) the minimum score threshold for each metric that must be met before shipping a change.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production: run evals in CI on every prompt or model change. Alert when production metrics deviate from the eval baseline by more than a threshold.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Evals are not optional for 'small' AI features. Even a simple classification prompt needs a test set.
  • LLM-as-judge is not a substitute for human review for high-stakes outputs. It is a complement.
  • Eval metrics should match business value, not just technical correctness. A system that answers precisely but rudely may score well on accuracy but badly on user satisfaction.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Eval strategy?

  • Once Eval strategy is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Evals are not optional for 'small' AI features. Even a simple classification prompt needs a test set.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Eval strategy into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: Production: run evals in CI on every prompt or model change. Alert when production metrics deviate from the eval baseline by more than a threshold.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Eval strategy in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Golden datasets

A golden dataset is a curated, versioned set of inputs with known-correct or preferred outputs — the fixed measuring stick that makes every model, prompt, and pipeline change comparable to the last one.

When it goes wrong

The team argued for an hour about whether the new prompt was better, trading screenshots of cherry-picked answers. They had no fixed set of questions with agreed answers — so 'better' had no referee, and the loudest opinion shipped.

Why this matters

Golden datasets matter because they are the difference between 'it seems better' and 'it is better on the 80 cases we've agreed define better' — every other eval technique stands on this foundation.

In plain terms

It is like the standard kilogram in a vault: not interesting in itself, but every scale in the country is honest only because a reference exists that doesn't drift with opinion.

How it works

The lifecycle: seed from production traffic → adjudicate expected outputs with named owners → version and store like code → run against every change → grow from new production failures → audit quarterly for staleness.

  • Golden means decided: each example carries the expected output or source, who decided it, and why — ambiguous cases get adjudicated once during curation, not re-argued every eval run. The deciding is the expensive, valuable part.
  • Composition beats size: 60 examples covering the real distribution — common cases, hard cases, edge cases, and deliberately unanswerable ones — outperform 600 scraped happy-path examples; every failure class from production earns a representative.
  • Provenance from production keeps it honest: seed from real (sanitized) traffic, not from what the team imagines users ask — imagination produces datasets your system aces while users suffer.
  • Golden sets are versioned artifacts: examples get added from new failures, corrected when the world changes (that policy was updated), and never silently edited — a score only means something against a named dataset version.
  • Leakage discipline: the golden set must stay out of prompts, few-shot examples, and fine-tuning data — a measuring stick the system has memorized measures memory, not capability.

A concrete example

A support assistant's golden set v7: 80 questions — 40 common (from top production intents), 20 hard (multi-policy, ambiguous phrasing), 12 edge (weird formatting, mixed languages), 8 unanswerable (correct behavior: abstain). Each row: input, expected answer, expected source IDs, decider, date. When legal changes the refund policy, three rows get updated in a reviewed PR — and the changelog is why last quarter's 91% and this quarter's 89% are known to be a real regression, not dataset drift.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Small sets are cheap to maintain but statistically noisy; large sets are trustworthy but expensive to adjudicate and keep current — most teams do best with a small, fiercely-curated core plus a larger, lightly-labeled extended set.

Hands-on lab

Task: Build a 20-row golden set for any AI feature you know: 10 common cases from realistic traffic, 5 hard, 3 edge, 2 unanswerable. For each, record expected output, expected sources if applicable, and who would adjudicate disagreements — then name the production signal that should add row 21.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-shaped slice: 50+ examples with provenance and owners, stored versioned next to the code, including at least five unanswerable cases, wired into CI so no prompt or model change ships without a run.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A golden dataset is not a benchmark you download; public benchmarks measure general capability, while your golden set measures your product's definition of correct — only one of those pays your bills.
  • It is not static; a set that never grows from production failures measures last year's problems, and its scores inflate as the system overfits to a frozen target.
  • High scores on it are not the goal; catching regressions is — a set everyone passes forever has stopped doing its job and needs harder examples.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Golden datasets?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A golden dataset is not a benchmark you download; public benchmarks measure general capability, while your golden set measures your product's definition of correct — only one of those pays your bills.
  • Once Golden datasets is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Golden datasets into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: A production-shaped slice: 50+ examples with provenance and owners, stored versioned next to the code, including at least five unanswerable cases, wired into CI so no prompt or model change ships without a run.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Golden datasets in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Unit evals

Unit evals are small, fast, targeted checks that verify one specific behavior of an AI component — one prompt's format compliance, one extraction field, one refusal rule — the AI analogue of unit tests, run in seconds and wired into the developer loop.

When it goes wrong

A one-word prompt tweak broke JSON output for 0.4% of requests. It reached production because checking meant manually pasting five test cases into a playground — and under deadline, nobody did. A 90-second suite would have caught it at the diff.

Why this matters

Unit evals matter because they move quality feedback from 'after deploy, via users' to 'before merge, in minutes' — they are what make prompt engineering feel like engineering.

In plain terms

It is like unit tests versus a full QA pass: you don't re-certify the whole product to check that one function still rounds correctly — you keep a fast, specific test that fails loudly the moment that one behavior breaks.

How it works

The flow: define one behavior → write targeted inputs including the known failure modes → assert deterministically (or thresholded over N runs) → wire into the pre-merge loop → add a new eval every time production teaches you a new failure.

  • The unit is a behavior, not a component: 'returns valid JSON matching the schema', 'extracts the invoice date as ISO-8601', 'refuses medical dosage questions', 'includes a citation when sources are provided' — each eval checks one assertable property on a handful of targeted inputs.
  • Assertions should be deterministic wherever possible: schema validation, regex and substring checks, enum membership, numeric bounds — cheap, unambiguous, and CI-friendly; save judge-based scoring for properties that genuinely resist deterministic checks.
  • Nondeterminism is managed, not ignored: run at temperature 0 where the task allows, and for genuinely sampled behaviors run N trials with a pass-threshold (e.g. 9/10) — a unit eval that flakes gets deleted or fixed, exactly like a flaky test.
  • Speed defines the loop they live in: a suite of 40 unit evals over cached or cheap-model calls should run in a couple of minutes, cheap enough that engineers run it on every prompt edit — the moment it's too slow for the inner loop, it silently stops being run.
  • Each production incident should leave a unit eval behind: the model that once emitted apology-prose into a JSON field gets a permanent 'no prose in JSON' eval — the suite becomes the accumulated scar tissue of everything that has ever gone wrong.

A concrete example

An invoice-extraction pipeline has 14 unit evals: schema validity on 5 synthetic invoices, date normalization on 3 formats (US, EU, written-out), currency handling on 2, a two-page invoice, an invoice with a missing total (expected: null, not a guess), and — added after an incident — a scanned invoice whose vendor name contains 'Total GmbH' (the model once returned it as the total). The suite runs in 90 seconds on every prompt change; the 'Total GmbH' eval has failed twice since, each time catching a regression before merge.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Coverage versus speed — the suite must stay fast enough to run constantly, so unit evals stay small and surgical while broad quality measurement lives in the full golden-set run.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one AI component you know, write six unit evals: the behavior asserted, the 2–3 targeted inputs, the deterministic assertion (or N-trial threshold), and which of the six encodes a failure you have actually seen happen.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-shaped slice: a suite of 10–20 behavior-specific evals with deterministic assertions, running in under two minutes on every prompt or model change, with at least three evals traceable to real past incidents.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Unit evals are not small golden-dataset runs; the golden set measures overall quality, while a unit eval asserts one behavior — different granularity, different failure meaning, different fix.
  • LLM outputs being nondeterministic does not make assertion impossible; most product-critical properties (format, fields, refusals, citations) are deterministic claims even when wording varies.
  • Passing unit evals does not mean the system is good; it means the specific behaviors you've encoded still hold — which is exactly enough to ship a prompt change without fear, and nothing more.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Unit evals?

  • Correct answer: Unit evals are not small golden-dataset runs; the golden set measures overall quality, while a unit eval asserts one behavior — different granularity, different failure meaning, different fix.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Unit evals is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Unit evals into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: A production-shaped slice: a suite of 10–20 behavior-specific evals with deterministic assertions, running in under two minutes on every prompt or model change, with at least three evals traceable to real past incidents.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Unit evals in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Regression evals

Regression evals compare a candidate change against the current baseline on the same fixed dataset — prompt edits, model upgrades, retrieval changes — to answer the only pre-ship question that matters: did anything that used to work stop working?

When it goes wrong

The model upgrade improved the average score by two points, so it shipped. Hidden inside: refund-policy answers regressed badly. Averages don't have a conscience — only a per-example diff would have shown the eight cases that flipped.

Why this matters

Regression evals matter because AI changes have invisible blast radii: nothing in a prompt diff or model changelog tells you which working behaviors just broke — only paired comparison on fixed inputs does.

In plain terms

It is like before-and-after lab tests around a medication change: the doctor doesn't ask whether the new numbers are abstractly good, but whether they moved — and refuses to change two prescriptions at once, because then nobody knows which one moved them.

How it works

The flow: freeze the dataset and settings → run baseline and candidate → diff per example → re-run flips to separate signal from sampling noise → review regressed examples by hand → apply the pre-agreed ship/block policy → the shipped change becomes the new baseline.

  • The comparison is paired, not absolute: run baseline and candidate on identical inputs with identical settings, and diff per-example — aggregate scores can stay flat while 8 examples break and 8 different ones improve, and the per-example diff is where the truth lives.
  • Regressions hide inside improvements: the prompt change that lifts average helpfulness by 3 points and breaks refund-policy answers is the classic case — which is why regression review means reading the flipped examples, not just the summary row.
  • One variable at a time: change the model and the prompt together and a regression is unattributable — disciplined teams stage changes (model first at fixed prompt, then prompt) or accept that debugging a mixed change costs more than the staging did.
  • Statistical honesty at small scale: with 60 examples and sampling nondeterminism, a 2% dip may be noise — run flipped examples multiple times, demand consistency before declaring regression, and treat the golden set's size as a known confidence limit.
  • The verdict needs a policy: define in advance what blocks a ship (any regression on the 'critical' tagged subset; net negative beyond noise on the rest) — otherwise every regression review becomes a negotiation the deadline wins.

A concrete example

A team upgrades to a new model version. Aggregate golden-set score: +2. Per-example diff: 6 improvements, 4 regressions — all 4 in multi-condition policy questions where the new model answers confidently instead of asking a clarifying question. Three re-runs confirm the flip is stable. Policy says the 'policy-critical' tag blocks: the upgrade ships with a prompt adjustment restoring clarифying behavior, verified by re-diff. Total process: one afternoon. The alternative was discovering it via a customer's compliance team.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Rigor versus release velocity — full regression discipline on every tiny edit is heavy, so teams tier it: fast unit evals on every edit, full regression runs on model changes, prompt-meaning changes, and anything touching tagged-critical behavior.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design the regression gate for one AI feature: the fixed dataset and settings, the per-example diff format, the noise-handling rule (how many re-runs make a flip real), the tagged-critical subset, and the written policy for what blocks a ship.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-shaped slice: a repeatable baseline-vs-candidate runner producing a per-example diff, a tagged critical subset with block authority, and a written ship policy — exercised at least once on a real model upgrade.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A better average is not a safe change; means hide bimodal damage, and the per-example flip list is the actual regression report.
  • Regression evals are not only for model swaps; one-line prompt edits move refusal rates and formats — behavioral size and diff size are unrelated in AI systems.
  • A red regression eval is not always a blocker; the world changes and some 'regressions' are corrections — but that call gets made by a human reading the example, never by rounding the aggregate.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Regression evals?

  • Once Regression evals is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A better average is not a safe change; means hide bimodal damage, and the per-example flip list is the actual regression report.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Regression evals into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: A production-shaped slice: a repeatable baseline-vs-candidate runner producing a per-example diff, a tagged critical subset with block authority, and a written ship policy — exercised at least once on a real model upgrade.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Regression evals in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

LLM-as-judge
