  • OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications

Agent attacks

Agent attacks exploit autonomous planning, tool access, memory, or approvals to make agents take harmful actions.

In plain terms

They are like social engineering a contractor who can access many systems.

How it works

In practice it comes down to goal hijacking, tool misuse, memory poisoning, approval spoofing, runaway loops, and privilege escalation.

A concrete example

A malicious issue comment convinces a repo agent to exfiltrate environment variables through a tool call.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Autonomy improves productivity but increases compound risk.

Try it in code

function enforcePolicy({ input, output, toolCall }) {
  return {
    inputAllowed: !containsPromptInjection(input),
    outputAllowed: hasNoPII(output) && hasCitations(output),
    toolAllowed: toolCall ? isLeastPrivilege(toolCall) : true
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Threat-model an agent with code search, file edit, and deployment tools.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not let agents read untrusted instructions and perform privileged actions in the same loop without controls.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Agent attacks?

  • Correct answer: Do not let agents read untrusted instructions and perform privileged actions in the same loop without controls.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Agent attacks is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Agent attacks in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

  • OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications

Secrets

Secrets are credentials, tokens, keys, or private material that must never be exposed to models, logs, users, or untrusted tools.

In plain terms

They are like master keys: useful only when tightly controlled.

How it works

In practice it comes down to secret scanning, vaults, redaction, environment isolation, least privilege, and output filtering.

A concrete example

A coding assistant should not paste an API key from a .env file into chat or logs.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Restricting secret access improves safety but may require better tool design.

Try it in code

function enforcePolicy({ input, output, toolCall }) {
  return {
    inputAllowed: !containsPromptInjection(input),
    outputAllowed: hasNoPII(output) && hasCitations(output),
    toolAllowed: toolCall ? isLeastPrivilege(toolCall) : true
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Add secret-handling rules for a local coding assistant workflow.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not rely on the model to decide what is secret.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Secrets?

  • Once Secrets is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not rely on the model to decide what is secret.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Secrets in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications

Identity

Identity controls who the user, agent, tool, and service are so permissions can be enforced correctly.

In plain terms

It is like badges for humans and service accounts for software.

How it works

In practice it comes down to authentication, authorization, delegation, service principals, scoped tokens, and audit attribution.

A concrete example

An AI assistant may read only documents the current user can access, not everything the backend service can see.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Delegated identity improves least privilege but complicates integrations.

Try it in code

function enforcePolicy({ input, output, toolCall }) {
  return {
    inputAllowed: !containsPromptInjection(input),
    outputAllowed: hasNoPII(output) && hasCitations(output),
    toolAllowed: toolCall ? isLeastPrivilege(toolCall) : true
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design identity propagation for a RAG assistant querying private documents.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not run all AI tool calls under one omnipotent service account.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Identity?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Identity is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not run all AI tool calls under one omnipotent service account.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Identity in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications

Sandboxing

Sandboxing isolates AI-generated code, tools, or file operations so failures and attacks cannot escape into sensitive systems.

In plain terms

It is like testing a chemical reaction inside a fume hood.

How it works

In practice it comes down to containers, filesystem isolation, network policies, resource limits, timeouts, and disposable environments.

A concrete example

A code agent runs untrusted tests in a container with no production credentials and a capped CPU budget.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Stronger sandboxes reduce risk but can make legitimate tasks harder.

Try it in code

function enforcePolicy({ input, output, toolCall }) {
  return {
    inputAllowed: !containsPromptInjection(input),
    outputAllowed: hasNoPII(output) && hasCitations(output),
    toolAllowed: toolCall ? isLeastPrivilege(toolCall) : true
  };
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not execute model-generated code on the host machine with developer credentials.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Sandboxing?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not execute model-generated code on the host machine with developer credentials.
  • Once Sandboxing is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Sandboxing in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
