Governance lab

Governance lab is a hands-on exercise where you design a governed AI workflow with policy-as-code, identity, shields, evidence, cost limits, and dashboard metrics.

What you're building, and why

Governance lab matters because enterprise AI fails when controls are scattered across prompts, tools, teams, and dashboards with no shared enforcement model.

The internal flow is classify the use case, verify identity, apply policy, enforce the shield, collect evidence, monitor behavior, and report status to owners.

Think of this layer as part of a zero-trust AI control plane: every request, model, tool, document, agent, and user action must be policy-aware, identity-aware, observable, and auditable.

The brief

Build a governance plan for one AI assistant including policies, shields, logs, owner, budget, and launch gate.

What your build must include

  • use-case intake: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • risk tiering: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • policy-as-code: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • identity boundary: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • shield checks: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • audit event: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • budget rule: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • approval gate: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • dashboard metric: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.

A worked reference

The lab governs an internal RAG assistant by requiring ACL-filtered retrieval, prompt injection checks, approved model routing, audit logs, and executive risk metrics.

Starting point in code

const aiGovernancePolicy = {
  control: "Governance lab",
  defaultDecision: "deny",
  appliesTo: ["prompts", "retrieval", "tools", "models", "agents", "apis"],
  requiredEvidence: ["identity", "policy_version", "risk_score", "audit_event"],
  allow(request) {
    return request.identity.verified &&
      request.riskScore <= request.policy.maxRisk &&
      request.auditEventWritten === true;
  }
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Complete coverage vs useful scope; start with one governed workflow and expand once controls are proven.

Definition of done

A production-shaped slice should implement one policy-as-code rule, one runtime enforcement point, one audit event, one owner approval path, and one dashboard metric for Governance lab.

Watch out for

  • Governance is not a PDF policy; it must become executable controls, defaults, evidence, and dashboards.
  • Security, compliance, cost, and adoption cannot be managed separately once AI systems connect to data and tools.
  • Do not wait until after deployment to define ownership, evidence, and rollback.

Going deeper

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework
  • ISO/IEC 42001 AI management system
  • EU AI Act overview

Capstone

The AI governance strategy capstone designs a complete enterprise governance platform across policy, security shields, data protection, models, agents, APIs, compliance, operations, analytics, and administration.

What you're building, and why

Capstone matters because enterprise AI fails when controls are scattered across prompts, tools, teams, and dashboards with no shared enforcement model.

The internal flow is classify the use case, verify identity, apply policy, enforce the shield, collect evidence, monitor behavior, and report status to owners.

Think of this layer as part of a zero-trust AI control plane: every request, model, tool, document, agent, and user action must be policy-aware, identity-aware, observable, and auditable.

The brief

Create a three-phase roadmap for an enterprise AI governance platform: minimum viable control plane, shield expansion, and executive operating model.

What your build must include

  • governance framework: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • AI shields: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • data protection: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • model registry: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • agent controls: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • API gateway: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • compliance evidence: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • runtime protection: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • cost governance: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • audit logs: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • AIOps: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • executive dashboard: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.

A worked reference

A capstone architecture shows how a global enterprise governs customer support AI, coding assistants, internal RAG, and autonomous agents with one control plane.

Starting point in code

const aiGovernancePolicy = {
  control: "Capstone",
  defaultDecision: "deny",
  appliesTo: ["prompts", "retrieval", "tools", "models", "agents", "apis"],
  requiredEvidence: ["identity", "policy_version", "risk_score", "audit_event"],
  allow(request) {
    return request.identity.verified &&
      request.riskScore <= request.policy.maxRisk &&
      request.auditEventWritten === true;
  }
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Enterprise completeness vs implementation sequencing; a roadmap must deliver useful controls in phases.

Definition of done

A production-shaped slice should implement one policy-as-code rule, one runtime enforcement point, one audit event, one owner approval path, and one dashboard metric for Capstone.

Watch out for

  • Governance is not a PDF policy; it must become executable controls, defaults, evidence, and dashboards.
  • Security, compliance, cost, and adoption cannot be managed separately once AI systems connect to data and tools.
  • Do not wait until after deployment to define ownership, evidence, and rollback.

  • ISO/IEC 42001 AI management system
  • EU AI Act overview

Interview pack

The AI governance strategy interview pack prepares you to explain enterprise AI governance, security shields, policy-as-code, compliance, audit, and executive metrics.

Why this matters

Interview pack matters because enterprise AI fails when controls are scattered across prompts, tools, teams, and dashboards with no shared enforcement model.

In plain terms

Think of this layer as part of a zero-trust AI control plane: every request, model, tool, document, agent, and user action must be policy-aware, identity-aware, observable, and auditable.

How it works

The internal flow is classify the use case, verify identity, apply policy, enforce the shield, collect evidence, monitor behavior, and report status to owners.

  • governance architecture questions: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • risk and compliance answers: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • AI security shield scenarios: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • agent governance examples: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • dashboard KPI discussion: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.
  • trade-off stories: define the policy, owner, enforcement point, evidence record, and failure response.

A concrete example

A strong interview answer explains how to stop shadow AI adoption without blocking useful engineering workflows.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const starAnswer = {
  situation: "Set the context in one sentence.",
  task: "State your responsibility and success criteria.",
  action: "Explain the decisions you personally made.",
  result: "Quantify the outcome and what you learned."
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare five governance interview answers: policy-as-code, RAG shield, model registry, agent approval, and executive dashboard.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Governance is not a PDF policy; it must become executable controls, defaults, evidence, and dashboards.
  • Security, compliance, cost, and adoption cannot be managed separately once AI systems connect to data and tools.
  • Do not wait until after deployment to define ownership, evidence, and rollback.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Governance is not a PDF policy; it must become executable controls, defaults, evidence, and dashboards.

Check yourself 2 of 2

Which preparation approach actually improves interview performance?

  • Correct answer: A production-shaped slice should implement one policy-as-code rule, one runtime enforcement point, one audit event, one owner approval path, and one dashboard metric for Interview pack.
  • Memorize polished answers word-for-word so nothing unexpected can happen in the room.
  • Skip mock practice — real interviews are the most efficient form of practice.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework
  • ISO/IEC 42001 AI management system
  • EU AI Act overview

AI in SDLC

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: AI in SDLC is not just adding a smart power tool to an old workshop; it is redesigning how the workshop operates so AI has clear stations, rules, owners, and inspection gates. Efficient usage, responsible AI, and adoption only work when the daily SDLC changes: where AI is allowed, who owns the output, how quality is checked, how cost is controlled, and when humans must approve.

AI operating model

An AI operating model defines how AI changes day-to-day software delivery: where AI is used in the SDLC, who owns AI-assisted output, which quality gates must pass, how cost is controlled, and how adoption is measured.

Why this matters

The AI operating model matters because bolting AI onto old habits creates messy ownership, unreviewed output, rising cost, uneven adoption, and quality risk.

In plain terms

It is like changing factory operations after adding robots. The robots may improve speed, but the real gain comes from redesigning stations, safety rules, inspection, ownership, and training.

How it works

The internal flow is policy and use-case selection, SDLC stage mapping, ownership assignment, AI-assisted work, quality gates, cost monitoring, adoption measurement, and continuous improvement.

  • AI adoption is not a tool rollout. It changes requirements, design, coding, testing, reviews, security, deployment, monitoring, and incident response.
  • Every AI-assisted handoff needs an owner. The human or team that accepts the output owns correctness, security, compliance, and maintainability.
  • Quality gates must move earlier: acceptance criteria, test plans, threat models, evals, and review checklists should exist before AI generates large changes.
  • Cost must be managed as an operating constraint with approved tools, model routing, token budgets, usage dashboards, and escalation rules.
  • Responsible AI and efficient usage follow from workflow design: clear rules, evidence, review, traceability, and adoption metrics.

Topic diagram

Diagram: AI operating model for SDLC for AI operating model

A concrete example

Instead of letting every developer use AI however they want, a team defines: AI can draft requirements but product owners approve them; AI can generate code but engineers own tests and review; AI can summarize incidents but SREs own actions; expensive model use needs a budget and observable business value.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Speed vs control: teams want faster delivery, but durable acceleration requires explicit rules for responsibility, review, observability, and escalation.

Try it in code

const aiOperatingModel = {
  allowedUse: {
    requirements: "draft and critique, product owner approves",
    code: "generate scoped changes, engineer owns correctness",
    tests: "suggest cases, CI is mandatory",
    review: "risk triage, human reviewer decides"
  },
  qualityGates: ["acceptance criteria", "tests", "security review", "evals", "traceable owner"],
  costControls: ["approved tools", "token budget", "model routing", "usage dashboard"],
  adoptionSignals: ["cycle time", "defect escape rate", "review load", "developer satisfaction"]
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Choose one team workflow, such as PR review or test generation. Write the AI operating model for it: allowed AI use, owner, required checks, cost guardrail, adoption metric, and stop condition.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-shaped slice should define one SDLC workflow, where AI is allowed, who owns each output, what checks must pass, which metrics prove value, and what stops unsafe or expensive usage.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • AI in SDLC is not just buying Copilot, Claude Code, or Codex seats.
  • Efficiency, responsible AI, and adoption do not sustain themselves if the underlying workflow still has unclear ownership and weak quality gates.
  • AI-generated work is not ownerless; the accepting team owns the result.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about AI operating model?

  • Correct answer: AI in SDLC is not just buying Copilot, Claude Code, or Codex seats.
  • Once AI operating model is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing AI operating model into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: A production-shaped slice should define one SDLC workflow, where AI is allowed, who owns each output, what checks must pass, which metrics prove value, and what stops unsafe or expensive usage.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain AI operating model in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework
  • GitHub Copilot Trust Center

AI requirements

AI requirements define what AI may do inside a product or delivery workflow, what evidence proves success, what risks are unacceptable, and who owns the final decision.

In plain terms

It is like writing a job description and operating policy before hiring an AI assistant into the team.

How it works

In practice it comes down to problem framing, allowed AI use, acceptance criteria, risk classification, data boundaries, owner assignment, and measurable success.

A concrete example

A story that says 'use AI to answer support tickets' becomes requirements for allowed sources, citation behavior, escalation rules, latency, cost, and human ownership.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Detailed AI requirements slow initial drafting but prevent expensive rework and unsafe ambiguity later.

Try it in code

const aiRequirements = {
  useCase: "AI drafts support-ticket replies",
  allowedSources: ["approved knowledge base"],
  ownerApproval: "product owner signs off before launch",
  qualityGate: "recall@5 >= 0.85 on golden eval set",
  costGuardrail: "$0.02 per reply, alert above",
  stopCondition: "no source found -> escalate, never guess"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Rewrite one vague AI feature idea into requirements with allowed use, owner, quality gate, cost guardrail, and stop condition.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not write AI requirements as vague capability wishes; make ownership, checks, and failure behavior explicit.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about AI requirements?

  • Correct answer: Do not write AI requirements as vague capability wishes; make ownership, checks, and failure behavior explicit.
  • Once AI requirements is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain AI requirements in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework
  • GitHub Copilot Trust Center

Design reviews

AI-era design reviews check not only architecture, but also where AI enters the workflow, what it is allowed to change, how evidence is verified, and how humans retain accountability.

In plain terms

It is like reviewing a bridge design after adding autonomous construction equipment: the equipment plan is part of the safety review.

How it works

In practice it comes down to AI touchpoints, data flow, prompt/tool boundaries, ownership, eval strategy, cost controls, security risks, and rollback.

A concrete example

A design review for AI-generated tests asks who approves tests, how flaky cases are handled, whether generated code can touch auth, and which CI gates block merge.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More AI assistance can reduce cycle time but increases review scope and traceability needs.

Try it in code

const designReviewChecklist = [
  "Where does AI enter this workflow, and what can it change?",
  "Who owns the accepted output if it's wrong?",
  "What evidence (tests, evals, logs) proves a change is safe?",
  "Which CI gates block merge for AI-generated changes?",
  "What is the rollback path if this goes wrong in production?"
];

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create a design-review checklist for an AI-assisted coding workflow.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not approve designs that say 'AI will handle it' without contracts, logs, and failure modes.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Design reviews?

  • Once Design reviews is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not approve designs that say 'AI will handle it' without contracts, logs, and failure modes.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Design reviews in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework
  • GitHub Copilot Trust Center

AI coding

AI coding uses coding assistants or agents to generate, modify, explain, or refactor code under explicit scope, ownership, review, and validation rules.

In plain terms

It is like pair-programming with a fast junior engineer who needs clear tasks and careful review.

How it works

In practice it comes down to task scope, context selection, generated diff, tests, review ownership, security checks, and rollback.

A concrete example

A developer asks an agent to add one API field, reviews the diff, runs targeted tests, checks security impact, and owns the merged code.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: AI speeds implementation but can produce plausible code that violates architecture or hidden constraints.

Try it in code

const aiCodingPolicy = {
  allowedTasks: ["scoped bug fixes", "test generation", "refactors with named files"],
  forbiddenFiles: ["auth/**", "billing/**", "*.env"],
  requiredBeforeMerge: ["tests pass", "human review", "no unrelated file changes"],
  reviewerOwnership: "accepting engineer owns correctness, not the tool"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Define an AI coding policy for one repository: allowed tasks, forbidden files, required tests, and reviewer responsibilities.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not merge AI-generated code because it looks correct; require tests and human ownership.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about AI coding?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not merge AI-generated code because it looks correct; require tests and human ownership.
  • Once AI coding is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain AI coding in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Claude Code best practices
  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework
  • GitHub Copilot Trust Center

Testing

AI-assisted testing uses AI to propose, generate, prioritize, or explain tests while the team keeps deterministic CI and coverage gates as the source of truth.

In plain terms

It is like having an assistant suggest edge cases, but the test suite still has to run in the real lab.

How it works

In practice it comes down to acceptance criteria, generated test cases, edge-case discovery, deterministic CI, flaky-test handling, coverage gaps, and ownership.

A concrete example

AI suggests tests for refund edge cases, but CI, code review, and product acceptance decide whether the test is valid.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: AI can broaden test ideas quickly but may generate brittle or irrelevant tests.

Try it in code

const generatedTests = await agent.generateTests("refundEdgeCases");
const classified = generatedTests.map(t => ({
  test: t,
  verdict: classify(t) // "useful" | "duplicate" | "brittle" | "invalid"
}));
// Only "useful" tests count toward real coverage.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Use AI to generate five edge tests for a feature, then classify each as useful, duplicate, brittle, or invalid.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not count generated tests as quality unless they fail for the right reason and pass in CI.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Testing?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Testing is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not count generated tests as quality unless they fail for the right reason and pass in CI.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Testing in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Claude Code common workflows
  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework
  • GitHub Copilot Trust Center

Security reviews

AI-era security reviews inspect AI-generated changes, prompts, tools, dependencies, data flows, and model interactions for exploitable risk before release.

In plain terms

It is like checking both the building and the robot that helped build it.

How it works

In practice it comes down to threat modeling, secrets, dependency risk, prompt injection, tool permissions, generated code review, and audit evidence.

A concrete example

A security review blocks an AI-generated helper that logs full prompts containing customer secrets.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: AI can surface security issues but can also introduce hidden ones at high speed.

Try it in code

const agentPermissionReview = {
  repoAccess: "read + scoped write (src/** only)",
  secretsAccess: "denied — no .env, no credentials store",
  networkAccess: "denied by default, allowlist only",
  promptLogging: "redact any customer PII before logging",
  reviewFinding: "block: helper logs full prompt including customer email"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a security-review checklist for an AI coding agent with repo read, file edit, and test-run permissions.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not let AI tools access secrets or production data without explicit controls.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Security reviews?

  • Correct answer: Do not let AI tools access secrets or production data without explicit controls.
  • Once Security reviews is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Security reviews in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications
  • Claude Code security
  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework
  • GitHub Copilot Trust Center

Evaluation

Evaluation in AI-assisted SDLC measures whether AI is improving delivery outcomes without increasing defects, risk, cost, or review burden.

In plain terms

It is like measuring factory throughput and defect rate after adding automation, not just counting how often the robot moved.

How it works

In practice it comes down to cycle time, defect escape rate, review quality, test pass rate, rework, cost, adoption, and developer experience.

A concrete example

A team tracks whether AI-generated PRs reduce cycle time while maintaining escaped-defect and review-comment quality thresholds.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Efficiency metrics can hide quality regressions if measured alone.

Try it in code

const sdlcScorecard = {
  cycleTime: "-18% vs baseline",
  defectEscapeRate: "unchanged (0.4%)",
  reviewCommentVolume: "+5% (more scrutiny, not less)",
  costPerMergedPr: "$1.20",
  adoption: "62% of PRs used AI assistance"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create a scorecard with speed, quality, cost, risk, and adoption metrics for an AI-assisted workflow.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not measure AI adoption only by seats purchased or prompts sent.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Evaluation?

  • Once Evaluation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not measure AI adoption only by seats purchased or prompts sent.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Evaluation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

  • Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena
  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework
  • GitHub Copilot Trust Center

Deployment

AI-assisted deployment defines how AI-generated or AI-operated changes reach environments through release gates, approvals, observability, and rollback rules.

In plain terms

It is like letting an assistant prepare a launch checklist, while humans and automation still control the launch button.

How it works

In practice it comes down to release gates, generated-change provenance, deployment approvals, smoke tests, rollout strategy, monitoring, and rollback.

A concrete example

An AI agent can draft deployment notes and run preflight checks, but production rollout requires CI, owner approval, and a rollback plan.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Automation speeds release but raises blast radius if approval boundaries are weak.

Try it in code

const deploymentGate = {
  requiredChecks: ["tests pass", "security scan", "reviewer approval"],
  approver: "named on-call engineer",
  smokeTest: "known-good request returns expected response",
  rollbackTrigger: "error rate > 2x baseline within 10 minutes"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a deployment gate for AI-assisted changes: required checks, approver, smoke test, and rollback trigger.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not let AI perform irreversible deployment actions without explicit approval and trace logs.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Deployment?

  • Correct answer: Do not let AI perform irreversible deployment actions without explicit approval and trace logs.
  • Once Deployment is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Deployment in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework
  • GitHub Copilot Trust Center

Monitoring

Monitoring for AI in SDLC tracks how AI-assisted work affects delivery health, quality, security, cost, and adoption after teams start using it.

In plain terms

It is like adding a dashboard after changing the factory workflow, not just after installing the tool.

How it works

In practice it comes down to usage telemetry, cost, cycle time, review load, incident rate, defect escape, policy violations, and satisfaction.

A concrete example

A dashboard shows AI-assisted PR volume, test failures, review comments, model/tool spend, and escaped defects by team.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More usage is not automatically better; adoption must correlate with better outcomes.

Try it in code

const sdlcDashboard = {
  aiAssistedPrVolume: "daily",
  testFailureRate: "daily",
  reviewCommentsPerPr: "weekly",
  modelToolSpend: "daily",
  escapedDefectsByTeam: "weekly"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Choose five metrics that prove AI is helping your SDLC rather than just being used.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not monitor AI tool usage without monitoring quality and cost impact.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Monitoring?

  • Once Monitoring is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not monitor AI tool usage without monitoring quality and cost impact.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

  1. Explain Monitoring in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.

Continuous eval

Continuous eval keeps AI-assisted SDLC workflows honest by repeatedly testing prompts, agents, tools, and policies against known delivery scenarios.

In plain terms

It is like regression testing the way the team uses AI, not just the product code.

How it works

In practice it comes down to golden workflow cases, policy tests, tool-call checks, generated-code checks, cost thresholds, and drift alerts.

A concrete example

A weekly eval verifies that an agent still refuses to edit auth code without approval and still generates valid test plans.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Continuous eval improves trust but requires maintaining realistic scenarios.

Try it in code

const workflowEvalCases = [
  { name: "allowed change", input: "fix typo in README", expect: "proceeds autonomously" },
  { name: "forbidden change", input: "edit auth/login.ts", expect: "blocked, requires human approval" },
  { name: "ambiguous change", input: "improve the API", expect: "asks for clarification, does not guess" }
];

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create three eval cases for an AI coding assistant: allowed change, forbidden change, and ambiguous change requiring clarification.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not assume an AI workflow remains safe after tool, prompt, model, or repo changes.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Continuous eval?

  • Correct answer: Do not assume an AI workflow remains safe after tool, prompt, model, or repo changes.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Continuous eval is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

Copilot workflows

Copilot workflows define how developers use GitHub Copilot in IDE, chat, agent, and review modes while preserving ownership, tests, and review standards.

In plain terms

It is like giving every developer a faster pair programmer with team rules for when to ask, accept, test, and reject suggestions.

How it works

In practice it comes down to IDE suggestions, chat prompts, agent tasks, code review, context boundaries, tests, and team standards.

A concrete example

A team allows Copilot to draft unit tests and refactors but requires developers to run tests and explain nontrivial accepted changes in PRs.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Copilot improves flow but can normalize unreviewed code acceptance if teams lack standards.

Try it in code

const copilotTeamGuide = {
  allowedUse: ["IDE completions", "chat for questions", "draft unit tests"],
  reviewRule: "explain any non-trivial accepted suggestion in the PR description",
  validationCommand: "npm run test:ci",
  neverAllowed: "accepting a suggestion without running it locally first"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a Copilot workflow guide for one team including allowed use, review rule, and validation command.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not treat accepted suggestions as exempt from normal engineering review.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Copilot workflows?

  • Correct answer: Do not treat accepted suggestions as exempt from normal engineering review.
  • Once Copilot workflows is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Copilot workflows in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

  • GitHub Copilot documentation
  • GitHub Copilot Trust Center
  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework

Claude Code workflows

Claude Code workflows define how teams assign scoped repository tasks to an agent, review its plan and diff, run validation, and preserve human accountability.

In plain terms

It is like delegating a ticket to an assistant who can edit files, but only within a clear contract.

How it works

In practice it comes down to task prompt, repository context, file scope, plan review, diff review, validation commands, and rollback.

A concrete example

A team asks Claude Code to refactor one module, then reviews changed files, runs tests, and rejects unrelated edits.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Agentic coding handles larger tasks but increases risk of broad, hard-to-review changes.

Try it in code

claude "Refactor the payments module to use the new pricing API.
Only touch src/payments/**. Run npm test before and after.
Stop and ask if any test outside this module starts failing."

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create a Claude Code task prompt with scope, constraints, validation command, and stop condition.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not give vague multi-system tasks without file scope and acceptance criteria.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Claude Code workflows?

  • Once Claude Code workflows is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not give vague multi-system tasks without file scope and acceptance criteria.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

  1. Explain Claude Code workflows in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.

Codex workflows

Codex workflows define how autonomous coding tasks are specified, constrained, reviewed, validated, and merged safely.

In plain terms

It is like creating a work order for an automated contractor that must return evidence, not just a patch.

How it works

In practice it comes down to task description, repo setup, constraints, generated diff, test output, review notes, and iteration loop.

A concrete example

A Codex-style task implements a small bug fix, reports changed files, includes test output, and asks for clarification when requirements conflict.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Autonomous implementation can save time but needs tight acceptance criteria and review.

Try it in code

codex "Fix the null pointer in checkout.ts line 88 when cart is
empty. Add a guard clause and a regression test. Report changed
files and test output. Ask if requirements are unclear."

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a Codex task for a bug fix with exact files, expected behavior, and validation.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not ask an agent to 'improve the codebase' without measurable success criteria.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Codex workflows?

  • Once Codex workflows is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not ask an agent to 'improve the codebase' without measurable success criteria.

Team enablement

Team enablement turns AI adoption into a managed capability through training, standards, champions, support channels, metrics, and governance.

In plain terms

It is like rolling out a new engineering practice, not just installing a new app.

How it works

In practice it comes down to training paths, usage standards, champions, office hours, policy, metrics, onboarding, and feedback loops.

A concrete example

A team creates AI coding playbooks, runs prompt clinics, reviews usage dashboards, and updates policy based on incidents and developer feedback.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Central enablement improves consistency but must adapt to different team workflows.

Try it in code

const enablementPlan = {
  week1: "training session + shared AGENTS.md / copilot-instructions.md",
  week2: "office hours, collect friction points",
  week3: "publish usage dashboard, review policy based on incidents",
  week4: "retro: what changed, what to adjust"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not measure enablement by license activation alone.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Team enablement?

  • Once Team enablement is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not measure enablement by license activation alone.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
