  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Incident response

AI incident response extends on-call discipline to failures that don't look like outages: quality regressions, prompt injections, cost runaways, and upstream model changes — with runbooks, mitigations, and postmortems shaped for statistical systems.

When it goes wrong

The model started refusing all legal questions at 2am. No deploy, no code change — an upstream model update. AI incidents don't look like outages; your runbook has to know that.

Why this matters

AI incidents hide inside normal operations: green infrastructure, confident answers, and a slowly burning quality or safety fire — response discipline built for outages misses them without AI-shaped detection and playbooks.

In plain terms

It is like food-safety response versus fire response: a fire is loud and obvious; contamination is quiet, spreads through normal operations, and is detected by testing — both need drills, but the playbooks are completely different.

How it works

The flow: detect via quality/cost/safety signals → classify by incident type → apply the class's mitigation lever (prompt rollback, model pin, guardrail tighten, fallback flip) → verify on evals → communicate honestly → postmortem on time-to-detect and the missing eval or alert.

  • The AI incident taxonomy is broader: besides downtime, there are quality incidents (answers got worse), safety incidents (injection succeeded, harmful output shipped), cost incidents (runaway loops, cache failure doubling spend), and data incidents (PII in traces, index leak across tenants) — each needs its own detection and playbook.
  • Detection is the hard half: quality incidents announce themselves through drifting distributions, rising thumbs-down, or a support-ticket theme — hours or days after onset — which is why quality alerts, drift monitors, and eval runs are incident-response infrastructure, not analytics.
  • AI mitigations are their own toolbox: roll back the prompt (not the code), pin the previous model version, disable a tool, tighten a guardrail threshold, flip to the fallback chain, pause a feature flag — the runbook lists which lever fits which incident class, because 3am is not when you want to derive this.
  • External root causes are normal: a provider model update at 2am changes behavior with no deploy on your side — your change log must include provider events, and your vendor relationship needs an incident channel, because 'nothing changed on our end' is routinely false in AI systems.
  • Postmortems ask AI-shaped questions: how long did the regression run before detection (time-to-detect is the metric to drive down), which eval would have caught it pre-ship, and which alert was missing — the action item is usually a new eval or a new signal, not just a code fix.

A concrete example

2am: the assistant starts refusing all questions containing 'legal' — no deploy, no config change. The on-call's AI runbook: check provider status and model-version log (update detected), pin the previous model version via gateway config (12 minutes), verify on the eval set, banner the degradation. Morning postmortem: time-to-detect was 90 minutes (refusal-rate alert worked), time-to-mitigate 12 (version pinning worked), action items — add refusal-by-topic drift alert, add the 'legal' eval slice to the pre-adoption suite for model updates.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Sensitive detection catches incidents early and pages people for noise — burn-rate-style alerting on quality signals and severity tiers keep the pager honest without going blind.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write the on-call runbook entry for two AI incident classes (quality regression, cost runaway): the detection signal, the first three diagnostic checks, the mitigation lever with exact commands or config, and the verification step that confirms recovery.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: Production AI incident readiness: runbooks per incident class with tested mitigation levers, provider events in the change timeline, quality alerts wired to on-call, and postmortems that ship a new eval or signal every time detection was late.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • 'No deploy happened' does not mean no change happened; provider-side model updates are a routine root cause and belong in your change log.
  • Rollback is not one thing in AI systems; code, prompt, model version, index snapshot, and guardrail config roll back independently — knowing which lever is the runbook's job.
  • A quality regression is not a bug ticket; running for days undetected at scale, it does more damage than most outages — it is an incident with severity, timeline, and postmortem.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Incident response?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Incident response is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: 'No deploy happened' does not mean no change happened; provider-side model updates are a routine root cause and belong in your change log.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Incident response into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: Production AI incident readiness: runbooks per incident class with tested mitigation levers, provider events in the change timeline, quality alerts wired to on-call, and postmortems that ship a new eval or signal every time detection was late.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Incident response in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Release strategy

Release strategy for AI systems governs how changes — prompts, models, indexes, guardrails, and code — reach users: eval gates before, staged rollouts during, and instant rollback after, because AI changes fail in ways diffs can't show.

When it goes wrong

The new prompt was 'obviously better.' Rolled to 100%, it broke a workflow used by your largest customer. Canaries and staged rollouts exist because AI changes don't behave like code changes.

Why this matters

AI changes are behavioral, not mechanical: the diff looks trivial, evals look good, and the regression appears only under real traffic — release discipline (gates, stages, rollback) is what makes iteration speed compatible with production trust.

In plain terms

It is like drug release, not software release: the change 'looks right' in the lab, but the only proof is controlled trials on real populations with monitoring — and a recall path when the trial says no.

How it works

The flow: version every behavioral artifact → eval gate in CI → shadow or 1% canary → staged expansion with quality signals per stage → full rollout → instant type-appropriate rollback on regression → the incident feeds a new eval slice.

  • The release surface is wider than code: prompt edits, model version adoptions, embedding/index updates, guardrail threshold changes, and RAG corpus updates all change behavior in production — each needs versioning, review, and a rollback path, or your 'no deploys today' claim is false daily.
  • Evals are the CI gate: every behavior-affecting change runs the eval suite pre-release — quality, safety probes, format validity, cost deltas — and a red suite blocks the ship exactly like failing tests, which requires the suite to exist and be trusted.
  • Staged rollout is non-negotiable for AI: offline evals miss real-traffic effects, so changes ship to 1% → 10% → 50% → 100% with quality signals (not just errors) watched at each stage — the 'obviously better' prompt that breaks one big customer's workflow is caught at 1%, or at 100%.
  • A/B testing answers what evals can't: user-level outcomes (task completion, retention, escalation rates) need controlled comparison on live traffic — shadow mode (new version runs silently beside old) derisks the riskiest changes before anyone sees them.
  • Rollback must match the change type: prompt rollback is config, model rollback is a gateway pin, index rollback needs snapshots — each tested, each fast, because a staged rollout without instant rollback is just staged damage.

A concrete example

A 'clearly better' prompt rewrite: evals pass (+3 points), ships to 1%. Quality dashboard at 1%: fine. At 10%: escalation rate up 40% for one enterprise tenant — the new prompt's tone breaks their configured workflow integration. Rollback via config in 4 minutes, tenant-specific eval slice added, re-ship next day with a tenant exemption. Total damage: one tenant, two hours, caught by the stage gate. The pre-strategy version of this story shipped to 100% on a Friday.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Gates and stages slow iteration on a technology whose whole promise is fast iteration — the resolution is proportionality: prompt tweaks ride a fast lane with auto-rollback, model swaps and guardrail changes take the full staircase.

Hands-on lab

Task: Define the release lanes for one AI product: which change types take which lane (fast lane vs full staircase), the eval gate contents per lane, the canary stages and the quality signals watched at each, and the tested rollback mechanism per change type.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production release setup: all five change types versioned and gated by evals, canary stages with quality (not just error) monitoring, tested rollback per change type, and a change log that includes prompts and model versions — the artifact incident response will ask for.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Passing evals does not mean ship to 100%; offline suites are necessary and insufficient — real traffic distribution is the final reviewer.
  • Prompt changes are not too small for process; one sentence in a system prompt can move refusal rates double digits — behavioral size and diff size are unrelated in AI.
  • Rollback-by-redeploy is not a rollback strategy for AI; by the time code redeploys, config-level prompt and model pins would have ended the incident twenty minutes earlier.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Release strategy?

  • Once Release strategy is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Passing evals does not mean ship to 100%; offline suites are necessary and insufficient — real traffic distribution is the final reviewer.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Release strategy into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: A production release setup: all five change types versioned and gated by evals, canary stages with quality (not just error) monitoring, tested rollback per change type, and a change log that includes prompts and model versions — the artifact incident response will ask for.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Release strategy in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Platform teams

An AI platform team builds and runs the shared machinery — gateway, evals, guardrails, observability, cost controls — so product teams ship AI features on paved roads instead of each rebuilding (and re-breaking) the same infrastructure.

When it goes wrong

Six product teams, six prompt-injection defenses, six eval frameworks — five of them abandoned. A platform team turns AI safety and quality from six hobbies into one product.

Why this matters

AI capability at organization scale is a platform problem: without shared infrastructure, every team pays full tuition, safety converges to the weakest implementation, and nobody can answer 'what is our AI spend and risk?'

In plain terms

It is like a city utility versus every building running its own generator: generators multiply cost, noise, and failure modes — the grid makes power boring, and boring is the point.

How it works

The flow: extract the duplicated needs (access, evals, safety, observability, cost) → build the paved road as self-service → drive adoption with speed, not mandates → hold the platform/product boundary explicitly → report leverage metrics that justify the investment.

  • The counterfactual is convergent duplication: six product teams independently build model access, retry logic, prompt storage, eval scripts, and injection defenses — five abandon maintaining them within a year, and the org's AI risk becomes the worst team's shortcuts.
  • The platform's product is leverage: gateway with budgets and routing, eval infrastructure teams can adopt in a day, guardrail defaults with product-specific tuning hooks, trace/observability schema, and a template that makes the paved road the fastest path — adoption is the KPI, mandates are the failure mode.
  • Platform teams run infrastructure, not review boards: if every AI feature needs the platform team's hands, the team is a bottleneck wearing a platform costume — self-service with defaults beats gatekeeping with meetings.
  • The boundary needs constant negotiation: model choice inside approved options is product-team territory; what's on the menu, spend policy, and security baselines are platform territory — drawn wrong in either direction it produces shadow AI or learned helplessness.
  • Platform earns its existence with evidence: time-to-ship for new AI features, incident rates on vs off the paved road, spend efficiency, audit readiness — measured and reported, because 'trust us, centralization is good' loses to the first team that wants to go around.

A concrete example

Year one, no platform: six teams, six injection defenses (five abandoned), three surprise five-figure invoices, one incident where a team's homegrown logging stored raw PII prompts for 90 days. Year two, a four-person platform team: gateway with per-team budgets, shared eval harness, guardrail defaults, trace schema. New-feature time-to-ship drops from ~8 weeks to ~2; the next provider incident is one gateway failover instead of six team scrambles; the security review has one architecture to audit instead of six.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Centralization buys consistency, safety, and cost control at the price of team autonomy and platform-team bottleneck risk — the healthy resolution is paved roads with escape hatches and a platform team that measures adoption instead of enforcing it.

Hands-on lab

Task: Inventory your organization's (or an imagined org's) duplicated AI infrastructure across teams. Propose the platform's first two quarters: the three components to build first, the adoption metric for each, and the boundary doc's answer to 'what do product teams still own?'

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production AI platform: a gateway most traffic actually flows through, eval and guardrail infrastructure with real adoption, published boundary docs, and a metrics page showing time-to-ship and incident deltas on vs off the road.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A platform team is not an AI governance committee; its output is running infrastructure and templates, and its authority comes from being genuinely faster to build on.
  • Centralization is not the opposite of team speed; the fastest teams in mature orgs are the ones that didn't have to build retry logic, evals, and injection defense before writing their feature.
  • One platform engineer embedded per team is not a platform; without shared ownership the 'platform' fragments back into six dialects with extra steps.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Platform teams?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A platform team is not an AI governance committee; its output is running infrastructure and templates, and its authority comes from being genuinely faster to build on.
  • Once Platform teams is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Platform teams into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: A production AI platform: a gateway most traffic actually flows through, eval and guardrail infrastructure with real adoption, published boundary docs, and a metrics page showing time-to-ship and incident deltas on vs off the road.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Platform teams in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Production lab

The production lab is a hands-on exercise to deploy a small AI service behind a gateway with rate limiting and basic observability, then load-test it and observe how it behaves under stress, connecting production-readiness theory to observed behavior.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to deploying a small AI-serving endpoint with a rate limiter and structured logging, then generating simulated concurrent load against it and observing latency, error rate, and rate-limit behavior under stress rather than assuming it would work.

It is like a fire drill for a system, deliberately creating a stressful condition to observe whether the safety mechanisms actually engage.

The brief

Deploy a small AI-serving endpoint with a rate limit, generate concurrent load exceeding that limit, and observe whether it degrades gracefully or fails in an unexpected way.

A worked reference

A lab might deploy a small model-serving endpoint with a rate limit of 10 requests per second, then send 50 concurrent requests and observe whether the rate limiter correctly rejects the excess rather than the service crashing or degrading silently.

Starting point in code

// Load test against a rate-limited endpoint
autocannon -c 50 -d 10 http://localhost:3000/summarize
// Expect: excess requests get 429s, not crashes or silent drops

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Mini project

The mini project ships a small, complete production-ready AI service, with an API gateway, rate limiting, observability, and a documented incident-response plan, that another engineer could operate.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a gateway with authentication and rate limiting, structured logging and basic dashboards, and a documented runbook for common failure scenarios into one reusable, operable service.

It is like building and documenting one small but genuinely production-grade service, in a way that could be handed to an on-call engineer who's never seen it before.

The brief

Deploy a small AI service with rate limiting and a dashboard, and write a one-page runbook for the most likely failure mode, then have a teammate use only the runbook to diagnose a simulated issue.

A worked reference

A mini project might deploy a small summarization API with rate limiting, a latency and error-rate dashboard, and a one-page runbook covering what to do if the model provider returns errors.

Starting point in code

export const summarizationService = {
  gateway: "rate limit 10 req/s per key",
  dashboard: ["p95 latency", "error rate"],
  runbook: "docs/runbook-model-provider-errors.md"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete production AI system architecture for a real product: gateway and resilience design, observability and SLOs, cost optimization, release strategy, and incident response, as one coherent operating system.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining an API gateway with rate limiting and resilience patterns, observability with defined SLOs, a cost-optimization plan, a phased release strategy, and a documented incident-response process into one complete production architecture.

It is like designing the complete operating manual for a factory, covering not just the production line but safety systems, quality monitoring, and emergency procedures.

The brief

Write a one-page production architecture for a real AI system: resilience and rate-limiting approach, one SLO, a release strategy, and an incident-response outline.

A worked reference

A capstone for a customer-facing AI assistant might specify a gateway with per-user rate limits, a 99.9 percent latency SLO with alerting, a caching strategy for cost optimization, a canary release process, and an incident-response runbook with defined severity levels.

Starting point in code

const productionArchitecture = {
  gateway: "per-user rate limits",
  slo: "99.9% requests < 2s",
  costOptimization: "response caching for repeated queries",
  releaseStrategy: "canary, 5% traffic first",
  incidentResponse: "runbook with severity levels"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Interview pack

The production AI systems interview pack prepares you to discuss operating AI systems reliably at scale: resilience patterns, SLOs, cost optimization, and incident response, backed by a concrete story about a real production incident or design decision.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a real operations team's actual incident history and process, not just describing the system's architecture diagram.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real production AI system you operated, scaled, or debugged, paired with technical answers distinguishing resilience patterns, SLO design, and incident-response process.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how would you make this AI system reliable at scale describes a specific rate-limiting and resilience approach, a concrete SLO, and a real or realistic incident-response story with what was learned and changed afterward.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "Model provider outage took down the whole feature",
  cause: "no fallback or circuit breaker existed",
  fix: "added a cached-response fallback + circuit breaker",
  result: "next provider outage degraded gracefully instead of failing hard"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a real or realistic production incident in an AI system, its root cause, how it was detected, and the specific process change made afterward.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing terms like SLO, rate limiting, and canary release is not the same as being able to describe how you'd actually set thresholds or respond to a real incident; interviewers probe for that applied operational judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • Correct answer: Knowing terms like SLO, rate limiting, and canary release is not the same as being able to describe how you'd actually set thresholds or respond to a real incident; interviewers probe for that applied operational judgment specifically.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Google SRE book

AWS AI Stack

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: The AWS AI stack is like a warehouse of managed building blocks: model access, storage, search, compute, identity, logs, and deployment lanes. Knowing the services helps you design secure and operable AI products without building every platform component yourself.

AWS overview

AWS AI architecture combines managed model services, compute, storage, search, identity, networking, and observability into production AI workflows.

In plain terms

It is like a toolbox where Bedrock, SageMaker, Lambda, S3, IAM, and CloudWatch each solve a different part of the system.

How it works

In practice it comes down to service selection, IAM boundaries, networking, data stores, model access, logging, and cost controls.

A concrete example

An AWS RAG app might use S3 for documents, Bedrock for generation, OpenSearch for retrieval, Lambda for APIs, and CloudWatch for traces.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Managed services accelerate delivery but can increase vendor coupling.

Set up and test AWS overview in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Hands-on lab

Task: Map one AI product to AWS services and identify the IAM role for each handoff.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not choose services before defining data flow, security boundary, and scaling needs.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about AWS overview?

  • Correct answer: Do not choose services before defining data flow, security boundary, and scaling needs.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once AWS overview is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain AWS overview in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Amazon Bedrock documentation

Bedrock

Amazon Bedrock is a managed AWS service for accessing foundation models, building generative AI applications, and integrating enterprise controls.

Why this matters

Bedrock matters because it gives teams managed access to foundation models inside AWS governance boundaries: IAM, logging, quotas, networking, billing, guardrails, agents, and knowledge bases.

In plain terms

It is like a model gateway inside AWS with managed access to multiple model providers.

How it works

The internal flow is account and region selection, model access approval, IAM permission, Bedrock Runtime request, model response, logging/metrics, and application-level validation.

Topic diagram

Diagram: Bedrock smoke-test path for Bedrock

A concrete example

A useful first Bedrock test is not a full chatbot. It is: confirm AWS identity, confirm model access in one region, call Bedrock Runtime with a deterministic prompt, record latency and request id, then intentionally break IAM or model id once so the team recognizes the failure mode.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Managed enterprise control vs AWS-specific setup: you gain IAM, service quotas, auditability, and integration, but must handle model availability, region differences, and AWS operational patterns.

Try it in code

# 1) Pick a region where the model is enabled, then confirm your identity.
export AWS_REGION=us-east-1
aws sts get-caller-identity

# 2) In the AWS console, enable model access for your chosen Bedrock model.
export BEDROCK_MODEL_ID=anthropic.claude-3-haiku-20240307-v1:0

# 3) Run a minimal Bedrock Runtime smoke test.
aws bedrock-runtime converse \
  --region "$AWS_REGION" \
  --model-id "$BEDROCK_MODEL_ID" \
  --messages '[{"role":"user","content":[{"text":"Reply with exactly: bedrock smoke test ok"}]}]' \
  --inference-config '{"maxTokens":64,"temperature":0}'

# Pass condition: the response text contains "bedrock smoke test ok".
# If AccessDeniedException appears, fix IAM permissions or model access first.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test in AWS Bedrock

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • AWS account with billing enabled and access to a region where your target foundation model is available.
  • Bedrock model access enabled in the AWS console for the model you plan to call.
  • AWS CLI configured with an IAM principal that can call bedrock:ListFoundationModels and bedrock:Converse.
  • A named region, model id, trace id, and expected response text for the first smoke test.

Smoke test

export AWS_REGION=us-east-1
export BEDROCK_MODEL_ID=anthropic.claude-3-haiku-20240307-v1:0

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws bedrock list-foundation-models --region "$AWS_REGION" --query "modelSummaries[0].modelId"
aws bedrock-runtime converse \
  --region "$AWS_REGION" \
  --model-id "$BEDROCK_MODEL_ID" \
  --messages '[{"role":"user","content":[{"text":"Reply with exactly: bedrock smoke test ok"}]}]' \
  --inference-config '{"maxTokens":64,"temperature":0}'

How to know it worked

  • If identity fails, fix AWS credentials before touching Bedrock.
  • If model listing works but converse fails, check model access, region, IAM action, and quota.
  • If the response is not exact, set temperature to 0 and verify you are using the intended model id.

Next step: After the smoke test passes, wrap it in a tiny script that records model id, region, latency, token count, status code, and the full request trace.

Hands-on lab

Task: Set up a Bedrock smoke test in a sandbox AWS account: enable one model, run a deterministic converse request, save the request id and latency, then document how AccessDenied, wrong region, and wrong model id failures look.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-shaped slice should include an AWS CLI smoke test, a least-privilege IAM policy, one deterministic model invocation, CloudWatch or application logs, latency measurement, and failure handling for AccessDenied, throttling, and unsupported model ids.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not assume Bedrock removes the need for evals, prompt versioning, or data permissions.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Bedrock?

  • Correct answer: Do not assume Bedrock removes the need for evals, prompt versioning, or data permissions.
  • Once Bedrock is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Bedrock into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.
  • Correct answer: A production-shaped slice should include an AWS CLI smoke test, a least-privilege IAM policy, one deterministic model invocation, CloudWatch or application logs, latency measurement, and failure handling for AccessDenied, throttling, and unsupported model ids.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Bedrock in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Amazon Bedrock documentation

SageMaker

SageMaker is AWS's managed platform for building, training, deploying, and monitoring machine-learning models.

In plain terms

It is like a managed ML factory with notebooks, training jobs, model registry, endpoints, and monitoring.

How it works

In practice it comes down to training jobs, datasets, model registry, endpoints, autoscaling, pipelines, and model monitor.

A concrete example

A fraud model can train in SageMaker, register an approved artifact, deploy to an endpoint, and monitor drift.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Managed ML pipelines improve repeatability but require AWS-specific operational knowledge.

Set up and test SageMaker in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Hands-on lab

Task: Sketch a SageMaker pipeline from data prep to model approval and endpoint deployment.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not use a notebook as the production training pipeline.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about SageMaker?

  • Correct answer: Do not use a notebook as the production training pipeline.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once SageMaker is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain SageMaker in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Amazon Bedrock documentation

Lambda

AWS Lambda runs short-lived serverless functions that can glue AI workflows, APIs, events, and background processing.

In plain terms

It is like a small worker that wakes up for one task and disappears.

How it works

In practice it comes down to event triggers, cold starts, timeouts, memory sizing, IAM role scope, and retries.

A concrete example

A Lambda function can receive a document upload event, extract metadata, and enqueue embedding work.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Lambda reduces server management but has runtime and timeout limits.

Set up and test Lambda in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a Lambda handler for validating and enqueueing uploaded documents.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not put long-running model inference in Lambda without checking limits and cold-start impact.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Lambda?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Lambda is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not put long-running model inference in Lambda without checking limits and cold-start impact.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Lambda in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Amazon Bedrock documentation

ECS

Amazon ECS is a managed container orchestration service that runs Docker containers as tasks and services across EC2 or Fargate compute, commonly used to host model-serving APIs, RAG backends, and agent orchestration layers.

In plain terms

It is like a dispatcher that keeps a fixed number of delivery trucks running, replacing any that break down, without you managing the depot yourself.

How it works

In practice it comes down to task definitions describing container image, CPU and memory, and environment variables, grouped into services that maintain a desired count of running tasks behind a load balancer, with Fargate removing the need to manage underlying EC2 instances.

A concrete example

A team runs their RAG API as an ECS service on Fargate with two tasks behind an Application Load Balancer, so a crashed container is automatically replaced without a human paging in at 2am.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Fargate removes server management and scales quickly but costs more per vCPU-hour than self-managed EC2 instances, which are cheaper at steady, predictable load but require patching and capacity planning

Try it in code

# Minimal ECS task definition for a model-serving API (excerpt)
{
  "family": "rag-api",
  "requiresCompatibilities": ["FARGATE"],
  "cpu": "512", "memory": "1024",
  "containerDefinitions": [{
    "name": "rag-api",
    "image": "123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/rag-api:latest",
    "healthCheck": { "command": ["CMD-SHELL", "curl -f http://localhost:8080/health || exit 1"] }
  }]
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test ECS in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write an ECS task definition for a small model-serving API with a health check, and decide whether Fargate or EC2 launch type fits a workload with predictable, steady traffic.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • ECS is not the same as Kubernetes; it has its own simpler scheduling model, and teams that need Kubernetes-specific tooling or portability should use EKS instead, not force ECS to behave like it.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about ECS?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once ECS is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: ECS is not the same as Kubernetes; it has its own simpler scheduling model, and teams that need Kubernetes-specific tooling or portability should use EKS instead, not force ECS to behave like it.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain ECS in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

  • Amazon ECS developer guide
  • Amazon Bedrock documentation

EKS

Amazon EKS is a managed Kubernetes control plane for running containerized workloads, commonly used for AI systems that need GPU node pools, complex scheduling, or portability across cloud and on-prem Kubernetes clusters.

In plain terms

It is like renting a fully maintained air-traffic control tower instead of building and operating your own, while you still own and fly the planes.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a managed control plane paired with worker node groups, including GPU-backed node pools for inference workloads, using IAM roles for service accounts to grant pods fine-grained AWS permissions and a cluster autoscaler or Karpenter to scale nodes with demand.

A concrete example

An inference platform runs GPU-backed node groups for model serving and CPU node groups for the API gateway in the same EKS cluster, using Karpenter to provision GPU nodes only when queued inference requests justify the cost.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: EKS gives Kubernetes-native flexibility and portability, but it has real operational overhead, upgrade cadence, and a steeper learning curve than ECS for teams that don't already need Kubernetes-specific features

Try it in code

# Taint a GPU node pool so only inference pods land there
kubectl taint nodes -l pool=gpu-inference nvidia.com/gpu=true:NoSchedule

# Pod spec requests the taint tolerance + a GPU
resources:
  limits:
    nvidia.com/gpu: 1
tolerations:
  - key: "nvidia.com/gpu"
    operator: "Exists"
    effect: "NoSchedule"

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test EKS in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a node group strategy for one EKS cluster serving both a lightweight API and a GPU-backed model: two node pools, their scaling triggers, and one taint that keeps API pods off GPU nodes.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Running GPU workloads on EKS is not just adding a GPU instance type; it requires the NVIDIA device plugin, correct node selectors and taints, and cost-aware autoscaling, or GPU nodes sit idle and expensive.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about EKS?

  • Once EKS is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Running GPU workloads on EKS is not just adding a GPU instance type; it requires the NVIDIA device plugin, correct node selectors and taints, and cost-aware autoscaling, or GPU nodes sit idle and expensive.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

OpenSearch

Amazon OpenSearch Service is a managed search and analytics engine that supports both traditional text search and vector similarity search through its k-NN plugin, making it usable as a retrieval backend for RAG or as a log and metrics analytics store.

In plain terms

It is like a single warehouse that can be searched both by exact keyword on the label and by which items look most similar to a photo you hand over.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a distributed, shard-based search index storing both inverted indexes for keyword search and vector fields with approximate nearest neighbor search, queried through a combined query supporting hybrid retrieval.

A concrete example

A RAG system stores document chunks in OpenSearch with both their text and their embedding vector, so a query can combine exact keyword matches with semantically similar chunks in one hybrid search request.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: OpenSearch's dual keyword-and-vector capability avoids running two separate systems, but a general-purpose search engine used for vector search can be more expensive and harder to tune for pure similarity search than a dedicated vector database at very large scale

Try it in code

PUT /rag-chunks
{
  "mappings": { "properties": {
    "text": { "type": "text" },
    "embedding": { "type": "knn_vector", "dimension": 1536 }
  }}
}
# Hybrid query: keyword match + vector k-NN in one request

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test OpenSearch in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design an OpenSearch index mapping with one text field for keyword search and one vector field for k-NN, and write the hybrid query that combines both signals for one sample query.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Enabling the k-NN plugin does not automatically give production-grade vector search; index settings like graph construction parameters and shard count need real tuning, or both recall and latency suffer under load.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about OpenSearch?

  • Once OpenSearch is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Enabling the k-NN plugin does not automatically give production-grade vector search; index settings like graph construction parameters and shard count need real tuning, or both recall and latency suffer under load.

  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

  • OpenSearch Service k-NN search
  • Amazon Bedrock documentation

Aurora

Amazon Aurora is a managed relational database compatible with MySQL and PostgreSQL, commonly used in AI systems for transactional application data and, via the pgvector extension on Aurora PostgreSQL, as a lightweight vector store colocated with other relational data.

In plain terms

It is like a high-performance version of a familiar database engine, maintained for you, that can also hold a searchable index of things that look similar right next to your regular tables.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a distributed storage layer decoupled from compute that replicates data across multiple availability zones for durability, with read replicas for scaling reads, and the pgvector extension adding vector columns and approximate nearest neighbor indexes directly inside PostgreSQL.

A concrete example

A support product stores tickets and their embeddings in the same Aurora PostgreSQL database using pgvector, avoiding a separate vector database for a workload that doesn't need dedicated vector-database scale.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Keeping vectors in Aurora avoids operating a second specialized system and keeps transactional and vector data consistent, but pgvector's approximate search doesn't match a dedicated vector database's performance at very high dimensionality or massive scale

Try it in code

-- Aurora PostgreSQL with pgvector
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS vector;
CREATE TABLE tickets (
  id serial PRIMARY KEY,
  body text,
  embedding vector(1536)
);
CREATE INDEX ON tickets USING hnsw (embedding vector_cosine_ops);
SELECT id, body FROM tickets ORDER BY embedding <=> $1 LIMIT 5;

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Aurora in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Hands-on lab

Task: Decide whether pgvector on Aurora or a dedicated vector database fits a product with under one million documents versus one expecting hundreds of millions, and justify the choice.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Adding a vector column to an existing Aurora database is not automatically fast at any embedding volume; without a proper index type and tuning, vector queries can degrade badly as the table grows.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Aurora?

  • Once Aurora is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Adding a vector column to an existing Aurora database is not automatically fast at any embedding volume; without a proper index type and tuning, vector queries can degrade badly as the table grows.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Aurora in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

  • pgvector extension

S3

Amazon S3 is object storage used across AI systems to hold training datasets, model artifacts, document sources for RAG ingestion, and logs, with lifecycle rules and access controls that scale from a few files to petabytes.

In plain terms

It is like an infinitely expandable warehouse where every item has a durable address, and you decide who can read or write to each aisle.

How it works

In practice it comes down to objects stored in buckets with versioning, lifecycle policies that transition or expire old data, and bucket policies or IAM roles scoping exactly which services or users can read or write specific prefixes.

A concrete example

A RAG ingestion pipeline reads source documents from one S3 prefix, writes extracted chunks to another, and uses a lifecycle rule to move raw documents older than 90 days to cheaper cold storage.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: S3's durability and low cost make it the default choice for large unstructured data, but its lack of query capability means it needs a companion index or database for anything requiring fast lookups

Try it in code

aws s3api put-bucket-lifecycle-configuration \
  --bucket rag-documents \
  --lifecycle-configuration '{
    "Rules": [{
      "ID": "archive-old-raw-docs", "Status": "Enabled",
      "Filter": { "Prefix": "raw/" },
      "Transitions": [{ "Days": 90, "StorageClass": "GLACIER" }]
    }]
  }'

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test S3 in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design an S3 bucket layout for a RAG pipeline with separate prefixes for raw documents, processed chunks, and embeddings, plus one lifecycle rule and one access-control rule.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Storing sensitive documents in S3 is not safe by default just because it's in the cloud; without explicit bucket policies, encryption, and blocking public access, misconfigured buckets are one of the most common real-world data exposure incidents.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about S3?

  • Correct answer: Storing sensitive documents in S3 is not safe by default just because it's in the cloud; without explicit bucket policies, encryption, and blocking public access, misconfigured buckets are one of the most common real-world data exposure incidents.
  • Once S3 is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

CloudWatch

Amazon CloudWatch collects metrics, logs, and alarms across AWS services, giving AI systems visibility into latency, error rate, cost-relevant usage, and model-serving health in one place.

In plain terms

It is like the instrument panel and flight recorder for everything running in your AWS account, combined.

How it works

In practice it comes down to custom and built-in metrics published on a time series, log groups aggregating structured application logs, and alarms that trigger notifications or automated actions when a metric crosses a threshold.

A concrete example

A model-serving API publishes custom metrics for p95 latency and token cost per request, with a CloudWatch alarm that pages on-call if p95 latency exceeds 2 seconds for 5 consecutive minutes.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Rich custom metrics and alarms catch problems fast, but every additional metric and log stream has a real cost, so instrumentation should be prioritized toward what an on-call engineer would actually act on

Try it in code

aws cloudwatch put-metric-alarm \
  --alarm-name rag-api-p95-latency \
  --namespace RagApi --metric-name P95LatencyMs \
  --statistic Average --period 300 --threshold 2000 \
  --comparison-operator GreaterThanThreshold --evaluation-periods 1

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test CloudWatch in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Hands-on lab

Task: Choose three CloudWatch metrics for an AI-serving API, such as latency, error rate, and token cost, and define one alarm threshold for each that would genuinely wake someone up.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Having logs in CloudWatch is not the same as having observability; without structured logging, meaningful metrics, and alarms tied to real thresholds, teams are just paying to store text nobody looks at until an incident forces a manual search.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about CloudWatch?

  • Correct answer: Having logs in CloudWatch is not the same as having observability; without structured logging, meaningful metrics, and alarms tied to real thresholds, teams are just paying to store text nobody looks at until an incident forces a manual search.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once CloudWatch is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

IAM

AWS IAM controls which users, services, and roles can perform which actions on which resources, forming the permission boundary around every other AWS service an AI system touches, from Bedrock model access to S3 document buckets.

In plain terms

It is like a building's keycard system where every door, elevator, and room is individually programmable for exactly who can enter and what they can do once inside.

How it works

In practice it comes down to policies attached to users, groups, or roles that explicitly allow or deny specific actions on specific resources, following least-privilege scoping, with roles assumed temporarily by services like ECS tasks or Lambda functions rather than long-lived credentials embedded in code.

A concrete example

An ECS task running a RAG API assumes an IAM role that can read from one specific S3 prefix and call Bedrock's Converse API, but cannot list all S3 buckets in the account or call unrelated services.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Tightly scoped least-privilege roles reduce blast radius if a service is compromised, but they require more upfront design work and more maintenance as services and their needs evolve, compared to broad permissive policies that are easy to set up but risky

Try it in code

{
  "Version": "2012-10-17",
  "Statement": [
    { "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "s3:GetObject",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::rag-documents/raw/*" },
    { "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "bedrock:InvokeModel",
      "Resource": "arn:aws:bedrock:*::foundation-model/anthropic.claude-3-haiku*" }
  ]
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test IAM in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a least-privilege IAM policy for a Lambda function that only needs to read from one S3 prefix and invoke one specific Bedrock model, denying everything else by default.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Attaching a broad managed administrator policy to get something working quickly is not a free shortcut; overly broad permissions in AI pipelines are a common root cause of real data exposure incidents once forgotten.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about IAM?

  • Correct answer: Attaching a broad managed administrator policy to get something working quickly is not a free shortcut; overly broad permissions in AI pipelines are a common root cause of real data exposure incidents once forgotten.
  • Once IAM is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
