  • Amazon Bedrock documentation

Reference deployment

A reference deployment for the AWS AI stack is a concrete, opinionated architecture combining compute, storage, search, and identity services into one coherent, deployable system rather than a list of services considered in isolation.

In plain terms

It is like a floor plan that shows how the plumbing, electrical, and structural systems of a building actually connect, instead of a pile of separate blueprints for each system.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a specific combination such as an ECS or EKS-hosted API calling Bedrock for inference, OpenSearch or Aurora with pgvector for retrieval, S3 for document storage, IAM roles scoping every service-to-service call, and CloudWatch for observability, wired together as one deployable unit.

A concrete example

A reference RAG deployment might run the API on ECS Fargate, store documents in S3, index them in OpenSearch, call Bedrock for generation, and use CloudWatch alarms plus IAM roles scoped per component, all defined in one infrastructure-as-code template.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A single opinionated reference architecture is faster to stand up and reason about than assembling services ad hoc, but it needs to be adapted, not copied blindly, since real traffic patterns, compliance needs, and cost constraints differ by product

Try it in code

# Reference RAG stack (one deployable unit)
compute:   ECS Fargate service, 2 tasks behind an ALB
retrieval: S3 (raw docs) -> OpenSearch (chunks + embeddings)
inference: Bedrock (Converse API)
identity:  one IAM role per component, least privilege
observability: CloudWatch alarms on p95 latency + error rate

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Reference deployment in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Hands-on lab

Task: Sketch a reference deployment diagram for one AI product using at least four AWS services, labeling exactly which IAM role connects each pair of services.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Choosing the best service in each category individually does not guarantee a good architecture; integration points, IAM boundaries, and data flow between services matter as much as any single component's specs.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Reference deployment?

  • Once Reference deployment is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Choosing the best service in each category individually does not guarantee a good architecture; integration points, IAM boundaries, and data flow between services matter as much as any single component's specs.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Reference deployment in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Amazon Bedrock documentation
  • Amazon ECS developer guide

Cost controls

Cost controls for the AWS AI stack are the deliberate practices and tools, such as budgets, tagging, and right-sizing, that keep spend on compute, model inference, and storage predictable and attributable, since AI workloads can scale cost quickly without corresponding scaling in value.

In plain terms

It is like fitting a utility meter and a budget alert to every room of a building, instead of discovering the total bill only at the end of the month.

How it works

In practice it comes down to AWS Budgets with alert thresholds, cost allocation tags per team or feature, Bedrock and compute usage tracked against per-feature cost, and scheduled right-sizing reviews of over-provisioned compute or storage tiers.

A concrete example

A team tags all Bedrock and ECS resources by product feature, sets a monthly budget alert at 80 percent of expected spend, and discovers a specific feature is driving 40 percent of inference cost with no corresponding usage growth, prompting a caching fix.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Granular cost tracking and alerts require tagging discipline and monitoring setup, but without them, AI inference cost in particular can silently spike well past budget before anyone notices, since usage-based pricing has no natural ceiling

Try it in code

aws budgets create-budget --account-id 123456789012 \
  --budget '{
    "BudgetName": "rag-feature-budget", "BudgetLimit": { "Amount": "500", "Unit": "USD" },
    "TimeUnit": "MONTHLY", "BudgetType": "COST",
    "CostFilters": { "TagKeyValue": ["user:feature$rag"] }
  }'

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Cost controls in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a tagging scheme for attributing AWS AI spend to product features, and set one budget alert threshold that would catch a cost regression within a day, not a month.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Cost control for AI workloads is not just about picking a cheaper model; unmonitored retry loops, redundant context, and unused provisioned capacity are common cost drivers that no model choice alone fixes.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Cost controls?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Cost control for AI workloads is not just about picking a cheaper model; unmonitored retry loops, redundant context, and unused provisioned capacity are common cost drivers that no model choice alone fixes.
  • Once Cost controls is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Cost controls in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • AWS Budgets user guide
  • Amazon Bedrock documentation

AWS lab

The AWS lab is a hands-on exercise to deploy one small AI workload across at least three real AWS services, such as Lambda, S3, and Bedrock, configure IAM roles between them, and verify it end to end with a real request.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to standing up a minimal but real deployment, such as a Lambda function that reads a document from S3 and calls Bedrock, with IAM roles scoping exactly what each step can access, then testing it with an actual request and checking CloudWatch logs for the trace.

It is like wiring a small working circuit on a breadboard before trusting yourself to wire a whole building.

The brief

Deploy a Lambda function that reads from S3 and calls Bedrock, using a least-privilege IAM role, and verify a real end-to-end request in CloudWatch logs.

A worked reference

A lab might deploy a Lambda function triggered by an S3 upload that calls Bedrock to summarize the new document and writes the result back to a different S3 prefix, verified with a real file upload and a CloudWatch log check.

Set up and test AWS lab in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Starting point in code

# Lab: S3 upload -> Lambda -> Bedrock -> S3, verified end to end
aws s3 cp report.pdf s3://rag-documents/raw/report.pdf
aws logs tail /aws/lambda/summarize-on-upload --since 2m
# Pass condition: summary object appears in s3://rag-documents/processed/

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A real, minimal multi-service deployment teaches IAM and service-integration lessons that reading documentation alone doesn't, but it takes longer to set up than a single-service toy example

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • The lab isn't complete when the Lambda function runs once in the console; it needs a real IAM role, not a wildcard-permission shortcut, and a real CloudWatch trace showing the actual request path.

Going deeper

References

  • Amazon Bedrock documentation
  • Amazon S3 user guide

Mini project

The mini project ships one small, real AI feature deployed on AWS with documented IAM roles, cost tags, and monitoring, that another engineer could redeploy from the instructions alone.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to packaging infrastructure as code or a clear manual runbook, the IAM roles used, one CloudWatch alarm, and cost tags into a reusable, documented unit rather than a one-off console click-through.

It is like handing off a fully labeled, working prototype instead of a working demo that only you know how to rebuild.

The brief

Take one small AI feature you've deployed on AWS, write it as infrastructure as code or a clear runbook, and have a teammate redeploy it using only your documentation.

A worked reference

A mini project might deploy a document-summarization API on ECS Fargate with S3 storage, Bedrock inference, one CloudWatch alarm, and an infrastructure-as-code template a teammate can redeploy in a different account.

Set up and test Mini project in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Starting point in code

# infra/main.tf (excerpt) — redeployable by a teammate
resource "aws_ecs_service" "rag_api" { desired_count = 2 }
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "documents" {}
resource "aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm" "p95_latency" {}
# terraform apply -var-file=prod.tfvars

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Documenting and packaging the deployment takes longer than getting it working once in the console, but an undocumented deployment is not reusable or auditable by anyone but its author

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A feature working in your own AWS account is not the same as a shippable mini project; without infrastructure as code or a runbook another engineer can follow, the work is not transferable.

Going deeper

References

  • AWS IAM user guide
  • Amazon Bedrock documentation

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete AWS AI reference architecture for a real product: compute choice, storage and retrieval layer, IAM boundary design, observability plan, and a cost-control strategy, as one coherent deployable system.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining ECS or EKS for compute, S3 plus OpenSearch or Aurora for storage and retrieval, IAM roles scoped per component, CloudWatch dashboards and alarms, and budget alerts into a single documented architecture with justified choices at each layer.

It is like designing the complete blueprint for a building, not just the individual rooms, including who holds which keys and what the utility bill alert threshold is.

The brief

Write a one-page AWS reference architecture for a real AI product: compute choice, storage and retrieval layer, one IAM role definition, and one cost-control mechanism.

A worked reference

A capstone for a customer-support RAG product might specify ECS Fargate for the API, S3 and OpenSearch for retrieval, per-component IAM roles, a CloudWatch dashboard tracking latency and cost, and a monthly budget alert.

Set up and test Capstone in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Starting point in code

const architecture = {
  compute: "ECS Fargate", retrieval: "S3 + OpenSearch",
  identity: "per-component least-privilege IAM roles",
  observability: "CloudWatch dashboard: latency, error rate, cost",
  costControl: "budget alert at 80% of monthly forecast"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A complete, justified architecture takes real design effort across every layer, but it prevents the common failure of a working demo that has no clear IAM boundaries, cost visibility, or production monitoring plan

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • An AWS AI architecture is not complete once inference works end to end; without IAM least-privilege design, cost controls, and observability, it is a prototype, not a production reference architecture.

Going deeper

References

  • Amazon Bedrock documentation
  • AWS IAM user guide

Interview pack

The AWS AI stack interview pack prepares you to discuss real AWS deployment decisions credibly: why you chose specific services, how you scoped IAM permissions, and how you controlled cost, backed by a concrete story rather than a list of service names.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a building's actual wiring diagram, not just naming the rooms it has.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real AWS AI deployment you built or debugged, paired with technical answers distinguishing ECS versus EKS, OpenSearch versus Aurora with pgvector, and how IAM and cost controls fit together.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how you would deploy a RAG system on AWS names a specific compute choice with a reason, a specific retrieval store with a reason, and proactively raises IAM scoping and cost controls without being asked.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "RAG feature cost spiked 3x with no traffic increase",
  cause: "a retry loop was re-embedding the same documents nightly",
  fix: "added an idempotency check before re-embedding + a cost alarm",
  result: "cost returned to baseline within a day of the fix"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Interview pack in AWS

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm the target AWS region, account, IAM role, service quota, and billing guardrail before building.
  • Start with a read-only identity check and one minimal service API call.
  • Record the exact resource names, ARNs, regions, and CloudWatch log locations used by the test.

Smoke test

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws configure get region
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • AccessDenied means IAM or resource policy is the first problem to solve.
  • ResourceNotFound usually means wrong region, wrong name, or missing setup.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, region, request id, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after the smoke test passes should you add model calls, data ingestion, write permissions, or automation.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about an AWS cost or security incident you encountered or would anticipate in an AI deployment, its root cause, and the specific fix.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that Bedrock, OpenSearch, and IAM exist is not the same as being able to justify when to use OpenSearch versus Aurora with pgvector, or how to scope an IAM role correctly; interviewers probe for that judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • Correct answer: Knowing that Bedrock, OpenSearch, and IAM exist is not the same as being able to justify when to use OpenSearch versus Aurora with pgvector, or how to scope an IAM role correctly; interviewers probe for that judgment specifically.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Amazon Bedrock documentation
  • AWS Budgets user guide

Azure AI Stack

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: The Azure AI stack is like an enterprise office campus: identity at the front door, AI services inside, search and storage nearby, and monitoring watching the building. Azure is common in enterprises that need identity, governance, private networking, and Microsoft ecosystem integration.

Azure overview

Azure AI architecture combines Azure OpenAI, AI Foundry, AI Search, compute, storage, identity, networking, and observability for enterprise AI systems.

In plain terms

It is like a Microsoft-native AI stack where identity, search, models, and monitoring share platform controls.

How it works

In practice it comes down to resource groups, managed identity, private networking, model deployments, search indexes, storage, and monitoring.

A concrete example

An enterprise assistant may use Azure OpenAI, AI Search, Blob Storage, Entra ID, Functions, and Application Insights.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Azure integration helps enterprises already on Microsoft but requires careful resource and identity design.

Set up and test Azure overview in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

Hands-on lab

Task: Map one RAG product to Azure services and mark every identity boundary.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not treat a model deployment as the whole architecture.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Azure overview?

  • Correct answer: Do not treat a model deployment as the whole architecture.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Azure overview is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Azure overview in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Azure OpenAI documentation

Azure OpenAI

Azure OpenAI provides managed access to OpenAI models through Azure deployments, identity, networking, quota, and enterprise governance controls.

Why this matters

Azure OpenAI matters because it lets teams use OpenAI models through Azure identity, networking, deployments, content filters, quota, and monitoring controls.

In plain terms

It is like using OpenAI models through an Azure control plane.

How it works

The internal flow is subscription and resource selection, model deployment, API version selection, identity or key authentication, chat completion request, response validation, and telemetry.

Topic diagram

Diagram: Azure OpenAI deployment path for Azure OpenAI

A concrete example

A company deploys a GPT model in Azure OpenAI and calls it from an internal app using managed identity and private networking.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Enterprise Azure control vs deployment/version complexity: deployment names, API versions, quotas, and private networking must be explicit in every environment.

Try it in code

# 1) Create an Azure OpenAI resource and deploy a chat model.
export AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT="https://YOUR-RESOURCE.openai.azure.com"
export AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY="YOUR-KEY"
export AZURE_OPENAI_DEPLOYMENT="YOUR-DEPLOYMENT"
export AZURE_OPENAI_API_VERSION="2024-10-21"

# 2) Run a minimal Azure OpenAI smoke test.
curl -s "$AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT/openai/deployments/$AZURE_OPENAI_DEPLOYMENT/chat/completions?api-version=$AZURE_OPENAI_API_VERSION" \
  -H "api-key: $AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Reply with exactly: azure smoke test ok"}],"temperature":0,"max_tokens":32}'

# Pass condition: the response contains "azure smoke test ok".

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test in Azure OpenAI

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Azure subscription with an Azure OpenAI resource in an approved region.
  • A deployed chat model with a deployment name; the model name and deployment name are not interchangeable.
  • Endpoint URL, API key or managed identity, API version, and a request logging location.
  • Network rules that allow your test machine or app environment to reach the resource.

Smoke test

export AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT="https://YOUR-RESOURCE.openai.azure.com"
export AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY="YOUR-KEY"
export AZURE_OPENAI_DEPLOYMENT="YOUR-DEPLOYMENT"
export AZURE_OPENAI_API_VERSION="2024-10-21"

curl -s "$AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT/openai/deployments/$AZURE_OPENAI_DEPLOYMENT/chat/completions?api-version=$AZURE_OPENAI_API_VERSION" \
  -H "api-key: $AZURE_OPENAI_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Reply with exactly: azure smoke test ok"}],"temperature":0,"max_tokens":32}'

How to know it worked

  • If the request returns 404, verify deployment name, endpoint, and API version.
  • If it returns 401 or 403, verify key, managed identity, role assignment, and network rules.
  • If it works locally but not in the app, compare environment variables and outbound networking.

Next step: Promote the smoke test into CI or a deployment health check that fails loudly when auth, deployment, or API-version drift breaks the integration.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create an Azure OpenAI deployment smoke test that records endpoint, deployment name, API version, auth method, latency, and the exact error messages for wrong deployment and wrong key.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-shaped slice should include a deployment-specific smoke test, managed identity or key handling, request logging, latency measurement, and failure handling for 401, 403, 404, quota, and content-filter responses.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not assume OpenAI public API examples map exactly to Azure deployment names and API versions.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Azure OpenAI?

  • Once Azure OpenAI is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not assume OpenAI public API examples map exactly to Azure deployment names and API versions.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Azure OpenAI into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Correct answer: A production-shaped slice should include a deployment-specific smoke test, managed identity or key handling, request logging, latency measurement, and failure handling for 401, 403, 404, quota, and content-filter responses.
  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Azure OpenAI in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Azure OpenAI documentation

AI Foundry

Azure AI Foundry is a workspace for building, evaluating, deploying, and managing AI applications and model assets on Azure.

In plain terms

It is like a project hub for model selection, prompt experiments, evaluations, and deployment workflows.

How it works

In practice it comes down to projects, model catalog, prompt flow, evaluations, deployments, safety tools, and monitoring.

A concrete example

A team can compare model deployments, run evals, and package a prompt flow for release.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Integrated tooling speeds iteration but can hide what needs to be codified for production.

Set up and test AI Foundry in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create a release checklist for promoting an AI Foundry experiment to production.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not leave production logic only in a click-built experiment.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about AI Foundry?

  • Once AI Foundry is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not leave production logic only in a click-built experiment.

Going deeper

  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Azure OpenAI documentation

Azure AI Search

Azure AI Search is a managed search service for keyword, vector, and hybrid retrieval in Azure RAG systems.

In plain terms

It is like a managed search engine that understands both exact terms and vector similarity.

How it works

In practice it comes down to indexes, fields, analyzers, vector profiles, semantic ranking, filters, and indexers.

A concrete example

A policy assistant stores chunks with embeddings, ACL metadata, and semantic captions in Azure AI Search.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Managed search simplifies operations but requires careful index design.

Set up and test Azure AI Search in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Azure AI Search is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

AKS

Azure Kubernetes Service is a managed Kubernetes control plane for running containerized workloads on Azure, used in AI systems for GPU-backed model serving, agent orchestration, and workloads needing Kubernetes-native scheduling and portability.

In plain terms

It is like renting a fully maintained control tower for your fleet of containers, while Azure handles the tower's upkeep and you manage the fleet.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a managed control plane paired with node pools, including GPU-enabled node pools for inference, using managed identities for pod-level Azure resource access, and the cluster autoscaler to scale node pools with demand.

A concrete example

An inference platform runs a GPU node pool for model serving and a separate CPU node pool for the API gateway in the same AKS cluster, scaling the GPU pool only when queued requests justify the cost.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: AKS gives Kubernetes-native flexibility and multi-cloud portability, but it carries real operational overhead and a steeper learning curve than a simpler managed compute option like Azure Functions for teams that don't need Kubernetes-specific features

Try it in code

# Taint a GPU node pool so only inference pods land there
az aks nodepool add --cluster-name rag-cluster --name gpupool \
  --node-vm-size Standard_NC6s_v3 --node-taints sku=gpu:NoSchedule

# Pod spec requests the taint tolerance + a GPU
resources:
  limits:
    nvidia.com/gpu: 1

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test AKS in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

Task: Design a node pool strategy for one AKS cluster serving both a lightweight API and a GPU-backed model: two node pools, their scaling triggers, and one taint that keeps API pods off GPU nodes.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Running GPU workloads on AKS is not just picking a GPU VM size; it requires the NVIDIA device plugin, correct node selectors and taints, and cost-aware autoscaling, or GPU nodes sit idle and expensive.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about AKS?

  • Correct answer: Running GPU workloads on AKS is not just picking a GPU VM size; it requires the NVIDIA device plugin, correct node selectors and taints, and cost-aware autoscaling, or GPU nodes sit idle and expensive.
  • Once AKS is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

Functions

Azure Functions is a serverless compute service that runs code in response to triggers such as HTTP requests, queue messages, or blob uploads, commonly used for lightweight AI tasks like document processing or calling a model API without managing servers.

In plain terms

It is like a light switch that only draws power while the light is actually on, instead of a generator running continuously whether or not anyone needs electricity.

How it works

In practice it comes down to trigger-bound functions that execute on events such as an HTTP call or a new blob upload, scaling automatically from zero to many concurrent executions, billed per execution and execution time rather than for idle capacity.

A concrete example

A document-ingestion pipeline uses a blob-triggered Azure Function that fires automatically when a new file lands in storage, extracts text, generates an embedding, and writes it to a vector index, with no server running between uploads.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Serverless functions eliminate idle cost and scale automatically, but cold starts and execution time limits make them a poor fit for long-running or latency-critical inference compared to an always-on service

Try it in code

# function.json trigger (blob-triggered ingestion)
{
  "bindings": [{
    "type": "blobTrigger", "direction": "in",
    "path": "raw-documents/{name}", "connection": "AzureWebJobsStorage"
  }]
}
# On upload: extract text -> embed -> write to processed container

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Functions in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

Hands-on lab

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Azure Functions is not a good default for every AI workload; a function with a long model-loading cold start can add unacceptable latency to a user-facing request that a persistent, pre-warmed service would avoid.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Functions?

  • Once Functions is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Azure Functions is not a good default for every AI workload; a function with a long model-loading cold start can add unacceptable latency to a user-facing request that a persistent, pre-warmed service would avoid.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

Blob Storage

Azure Blob Storage is object storage used across AI systems to hold datasets, model artifacts, RAG source documents, and logs, with access tiers and lifecycle rules that scale from a few files to petabytes.

In plain terms

It is like an expandable warehouse with different-priced storage zones depending on how often you need to retrieve an item.

How it works

In practice it comes down to objects stored in containers with hot, cool, and archive access tiers priced by retrieval frequency, lifecycle policies that automatically move aging data to cheaper tiers, and role-based access control scoping exactly which identities can read or write specific containers.

A concrete example

A RAG ingestion pipeline reads source documents from one blob container, writes processed chunks to another, and a lifecycle rule moves raw documents older than 90 days to the archive tier to cut storage cost.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Tiered storage saves significant cost for infrequently accessed data, but archive-tier retrieval can take hours, so choosing the wrong tier for actively used RAG source documents can silently break a retrieval pipeline's latency

Try it in code

az storage account management-policy create \
  --account-name ragstorage --policy '{
    "rules": [{
      "name": "archiveRawDocs", "enabled": true,
      "definition": {
        "actions": { "baseBlob": { "tierToArchive": { "daysAfterModificationGreaterThan": 90 } } },
        "filters": { "prefixMatch": ["raw/"] }
      }
    }]
  }'

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Blob Storage in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Common mistakes

  • Storing sensitive documents in Blob Storage is not safe by default just because it's managed; without explicit access controls, private endpoints, and encryption settings reviewed, misconfigured containers are a common real-world data exposure path.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Blob Storage?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Blob Storage is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Storing sensitive documents in Blob Storage is not safe by default just because it's managed; without explicit access controls, private endpoints, and encryption settings reviewed, misconfigured containers are a common real-world data exposure path.

  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

Entra ID

Microsoft Entra ID, formerly Azure Active Directory, is Azure's identity platform, controlling which users, services, and applications can authenticate and what they're authorized to do across every other Azure service an AI system touches, including Azure OpenAI.

In plain terms

It is like the single keycard system for an entire campus, where every building, elevator, and room checks the same badge instead of each having its own separate lock.

How it works

In practice it comes down to managed identities assigned to compute resources like AKS pods or Functions, granting them scoped Azure role-based access control permissions without embedding long-lived credentials in code, combined with conditional access policies for human users.

A concrete example

An AKS pod running a RAG API uses a managed identity scoped to read one specific Blob Storage container and call Azure OpenAI, with no API keys stored in code or environment variables.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Managed identities remove the risk of leaked long-lived credentials and simplify rotation, but they require more upfront role-assignment design than just embedding a key, and misconfigured scope can either block legitimate access or grant more than intended

Try it in code

# Assign a managed identity least-privilege access
az role assignment create \
  --assignee <managed-identity-object-id> \
  --role "Storage Blob Data Reader" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/.../containers/raw-documents"
# plus a role assignment scoped only to the Azure OpenAI resource

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Entra ID in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a managed identity and role assignment for one AKS pod that needs to read from Blob Storage and call Azure OpenAI, scoped to only those two permissions.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Using an API key for Azure OpenAI instead of a managed identity is not just a minor convenience trade-off; it reintroduces exactly the credential-leakage risk that managed identities exist to remove.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Entra ID?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Using an API key for Azure OpenAI instead of a managed identity is not just a minor convenience trade-off; it reintroduces exactly the credential-leakage risk that managed identities exist to remove.
  • Once Entra ID is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Application Insights

Azure Application Insights, part of Azure Monitor, collects application telemetry such as request latency, dependency calls, and exceptions, giving AI systems visibility into model-call latency, error rates, and downstream dependency health.

In plain terms

It is like a flight recorder for every request your application handles, showing exactly which internal calls it made and how long each took.

How it works

In practice it comes down to an instrumentation SDK or auto-instrumentation capturing requests, dependencies such as calls to Azure OpenAI, and exceptions as structured telemetry, queryable and alertable through Azure Monitor.

A concrete example

A chat API instruments its call to Azure OpenAI as a tracked dependency, so Application Insights shows exactly how much of total request latency comes from the model call versus the application's own processing.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Rich dependency tracing pinpoints exactly where latency or errors originate, but verbose instrumentation on every call adds real telemetry cost and volume that should be scoped to what's actually actionable

Try it in code

// Track the Azure OpenAI call as a dependency
const start = Date.now();
const result = await openaiClient.getChatCompletions(deployment, messages);
telemetryClient.trackDependency({
  name: "azure-openai-chat", duration: Date.now() - start,
  success: true, dependencyTypeName: "HTTP"
});

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Application Insights in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Having Application Insights enabled by default instrumentation is not the same as having real observability into an AI feature; custom telemetry for token usage, retrieval hit rate, or answer quality signals has to be added deliberately.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Application Insights?

  • Correct answer: Having Application Insights enabled by default instrumentation is not the same as having real observability into an AI feature; custom telemetry for token usage, retrieval hit rate, or answer quality signals has to be added deliberately.
  • Once Application Insights is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Networking

Networking for the Azure AI stack covers virtual networks, private endpoints, and network security groups that control how AI services like Azure OpenAI, storage, and compute communicate, keeping traffic off the public internet where required.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between mailing a document through the public postal system versus sending it through a private, locked courier route between two specific buildings.

How it works

In practice it comes down to virtual networks segmenting resources, private endpoints giving services like Azure OpenAI or Blob Storage a private IP inside the network instead of a public endpoint, and network security groups restricting which subnets or IPs can reach which resources.

A concrete example

A regulated customer support system accesses Azure OpenAI through a private endpoint inside its virtual network, so model calls never traverse the public internet and can be restricted to specific internal subnets only.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Private networking meaningfully reduces exposure for sensitive AI workloads, but it adds real configuration complexity and can break service integrations that expect public endpoint access unless DNS and routing are set up correctly

Try it in code

az network private-endpoint create \
  --name openai-pe --vnet-name rag-vnet --subnet ai-subnet \
  --private-connection-resource-id <azure-openai-resource-id> \
  --group-id account --connection-name openai-connection

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Networking in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Enabling a private endpoint for one service is not the same as securing the whole data path; every hop a request takes, including any intermediate API gateway, needs the same private-networking treatment or the gap remains open.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Networking?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Enabling a private endpoint for one service is not the same as securing the whole data path; every hop a request takes, including any intermediate API gateway, needs the same private-networking treatment or the gap remains open.
  • Once Networking is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

  • Azure OpenAI documentation

Governance

Governance for the Azure AI stack uses Azure Policy, management groups, and cost management tooling to enforce organization-wide rules about which AI services can be used, how they're configured, and how spend is tracked, across every subscription in an organization.

In plain terms

It is like a company-wide building code that automatically flags or blocks any new construction that doesn't meet safety and budget standards, instead of relying on each team to remember the rules.

How it works

In practice it comes down to Azure Policy definitions that enforce or audit configuration rules, such as requiring private endpoints for Azure OpenAI resources, applied across management groups spanning multiple subscriptions, combined with Azure Cost Management for spend tracking and budgets.

A concrete example

An organization enforces an Azure Policy that denies creating any Azure OpenAI resource without a private endpoint, applied at the management-group level so no individual subscription can opt out.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Centralized policy enforcement gives security and finance teams consistent guarantees across every team's Azure usage, but overly strict policies can block legitimate experimentation if not designed with real usage patterns in mind

Try it in code

# Azure Policy (excerpt): deny Azure OpenAI without a private endpoint
{
  "if": {
    "allOf": [
      { "field": "type", "equals": "Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts" },
      { "field": "Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/publicNetworkAccess", "equals": "Enabled" }
    ]
  },
  "then": { "effect": "deny" }
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Governance in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.
