  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Governance is not just a written policy document; Azure Policy definitions are actually enforced or audited automatically at resource-creation time, which is a materially stronger guarantee than a guideline teams are asked to follow.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Governance?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Governance is not just a written policy document; Azure Policy definitions are actually enforced or audited automatically at resource-creation time, which is a materially stronger guarantee than a guideline teams are asked to follow.
  • Once Governance is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Governance in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Azure Policy overview
  • Azure OpenAI documentation

Reference deployment

A reference deployment for the Azure AI stack is a concrete, opinionated architecture combining compute, storage, identity, and networking services into one coherent, deployable system rather than a list of services considered in isolation.

In plain terms

It is like a floor plan showing exactly how the plumbing, electrical, and structural systems of a building connect, instead of a pile of separate blueprints for each system.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a specific combination such as an AKS or Functions-hosted API calling Azure OpenAI for inference, Azure AI Search or Blob Storage for retrieval, Entra ID managed identities scoping every service-to-service call, private networking where required, and Application Insights for observability, wired together as one deployable unit.

A concrete example

A reference RAG deployment might run the API on AKS, store documents in Blob Storage, index them in Azure AI Search, call Azure OpenAI for generation, route traffic through private endpoints, and use Application Insights plus managed identities scoped per component.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A single opinionated reference architecture is faster to stand up and reason about than assembling services ad hoc, but it needs to be adapted, not copied blindly, since real compliance needs and traffic patterns differ by product

Try it in code

# Reference RAG stack (one deployable unit)
compute:   AKS deployment, 2 replicas behind an ingress
retrieval: Blob Storage (raw docs) -> Azure AI Search (chunks)
inference: Azure OpenAI (chat completions)
identity:  managed identity per component, least privilege
observability: Application Insights dashboard + alerts

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Reference deployment in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

Hands-on lab

Task: Sketch a reference deployment diagram for one AI product using at least four Azure services, labeling exactly which managed identity connects each pair of services.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Choosing the best service in each category individually does not guarantee a good architecture; integration points, identity boundaries, and network design between services matter as much as any single component's specs.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Reference deployment?

  • Correct answer: Choosing the best service in each category individually does not guarantee a good architecture; integration points, identity boundaries, and network design between services matter as much as any single component's specs.
  • Once Reference deployment is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Reference deployment in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Azure OpenAI documentation
  • Azure Kubernetes Service overview

AWS vs Azure

AWS vs Azure comparison for AI systems evaluates equivalent services across both clouds, such as Bedrock versus Azure OpenAI, EKS versus AKS, and IAM versus Entra ID, so a team can make an informed platform choice or support both.

In plain terms

It is like comparing two countries' road systems: many equivalent categories of infrastructure exist in both, but the specific rules of the road differ enough that you can't drive on autopilot from one to the other.

How it works

In practice it comes down to matching each AWS service to its closest Azure equivalent, noting where the abstractions genuinely differ, such as Bedrock's multi-vendor model marketplace versus Azure OpenAI's dedicated OpenAI-model focus, rather than assuming a one-to-one feature match.

A concrete example

A team choosing between Bedrock and Azure OpenAI for a new product notes that Bedrock offers multiple model vendors including Anthropic's Claude behind one API, while Azure OpenAI is scoped specifically to OpenAI models with deep Microsoft ecosystem integration.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Standardizing on one cloud simplifies operations, identity, and networking, but a multi-cloud approach can hedge against vendor-specific outages or pricing changes at the cost of doubled operational complexity

Try it in code

const comparison = {
  inference: { aws: "Bedrock (multi-vendor)", azure: "Azure OpenAI (OpenAI models)" },
  compute:   { aws: "EKS / ECS", azure: "AKS / Functions" },
  identity:  { aws: "IAM roles", azure: "Entra ID managed identities" },
  vectorSearch: { aws: "OpenSearch / Aurora pgvector", azure: "Azure AI Search" }
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test AWS vs Azure in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

Hands-on lab

Task: Pick one AI capability, such as model inference, vector search, or identity, and write a two-column comparison of how AWS and Azure implement it, noting one genuine difference beyond naming.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Assuming AWS and Azure AI services are interchangeable with a simple service-name swap is not accurate; identity models, networking defaults, and specific model availability differ enough to require real re-architecture, not just a find-and-replace.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about AWS vs Azure?

  • Once AWS vs Azure is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Assuming AWS and Azure AI services are interchangeable with a simple service-name swap is not accurate; identity models, networking defaults, and specific model availability differ enough to require real re-architecture, not just a find-and-replace.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain AWS vs Azure in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Azure OpenAI documentation
  • Amazon Bedrock documentation

Azure lab

The Azure lab is a hands-on exercise to deploy one small AI workload across at least three real Azure services, such as Functions, Blob Storage, and Azure OpenAI, configure managed identities between them, and verify it end to end with a real request.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to standing up a minimal but real deployment, such as a blob-triggered Function that calls Azure OpenAI, with a managed identity scoping exactly what each step can access, then testing it with an actual file upload and checking Application Insights for the trace.

It is like wiring a small working circuit on a breadboard before trusting yourself to wire a whole building.

The brief

Deploy a blob-triggered Azure Function that calls Azure OpenAI, using a managed identity, and verify a real end-to-end request in Application Insights.

A worked reference

A lab might deploy a blob-triggered Azure Function that summarizes a newly uploaded document via Azure OpenAI and writes the result back to a different blob container, verified with a real file upload and an Application Insights trace check.

Set up and test Azure lab in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

Starting point in code

# Lab: blob upload -> Function -> Azure OpenAI -> blob, verified end to end
az storage blob upload --account-name ragstorage \
  --container-name raw-documents --file report.pdf --name report.pdf
# Pass condition: summary blob appears in the processed container,
# visible as a dependency call in Application Insights

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A real, minimal multi-service deployment teaches identity and service-integration lessons that reading documentation alone doesn't, but it takes longer to set up than a single-service toy example

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • The lab isn't complete when the function runs once in the portal; it needs a real managed identity, not an embedded API key shortcut, and a real Application Insights trace showing the actual request path.

Going deeper

References

  • Azure OpenAI documentation
  • Azure Functions overview

Mini project

The mini project ships one small, real AI feature deployed on Azure with documented managed identities, network configuration, and monitoring, that another engineer could redeploy from the instructions alone.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to packaging infrastructure as code or a clear manual runbook, the managed identities used, one Application Insights alert, and network configuration into a reusable, documented unit rather than a one-off portal click-through.

It is like handing off a fully labeled, working prototype instead of a working demo that only you know how to rebuild.

The brief

Take one small AI feature you've deployed on Azure, write it as infrastructure as code or a clear runbook, and have a teammate redeploy it using only your documentation.

A worked reference

A mini project might deploy a document-summarization API on Azure Functions with Blob Storage, Azure OpenAI inference, one Application Insights alert, and an infrastructure-as-code template a teammate can redeploy in a different subscription.

Set up and test Mini project in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

Starting point in code

# main.bicep (excerpt) — redeployable by a teammate
resource ragFunction 'Microsoft.Web/sites@2023-01-01' = { }
resource ragStorage 'Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts@2023-01-01' = { }
resource insightsAlert 'Microsoft.Insights/metricAlerts@2018-03-01' = { }
// az deployment group create --template-file main.bicep

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Documenting and packaging the deployment takes longer than getting it working once in the portal, but an undocumented deployment is not reusable or auditable by anyone but its author

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A feature working in your own Azure subscription is not the same as a shippable mini project; without infrastructure as code or a runbook another engineer can follow, the work is not transferable.

Going deeper

References

  • Microsoft Entra ID overview
  • Azure OpenAI documentation

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete Azure AI reference architecture for a real product: compute choice, storage and retrieval layer, identity and network design, observability plan, and a governance strategy, as one coherent deployable system.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining AKS or Functions for compute, Blob Storage plus Azure AI Search for storage and retrieval, managed identities and private networking scoped per component, Application Insights dashboards and alerts, and Azure Policy governance into a single documented architecture with justified choices at each layer.

It is like designing the complete blueprint for a building, not just the individual rooms, including who holds which keys and what policy automatically blocks unsafe construction.

The brief

Write a one-page Azure reference architecture for a real AI product: compute choice, storage and retrieval layer, one managed identity definition, and one governance policy.

A worked reference

A capstone for a customer-support RAG product might specify AKS for the API, Blob Storage and Azure AI Search for retrieval, managed identities and private endpoints per component, an Application Insights dashboard tracking latency and cost, and an Azure Policy requiring private endpoints on all AI resources.

Set up and test Capstone in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

Starting point in code

const architecture = {
  compute: "AKS", retrieval: "Blob Storage + Azure AI Search",
  identity: "managed identities per component",
  observability: "Application Insights: latency, error rate, cost",
  governance: "Azure Policy requiring private endpoints on AI resources"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A complete, justified architecture takes real design effort across every layer, but it prevents the common failure of a working demo that has no clear identity boundaries, network protections, or governance plan

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • An Azure AI architecture is not complete once inference works end to end; without identity design, network protections, and governance policy, it is a prototype, not a production reference architecture.

Going deeper

References

  • Azure OpenAI documentation
  • Azure Policy overview

Interview pack

The Azure AI stack interview pack prepares you to discuss real Azure deployment decisions credibly: why you chose specific services, how you scoped identity and networking, and how governance fit in, backed by a concrete story rather than a list of service names.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a building's actual wiring diagram, not just naming the rooms it has.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real Azure AI deployment you built or debugged, paired with technical answers distinguishing AKS versus Functions, Azure AI Search versus Blob Storage alone, and how managed identity and governance fit together.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how you would deploy a RAG system on Azure names a specific compute choice with a reason, a specific retrieval store with a reason, and proactively raises managed identity scoping and governance policy without being asked.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "Azure OpenAI resource was reachable over the public internet",
  cause: "private endpoint was never configured for that resource",
  fix: "added a private endpoint + an Azure Policy to prevent recurrence",
  result: "policy now blocks any new public-facing AI resource org-wide"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Interview pack in Azure

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Confirm subscription, resource group, region, identity, role assignment, and networking before building.
  • Know whether the service uses keys, managed identity, private endpoints, or public endpoints.
  • Record resource names, deployment names, API versions, and Application Insights locations.

Smoke test

az account show --query "{subscription:id, tenant:tenantId}"
az group list --query "[0].name"
# Then run the smallest read-only command for the service in this chapter and save the request id.

How to know it worked

  • Authentication failures usually mean the wrong tenant, subscription, role, key, or managed identity.
  • Not found errors often mean wrong resource group, region, deployment name, or API version.
  • A useful smoke test records identity, resource, API version, latency, and expected output.

Next step: Only after identity and network access are proven should you add production prompts, data, or write actions.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about an Azure cost, security, or governance incident you encountered or would anticipate in an AI deployment, its root cause, and the specific fix.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that AKS, Azure AI Search, and Entra ID exist is not the same as being able to justify when to use AKS versus Functions, or how to scope a managed identity correctly; interviewers probe for that judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • Correct answer: Knowing that AKS, Azure AI Search, and Entra ID exist is not the same as being able to justify when to use AKS versus Functions, or how to scope a managed identity correctly; interviewers probe for that judgment specifically.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Azure OpenAI documentation
  • Azure Private Link / private endpoints

Building Products

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: Building AI products is like designing a service business with software employees. You define the job, train the workflow, measure quality, and decide when humans must step in. The winning skill is turning model capability into a reliable user outcome with requirements, evaluation, rollout, and operations.

Product strategy

Product strategy decides which user problem an AI capability should solve, why AI is needed, and how success will be measured.

In plain terms

It is like choosing the mountain before choosing climbing gear.

How it works

In practice it comes down to user pain, jobs to be done, AI fit, risk, differentiation, metrics, and rollout path.

A concrete example

A support product chooses AI draft replies because agents spend time composing repeated answers, not because chatbots are trendy.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Ambitious AI can differentiate a product but increases uncertainty and operating cost.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a one-page strategy for an AI feature with user, pain, metric, and risk.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not start with a model before identifying the workflow bottleneck.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Product strategy?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Product strategy is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not start with a model before identifying the workflow bottleneck.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Product strategy in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Requirements

Requirements translate an AI product idea into explicit capabilities, constraints, data needs, success metrics, and failure behavior.

In plain terms

They are like a contract between product, engineering, safety, and operations.

How it works

In practice it comes down to functional requirements, nonfunctional requirements, data requirements, quality bars, safety constraints, and launch criteria.

A concrete example

A RAG assistant requirement might say answers must cite approved docs and abstain when sources are missing.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Precise requirements reduce ambiguity but may evolve as evals expose reality.

Hands-on lab

Task: Turn one AI idea into five testable requirements.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not write 'use AI to answer questions' as a requirement.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Requirements?

  • Correct answer: Do not write 'use AI to answer questions' as a requirement.
  • Once Requirements is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Requirements in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Architecture

Architecture for an AI product is the overall system design connecting the user-facing application, the model or model gateway, retrieval and tool integrations, guardrails, and observability into one coherent, deployable system, distinct from any single component's internal design.

In plain terms

It is like the blueprint for an entire building, showing how plumbing, electrical, and structural systems connect, rather than the detailed design of any one room.

How it works

In practice it comes down to defining how a request flows from the user interface through authentication, any retrieval or tool calls, the model invocation, guardrail checks, and back to the user, with clear boundaries and contracts between each component so they can be built, tested, and scaled somewhat independently.

A concrete example

A customer support product's architecture routes a user message through an auth check, a retrieval step against a knowledge base, a guardrail-wrapped model call, and a logging step, with each stage owned by a clear contract so the retrieval system can be upgraded without touching the guardrail logic.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A well-decoupled architecture makes individual components easier to test, scale, and replace independently, but designing clean boundaries between components takes more upfront design effort than building one tightly coupled system quickly

Try it in code

const requestFlow = [
  "auth check",
  "retrieval (knowledge base)",
  "guardrail-wrapped model call",
  "logging + trace ID",
  "response to user"
];

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Sketch the architecture for one real AI product, showing the request flow from user input through every major component to the final response, and identify one boundary that should be a clean, independent contract.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Product deployment variants: AWS and Azure

Use these as implementation-ready starting points. Each product has a cloud mapping, a first smoke test, and the metrics that prove the deployment is useful rather than just online.

Enterprise RAG

Private documentation assistant with citations, ACLs, freshness checks, and retrieval evals.

AWS variantAzure variant
CloudFront/API Gateway
Lambda or ECS
S3 document store
Bedrock
OpenSearch hybrid index
IAM + CloudWatch		 Azure Front Door/API Management
Functions or AKS
Blob Storage
Azure OpenAI
Azure AI Search
Entra ID + Application Insights

Smoke test: Ask one known policy question, verify expected source id appears in top-5 retrieval, answer cites that source, and unauthorized docs are filtered out.

Watch: source recall@5, citation precision, abstention rate, p95 latency, stale-document count

API design

API design for AI products defines request/response contracts, streaming behavior, errors, auth, idempotency, and observability fields.

In plain terms

It is like designing a doorway that both humans and software can use safely.

How it works

In practice it comes down to schemas, versioning, auth, rate limits, idempotency keys, streaming events, and error codes.

A concrete example

A generation API returns answer text, citations, trace ID, safety status, and model version.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Rich APIs improve integration but create compatibility burden.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design an API response for a cited answer including trace and validation fields.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not expose raw model responses as your product API.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about API design?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once API design is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not expose raw model responses as your product API.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain API design in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Database design

Database design for AI products stores prompts, traces, evals, documents, embeddings, feedback, and user state with clear ownership and retention.

In plain terms

It is like arranging a workshop so every artifact has a shelf and label.

How it works

In practice it comes down to schemas, indexes, vector stores, audit tables, retention, permissions, and migration strategy.

A concrete example

A feedback table links user rating, answer ID, prompt version, source IDs, and trace ID for later analysis.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More telemetry helps learning but increases privacy and storage risk.

Hands-on lab

Task: Sketch tables for prompts, runs, retrieved sources, and user feedback.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not store prompts and documents without retention and deletion policies.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Database design?

  • Correct answer: Do not store prompts and documents without retention and deletion policies.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Database design is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Database design in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Prompt library

A prompt library stores reusable, versioned prompt templates with owners, variables, eval results, and release history.

In plain terms

It is like source control for instructions that shape model behavior.

How it works

In practice it comes down to template variables, versions, changelogs, owners, eval gates, localization, and rollback.

A concrete example

A support prompt v12 is tied to regression eval results and can be rolled back if complaints rise.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Central prompts improve reuse but can become too generic for product needs.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create a prompt library record with template, variables, owner, eval score, and rollout status.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not edit production prompts without review and version history.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Prompt library?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not edit production prompts without review and version history.
  • Once Prompt library is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Prompt library in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Testing

Testing for AI products combines traditional deterministic software tests for the surrounding application code with AI-specific evaluation for the probabilistic model behavior, since standard unit tests alone cannot verify whether generated output is actually good.

In plain terms

It is like testing a restaurant: you can deterministically test that the kitchen equipment turns on and orders route correctly, but you still need a separate process, tasting the food, to judge whether the actual dishes are good.

How it works

In practice it comes down to applying standard unit and integration tests to deterministic code such as request routing, authentication, and data handling, while applying AI-specific evaluation, golden datasets, LLM-as-judge, or human review, to the parts of the system where output quality depends on model behavior.

A concrete example

A RAG product's testing suite includes standard unit tests for its API endpoints and retrieval logic, plus a separate golden evaluation set checking whether generated answers are accurate and properly cited, since a passing unit test says nothing about answer quality.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Building both deterministic tests and AI-specific evaluation covers more of what can actually go wrong in an AI product, but it requires maintaining two different testing disciplines with different tools and mindsets, which is more work than either alone

Try it in code

// Deterministic test (traditional)
test("auth rejects invalid token", () => { ... });

// AI-specific evaluation (not a unit test)
runEval(goldenSet, { minAccuracy: 0.85 });

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real AI product feature, list one thing that should be tested with a standard deterministic test and one thing that requires AI-specific evaluation instead, and explain the difference.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A full suite of passing unit tests does not mean an AI product's actual output quality is verified; unit tests can confirm the plumbing works correctly while the model itself produces inaccurate, unsafe, or poorly formatted answers that only AI-specific evaluation would catch.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Testing?

  • Correct answer: A full suite of passing unit tests does not mean an AI product's actual output quality is verified; unit tests can confirm the plumbing works correctly while the model itself produces inaccurate, unsafe, or poorly formatted answers that only AI-specific evaluation would catch.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Testing is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Testing in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena

Evaluation

Evaluation in the context of building AI products means deciding, at the product level, what quality actually means for this specific feature, accuracy, tone, safety, latency, and building the measurement process that proves whether the product meets that bar before and after every change.

In plain terms

It is like a product manager defining what success actually looks like for a feature before engineering starts building it, rather than discovering the definition of success only after launch.

How it works

In practice it comes down to translating product requirements into specific, measurable evaluation criteria, building a representative test set reflecting real user needs, and using that evaluation both as a pre-launch gate and an ongoing production quality signal, tied back to product decisions rather than treated as a purely engineering concern.

A concrete example

A product team defines that a summarization feature must preserve all numeric facts and stay under 100 words, translates that into an automated evaluation check, and uses failing evaluation results as a real go or no-go gate for launch, not just an engineering nice-to-have.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Product-level evaluation criteria that reflect real user needs produce more meaningful quality signals than generic technical metrics alone, but defining and maintaining evaluation criteria this specific requires ongoing collaboration between product and engineering, not a one-time engineering task

Try it in code

const launchGate = {
  requirement: "summaries preserve all numeric facts, stay under 100 words",
  evalCheck: (output) => preservesNumbers(output) && wordCount(output) < 100,
  gate: "no launch until 95% of golden set passes"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real AI product feature, translate its product requirements into three specific, measurable evaluation criteria that would function as a launch gate.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Evaluation is not solely an engineering concern to be handled after a feature is built; product decisions about what quality actually means for users need to shape the evaluation criteria from the start, or engineering ends up measuring something that doesn't reflect real product success.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Evaluation?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Evaluation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Evaluation is not solely an engineering concern to be handled after a feature is built; product decisions about what quality actually means for users need to shape the evaluation criteria from the start, or engineering ends up measuring something that doesn't reflect real product success.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Evaluation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena

CI/CD

CI/CD for AI systems automates tests, evals, builds, deployments, and rollbacks for prompts, models, tools, and application code.

In plain terms

It is like a release conveyor belt with quality gates at each station.

How it works

In practice it comes down to unit tests, golden evals, safety evals, artifact versioning, canaries, feature flags, and rollback.

A concrete example

A prompt PR runs schema tests, RAG evals, safety probes, and cost checks before deployment.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Automation speeds releases but requires trustworthy evals.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a CI workflow for changing a prompt template.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not deploy model or prompt changes outside the same release discipline as code.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about CI/CD?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once CI/CD is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not deploy model or prompt changes outside the same release discipline as code.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain CI/CD in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Deployment

Deployment moves an AI feature into a controlled runtime with scaling, secrets, networking, monitoring, and rollback in place.

In plain terms

It is like opening a service to real customers after rehearsals and safety checks.

How it works

In practice it comes down to containerization, model endpoints, config, secrets, autoscaling, health checks, observability, and release gates.

A concrete example

An AI API deploys with separate prompt config, model routing, trace logging, and a rollback flag.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Fast deployment improves iteration but raises incident risk without gates.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a deployment checklist for an AI endpoint.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Product deployment variants: AWS and Azure

Use these as implementation-ready starting points. Each product has a cloud mapping, a first smoke test, and the metrics that prove the deployment is useful rather than just online.

Enterprise RAG

Private documentation assistant with citations, ACLs, freshness checks, and retrieval evals.

AWS variantAzure variant
CloudFront/API Gateway
Lambda or ECS
S3 document store
Bedrock
OpenSearch hybrid index
IAM + CloudWatch		 Azure Front Door/API Management
Functions or AKS
Blob Storage
Azure OpenAI
Azure AI Search
Entra ID + Application Insights

Smoke test: Ask one known policy question, verify expected source id appears in top-5 retrieval, answer cites that source, and unauthorized docs are filtered out.

Watch: source recall@5, citation precision, abstention rate, p95 latency, stale-document count

Monitoring

Monitoring tracks live AI behavior so teams notice quality, safety, latency, cost, and reliability changes after deployment.

In plain terms

It is like a dashboard for a system whose behavior can drift without code changes.

How it works

In practice it comes down to metrics, alerts, traces, feedback, drift signals, eval sampling, and dashboards.

A concrete example

A dashboard shows answer thumbs-down rate, citation failures, p95 latency, token cost, and guardrail block rate.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More monitoring creates noise unless alerts map to action.

Hands-on lab

Task: Choose five production metrics and one alert threshold for a RAG product.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not monitor only uptime for an AI feature.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Monitoring?

  • Correct answer: Do not monitor only uptime for an AI feature.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Monitoring is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Monitoring in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Documentation

Documentation for AI products explains behavior, limits, data use, operations, evaluation, and user responsibilities.

In plain terms

It is like the instruction manual and safety label for probabilistic software.

How it works

In practice it comes down to user docs, API docs, model cards, eval reports, runbooks, data lineage, and known limitations.

A concrete example

An internal assistant doc explains supported questions, source freshness, privacy rules, and escalation behavior.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Detailed docs build trust but must stay current with prompts and models.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a limitations section for an AI support assistant.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not hide known limitations; users need them to make safe decisions.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Documentation?

  • Once Documentation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not hide known limitations; users need them to make safe decisions.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Documentation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Release

Release is the coordinated launch of an AI capability with gating, communication, monitoring, feedback collection, and rollback readiness.

In plain terms

It is like opening night after rehearsals, with the stage crew ready for problems.

How it works

In practice it comes down to launch criteria, rollout segments, comms, dashboards, support plan, rollback, and post-launch review.

A concrete example

A team releases a summarization feature to internal users first, watches quality feedback, then expands gradually.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Gradual release lowers risk but delays full value.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create a release plan for a new AI feature with three rollout stages.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Product deployment variants: AWS and Azure

Use these as implementation-ready starting points. Each product has a cloud mapping, a first smoke test, and the metrics that prove the deployment is useful rather than just online.

Enterprise RAG

Private documentation assistant with citations, ACLs, freshness checks, and retrieval evals.

AWS variantAzure variant
CloudFront/API Gateway
Lambda or ECS
S3 document store
Bedrock
OpenSearch hybrid index
IAM + CloudWatch		 Azure Front Door/API Management
Functions or AKS
Blob Storage
Azure OpenAI
Azure AI Search
Entra ID + Application Insights

Smoke test: Ask one known policy question, verify expected source id appears in top-5 retrieval, answer cites that source, and unauthorized docs are filtered out.

Product lab

Product lab is the capstone-style practice of turning AI concepts into a runnable product slice with requirements, architecture, cloud deployment variants, smoke tests, evals, observability, security, and release criteria.

What you're building, and why

Product lab matters because AI engineering skill is proven by shipping a working, measurable product slice, not by naming isolated concepts.

The internal flow is user problem, requirements, architecture, AWS/Azure service mapping, implementation slice, smoke tests, evals, deployment, monitoring, and iteration.

It is like a flight simulator for AI product delivery: you practice the full route from user problem to deployment, failure handling, and operational metrics before flying with real users.

The brief

Choose one product lab and write its AWS variant, Azure variant, three smoke tests, three metrics, and one rollback trigger.

What your build must include

  • Start from a user job and define the smallest product slice that proves value.
  • Map the same product to AWS and Azure so the architecture is portable at the capability level even when services differ.
  • Define smoke tests before implementation: known-good request, known-failure request, permission failure, and latency budget.
  • Add evals that measure the product outcome, not just model output: citation support, extraction accuracy, safe escalation, or review usefulness.
  • Launch only when observability, cost controls, rollback, and ownership are explicit.

A worked reference

For Enterprise RAG, the product lab maps S3/OpenSearch/Bedrock/IAM/CloudWatch on AWS to Blob Storage/Azure AI Search/Azure OpenAI/Entra ID/Application Insights on Azure, then proves the deployment with known-source retrieval and unauthorized-document filtering tests.

Topic diagram

Diagram: Product deployment lab for Product lab

Starting point in code

const productDeploymentPlan = {
  product: "Enterprise RAG",
  aws: ["S3", "OpenSearch", "Bedrock", "Lambda/ECS", "IAM", "CloudWatch"],
  azure: ["Blob Storage", "Azure AI Search", "Azure OpenAI", "Functions/AKS", "Entra ID", "Application Insights"],
  smokeTests: [
    "known question returns expected source id",
    "unauthorized document is filtered out",
    "unanswerable question abstains"
  ],
  launchMetrics: ["source recall@5", "citation precision", "p95 latency", "CSAT"]
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Mini project

The mini project ships a small, complete AI product feature end to end, including architecture, tests, and evaluation, deployed and documented well enough that another engineer could run, test, and extend it.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a documented architecture, both deterministic tests and AI-specific evaluation, and a working deployment into one small but genuinely complete product feature.

It is like building and shipping one small but fully functioning product feature, with all the surrounding quality infrastructure, rather than just a demo of the core capability.

The brief

Build one small AI product feature end to end, with a documented architecture diagram, both deterministic tests and an evaluation set, and a working deployment.

A worked reference

A mini project might build a small internal tool that summarizes meeting notes, with a documented architecture, unit tests for the application code, an evaluation set checking summary accuracy, and a deployed, usable version.

Starting point in code

export const meetingSummarizer = {
  architecture: "auth -> transcript fetch -> model call -> log",
  tests: ["unit: transcript fetch", "eval: 10-case golden set"],
  deployed: true
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete AI product from requirements through architecture, testing and evaluation strategy, deployment, and monitoring, as one coherent plan for a real product idea.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining product requirements translated into measurable success criteria, a documented system architecture, a testing and evaluation strategy covering both deterministic and AI-specific quality, and a deployment and monitoring plan into one complete product design.

It is like a complete business and engineering plan for launching a new product line, not just a technical spec for one component.

The brief

Write a one-page product plan for a real AI product idea: requirements translated into success metrics, an architecture diagram, and a testing and evaluation strategy.

A worked reference

A capstone for an AI-powered document review product might specify user requirements and success metrics, a full architecture diagram, a testing and evaluation strategy including a golden dataset, and a phased deployment and monitoring plan.

Starting point in code

const productPlan = {
  requirements: "preserve numeric facts, <100 words, <2s latency",
  architecture: "diagram: auth -> retrieval -> model -> guardrail -> response",
  testStrategy: "unit tests + 30-case golden eval",
  monitoring: "accuracy dashboard + cost tracking"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Production build path

Use this chapter as a building block in one of the platform projects below. The learning target is not memorizing the term; it is proving you can design, implement, evaluate, and operate it inside a product.

Enterprise RAG

Build a private documentation assistant with ingestion, chunking, hybrid search, citations, evals, access control, and freshness monitoring.

Interview pack

The building products interview pack prepares you to discuss end-to-end AI product delivery credibly: translating requirements into evaluation criteria, architecture trade-offs, and testing strategy, backed by a concrete story about a real product you shipped.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a real product's actual launch decisions and trade-offs, not just describing the feature's capabilities.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real AI product feature you built end to end, paired with technical answers distinguishing architecture decisions, testing versus evaluation, and how you translated product requirements into measurable criteria.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how did you know this feature was ready to launch describes specific evaluation criteria translated from product requirements, a testing strategy covering both code and AI behavior, and a concrete go or no-go decision made from real data.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "Team debated when a summarization feature was ready to launch",
  cause: "no measurable launch gate existed, just subjective review",
  fix: "defined 3 testable evaluation criteria as a launch gate",
  result: "launch decision became evidence-based, not opinion-based"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a real AI product feature you shipped, including one architecture trade-off you made and one evaluation criterion that gated the launch decision.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Being able to describe what an AI feature does is not the same as demonstrating you know how to architect, test, and evaluate it responsibly; interviewers probe specifically for that end-to-end product-delivery judgment.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Being able to describe what an AI feature does is not the same as demonstrating you know how to architect, test, and evaluate it responsibly; interviewers probe specifically for that end-to-end product-delivery judgment.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena

Local AI

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: Local AI is like running a small workshop on your own laptop instead of renting a large factory in the cloud. You gain privacy and control, but capacity is limited. Local models are useful for offline development, privacy-sensitive workflows, demos, and cost control.

Local AI overview

Local AI runs models on developer machines or private infrastructure instead of relying entirely on hosted APIs.

In plain terms

It is like keeping a workshop in-house for privacy, offline access, and experimentation.

How it works

In practice it comes down to local runtimes, model files, hardware limits, quantization, private data, developer workflow, and packaging.

A concrete example

A security team can analyze internal logs with a local model when data cannot leave the network.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Local control improves privacy but shifts operations and model-quality responsibility to your team.

Set up and test Local AI overview locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Hands-on lab

Task: Pick one workflow that benefits from local AI and list hardware, model, and privacy requirements.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not assume local models can replace frontier models for every task.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Local AI overview?

  • Correct answer: Do not assume local models can replace frontier models for every task.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Local AI overview is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Local AI overview in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Ollama

Ollama runs local language models behind a simple developer API, making offline experimentation and private local workflows easier.

Why this matters

Ollama matters because it gives developers a fast way to test local models, private prompts, and offline workflows before deciding whether local AI is good enough for a product path.

In plain terms

It is like Docker for local models: pull a model, run it, and call it from local tools.

How it works

The internal flow is install runtime, pull model, start local server, call the local API, measure latency and quality, then decide what data and tools the local workflow may access.

A concrete example

A developer can run a local coding assistant with a quantized model for private repo exploration without sending prompts to a cloud API.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Privacy and control vs model quality and hardware limits: local models reduce data exposure but may be slower or weaker than hosted frontier models.

Try it in code

# 1) Install Ollama, start it, and pull a small model.
ollama pull llama3.2:3b

# 2) Run a local API smoke test.
curl -s http://localhost:11434/api/chat \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"llama3.2:3b","stream":false,"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Reply with exactly: ollama smoke test ok"}]}'

# Pass condition: the response message contains "ollama smoke test ok".
# If it is slow, record model size, CPU/GPU, memory, and prompt tokens.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test a local Ollama model

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Ollama installed and running on the machine where you will test.
  • A model pulled locally, with model size chosen for your CPU/GPU and memory.
  • A known prompt, expected response, and a note of latency on your hardware.
  • A privacy decision about which local files and tools the model workflow may access.

Smoke test

ollama pull llama3.2:3b

curl -s http://localhost:11434/api/chat \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"model":"llama3.2:3b","stream":false,"messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Reply with exactly: ollama smoke test ok"}]}'

How to know it worked

  • If the server is unreachable, confirm Ollama is running and listening on port 11434.
  • If generation is too slow, record model size, quantization, CPU/GPU, RAM, and prompt length.
  • If the model fails the exact-response test, try a smaller prompt, lower temperature, or a stronger local model.

Next step: Keep a local model scorecard with latency, memory use, context limit, privacy constraints, and task quality before choosing it for a workflow.

Hands-on lab

Task: Run one local Ollama model on a deterministic prompt and a realistic task prompt, then record model tag, hardware, latency, memory, context length, and whether quality is good enough for the workflow.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

To make it production-shaped: A production-shaped slice should include a reproducible model pull, local API smoke test, latency and memory notes, a task-quality scorecard, and explicit rules for local file/tool access.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not assume local means secure; local tools can still read secrets or execute unsafe code.

Check yourself 1 of 2

Which of these is sound guidance about Ollama?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Ollama is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not assume local means secure; local tools can still read secrets or execute unsafe code.

Check yourself 2 of 2

A team is bringing Ollama into their product. Which approach shows production judgment?

  • Ship the first version that produces plausible output; real users will surface problems faster than internal evaluation.
  • Correct answer: A production-shaped slice should include a reproducible model pull, local API smoke test, latency and memory notes, a task-quality scorecard, and explicit rules for local file/tool access.
  • Add every safeguard imaginable before measuring anything, so that no monitoring is needed after launch.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Ollama in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Ollama documentation

Kimi

Kimi is a family of large language models, including open-weight releases, that can be run locally or self-hosted, offering an alternative to closed, API-only models for teams that need to run inference on their own infrastructure.

In plain terms

It is like choosing a car you can service and modify yourself in your own garage, versus one that only the dealership can touch.

How it works

In practice it comes down to downloading published model weights and running them through a local or self-hosted inference runtime, giving teams control over where the model runs, what data touches it, and how it's customized, at the cost of managing the infrastructure and operational burden themselves.

A concrete example

A team with strict data-residency requirements runs a Kimi model on their own infrastructure so no customer data ever leaves their network, something a closed API-only model wouldn't allow.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Open-weight models like Kimi give full control over deployment and data handling, but the team takes on the operational burden of hosting, scaling, and keeping the model updated, work a hosted API provider would otherwise handle

Try it in code

# Run a self-hosted open-weight model
ollama pull kimi
ollama run kimi "summarize this document"
# Data never leaves your own infrastructure

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Kimi locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real use case, decide whether the control and data-locality benefits of a self-hosted model like Kimi would outweigh the operational burden of hosting it yourself, compared to a hosted API.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Being able to run a model locally does not mean it will match the newest closed frontier model's capability on every task; open-weight models trade some cutting-edge capability for control, cost predictability, and data locality, and the right choice depends on which of those actually matters most for a given use case.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Kimi?

  • Once Kimi is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Being able to run a model locally does not mean it will match the newest closed frontier model's capability on every task; open-weight models trade some cutting-edge capability for control, cost predictability, and data locality, and the right choice depends on which of those actually matters most for a given use case.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?
