LM Studio

LM Studio is a desktop application that lets developers download, run, and experiment with open-weight language models locally through a graphical interface, without needing to manually configure a runtime from the command line.

In plain terms

It is like a user-friendly control panel for running your own local model, compared to manually wiring together a runtime from raw components.

How it works

In practice it comes down to providing a graphical interface to browse and download quantized model files, load them into a local inference runtime, and interact with them through a chat interface or a local API server compatible with common API formats.

A concrete example

A developer uses LM Studio to quickly download a quantized 7B model, test it interactively in the app's chat window, and then point their existing code at LM Studio's local API server instead of a cloud provider's endpoint.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A graphical tool like LM Studio makes experimenting with local models far more accessible than manual command-line setup, but it adds a layer of abstraction that can obscure exactly what runtime settings, like context length or quantization, are actually in effect

Try it in code

# LM Studio exposes a local OpenAI-compatible API
curl http://localhost:1234/v1/chat/completions \
  -d '{"model": "local-model", "messages": [{"role":"user","content":"hi"}]}'

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test LM Studio locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Hands-on lab

Task: Describe one real prototyping task where using a tool like LM Studio would let you validate an idea faster than setting up a cloud API integration first.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Using a convenient desktop app to run a local model does not remove the underlying trade-offs of local inference, hardware limits, quantization quality loss, and no built-in production-grade serving; it just makes experimentation easier, not production deployment.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about LM Studio?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once LM Studio is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Using a convenient desktop app to run a local model does not remove the underlying trade-offs of local inference, hardware limits, quantization quality loss, and no built-in production-grade serving; it just makes experimentation easier, not production deployment.

GGUF

GGUF is a file format commonly used to package quantized local LLM weights and metadata for efficient inference engines.

In plain terms

It is like a compressed model artifact that local runtimes know how to load.

How it works

In practice it comes down to quantization type, tokenizer metadata, architecture metadata, tensor storage, and runtime compatibility.

A concrete example

A 7B model may be distributed as Q4_K_M GGUF so it fits on a laptop with acceptable quality.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: More aggressive quantization reduces memory but can hurt reasoning and coding quality.

Set up and test GGUF locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Task: Compare two GGUF quantization levels for memory, speed, and answer quality.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not download random model files without checking license, provenance, and checksum.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about GGUF?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once GGUF is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Do not download random model files without checking license, provenance, and checksum.

Quantization

Quantization stores model weights or activations with fewer bits to reduce memory and speed inference, usually with some quality trade-off.

In plain terms

It is like saving a high-resolution photo at a smaller file size: faster and smaller, but some detail may be lost.

How it works

In practice it comes down to int8, int4, activation quantization, calibration, per-channel scales, and quality evaluation.

Topic diagram

Diagram: The quantization trade for Quantization

A concrete example

A 13B model quantized to 4-bit may fit on consumer hardware but produce weaker answers on math tasks.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Lower precision improves cost and deployment reach but can degrade accuracy.

Set up and test Quantization locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Hands-on lab

Task: Run the same prompt on two quantized models and record quality, latency, and memory.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not choose quantization only by file size; evaluate task quality.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Quantization?

  • Correct answer: Do not choose quantization only by file size; evaluate task quality.
  • Once Quantization is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

CPU vs GPU

CPU vs GPU selection determines whether local or production inference optimizes for availability, cost, throughput, or latency.

In plain terms

A CPU is a flexible generalist; a GPU is a parallel factory for tensor math.

How it works

In practice it comes down to parallelism, memory bandwidth, batch size, model size, quantization, latency, throughput, and utilization.

A concrete example

A small quantized model may run acceptably on CPU, while multi-user serving of larger models needs GPUs.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: GPUs improve throughput but cost more and require capacity planning.

Set up and test CPU vs GPU locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Task: Benchmark one model on CPU and GPU with the same prompt and context length.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not compare CPU and GPU without specifying model size, quantization, batch, and context length.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about CPU vs GPU?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not compare CPU and GPU without specifying model size, quantization, batch, and context length.
  • Once CPU vs GPU is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Model selection

Model selection chooses the smallest, safest, fastest model that satisfies the product's quality and operational requirements.

In plain terms

It is like choosing a vehicle: a truck, bike, and race car are each right for different jobs.

For non-technical readers

For a budget owner, the practical rule is: don't default to the newest, most expensive model just because it's the most capable one available. A simpler, cheaper model is often good enough for a well-defined task like sorting emails into categories, while only your hardest problems, like drafting a nuanced legal argument, actually need the top-tier, more expensive model. Asking your team to justify model choice against actual task difficulty, not just default to the flagship model, is one of the simplest ways to control AI costs.

How it works

In practice it comes down to task fit, context length, tool use, multilingual ability, latency, cost, safety, license, and deployment target.

A concrete example

A classifier may use a small fast model while a complex legal drafting workflow uses a larger model with stronger eval performance.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Larger models improve capability but increase cost, latency, and governance burden.

Set up and test Model selection locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create a model scorecard with quality, latency, cost, privacy, and operations columns.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not pick a model from hype; pick it from eval results on your tasks.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Model selection?

  • Correct answer: Do not pick a model from hype; pick it from eval results on your tasks.
  • Once Model selection is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Offline development

Offline development means building and testing an AI feature using a local model with no dependency on an internet connection or a cloud API, useful for privacy-sensitive work, unreliable connectivity, or reducing development-time API costs.

In plain terms

It is like having a fully stocked workshop at home instead of needing to drive to a rented facility every time you want to test something.

How it works

In practice it comes down to running a local model on a developer's own machine during development and testing, switching to a cloud-hosted model only for production if the local model's capability or performance isn't sufficient for real users, keeping development iteration independent of network reliability or API cost.

A concrete example

A developer iterates on prompt design against a local model on their laptop during a flight with no internet access, and only tests against the production cloud model once connectivity is available again.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Offline development removes network dependency and API cost during iteration, but a local model may behave differently from the production model, so final validation against the actual production model is still necessary before shipping

Try it in code

const model = process.env.CI
  ? cloudModel        // production validation
  : localModel;       // fast offline iteration
await model.complete(prompt);

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Offline development locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real development workflow, identify one part that could be done offline against a local model, and one part that still requires validation against the actual production model.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Validating a prompt or feature entirely against a local model is not sufficient before shipping; local and cloud models can behave differently enough that final testing against the actual production model is still required.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Offline development?

  • Correct answer: Validating a prompt or feature entirely against a local model is not sufficient before shipping; local and cloud models can behave differently enough that final testing against the actual production model is still required.
  • Once Offline development is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

Privacy

Privacy as a driver for local AI means keeping sensitive data on infrastructure the organization controls, rather than sending it to a third-party API, which matters for data that's regulated, confidential, or contractually restricted from leaving a specific environment.

In plain terms

It is like keeping sensitive documents in an in-house filing room instead of mailing them to an outside service, even a trusted one, because the data itself can't leave the building.

How it works

In practice it comes down to running inference on infrastructure the organization directly controls, so sensitive input data never transmits to or is retained by a third-party provider, which is often required for regulated data categories like health records or classified information.

A concrete example

A healthcare team analyzing patient records locally rather than through a cloud API, because sending identifiable patient data to a third-party service would violate their regulatory obligations regardless of that provider's own privacy protections.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Running models locally for privacy avoids third-party data exposure entirely, but it means taking on the responsibility for the model's own security and access controls, rather than relying on a vendor's security practices

Try it in code

const inferenceTarget = containsRegulatedData(input)
  ? "local"   // data cannot leave this environment
  : "cloud";  // no data-locality requirement

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Privacy locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real or hypothetical dataset your organization handles, determine whether it has a hard data-locality requirement that would mandate local inference regardless of a cloud vendor's privacy practices.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A cloud API provider's privacy policy and security certifications do not eliminate the need for local inference when data literally cannot leave a specific environment due to regulation or contract; for those cases, the data boundary itself is the requirement, not just trust in a vendor's practices.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Privacy?

  • Correct answer: A cloud API provider's privacy policy and security certifications do not eliminate the need for local inference when data literally cannot leave a specific environment due to regulation or contract; for those cases, the data boundary itself is the requirement, not just trust in a vendor's practices.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Privacy is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

Performance

Performance for local AI covers the practical latency and throughput a model achieves on real local hardware, which varies enormously by model size, quantization level, and whether inference runs on CPU or GPU.

In plain terms

It is like knowing exactly how fast your own car actually goes on your specific roads, rather than trusting the manufacturer's spec sheet measured under ideal conditions.

How it works

In practice it comes down to measuring actual tokens-per-second and time-to-first-token on the specific hardware and model configuration in use, since published benchmarks from a model provider rarely reflect the exact combination of hardware, quantization, and context length a specific local deployment will use.

A concrete example

A quantized 7B model might generate 40 tokens per second on a laptop's GPU but drop to 5 tokens per second running on CPU alone, a difference that matters enormously for whether a local feature feels responsive.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Higher quantization and smaller models improve local performance and let more hardware run them acceptably, but each trades away some model quality, so performance tuning for local AI is fundamentally a three-way trade-off between speed, hardware cost, and capability

Try it in code

const results = {
  gpu: await benchmark({ device: "gpu" }),   // ~40 tok/s
  cpu: await benchmark({ device: "cpu" })    // ~5 tok/s
};
console.log(results); // measure on YOUR hardware, don't trust the spec sheet

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Performance locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one local model you have access to, or a hypothetical one, measure or estimate tokens per second on your actual hardware, and compare it against what would be needed for the use case to feel responsive.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A model's published benchmark performance is not what you should expect on your own hardware; actual local performance depends heavily on your specific device, quantization choice, and context length, and needs to be measured directly rather than assumed from a spec sheet.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Performance?

  • Once Performance is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A model's published benchmark performance is not what you should expect on your own hardware; actual local performance depends heavily on your specific device, quantization choice, and context length, and needs to be measured directly rather than assumed from a spec sheet.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Performance in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Ollama documentation

Developer workflow

Developer workflow for local AI covers how local models fit into a team's daily development process, such as using a local model for fast iteration and a cloud model for final validation, or switching between them through a consistent interface.

In plain terms

It is like a mechanic using a quick home diagnostic tool for daily troubleshooting, while still sending a car to the certified shop for the official inspection that actually matters.

How it works

In practice it comes down to structuring development tooling so switching between a local model and a cloud model requires minimal code change, often through a shared API-compatible interface, so developers can iterate quickly locally and validate against the production model before shipping.

A concrete example

A team's development setup points at a local model by default for fast iteration, with a single environment variable switching to the cloud production model for final testing before a pull request is submitted.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A workflow that makes switching between local and cloud models easy speeds up iteration significantly, but it requires upfront tooling investment to keep the two environments interchangeable rather than diverging into inconsistent behavior

Try it in code

# .env.local
MODEL_ENDPOINT=http://localhost:1234/v1  # fast local iteration

# .env.production
MODEL_ENDPOINT=https://api.provider.com/v1  # final validation before ship

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Developer workflow locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a developer workflow for one real team that uses a local model for fast iteration, specifying exactly what triggers switching to validation against the production model.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Developing entirely against a local model without ever validating against the production model is not a safe shortcut; behavioral differences between the two mean final validation against production still matters before shipping.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Developer workflow?

  • Once Developer workflow is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Developing entirely against a local model without ever validating against the production model is not a safe shortcut; behavioral differences between the two mean final validation against production still matters before shipping.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Developer workflow in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Ollama documentation

Packaging

Packaging for local AI means bundling a model, its runtime, and its dependencies into a distributable, reproducible unit so it can be deployed consistently across different machines without each one requiring manual setup.

In plain terms

It is like shipping a fully assembled appliance instead of a box of parts and an instruction manual, so it works the same way regardless of who sets it up.

How it works

In practice it comes down to containerizing the model weights, the inference runtime, and required dependencies into one deployable artifact with a pinned version, so deploying to a new machine or environment reproduces the exact same behavior rather than depending on manual, potentially inconsistent setup steps.

A concrete example

A team packages their quantized local model and inference server into a container image with a pinned model version, so every developer's machine and every deployment target runs the identical configuration rather than whatever version each person happened to download.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Packaging a model and runtime together makes deployment reproducible and consistent, but it adds build and maintenance overhead compared to each developer manually installing whatever version is convenient at the time

Try it in code

# Dockerfile (excerpt) — pins model + runtime together
FROM local-inference-runtime:1.4
COPY model-7b-q4.gguf /models/
ENV MODEL_PATH=/models/model-7b-q4.gguf
# Reproducible: same image = same behavior on any machine

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Packaging locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real local model your team uses, describe what would need to be packaged together, model version, runtime version, and configuration, to guarantee reproducible behavior across machines.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Distributing just a model file is not the same as a reproducible local AI deployment; without pinning the runtime version and dependencies alongside it, two people running the same model file can still get different behavior.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Packaging?

  • Once Packaging is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Distributing just a model file is not the same as a reproducible local AI deployment; without pinning the runtime version and dependencies alongside it, two people running the same model file can still get different behavior.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Packaging in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Ollama documentation

Local AI lab

The local AI lab is a hands-on exercise to run one real model locally, measure its actual tokens-per-second performance on your hardware, and compare quality and speed across two different quantization levels.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to downloading and running a quantized model locally, measuring actual generation speed and quality on a few representative prompts, then repeating with a different quantization level to compare the real trade-off rather than assuming it.

It is like test-driving two versions of the same car trim to feel the actual difference, rather than comparing spec sheets alone.

The brief

Run one real model locally at two different quantization levels, measure tokens per second for each, and compare answer quality on five representative prompts.

A worked reference

A lab might run the same model at two quantization levels on the same hardware, measuring tokens per second and comparing answer quality on five representative prompts for each.

Set up and test Local AI lab locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Starting point in code

const q4 = await benchmark({ quantization: "q4" });
const q8 = await benchmark({ quantization: "q8" });
console.log({ q4, q8 }); // compare tokens/sec AND answer quality

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Measuring the actual trade-off on your own hardware and prompts takes more setup than reading published quantization benchmarks, but it reflects your real use case rather than a generic comparison

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • The lab is not complete once a model runs successfully at one quantization level; without comparing at least two levels on your own hardware, you have no real evidence of the speed-versus-quality trade-off for your specific setup.

Going deeper

References

  • Ollama documentation

Mini project

The mini project ships a small, complete local AI feature, packaged and documented, with a measured performance profile and a clear privacy justification, that another engineer could deploy and run.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a packaged model and runtime, a measured performance profile on representative hardware, and a documented reason for choosing local inference over a cloud API into one reusable, documented feature.

It is like shipping one small but fully functioning offline appliance, documented well enough that someone else could set it up without your help.

The brief

Package one small local AI feature with a measured performance profile and a documented privacy or offline justification, and hand it to a teammate to deploy.

A worked reference

A mini project might package a local document-summarization tool for a privacy-sensitive use case, with a measured tokens-per-second profile on typical laptop hardware and a documented reason data can't leave the local machine.

Set up and test Mini project locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Starting point in code

export const localSummarizer = {
  model: "7b-q4",
  measuredPerformance: "18 tok/s on M2 laptop",
  justification: "customer documents cannot leave the local machine"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A narrowly scoped, fully packaged local feature with real measurements is more valuable than a broader local AI experiment that was never packaged or benchmarked for reuse

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A local model that runs on your own machine is not the same as a complete mini project; without packaging, a measured performance profile, and a documented reason for going local, it's not something another engineer could adopt or trust.

Going deeper

References

  • Ollama documentation

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete local AI deployment for a real use case: model and hardware choice justified by measured performance, a packaging and reproducibility plan, a privacy justification, and a developer workflow for iterating against it.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a model and quantization choice justified by measured performance on target hardware, a packaging plan ensuring reproducible deployment, a documented privacy or offline requirement driving the local-versus-cloud decision, and a developer workflow for fast iteration into one complete, documented deployment.

It is like designing the complete plan for a self-contained offline appliance, from component selection through packaging to the operating instructions, not just picking a model file.

The brief

Write a one-page local AI deployment plan for a real use case: model and quantization choice with measured justification, packaging plan, and privacy or offline requirement.

A worked reference

A capstone for an on-premises document analysis tool might specify a quantized model chosen after measuring performance on the target hardware, a containerized packaging plan, a data-residency requirement driving the local choice, and a developer workflow with a local-to-cloud validation switch.

Set up and test Capstone locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Starting point in code

const deploymentPlan = {
  model: "7b, q4 quantization (measured 18 tok/s on target hardware)",
  packaging: "container image, pinned model + runtime version",
  privacyRequirement: "data residency — cannot leave on-prem environment",
  workflow: "local for dev, cloud model for final validation"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A complete local AI deployment plan spanning model choice, packaging, and workflow takes more design work than just running a model locally once, but it reflects what's actually needed to deploy and maintain a local AI feature reliably

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A local AI deployment is not complete once a model runs successfully on one machine; without a packaging plan for reproducibility and a documented reason for choosing local over cloud, it's a one-off experiment, not a maintainable deployment.

Going deeper

References

  • Ollama documentation
  • NIST AI Risk Management Framework

Interview pack

The local AI interview pack prepares you to discuss local model deployment credibly: hardware and quantization trade-offs, privacy-driven decisions, and packaging for reproducibility, backed by a concrete story rather than general enthusiasm for running models locally.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a real offline-appliance deployment's actual engineering decisions, not just that running things locally is possible.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real local AI deployment you built or debugged, paired with technical answers distinguishing quantization trade-offs, privacy requirements, and packaging for reproducible deployment.

A concrete example

A strong answer to when would you use a local model instead of a cloud API names a specific data-locality or cost requirement, describes a measured performance trade-off at a specific quantization level, and proactively raises how the deployment was packaged for reproducibility.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "Needed to justify local vs cloud model for a client",
  cause: "client's contract required data to never leave their environment",
  fix: "measured local quantized-model performance, packaged reproducibly",
  result: "met the data-residency requirement at acceptable latency"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Set up and test Interview pack locally

Make the first win concrete: prove identity, permissions, endpoint reachability, and one expected model or service response before building a full app.

Prerequisites

  • Record the runtime, model file, quantization, hardware, memory, and context limit.
  • Start with a deterministic prompt before connecting private files, tools, or agents.
  • Decide what the local workflow is allowed to read, write, log, and retain.

Smoke test

# Run the runtime's smallest local prompt test first.
# Capture: model name, model size, hardware, latency, memory, and response text.

How to know it worked

  • If quality is poor, compare against a stronger model before changing product logic.
  • If latency is poor, check model size, quantization, GPU use, and context length.
  • If privacy is the reason for local AI, verify logs and tool access do not leak the same data elsewhere.

Next step: Treat local setup as an environment contract: another engineer should be able to reproduce the same model, prompt, and latency notes.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a local AI deployment decision you made or would anticipate, such as a quantization trade-off or a privacy requirement, and the specific measurement or packaging step that backed it up.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that local models exist and can be quantized is not the same as understanding how to measure the real performance trade-off or package a deployment reproducibly; interviewers probe for that applied deployment judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Knowing that local models exist and can be quantized is not the same as understanding how to measure the real performance trade-off or package a deployment reproducibly; interviewers probe for that applied deployment judgment specifically.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Ollama documentation

Claude Code

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: Claude Code is like a pair programmer that can read the repo, run tools, make edits, and remember project conventions when you give it a clear operating lane. The value comes from workflow design: small tasks can be direct, larger tasks need plans, review, permissions, and verification.

Claude Code overview

Claude Code is Anthropic's agentic coding CLI that reads a repository, plans a change, edits files, runs commands, and iterates inside the developer's own terminal, IDE, or CI environment, instead of only answering questions in a chat window.

In plain terms

It is like adding a capable teammate who can actually open your editor, run your test suite, and commit changes, rather than a search engine that only describes what to do.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a read-plan-edit-run loop where the agent inspects files and shell output through tools, proposes edits, executes commands, and re-reads results to decide the next step, all inside a permission boundary you control.

A concrete example

A developer asks Claude Code to add a rate limiter to an API; it reads the router, drafts a plan, edits the middleware file, runs the test suite, and reports the diff and test output instead of only pasting code.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Broader repository access and command execution make it far more useful than a chat assistant, but that same access raises the blast radius of a mistake, so permission scope and review discipline matter more than with read-only tools

Try it in code

# Ask Claude Code to read, plan, edit, and verify in one pass.
cd your-project
claude "add a rate limiter to the /login route, then run the tests"

# Claude Code will: read the router and middleware, propose a plan,
# edit the relevant files, run the test command, and report the diff.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Open a real repository, ask Claude Code to fix one small, well-scoped bug, and compare the diff and test output it produces against what you would have written by hand.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Claude Code is not a chat bot that happens to know your file paths; it actually reads, edits, and runs code in your real environment, so treat its actions with the same review discipline as a new teammate's pull request.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Claude Code overview?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Claude Code overview is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Claude Code is not a chat bot that happens to know your file paths; it actually reads, edits, and runs code in your real environment, so treat its actions with the same review discipline as a new teammate's pull request.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Claude Code overview in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Claude Code overview
  • Claude Code quickstart

Command reference

Claude Code's command surface includes slash commands typed inside a session, such as /clear, /compact, /model, and /agents, plus CLI flags used to launch or script it, such as -p for headless print mode.

In plain terms

Slash commands are like keyboard shortcuts for a running session, while CLI flags are like the options you pass when starting the program.

How it works

In practice it comes down to in-session commands that reset context with /clear, summarize and compact history with /compact, switch models with /model, manage subagents with /agents, or open configuration with /config, plus command-line flags that control permission mode, output format, and non-interactive execution for scripts and CI.

A concrete example

A long debugging session that has filled the context window can run /compact to summarize progress before continuing, while a CI pipeline can call claude -p to get a single scripted answer without an interactive session.

Command reference you can actually use

Use commands at the start of a message. Availability can vary by tool version, plan, and environment, so treat this as the working map and confirm with the tool's built-in help when needed.

Command or workflowWhen to use it
/initCreate a starter CLAUDE.md so the repo has project memory and conventions.
/memoryInspect or refine memory that guides future sessions.
/planEnter planning mode before a risky or broad change.
/modelSwitch or inspect the active model.
/effortTune reasoning effort for harder or easier tasks.
/contextSee what is filling the context window.
/compactSummarize the conversation to free context.
/permissionsSet tool approval and permission behavior.
/mcpConfigure or inspect MCP servers available to Claude Code.
/diffReview changed files before shipping.
/code-reviewRun a code review workflow on the current diff or PR.
/security-reviewReview changes for security vulnerabilities.
/doctorDiagnose installation, configuration, and environment problems.
/clearStart a fresh task while keeping project memory.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Interactive slash commands are convenient for exploratory work, and headless CLI flags are what make Claude Code usable inside automation, but scripting it removes the human-in-the-loop confirmation step, so permission flags must be set deliberately

Try it in code

# In-session slash commands
/clear      # wipe context and start fresh
/compact    # summarize history, keep the gist, free up context
/model      # switch the active model for this session
/agents     # list or configure available subagents

# CLI flags for scripting (headless / non-interactive)
claude -p "summarize the last 3 commits" --output-format json
claude -p "check for TODOs touching auth.ts" --permission-mode plan

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Start a session, deliberately fill it with a long exploratory conversation, then compare how the agent's next answer differs after /clear versus after /compact.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Slash commands are not just cosmetic shortcuts; /clear and /compact directly change what context the model can see, which changes its behavior on the next turn.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Command reference?

  • Correct answer: Slash commands are not just cosmetic shortcuts; /clear and /compact directly change what context the model can see, which changes its behavior on the next turn.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Command reference is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Command reference in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Claude Code CLI reference

Project setup

Project setup for Claude Code means writing a CLAUDE.md memory file and a .claude/settings.json configuration so the agent starts every session already knowing how to build, test, and navigate the repository, instead of rediscovering it each time.

In plain terms

It is like writing an onboarding doc and a permissions policy for a new engineer's first day, except the new engineer re-reads it at the start of every session.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a CLAUDE.md file at the project root, and optionally nested in subdirectories, that documents build commands, conventions, and constraints, combined with a .claude/settings.json for permissions, hooks, and MCP servers scoped to that repository.

A concrete example

A CLAUDE.md might state the exact test command, a linting rule about default exports, and a note that database migrations require a specific script, so the agent never proposes a change that skips migrations.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A detailed CLAUDE.md reduces repeated mistakes and back-and-forth, but an overlong or stale one wastes context budget and can mislead the agent if it drifts from the real codebase

Try it in code

# .claude/settings.json (project-scoped)
{
  "permissions": {
    "allow": ["Bash(npm test)", "Bash(git status)"],
    "deny": ["Read(./.env)", "Read(./**/*.pem)"]
  }
}

# CLAUDE.md (project root)
# - Test command: npm test
# - Convention: named exports only, no default exports
# - Never edit files under /generated, they are build output

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a CLAUDE.md for a real project with the exact test command, one formatting convention, and one thing the agent should never touch, then verify the agent respects it unprompted.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • CLAUDE.md is not a wishlist of rules the model might follow; it is loaded as real context on every session, so contradictions or outdated instructions actively degrade output quality.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Project setup?

  • Once Project setup is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: CLAUDE.md is not a wishlist of rules the model might follow; it is loaded as real context on every session, so contradictions or outdated instructions actively degrade output quality.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Project setup in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Claude Code memory (CLAUDE.md)
  • Claude Code settings

Agent workflows

Agent workflows in Claude Code are patterns for structuring larger tasks: using the main session for direct work, spawning subagents for parallelizable or context-heavy research, and using plan mode to align on an approach before any file is touched.

In plain terms

It is like deciding whether to do a task yourself or delegate a well-scoped piece of it to a colleague who reports back a single summary instead of every step they took.

How it works

In practice it comes down to the main agent loop delegating scoped sub-tasks to subagents that run with their own context window and tool access, returning a single summarized result to the parent conversation instead of polluting it with intermediate exploration.

A concrete example

For a cross-cutting rename across 40 files, an engineer might use plan mode first to see the intended change set, then let the agent execute file-by-file with test runs between batches rather than one giant unreviewed diff.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Subagents keep the main conversation's context clean and let independent research run in parallel, but each subagent starts cold and cannot see the parent's full history, so tasks handed to them must be self-contained

Try it in code

# Dispatch independent research to subagents, then synthesize.
claude "Use three subagents to research how auth, billing, and
search each handle rate limiting in this repo, then propose one
consistent rate-limiting approach for all three."

# Each subagent returns a summary; the main session never sees
# their raw exploration, only the final result.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take a task that requires researching three unrelated parts of a codebase, dispatch three subagents for the research, and compare the time and context cost against doing the same research serially in the main session.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Delegating to a subagent does not mean lower oversight is fine; the parent session is still responsible for reviewing what the subagent changed before treating the task as done.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Agent workflows?

  • Correct answer: Delegating to a subagent does not mean lower oversight is fine; the parent session is still responsible for reviewing what the subagent changed before treating the task as done.
  • Once Agent workflows is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Agent workflows in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Claude Code subagents
  • Claude Agent SDK overview

Context management

Context management is the discipline of controlling what enters Claude Code's limited context window so the model reasons from relevant, current information instead of stale or irrelevant history.

In plain terms

It is like keeping a workbench clear of tools you no longer need for the current step, instead of letting every tool you've ever used pile up around you.

How it works

In practice it comes down to the finite context window being filled by conversation history, file contents, and tool output, managed through /clear for a full reset, /compact to summarize and continue, and deliberate scoping of what files or search results get read into context in the first place.

A concrete example

A session that has explored five wrong hypotheses while debugging should be compacted or cleared before the actual fix, so the model's next answer is not anchored to the dead ends it already ruled out.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Keeping full history preserves nuance the model might reference later, but it consumes context budget and can anchor the model to earlier, possibly wrong, reasoning, while compacting saves space but can lose detail the summary omitted

Try it in code

# Symptom: the agent keeps referencing a dead-end hypothesis
# from 30 messages ago.

/compact   # summarize the debugging history into a few sentences
# or, if the history is pure noise:
/clear     # start the next step with zero prior context

# Rule of thumb: compact when history has signal worth keeping,
# clear when it's just dead ends.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Run the same non-trivial task twice, once in a fresh session with only the necessary files and once in a session already cluttered with unrelated exploration, and compare answer quality.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A bigger context window does not mean context management stops mattering; irrelevant or contradictory context degrades output quality well before the window is technically full.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Context management?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A bigger context window does not mean context management stops mattering; irrelevant or contradictory context degrades output quality well before the window is technically full.
  • Once Context management is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Context management in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

  • Claude Code memory (CLAUDE.md)
  • Claude Code best practices

Testing workflows

Testing workflows use Claude Code's ability to actually execute a project's test suite, read the failure output, and revise its own edit, closing the loop between writing code and verifying it instead of leaving verification to the human.

In plain terms

It is like a developer who runs the tests after every change instead of just assuming the code works because it compiles.

How it works

In practice it comes down to the agent running the project's real test command after edits, reading failures from stdout and stderr, and iterating on the diff until tests pass, rather than only generating code it assumes is correct.

A concrete example

After adding a feature, the agent runs the test command, sees one failing assertion, reads the stack trace, and fixes the specific line rather than regenerating the whole file.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Letting the agent run tests autonomously speeds iteration, but a test suite with gaps will let a subtly wrong change pass, so test quality is a precondition, not a side effect, of trusting agentic edits

Try it in code

claude "add input validation to parseOrder(), then run npm test
and fix any failures you introduce"

# Expected loop:
# 1. edit parseOrder() to validate input
# 2. run: npm test
# 3. read the failing assertion's stack trace
# 4. fix the specific line, not the whole file
# 5. rerun until green

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Ask the agent to implement a small feature with a deliberately incomplete test file, observe what bug slips through, then add the missing test case and rerun.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Passing tests after an agentic change does not prove correctness beyond what those tests actually check; an agent can satisfy weak or incomplete tests just as easily as a human can.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Testing workflows?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Testing workflows is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Passing tests after an agentic change does not prove correctness beyond what those tests actually check; an agent can satisfy weak or incomplete tests just as easily as a human can.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Testing workflows in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

Refactoring

Refactoring with Claude Code means using it to restructure code without changing behavior, verified by running the existing test suite before and after the change, not just by reading the diff.

In plain terms

It is like reorganizing a warehouse's shelving without moving any of the products to the wrong section.

How it works

In practice it comes down to scoping a refactor to an explicit, bounded set of files and behavior-preserving goals, then asking the agent to make the change and run tests before and after to confirm behavior did not shift.

A concrete example

Extracting a shared validation function used in four places works well when the agent is told the exact four call sites and the test command to confirm nothing else changed; an unscoped request risks touching unrelated code.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A tightly scoped refactor request is easy to review and verify, while a broad request produces a larger diff that is harder to check line by line and more likely to include unintended behavior changes

Try it in code

claude "extract the duplicated email-validation logic in
signup.ts, login.ts, and resetPassword.ts into one shared
function in validators.ts. Only touch these four files.
Run npm test before and after to confirm behavior is unchanged."

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Pick a function with duplicated logic in three places, ask the agent to extract a shared helper, and verify test results are identical before and after.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A refactor that compiles and looks cleaner is not automatically safe; without a passing test suite covering the affected behavior, a refactor can silently change edge-case behavior.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Refactoring?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A refactor that compiles and looks cleaner is not automatically safe; without a passing test suite covering the affected behavior, a refactor can silently change edge-case behavior.
  • Once Refactoring is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Refactoring in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

Code review

Code review with Claude Code means using the agent to read a diff and surface correctness bugs, missing edge cases, and security concerns before a human reviewer spends time on it, not to replace human sign-off.

In plain terms

It is like having a sharp junior reviewer do a first pass so the senior reviewer's time goes to judgment calls instead of mechanical checks.

How it works

In practice it comes down to the agent reading a diff or pull request, checking it against stated conventions, tests, and risk areas such as auth, migrations, and secrets, and producing findings ranked by severity rather than generic style comments.

A concrete example

Reviewing a pull request that adds a new database migration, the agent flags that the migration lacks a rollback path and that one new field is nullable in code but not null in the schema.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Automated review catches mechanical issues fast and consistently, but it can miss product-context judgment a human reviewer would catch, and it can also flag false positives that waste reviewer time if not tuned

Try it in code

claude -p "review this diff for correctness bugs, missing edge
cases, and any file touching auth or migrations" < pr-1234.diff

# Good review findings are specific and falsifiable:
# "line 42: new column is NOT NULL in schema.sql but nullable
#  in the TypeScript type — insert will fail on missing value"

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take a real merged pull request from your repository, ask Claude Code to review the diff cold, and compare its findings against the actual human review comments it received.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • An AI review passing a diff is not equivalent to approval; it is a first pass that should reduce human reviewer workload, not replace the accountability of a human approver.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Code review?

  • Correct answer: An AI review passing a diff is not equivalent to approval; it is a first pass that should reduce human reviewer workload, not replace the accountability of a human approver.
  • Once Code review is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Code review in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?
