  • Claude Code GitHub code review
  • Claude Code GitHub Actions

Security practices

Security practices for Claude Code configure what the agent is allowed to touch: which directories, which shell commands, and which secrets it can access, so agentic capability does not become an uncontrolled attack surface.

In plain terms

It is like giving a new contractor a keycard that only opens the rooms they actually need, instead of a master key.

How it works

In practice it comes down to permission modes for ask, allow, or deny per tool, a settings.json allowlist or denylist for specific commands, and hooks that can block or log tool calls before they execute, forming a boundary around what the agent can read, write, or run.

A concrete example

A team denies the agent access to .env and credentials files by pattern, requires confirmation before any git push, and logs every tool call through a hook for audit, so an agent cannot exfiltrate secrets or push without a human seeing it first.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Tighter permission scopes reduce risk but require more human confirmation steps, slowing down agentic workflows, while looser scopes are faster but increase the damage a wrong or manipulated action can cause

Try it in code

// .claude/settings.json
{
  "permissions": {
    "deny": ["Read(./.env)", "Read(./secrets/**)"],
    "ask": ["Bash(git push*)", "Bash(curl*)"]
  },
  "hooks": {
    "PreToolUse": [{ "matcher": "Bash", "hooks": [{ "type": "command", "command": "./scripts/log-tool-call.sh" }] }]
  }
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Configure a settings.json that denies read access to one sensitive directory and requires explicit confirmation for git push, then verify the agent actually respects both boundaries.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Prompting the agent to be careful with secrets is not a security control; only enforced permission rules and denylisted paths reliably prevent access, because instructions in context can be overridden by malicious file content.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Security practices?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Prompting the agent to be careful with secrets is not a security control; only enforced permission rules and denylisted paths reliably prevent access, because instructions in context can be overridden by malicious file content.
  • Once Security practices is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Security practices in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

  • Claude Code security
  • Claude Code permissions

Enterprise usage

Enterprise usage of Claude Code involves organization-wide managed settings, centralized permission policy, and audit logging so security and platform teams can enforce consistent rules across every developer's agent, rather than relying on each developer configuring safety individually.

In plain terms

It is like a company-wide security policy that applies the same way to every laptop, instead of trusting each employee to configure their own firewall.

How it works

In practice it comes down to centrally managed policy settings that IT or security teams push to every developer's machine, controlling allowed tools, MCP servers, and model access, layered underneath and unable to be overridden by individual or project-level settings.

A concrete example

A platform team ships a managed settings file that blocks all agents from running curl to external hosts by default and requires an approved MCP server allowlist, applied the same way whether a developer is new or senior.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Centralized policy gives security teams confidence and consistency across the org, but overly restrictive managed settings can block legitimate workflows and push developers toward workarounds if the policy is not tuned with real usage data

Try it in code

// Managed settings (deployed by IT/security, cannot be
// overridden by project or user settings)
{
  "permissions": {
    "deny": ["Bash(curl*)", "Bash(wget*)"]
  },
  "mcpServers": {
    "allowedServers": ["internal-docs", "jira"]
  }
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Draft a managed-policy settings file for a hypothetical fintech engineering org: list three tool categories to deny by default and one MCP server to allow, with a one-sentence justification for each.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Enterprise policy is not just documentation that developers are asked to follow; managed settings are enforced at the tool level and take precedence over a developer's local configuration.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Enterprise usage?

  • Once Enterprise usage is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Enterprise policy is not just documentation that developers are asked to follow; managed settings are enforced at the tool level and take precedence over a developer's local configuration.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Enterprise usage in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

  • Claude Code monitoring usage

Team standards

Team standards for Claude Code means committing a shared CLAUDE.md, custom slash commands, and settings to source control so agentic workflows are consistent, reviewable, and improve over time as a team artifact, not tribal knowledge held by one developer.

In plain terms

It is like maintaining a shared style guide and toolbox instead of every engineer building their own private version.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a shared CLAUDE.md and slash-command library checked into the repository so every developer's agent follows the same conventions, test commands, and review expectations instead of each person building private, inconsistent habits.

A concrete example

A team creates a custom command that runs the same pre-deploy validation for every engineer, instead of each person remembering the steps or writing slightly different prompts.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Shared, versioned agent configuration improves consistency and onboarding speed, but it needs an owner and review process just like any other shared code, or it drifts out of date the same way stale documentation does

Try it in code

# .claude/commands/deploy-check.md
---
description: Run the team's pre-deploy validation checklist
---
Run the full test suite, check for uncommitted migrations,
verify the changelog was updated, and report pass/fail for
each check before recommending a deploy.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write one custom slash command for a repetitive task your team does often, and commit it to .claude/commands so the whole team shares it.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A CLAUDE.md is not set once and forgotten; like a README, it needs the same maintenance and pull-request review as the code it describes, or it starts giving the agent wrong instructions.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Team standards?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Team standards is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A CLAUDE.md is not set once and forgotten; like a README, it needs the same maintenance and pull-request review as the code it describes, or it starts giving the agent wrong instructions.

Going deeper

  1. Explain Team standards in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

  • Claude Code skills

Automation

Automation with Claude Code means running it non-interactively, typically in CI or a script, to perform a scoped task like generating a summary, checking a diff against a policy, or drafting release notes, without a human present to approve each step in real time.

In plain terms

It is like a factory inspection station that runs the same automated check on every item passing through, instead of a human checking each one by hand.

How it works

In practice it comes down to headless print mode invoked non-interactively from scripts or CI pipelines, returning structured output that downstream tooling can parse, combined with hooks that trigger on specific lifecycle events.

A concrete example

A CI pipeline runs Claude Code in headless mode on every pull request to summarize the diff and flag if it touches a restricted file list, posting the result as a comment automatically.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Automation removes the human confirmation step for speed and scale, so it should be reserved for lower-risk, reviewable-after-the-fact tasks, while higher-risk actions like merging or deploying should keep a human approval gate

Try it in code

# .github/workflows/pr-summary.yml (excerpt)
- name: Summarize PR with Claude Code
  run: |
    claude -p "summarize this diff and flag if it touches
    /billing or /auth" --output-format json < pr.diff

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a small CI step that runs Claude Code in headless mode to check whether a pull request diff modifies any file in a protected list, and have it fail the build if so.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Running Claude Code headlessly does not make its output automatically trustworthy just because a human isn't watching in real time; automated agent output still needs the same evals and spot-checks as any other automated system.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Automation?

  • Correct answer: Running Claude Code headlessly does not make its output automatically trustworthy just because a human isn't watching in real time; automated agent output still needs the same evals and spot-checks as any other automated system.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Automation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

  1. Explain Automation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

Troubleshooting

Troubleshooting Claude Code sessions means diagnosing why an agent produced a bad result: was it missing context, blocked by a permission it needed, confused by stale conversation history, or simply wrong, and applying the fix that matches the actual cause.

In plain terms

It is like debugging a system outage by checking logs and permissions before assuming the code itself is broken.

How it works

In practice it comes down to distinguishing between context problems from stale or missing information, permission problems from a blocked tool call, and model problems from a genuinely wrong plan, and using /clear, /compact, or a plan-mode restart to recover instead of repeating the same failing prompt.

A concrete example

An agent repeatedly fails to run a build command; the real cause turns out to be a permission denial it silently retried around, not a misunderstanding of the build system, so the fix is adjusting settings.json, not rewording the prompt.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Restarting a session with a fresh /clear is the fastest recovery but throws away useful context, while digging into why a specific step failed takes longer but prevents the same failure from recurring

Try it in code

# Before rewording your prompt, check the actual cause:

/permissions   # did a tool call get silently denied?
/compact       # is context stale or contradictory?
/cost          # did the session run out of budget mid-task?

# Rewording only helps if the cause was genuinely a bad prompt,
# not a permission, context, or budget problem.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Next time an agent produces a clearly wrong result, before rewriting your prompt, check whether it hit a permission denial, ran out of relevant context, or actually reasoned incorrectly, and note which one it was.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A wrong or stuck agent is not always a bad-prompt problem; it is often a context, permission, or tooling issue that no amount of prompt rewording will fix.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Troubleshooting?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Troubleshooting is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A wrong or stuck agent is not always a bad-prompt problem; it is often a context, permission, or tooling issue that no amount of prompt rewording will fix.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Troubleshooting in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Claude Code troubleshooting
  • Claude Code install troubleshooting

Claude lab

The Claude Code lab is a hands-on exercise where you run one real, moderately complex task end to end through the agent, from planning to tests passing, then critique the workflow itself rather than just the code output.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to picking one real repository task such as a bug fix, small feature, or refactor, running it fully through Claude Code with plan mode, execution, and test verification, then writing down what worked, what needed correction, and what permission or context change would improve the next run.

It is like a flight simulator session where the debrief afterward matters as much as the flight.

The brief

Pick one real, non-trivial issue in a repository you own, run it fully through Claude Code including tests, and write three sentences on what you would configure differently before doing this again.

A worked reference

A lab might take a real open issue from a personal project, run it through plan mode, review the plan, execute, run tests, and produce a short retro on which parts needed human correction.

Starting point in code

# Lab: run one real issue through the full loop.
claude "read issue #142, propose a plan, implement it, and run
the test suite. Stop and ask before touching any file outside
src/orders/."

# After it finishes, write down: what did you have to correct,
# and what CLAUDE.md rule would have prevented that correction?

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A realistic, messy task teaches more about agentic workflow limits than a clean toy example, but it also takes longer and can surface unrelated codebase problems that distract from the learning goal

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • The goal of this lab is not a perfect diff on the first try; it is learning where you had to intervene and why, since that is exactly the judgment production usage requires.

Going deeper

References

  • Claude Code common workflows

Mini project

The mini project asks you to ship one complete, reusable Claude Code workflow artifact, such as a project CLAUDE.md, a custom command, or a CI automation, that a teammate could adopt immediately.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to building a small internal tool or workflow around Claude Code, such as a custom slash command, a CI check, or an onboarding CLAUDE.md, that another engineer can pick up and use without you explaining it.

It is like building a shared tool for the toolbox instead of a one-off trick only you know how to use.

The brief

Build a starter kit with a settings.json, one custom command, and a CLAUDE.md template for a stack you use often, then hand it to a teammate and see if they can use it with zero explanation.

A worked reference

A mini project might be a starter directory template for a common stack with a test command, a lint rule, and a pull-request summary command that a new repository can copy on day one.

Starting point in code

# .claude/commands/pr-summary.md
---
description: Summarize the current diff for a PR description
---
Read the current git diff against main. Write a PR summary with
a one-line title, a bullet list of changes, and a test plan.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A narrow, well-documented artifact is more likely to actually get adopted by teammates than an ambitious but half-finished framework

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A working demo on your machine is not the same as a reusable artifact; if a teammate can't run it without your help, the mini project isn't done.

Going deeper

References

  • Claude Code skills

Capstone

The capstone project designs a full operating model for using Claude Code across a real engineering team: scope of allowed autonomous work, required review gates, shared configuration, and the metrics that prove it is actually improving delivery rather than just increasing AI usage.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to designing a complete agentic-coding operating model for a team, including which tasks agents are allowed to do unsupervised, which need human approval, what CLAUDE.md and permission settings enforce that, and how success is measured over time.

It is like writing the operating manual for a new department before it launches, not after something goes wrong.

The brief

Write a one-page operating model for a five-person engineering team: three task types agents may do unsupervised, two that always need human review, and two metrics that would tell you the rollout is working.

A worked reference

A capstone might define that agents can autonomously fix lint failures and write tests, but any change touching authentication or billing requires a named human reviewer and a documented reason in the pull request.

Starting point in code

# One-page operating model (excerpt)
autonomous:   ["fix lint failures", "write missing tests"]
needs_review: ["schema migrations", "auth or billing changes"]
metrics:      ["% PRs from agents merged without rework",
               "checkpoint rejection rate", "cost per merged PR"]

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A comprehensive operating model gives clear guardrails and measurement, but it takes real effort to design and maintain, so it should be scoped to the team's actual risk profile rather than copied wholesale from another org

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • An agentic-coding rollout is not complete once the tool is installed; without agreed scope, review gates, and metrics, usage becomes inconsistent and risk becomes invisible.

Going deeper

References

  • Claude Code settings
  • Claude Code monitoring usage

Interview pack

The Claude Code interview pack prepares you to discuss agentic coding tools credibly in interviews: what you configured, what went wrong, how you scoped risk, and what technical trade-offs you understand deeply enough to defend under follow-up questions.

In plain terms

It is like preparing case studies from real incidents instead of a rehearsed pitch about how exciting the tool is.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real agentic-coding rollout you led or contributed to, paired with technical answers about permission models, context management, and where autonomous coding tools fail, so you can speak to both the judgment and the mechanics.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how you evaluate whether an AI coding tool is safe to give more autonomy describes a concrete permission model, a review gate, and a measured outcome, not a general opinion about AI coding being the future.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

// Structure a technical answer, don't just narrate features.
const answer = {
  situation: "Agent had broad file-write access with no test gate",
  risk: "A wrong autonomous edit could reach main unreviewed",
  fix: "Added a required-tests-pass hook + human checkpoint on auth/**",
  result: "Zero unreviewed auth changes in the following quarter"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a time an agentic coding tool made a risky or wrong change, what safeguard was missing, and what you changed afterward.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Reciting feature names like subagents, hooks, or MCP is not the same as showing judgment about when and why you'd use them; interviewers probe for the trade-off reasoning behind the feature.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Reciting feature names like subagents, hooks, or MCP is not the same as showing judgment about when and why you'd use them; interviewers probe for the trade-off reasoning behind the feature.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Claude Code overview
  • Claude Code security

GitHub Copilot

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: GitHub Copilot is like an IDE-native teammate: best at staying close to the code you are editing, tests you are running, and pull requests you are reviewing. It accelerates SDLC work when prompts include intent, constraints, files, and acceptance criteria.

Copilot overview

GitHub Copilot is an AI pair programmer embedded directly in the developer's editor, offering inline code completions, a chat interface for questions and multi-file changes, and an agent mode that can autonomously plan and execute larger coding tasks.

In plain terms

It is like having a fast typing partner sitting next to you who can also step away to handle a whole task on their own when asked.

How it works

In practice it comes down to an IDE-integrated completion engine offering inline ghost-text suggestions, combined with a chat interface and an increasingly autonomous agent mode that can make multi-file edits and run terminal commands inside the editor, all grounded in the open files and workspace context.

A concrete example

A developer typing a function signature sees an inline suggestion for the likely implementation, then switches to chat to ask where else that function is called before accepting a broader refactor via agent mode.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Inline completions are fast and low-risk since the developer approves every keystroke, while agent mode is more powerful for multi-file tasks but requires the same diff review discipline as any other agentic tool

Try it in code

// Same task, three modes of increasing autonomy:
// 1) inline completion — accept/reject per keystroke
// 2) chat — "@workspace where is validateOrder() called?"
// 3) agent mode — "add pagination to /orders, update tests"

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one coding task, use inline completion, then chat, then agent mode if available, and compare how much you had to verify manually in each mode.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Copilot is not one single feature; inline completions, chat, and agent mode have different risk profiles and review needs, so treating all three the same way misses where actual review attention is needed.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Copilot overview?

  • Once Copilot overview is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Copilot is not one single feature; inline completions, chat, and agent mode have different risk profiles and review needs, so treating all three the same way misses where actual review attention is needed.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Copilot overview in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation

IDE workflows

IDE workflows are the day-to-day patterns for using Copilot inside an editor: accepting or rejecting inline suggestions, using inline chat for small scoped fixes, and using the chat panel for questions that need broader workspace context.

In plain terms

It is like knowing when to use a quick keyboard shortcut versus when to stop and have a longer conversation with a colleague.

How it works

In practice it comes down to ghost-text completions accepted with a keystroke, inline chat for quick fixes scoped to a selection, and full chat panels with workspace context for questions spanning multiple files, each suited to different task sizes.

A concrete example

A developer uses inline completion for boilerplate, inline chat to fix one broken line without leaving the cursor, and the chat panel to ask a cross-file question with workspace context pulled in automatically.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Inline completions keep the developer in flow with minimal interruption but only see local context, while chat with workspace context sees more but requires switching attention out of the code

Try it in code

// Inline chat: scoped fix without leaving the cursor
// select the broken line, then:
// Cmd+I -> "this throws on empty input, add a guard clause"

// Chat panel: cross-file question
// "@workspace why does OrderTest#testEmptyCart fail?"

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Time yourself completing one small task using only inline completions, then again using only chat, and note which felt faster and which produced code you trusted more.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Accepting an inline suggestion is not free of review responsibility just because it appeared instantly; it still needs the same scrutiny as any line of code you'd write yourself.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about IDE workflows?

  • Once IDE workflows is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Accepting an inline suggestion is not free of review responsibility just because it appeared instantly; it still needs the same scrutiny as any line of code you'd write yourself.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain IDE workflows in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation

Agent mode

Agent mode is Copilot's autonomous execution mode: instead of suggesting one completion at a time, it takes a task description, plans a multi-file change, executes it, and can run commands to verify the result.

In plain terms

It is like handing off a whole ticket to a capable teammate instead of dictating each line they should type.

How it works

In practice it comes down to given a task description, agent mode reads relevant files, proposes a multi-step plan, edits multiple files, and can run terminal commands, iterating based on command output, similar in shape to other agentic coding tools but embedded in the IDE session.

A concrete example

Asked to add pagination to an API endpoint, agent mode identifies the route file, the response schema, and the relevant test file, edits all three, and runs the test suite to check its own work.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Agent mode handles larger tasks in one request than inline completion ever could, but the larger the diff it produces in one pass, the more review effort is needed before merging, so scope still matters

Try it in code

// Prompt with explicit scope to keep the diff reviewable:
// "Add pagination to GET /orders. Only touch orders.controller.ts,
//  orders.service.ts, and orders.test.ts. Run tests when done."

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Give agent mode a task with an explicit file scope versus the same task with no scope, and compare the size and relevance of the resulting diff.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Agent mode succeeding on a task once does not mean it always scopes changes correctly; like any agentic tool, unscoped or ambiguous requests risk producing a broader diff than intended.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Agent mode?

  • Once Agent mode is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Agent mode succeeding on a task once does not mean it always scopes changes correctly; like any agentic tool, unscoped or ambiguous requests risk producing a broader diff than intended.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Agent mode in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation

Prompting

Prompting Copilot effectively means giving it explicit context such as open files, selections, and workspace references, plus a specific instruction, since it can only reason about what it can actually see, not the intent in your head.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between telling a contractor fix the bathroom versus pointing at the exact leaking pipe and describing the fix you want.

How it works

In practice it comes down to supplying explicit context such as file references, workspace mentions, and selected code, along with a specific, scoped instruction rather than a vague request, since Copilot's suggestion quality is highly sensitive to what context is actually visible to it.

A concrete example

Asking chat to fix the bug produces a guess; asking it to add a guard clause for empty array input and a test for that case gives Copilot enough to act correctly on the first try.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Vague prompts are faster to type but produce more generic or wrong suggestions requiring rework, while specific prompts take longer to write but usually need less correction afterward

Try it in code

// Vague — produces a guess:
// "fix the bug"

// Specific — gives Copilot enough to act correctly:
// "parseOrder() throws when items is an empty array; add a guard
//  clause that returns an empty total, and add a test for it"

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take one real bug you fixed recently, write the vaguest possible prompt and the most specific possible prompt for it, and compare what each produces.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A wrong Copilot suggestion is not necessarily a model limitation; it is often a context or specificity problem in what was asked, the same way a vague ticket produces a wrong implementation from a human engineer.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Prompting?

  • Correct answer: A wrong Copilot suggestion is not necessarily a model limitation; it is often a context or specificity problem in what was asked, the same way a vague ticket produces a wrong implementation from a human engineer.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Prompting is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Prompting in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation

Workspace setup

Workspace setup means configuring repository-level context, most notably a copilot-instructions.md file describing conventions, build commands, and constraints, so every developer's Copilot suggestions in that repo start from the same baseline.

In plain terms

It is like posting the house rules on the wall so every new visitor follows the same conventions without being told individually.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a repository-wide instructions file that gives context to every Copilot interaction in that repo, similar in purpose to other agent memory files, plus IDE and extension configuration.

A concrete example

A copilot-instructions.md might state the project uses strict TypeScript, prefers named exports, and requires new endpoints to include API annotations, so suggestions default to those conventions without each developer repeating them.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Repository-level instructions reduce repeated context-setting across a team, but like any shared configuration, they need an owner and review or they drift out of sync with actual codebase conventions

Try it in code

<!-- .github/copilot-instructions.md -->
# Conventions
- Strict TypeScript, no implicit any
- Named exports only
- New endpoints must include OpenAPI annotations
- Test command: npm run test:ci

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a copilot-instructions.md for a real repository with the exact test command, one naming convention, and one thing suggestions should never do.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Workspace instructions are not guaranteed to be followed on every single suggestion the same way a lint rule is enforced; they bias the model's behavior but don't replace CI checks for real conformance.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Workspace setup?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Workspace instructions are not guaranteed to be followed on every single suggestion the same way a lint rule is enforced; they bias the model's behavior but don't replace CI checks for real conformance.
  • Once Workspace setup is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Workspace setup in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation

Code review

Copilot's code review feature reads a pull request diff and generates comments flagging likely bugs, style violations, or missing tests, intended to reduce the load on human reviewers rather than replace their approval.

In plain terms

It is like a fast first-pass reviewer who flags the obvious issues so the senior reviewer's time goes to the judgment calls.

How it works

In practice it comes down to Copilot reading a pull request's diff and generating review comments on potential bugs, missing tests, or convention violations, surfaced directly in the PR interface alongside human reviewer comments.

A concrete example

On a pull request that adds a new database query, Copilot flags a missing index consideration and an unhandled null case, which the human reviewer then evaluates rather than raising from scratch.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Automated first-pass review catches mechanical issues quickly and consistently, but it can generate false positives that erode reviewer trust if not tuned, and it can miss product-context judgment a human would catch

Try it in code

// Copilot review comment on a PR diff:
// "Line 58: new column is nullable here but NOT NULL in schema.sql —
//  inserts without this field will fail. Consider a default value
//  or making the field required in the type."

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take a real pull request, read Copilot's generated review comments or simulate this by asking chat to review the diff, and judge how many were genuinely useful versus noise.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • An approving or quiet automated review pass is not equivalent to human sign-off; the human reviewer remains accountable for what merges, regardless of what an AI review pass flagged or missed.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Code review?

  • Correct answer: An approving or quiet automated review pass is not equivalent to human sign-off; the human reviewer remains accountable for what merges, regardless of what an AI review pass flagged or missed.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Code review is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Code review in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation

Testing

Using Copilot for testing means generating candidate test cases for existing or new code, then verifying that those tests actually assert meaningful behavior and pass in CI, not treating generation itself as proof of coverage.

In plain terms

It is like having someone suggest exam questions for you: useful, but you still have to check the questions actually test the right thing.

How it works

In practice it comes down to generating test cases from an existing function's signature and behavior, or from a natural-language description of expected behavior, then running them to check they actually exercise meaningful edge cases rather than trivially passing.

A concrete example

Copilot suggests three tests for a validation function covering valid, empty, and malformed input, but a developer still checks that the malformed-input test asserts the correct error rather than just calling the function without failing.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Generated tests broaden coverage quickly, but a generated test that trivially passes or asserts something too weak gives false confidence, so a human still has to judge test quality, not just test presence

Try it in code

// Generated tests for validate(input) — classify before trusting:
test("valid input passes", () => { expect(validate(good)).toBe(true); });
test("empty input rejected", () => { expect(validate([])).toBe(false); });
test("malformed input rejected", () => {
  expect(() => validate(malformed)).toThrow("invalid shape"); // check the assertion, not just "no crash"
});

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Ask Copilot to generate tests for one function, then classify each generated test as meaningful, redundant, or too weak to catch a real bug.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A green test suite that Copilot helped generate is not automatically strong coverage; a test that never fails for the right reason provides no real safety net.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Testing?

  • Correct answer: A green test suite that Copilot helped generate is not automatically strong coverage; a test that never fails for the right reason provides no real safety net.
  • Once Testing is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Testing in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation

Security

Copilot's security features include vulnerability pattern detection in suggested code and organizational controls over what repositories, code, and public-code matches Copilot can draw from or expose.

In plain terms

It is like a spellchecker for common security mistakes, catching well-known error patterns before they get committed.

How it works

In practice it comes down to flagging common vulnerability patterns such as SQL injection, hardcoded secrets, and insecure deserialization in generated or existing code via built-in security scanning, plus org-level policies that restrict what Copilot can suggest or access.

A concrete example

Copilot flags a suggested SQL query built with string concatenation as an injection risk and proposes a parameterized query instead, before the developer ever commits the vulnerable version.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Built-in vulnerability detection catches common, well-known patterns cheaply, but it is not a substitute for a dedicated security review or static analysis tooling on code that reaches production

Try it in code

// Suggested (vulnerable):
// db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + userId);

// Copilot-flagged fix (parameterized):
db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?", [userId]);

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Ask Copilot to generate a small piece of code that handles user input like a search query, then intentionally check whether the suggestion is vulnerable to injection before assuming it's safe.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Passing Copilot's security suggestions is not equivalent to a security review; it catches known common patterns, not business-logic vulnerabilities or the specific threat model of your application.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Security?

  • Once Security is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Passing Copilot's security suggestions is not equivalent to a security review; it catches known common patterns, not business-logic vulnerabilities or the specific threat model of your application.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Security in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation
  • OWASP Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications

Enterprise controls

Enterprise controls let administrators govern Copilot at the organization level: which features are enabled, which files or repositories are excluded from suggestions, whether suggestions matching public code are blocked, and what usage is logged for audit.

In plain terms

It is like a company-wide access policy that applies consistently across every employee's account, instead of trusting each person to configure their own restrictions.

How it works

In practice it comes down to org-level policy settings such as enabling or disabling features per team, content exclusion for specific file paths, and public code matching filters, plus audit logging so administrators can govern usage across every seat rather than relying on individual configuration.

A concrete example

An enterprise admin excludes a repository containing licensed third-party code from Copilot's context and blocks suggestions that match public code closely enough to raise licensing risk.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Centralized policy gives legal and security teams confidence and consistency, but overly broad exclusions can degrade suggestion quality in unrelated parts of a large monorepo if scoping is too coarse

Try it in code

{
  "content_exclusions": ["licensed-vendor-code/**"],
  "public_code_matching": "block",
  "auditLogging": true
}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Draft a content-exclusion policy for one hypothetical repository containing sensitive licensing terms, specifying exactly what should be excluded and why.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Enterprise policy is not just a compliance checkbox; content exclusion and public-code filtering are enforced controls that materially change what Copilot can see and suggest, not just guidelines.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Enterprise controls?

  • Correct answer: Enterprise policy is not just a compliance checkbox; content exclusion and public-code filtering are enforced controls that materially change what Copilot can see and suggest, not just guidelines.
  • Once Enterprise controls is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Enterprise controls in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation
  • GitHub Copilot Trust Center

Team rollout

Team rollout is the process of introducing Copilot across an engineering organization deliberately: starting with a pilot, establishing shared conventions and instructions, training developers on effective use, and expanding based on measured outcomes.

In plain terms

It is like introducing any new tool to a factory floor: a pilot line first, feedback collected, then a wider rollout once the kinks are worked out.

How it works

In practice it comes down to phased adoption through a pilot team and feedback loop, then broader rollout, paired with shared workspace configuration and training, rather than enabling the tool org-wide on day one with no usage standards.

A concrete example

A rollout might start with one team using Copilot for two weeks, collect feedback on which suggestion types were useful versus noisy, then codify a shared instructions file before expanding company-wide.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A phased rollout takes longer to reach full adoption but surfaces workflow and policy issues on a small group before they affect the whole org, while an immediate org-wide rollout is faster but can normalize inconsistent, unreviewed usage habits early

Try it in code

const rolloutPlan = [
  { stage: "pilot", team: "checkout", durationWeeks: 2 },
  { stage: "feedback", action: "classify suggestions: useful vs noisy" },
  { stage: "expansion", gate: "shared copilot-instructions.md exists" }
];

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design a three-stage rollout plan of pilot, feedback, and expansion for introducing Copilot to a twenty-person engineering org, with one success criterion per stage.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Rollout success is not the same as license activation count; a team using Copilot without shared standards or review discipline can look adopted while quietly accumulating unreviewed code.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Team rollout?

  • Correct answer: Rollout success is not the same as license activation count; a team using Copilot without shared standards or review discipline can look adopted while quietly accumulating unreviewed code.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Team rollout is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Team rollout in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation

Metrics

Metrics for Copilot adoption combine usage data such as acceptance rate and active seats with downstream engineering outcomes such as cycle time and defect rate, to judge whether the tool is actually improving delivery, not just being used.

In plain terms

It is like judging a new factory machine not just by how often it's switched on, but by whether output quality and speed actually improved.

How it works

In practice it comes down to org-level usage dashboards and APIs reporting suggestion acceptance rate, active users, and language or IDE breakdown, which should be paired with downstream engineering metrics such as cycle time and defect rate to judge actual impact, not just usage volume.

A concrete example

A team notices high suggestion-acceptance rate but no improvement in cycle time, prompting an investigation into whether accepted suggestions are creating more rework during review than they save during writing.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Usage metrics are easy to collect automatically but only measure activity, while outcome metrics like defects and cycle time are harder to attribute directly to Copilot but are what actually matters to leadership

Try it in code

const copilotMetrics = {
  usage: "acceptance rate, active seats, suggestions/day",
  outcome: "cycle time, review comment volume, defect-escape rate"
};
// Track both together — usage alone doesn't prove impact.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Pick one usage metric and one outcome metric you would track together for a Copilot rollout, and describe what pattern in both would tell you the rollout is genuinely working.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A high acceptance rate does not by itself prove Copilot is improving quality or speed; it only proves suggestions are being accepted, which could include accepting weak code under time pressure.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Metrics?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A high acceptance rate does not by itself prove Copilot is improving quality or speed; it only proves suggestions are being accepted, which could include accepting weak code under time pressure.
  • Once Metrics is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Metrics in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation

Best practices

Best practices for Copilot usage bundle prompting discipline, shared workspace configuration, and unchanged review standards into a repeatable team habit, so suggestion quality and code quality stay consistent across developers.

In plain terms

It is like a team's shared checklist for using any powerful tool safely and consistently, rather than everyone improvising their own rules.

How it works

In practice it comes down to combining well-scoped prompts, repository-level instructions, mandatory test verification, and human review discipline into a consistent team habit, rather than relying on ad hoc individual usage patterns.

A concrete example

A team's best-practice checklist might require running tests on any agent-mode change before requesting review, and treating an accepted suggestion in a pull request the same as hand-written code for review purposes.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Codified best practices take effort to write and enforce, but without them, usage quality varies wildly by individual developer habit, which is harder to fix after code has already merged

Try it in code

const checklist = [
  "run tests on any agent-mode change before requesting review",
  "review accepted suggestions like hand-written code, not exempt from it",
  "keep copilot-instructions.md in sync with real conventions",
  "scope agent-mode prompts to specific files when the task allows it"
];

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a five-item Copilot best-practices checklist for your team, focused on what changes about review, not what changes about writing code.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Best practices for Copilot are not fundamentally different from best practices for any code contribution; the review bar should not lower just because a suggestion came from the tool instead of a person.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Best practices?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Best practices for Copilot are not fundamentally different from best practices for any code contribution; the review bar should not lower just because a suggestion came from the tool instead of a person.
  • Once Best practices is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Best practices in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation

Troubleshooting

Troubleshooting Copilot issues means diagnosing whether a bad or missing suggestion is caused by insufficient visible context, an organizational policy blocking behavior, or an actual model limitation, and applying the matching fix.

In plain terms

It is like checking whether a stalled process is blocked by a permission setting before assuming the tool itself is broken.

How it works

In practice it comes down to distinguishing between context problems where Copilot can't see the relevant file, configuration problems where a policy or exclusion is blocking expected suggestions, and genuine model limitations, and fixing the actual cause rather than repeatedly rephrasing.

A concrete example

A developer confused why Copilot won't suggest anything in a file discovers the repository's content-exclusion policy blocks that path, which no amount of prompt rewording would fix.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Rephrasing a prompt is the fastest thing to try but only fixes prompt-caused problems, while checking configuration and context takes longer but is required when the cause is not the wording at all

Try it in code

// "Copilot won't suggest anything in this file" — check first:
// 1. is the file open / referenced?
// 2. is this path in content_exclusions?
// 3. is there an org policy disabling this feature for this repo?
// Only reword the prompt after ruling out 1-3.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Next time Copilot gives an unexpectedly poor suggestion, check whether the relevant file was actually open or referenced before concluding it's a model limitation.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A missing or wrong suggestion is not always a model-limitation problem; org policy, content exclusion, and closed files are common, checkable causes that get missed.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Troubleshooting?

  • Once Troubleshooting is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A missing or wrong suggestion is not always a model-limitation problem; org policy, content exclusion, and closed files are common, checkable causes that get missed.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Troubleshooting in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation

Copilot lab

The Copilot lab is a hands-on comparison exercise: complete the same real task using inline completion, chat, and agent mode, and record how much verification and correction each required.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to running one real task through inline completion, chat, and agent mode within the same session, recording how much manual correction each required, to build calibrated judgment about which mode fits which task size.

It is like timing yourself doing the same errand three different ways to learn which one actually suits which situation.

The brief

Pick one small real task, complete it using each of the three modes separately, and write down which required the least correction and why.

A worked reference

A lab might implement one small feature three times using inline-only, chat-assisted, and agent-mode approaches, and compare time spent, lines changed, and review issues found in each.

Starting point in code

// Lab: implement the same small feature 3 ways, compare.
const results = {
  inlineOnly: { timeMin: null, linesChanged: null, reviewIssues: null },
  chatAssisted: { timeMin: null, linesChanged: null, reviewIssues: null },
  agentMode: { timeMin: null, linesChanged: null, reviewIssues: null }
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Comparing all three modes on one task takes longer than just using whichever mode is habitual, but it builds real calibration for when each mode is worth using

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • The best mode is not fixed; it depends on task size and risk, so the lab's value is calibration, not proving one mode is universally superior.

Going deeper

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation

Mini project

The mini project ships a complete, reusable Copilot setup for a real repository: instructions file, review checklist, and any team policy notes, documented well enough for a teammate to adopt directly.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to shipping one reusable Copilot configuration artifact for a team, such as a copilot-instructions.md plus a documented review checklist, that another developer can adopt without further explanation.

It is like handing over a fully labeled toolbox instead of a pile of tools with no instructions.

The brief

Write and commit a copilot-instructions.md plus a short review checklist for a real repository, then ask a teammate to follow it without additional explanation.

A worked reference

A mini project might produce a copilot-instructions.md for a real repo plus a one-page review policy for Copilot-assisted pull requests that a new hire could follow unaided.

Starting point in code

<!-- .github/copilot-instructions.md + review-checklist.md -->
# Review checklist for Copilot-assisted PRs
- [ ] Tests run and pass locally
- [ ] Non-trivial accepted suggestions explained in PR description
- [ ] No new dependency added without approval

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A tightly scoped, well-documented setup is more likely to be adopted than an ambitious org-wide policy proposal built without real usage feedback

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A working instructions file on your machine is not the same as a shared team artifact; if it isn't committed and explained, it won't outlast your own usage.

Going deeper

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete organizational Copilot program: phased rollout, shared configuration standards, enterprise policy such as exclusions and audit, unchanged review requirements, and the usage-plus-outcome metrics used to judge success.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to designing the complete organizational approach to Copilot, including rollout plan, workspace configuration standards, enterprise policy, review standards, and the metrics used to judge success, as one coherent document.

It is like writing the full playbook for introducing a powerful new tool into an organization responsibly, not just switching it on.

The brief

Write a one-page Copilot program document for a hundred-engineer org: rollout stages, one required configuration standard, one enterprise policy, and two metrics.

A worked reference

A capstone might specify a three-stage rollout, a required instructions file per repo, an enterprise content-exclusion list, and a monthly report combining acceptance rate with defect-escape rate.

Starting point in code

const copilotProgram = {
  rollout: ["pilot", "feedback", "org-wide"],
  requiredPerRepo: "copilot-instructions.md",
  enterprisePolicy: { contentExclusions: ["vendor-licensed/**"] },
  metrics: ["acceptance rate", "defect-escape rate"]
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A complete program forces explicit policy and metric decisions up front, which takes more effort than an informal rollout where everyone just gets a license, but it avoids the common failure of inconsistent, unmeasured adoption

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • An organizational Copilot program is not complete once licenses are distributed; without configuration standards, review policy, and outcome metrics, adoption data alone can't show whether it is helping.

Going deeper

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation
  • GitHub Copilot Trust Center

Interview pack

The Copilot interview pack prepares you to discuss AI pair-programming tool adoption credibly: the differences between completion, chat, and agent mode, how you'd roll it out safely, and how you'd measure real impact versus usage alone.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a tool rollout with real before-and-after data, not just describing the tool's features.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real Copilot workflow you designed or improved, paired with technical answers distinguishing completion, chat, and agent mode, and how you'd measure whether adoption is actually working.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how you would roll out Copilot to a team describes a phased plan, a shared instructions file, unchanged review standards, and a metric combining acceptance with defect rate, not just enthusiasm for the tool.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "Team's high acceptance rate hid a rise in review rework",
  cause: "suggestions were accepted under time pressure, not reviewed",
  fix: "added 'review accepted code like your own' to the checklist",
  result: "review comment volume dropped the next sprint"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a time a team's Copilot usage caused a problem, such as an unreviewed suggestion, a licensing concern, or noisy metrics, and what you changed as a result.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Naming Copilot's features like agent mode, chat, and completions is not the same as showing judgment about rollout risk, review standards, and measurement, which is what technical interviews actually probe.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Naming Copilot's features like agent mode, chat, and completions is not the same as showing judgment about rollout risk, review standards, and measurement, which is what technical interviews actually probe.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • GitHub Copilot documentation

OpenAI Codex

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: Codex-style agents are like task-focused coding workers: give them a repository, a goal, constraints, and a validation path. They are most useful when the work can be expressed as a verifiable code change rather than an open-ended conversation.

Codex overview

OpenAI Codex is an agentic coding tool available as a CLI, IDE extension, and cloud/web interface that reads a codebase, plans changes, edits files, and runs commands, with configurable levels of autonomy through sandboxing and approval policies.

In plain terms

It is like a contractor who can work inside your house alone once you've told them exactly which rooms they're allowed in, versus one who calls you for permission before touching anything.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a CLI and cloud agent that reads repository context, including an AGENTS.md instructions file, proposes a plan, edits files, and runs commands, with a sandbox mode governing filesystem and network access and an approval policy governing which actions still require explicit user confirmation.

A concrete example

A developer runs Codex CLI in workspace-write mode inside a project directory; it can read and edit files and run routine commands autonomously, but any command that would reach outside the workspace or touch the network still prompts for approval.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Higher autonomy modes let Codex complete larger tasks without interruption, but they increase the damage radius if the agent is manipulated or makes a mistake, so sandbox mode should match the actual risk of the repository and task

Try it in code

# Launch Codex with an explicit sandbox + approval policy
codex --sandbox workspace-write --ask-for-approval on-request \
  "add a rate limiter to the /login route, then run the tests"

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real repository, decide which sandbox mode, read-only, workspace-write, or danger-full-access, fits the task, and justify the choice based on what could go wrong.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.
