Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Codex is not a single fixed tool with one behavior; its actual capability and risk profile is defined by which sandbox mode and approval policy are configured, and two teams running Codex can have very different safety postures from the same underlying tool.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Codex overview?

  • Once Codex overview is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Codex is not a single fixed tool with one behavior; its actual capability and risk profile is defined by which sandbox mode and approval policy are configured, and two teams running Codex can have very different safety postures from the same underlying tool.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Codex overview in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI Codex overview

Model selection

Model selection chooses the smallest, safest, fastest model that satisfies the product's quality and operational requirements.

In plain terms

It is like choosing a vehicle: a truck, bike, and race car are each right for different jobs.

For non-technical readers

For a budget owner, the practical rule is: don't default to the newest, most expensive model just because it's the most capable one available. A simpler, cheaper model is often good enough for a well-defined task like sorting emails into categories, while only your hardest problems, like drafting a nuanced legal argument, actually need the top-tier, more expensive model. Asking your team to justify model choice against actual task difficulty, not just default to the flagship model, is one of the simplest ways to control AI costs.

How it works

In practice it comes down to task fit, context length, tool use, multilingual ability, latency, cost, safety, license, and deployment target.

A concrete example

A classifier may use a small fast model while a complex legal drafting workflow uses a larger model with stronger eval performance.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Larger models improve capability but increase cost, latency, and governance burden.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create a model scorecard with quality, latency, cost, privacy, and operations columns.

  1. Build or sketch the smallest version that actually runs or can be reviewed.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Do not pick a model from hype; pick it from eval results on your tasks.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Model selection?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Do not pick a model from hype; pick it from eval results on your tasks.
  • Once Model selection is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Model selection in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI API documentation
  • Anthropic Claude documentation

Prompting

Prompting Codex effectively means giving it a specific, scoped task description plus the repository context it needs, via AGENTS.md or explicit file references, since a vague instruction produces a vague or overly broad plan.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between telling a contractor fix the house versus pointing at the exact leaking pipe and the tools available to fix it.

How it works

In practice it comes down to combining a specific task instruction with relevant repository context, either automatically discovered through AGENTS.md files or explicitly referenced, so Codex's plan and edits stay scoped to the actual intended change rather than guessing at scope.

A concrete example

Asking Codex to fix the bug in the login flow produces a broader, riskier plan than asking it to fix the null pointer exception in auth login when the session token is missing, adding a guard clause and a test.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Detailed, scoped prompts take more time to write but produce smaller, more reviewable diffs, while vague prompts are faster to type but risk a broader, harder-to-review change

Try it in code

# Vague — produces a guess:
codex "fix the bug in the login flow"

# Specific — gives Codex enough to act correctly:
codex "auth/login.ts throws a null pointer when session token is
missing; add a guard clause and a test for that case"

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take one real bug you've fixed, write the vaguest possible Codex prompt and the most specific possible prompt for it, and compare the resulting plans.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A wrong or overly broad Codex change is not necessarily a model limitation; it's often a scope and specificity problem in the prompt, the same way a vague ticket produces a wrong implementation from a human engineer.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Prompting?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: A wrong or overly broad Codex change is not necessarily a model limitation; it's often a scope and specificity problem in the prompt, the same way a vague ticket produces a wrong implementation from a human engineer.
  • Once Prompting is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Prompting in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI Codex overview

Agent workflows

Agent workflows for Codex are patterns for structuring how a task moves from description to a mergeable change: local CLI sessions for interactive work, and cloud or web sessions for longer-running or delegated tasks that continue running after you step away.

In plain terms

It is like the difference between working alongside a contractor in person versus handing them a work order and checking back later once the job is done.

How it works

In practice it comes down to using the CLI for interactive, supervised sessions where you review each step, and cloud sessions for tasks that can run unattended in an isolated environment and return a diff and test results when complete, choosing based on task length and how much supervision it needs.

A concrete example

A quick, well-understood bug fix runs interactively in the CLI so the developer can review each step, while a larger refactor spanning many files is delegated to a cloud session that runs overnight and returns a complete diff and test output in the morning.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Interactive CLI sessions give tighter, real-time oversight but require the developer's attention throughout, while cloud sessions free up the developer's time but mean more change accumulates before the first human review point

Try it in code

# Interactive (CLI) — supervised, step by step
codex "fix the failing test in checkout.test.ts"

# Delegated (cloud) — runs unattended, returns a diff to review
codex cloud run "refactor the payments module for the new API, run
tests, open a PR with results" --repo org/payments-service

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real task, decide whether it fits an interactive CLI session or a delegated cloud session, and justify the choice based on task length and risk.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Delegating a task to a cloud session does not mean the review bar can be lower; the diff still needs the same scrutiny before merging, regardless of whether a human watched it happen step by step or reviewed it after the fact.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Agent workflows?

  • Once Agent workflows is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Delegating a task to a cloud session does not mean the review bar can be lower; the diff still needs the same scrutiny before merging, regardless of whether a human watched it happen step by step or reviewed it after the fact.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Agent workflows in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI Codex overview

Repository setup

Repository setup for Codex means adding an AGENTS.md file describing build commands, conventions, and constraints, following a hierarchical discovery order from global to project-specific, so Codex starts every session with consistent expectations.

In plain terms

It is like posting a job-site safety and procedure sheet that applies to every worker who shows up, with additional site-specific notes layered on top for a particular project.

How it works

In practice it comes down to a global AGENTS.md in the Codex home directory providing default conventions, project-level AGENTS.md files from the git root down to the working directory layering more specific rules, with closer files overriding earlier ones, and AGENTS.override.md files for temporary, higher-priority overrides.

A concrete example

A global AGENTS.md states a general preference for descriptive commit messages, while one specific repository's AGENTS.md adds that database migrations must always include a rollback script, and Codex respects both layered together.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Layered AGENTS.md files let teams share broad defaults while customizing for specific repositories, but the discovery and override order needs to be understood, or a repository-specific rule can be silently overridden by an unexpected global or override file

Try it in code

# ~/.codex/AGENTS.md (global defaults)
- Use descriptive commit messages

# repo-root/AGENTS.md (project-specific, layered on top)
- Test command: npm run test:ci
- Every migration must include a rollback script

# AGENTS.override.md (temporary, highest precedence)
- Do not touch billing/** this sprint

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a project-level AGENTS.md with a specific test command and one repository-specific constraint, and explain how it would combine with a hypothetical global AGENTS.md.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • AGENTS.md is not a single flat file; it resolves through a specific discovery and merge order across global, project, and override scopes, so debugging unexpected Codex behavior sometimes means checking which AGENTS.md files are actually being merged, not just the one in the current directory.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Repository setup?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Repository setup is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: AGENTS.md is not a single flat file; it resolves through a specific discovery and merge order across global, project, and override scopes, so debugging unexpected Codex behavior sometimes means checking which AGENTS.md files are actually being merged, not just the one in the current directory.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Repository setup in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Codex AGENTS.md convention

Testing

Testing with Codex means having it run the project's real test suite after making changes and iterate based on actual failures, closing the loop between generating a change and verifying it rather than trusting the diff alone.

In plain terms

It is like a contractor who tests the plumbing after a repair instead of assuming it's fixed because the pipe looks connected.

How it works

In practice it comes down to Codex running the repository's test command after an edit, reading the actual failure output, and revising the specific failing part rather than regenerating the whole change, within whatever sandbox mode allows command execution.

A concrete example

After adding a feature, Codex runs the test suite, sees one assertion fail, reads the stack trace, and adjusts the specific line causing the failure rather than rewriting the entire file.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Letting Codex run and iterate on tests autonomously speeds up development, but it requires workspace-write or higher sandbox access to actually execute commands, so this workflow needs a sandbox mode that permits it and a test suite thorough enough to catch real problems

Try it in code

codex --sandbox workspace-write \
  "add input validation to parseOrder(), run npm test, and fix any
  failures you introduce"
# Expected loop: edit -> run tests -> read failure -> fix -> re-run

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Give Codex a task with a deliberately incomplete test file, observe what bug slips through undetected, then add the missing test case and have it re-verify.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Codex reporting that tests pass is not proof of correctness beyond what those specific tests check; a weak or incomplete test suite lets a subtly wrong change pass just as easily as a strong one would catch it.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Testing?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Testing is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: Codex reporting that tests pass is not proof of correctness beyond what those specific tests check; a weak or incomplete test suite lets a subtly wrong change pass just as easily as a strong one would catch it.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Testing in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI Codex overview

Refactoring

Refactoring with Codex means using it to restructure code without changing behavior, verified by running the existing test suite before and after, with the task scoped to an explicit set of files to keep the diff reviewable.

In plain terms

It is like reorganizing a warehouse's shelving without moving any product to the wrong section, and checking inventory counts before and after to prove nothing was lost.

How it works

In practice it comes down to scoping the refactor to specific named files and a behavior-preserving goal, then having Codex make the change and run tests before and after to confirm output didn't shift, rather than issuing an open-ended cleanup request.

A concrete example

Asking Codex to extract duplicated validation logic from three named files into one shared function, with the exact test command to verify behavior, produces a smaller and more verifiable diff than asking it to clean up the codebase generally.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: A tightly scoped refactor request is easy to review and verify quickly, while an open-ended request produces a larger diff that's harder to check line by line and more likely to touch unrelated code

Try it in code

codex "extract the duplicated email-validation logic in signup.ts,
login.ts, and resetPassword.ts into validators.ts. Only touch these
four files. Run npm test before and after to confirm behavior is
unchanged."

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Pick one function with duplicated logic in a few places, give Codex a scoped refactor request naming the exact files and test command, and verify results are identical before and after.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • A refactor that passes existing tests is not automatically safe if those tests don't actually cover the affected behavior; the safety of a Codex-driven refactor is only as good as the test coverage verifying it.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Refactoring?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Once Refactoring is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: A refactor that passes existing tests is not automatically safe if those tests don't actually cover the affected behavior; the safety of a Codex-driven refactor is only as good as the test coverage verifying it.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Refactoring in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Codex AGENTS.md convention

Review

Codex's code review feature reads a pull request's diff on GitHub and generates review comments flagging likely bugs, missing edge cases, and convention violations, intended to give human reviewers a useful first pass rather than replace their approval.

In plain terms

It is like a thorough junior reviewer doing a first pass on every pull request so the senior reviewer's attention goes to judgment calls, not mechanical checks.

How it works

In practice it comes down to reading a pull request's diff in the context of the repository's conventions and AGENTS.md, and posting review comments on specific lines flagging potential bugs, missing tests, or risk areas, surfaced alongside human reviewer comments in the normal GitHub review flow.

A concrete example

On a pull request adding a new API endpoint, Codex's review flags that the new endpoint has no corresponding test file and that one input field lacks validation, before a human reviewer even opens the diff.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Automated review catches mechanical issues consistently and fast, but it can also produce false positives that erode reviewer trust if not tuned to the repository's actual conventions, and it can miss product-context judgment a human reviewer would catch

Try it in code

# Codex reviews a PR diff automatically on GitHub, posting comments like:
# "Line 42: new endpoint has no corresponding test file."
# "Line 58: 'email' field accepts input with no format validation."

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Take a real pull request, read Codex's review comments or simulate the review with a direct prompt, and judge how many were genuinely useful versus noise.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Codex's review comments appearing on a pull request, or being silent, is not equivalent to human approval; the human reviewer remains accountable for what actually merges, regardless of what the automated review flagged or missed.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Review?

  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Codex's review comments appearing on a pull request, or being silent, is not equivalent to human approval; the human reviewer remains accountable for what actually merges, regardless of what the automated review flagged or missed.
  • Once Review is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Review in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI Codex overview

Security

Security for Codex centers on sandbox mode, read-only, workspace-write, or danger-full-access, and approval policy, untrusted, on-request, or never, which together define exactly what the agent can touch and when it needs explicit human confirmation.

In plain terms

It is like choosing how much of a building a new contractor can access unsupervised, versus which rooms always require someone to unlock the door for them.

How it works

In practice it comes down to platform-native sandboxing, Seatbelt on macOS, native sandboxing or WSL2 on Windows, bubblewrap on Linux, enforcing filesystem and network boundaries according to the configured sandbox mode, combined with an approval policy that determines which actions still prompt for human confirmation even within that sandbox.

A concrete example

A team runs Codex in workspace-write mode with an on-request approval policy for anything touching files outside the project directory, so routine in-scope edits proceed autonomously while anything reaching outside the workspace still requires a human to confirm.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Looser sandbox modes and approval policies let Codex complete more of a task without interruption, but they increase the damage a manipulated or mistaken action can cause, so the configuration should be matched to the actual sensitivity of the repository and task

Try it in code

# Sandbox modes:  read-only | workspace-write | danger-full-access
# Approval policy: untrusted | on-request | never

codex --sandbox workspace-write --ask-for-approval on-request
# Routine in-scope edits proceed; anything outside the workspace
# or touching the network still prompts for confirmation.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: For one real repository, choose a sandbox mode and approval policy pairing, and identify one specific action that pairing would still block or require confirmation for.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Codex's sandboxing is not just a suggestion enforced by the model's good behavior; it's enforced at the operating-system level through platform-native mechanisms, which is a materially stronger guarantee than trusting the model to stay in scope on its own.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Security?

  • Correct answer: Codex's sandboxing is not just a suggestion enforced by the model's good behavior; it's enforced at the operating-system level through platform-native mechanisms, which is a materially stronger guarantee than trusting the model to stay in scope on its own.
  • Once Security is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Security in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Codex sandboxing

Automation

Automation with Codex means running it non-interactively as part of CI or a scheduled job, typically through cloud sessions, to perform a scoped task like dependency updates or issue triage without a human present for each step.

In plain terms

It is like a maintenance crew that runs a checklist overnight and leaves a report in the morning, rather than requiring someone to supervise every step live.

How it works

In practice it comes down to delegating a scoped, well-defined task to a Codex cloud session triggered by a schedule or an event such as a new issue, running in an isolated environment, and returning a diff, test results, or a report for human review rather than merging or acting autonomously.

A concrete example

A scheduled Codex session runs weekly to update outdated dependencies, run the test suite, and open a pull request summarizing what changed and any test failures, which a human then reviews before merging.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Automating well-scoped, lower-risk tasks frees up real developer time, but automation should stop short of autonomous merging or deployment for anything with real consequences, keeping a human approval gate on the actions that matter

Try it in code

# .github/workflows/weekly-deps.yml (excerpt)
- name: Codex dependency update
  run: |
    codex cloud run "update outdated dependencies, run tests, open
    a PR summarizing changes and any failures" --repo ${{ github.repository }}

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Design one automated Codex task for a real repository, such as a weekly dependency update, specifying exactly what it's allowed to do autonomously and what still requires human approval.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Running Codex automatically on a schedule does not mean its output can be trusted without review just because a human wasn't watching in real time; automated output still needs the same evaluation and spot-checking as any other automated system.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Automation?

  • Once Automation is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Running Codex automatically on a schedule does not mean its output can be trusted without review just because a human wasn't watching in real time; automated output still needs the same evaluation and spot-checking as any other automated system.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Automation in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • OpenAI Codex overview

Team workflow

Team workflow for Codex means establishing shared conventions, a checked-in AGENTS.md, and consistent review standards so every developer's use of the tool produces predictable, reviewable results rather than each person developing incompatible habits.

In plain terms

It is like a shared style guide and toolset for a team, rather than every engineer building their own private, inconsistent version.

How it works

In practice it comes down to committing a shared AGENTS.md with team conventions and constraints to source control, agreeing on which sandbox modes are appropriate for which kinds of tasks, and treating Codex-assisted pull requests with the same review standards as any other contribution.

A concrete example

A team's shared AGENTS.md specifies the exact test command, a rule against modifying generated files, and a note that any change touching authentication requires a named human reviewer, so every developer's Codex sessions start from the same expectations.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Shared, versioned conventions improve consistency and onboarding speed across a team, but they require an owner and periodic review, the same as any other piece of shared documentation, or they drift out of date

Try it in code

# Shared repo-root/AGENTS.md (checked into source control)
- Test command: npm run test:ci
- Never modify files under /generated
- Changes touching auth/** require a named human reviewer

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write one team-wide AGENTS.md rule for a real repository that would prevent a specific mistake you've seen happen, and explain why it belongs at the team level rather than left to individual discretion.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Team AGENTS.md conventions are not a one-time setup task; like a README, they need the same maintenance and review as the code they describe, or they start giving every developer's Codex sessions outdated guidance.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Team workflow?

  • Correct answer: Team AGENTS.md conventions are not a one-time setup task; like a README, they need the same maintenance and review as the code they describe, or they start giving every developer's Codex sessions outdated guidance.
  • Once Team workflow is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Team workflow in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Codex AGENTS.md convention

Best practices

Best practices for Codex combine scoped prompting, an up-to-date AGENTS.md, sandbox and approval settings matched to task risk, and mandatory test verification into a consistent habit, so output quality doesn't depend on any one developer's individual discipline.

In plain terms

It is like a shared safety checklist for operating powerful equipment, followed the same way by everyone regardless of experience level.

How it works

In practice it comes down to combining specific, scoped task prompts, a maintained AGENTS.md, sandbox and approval settings appropriate to the task's risk, and a requirement that tests pass before any Codex-assisted change is considered done.

A concrete example

A team's Codex best-practices checklist requires running the full test suite on any workspace-write session before requesting review, and treating an accepted Codex diff with the same scrutiny as hand-written code.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Codifying and enforcing best practices takes real effort to write down and socialize, but without them, usage quality varies wildly between developers, which is harder to fix after problematic code has already merged

Try it in code

const checklist = [
  "scope every prompt to specific files when the task allows it",
  "run the full test suite before requesting review",
  "review an accepted Codex diff like hand-written code",
  "keep AGENTS.md in sync with real repo conventions"
];

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write a five-item Codex best-practices checklist for a real team, focused specifically on what changes about review and verification, not what changes about writing code.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • Best practices for Codex are not fundamentally different from best practices for any code contribution; the review bar should not lower just because a change came from an agent instead of a person typing it directly.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Best practices?

  • Once Best practices is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.
  • Correct answer: Best practices for Codex are not fundamentally different from best practices for any code contribution; the review bar should not lower just because a change came from an agent instead of a person typing it directly.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Best practices in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Codex sandboxing

Troubleshooting

Troubleshooting Codex means diagnosing whether an unexpected result comes from insufficient repository context, a sandbox or approval setting blocking an action, or a genuinely wrong plan, and applying the fix that matches the actual cause rather than just rewording the prompt.

In plain terms

It is like debugging why a contractor didn't finish a job, checking whether they were missing a key, blocked by a locked door, or simply made a wrong judgment call, before assuming they're incompetent.

How it works

In practice it comes down to checking whether the relevant file or convention was actually discoverable through AGENTS.md, whether the sandbox mode or approval policy silently blocked or altered an intended action, or whether the plan itself was wrong given correct context and permissions, and fixing the specific layer responsible.

A concrete example

A developer confused why Codex won't run a specific command discovers the sandbox mode was set to read-only, which no amount of prompt rewording would fix, since the block is enforced below the model layer.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Rewording a prompt is the fastest thing to try but only fixes prompt-caused problems, while checking AGENTS.md discovery and sandbox configuration takes longer but is necessary when the cause isn't the prompt at all

Try it in code

# "Codex won't run this command" — check before rewording the prompt:
codex sandbox status        # is sandbox mode set to read-only?
codex agents status         # is the expected AGENTS.md being discovered?
# Rewording only helps if the cause was genuinely a bad prompt.

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Next time Codex produces an unexpected or incomplete result, check the sandbox mode and AGENTS.md discovery before concluding the model reasoned incorrectly.

  1. Build or configure it, using the code example above as your starting point.
  2. Test it against one normal case and one edge case, and write down both actual outputs.
  3. Note what you would log, and which single metric would reveal this breaking in production.

Done when: the edge case is handled correctly or fails safely, not just the normal case.

Common mistakes

  • An unexpectedly blocked or incomplete Codex action is not always a sign the model misunderstood the task; sandbox mode and approval policy commonly block actions silently in ways that look like a model failure but are actually working as configured.

Check yourself

Which of these is sound guidance about Troubleshooting?

  • Once Troubleshooting is configured correctly at launch, it will keep behaving the same way as the product and its data evolve.
  • Correct answer: An unexpectedly blocked or incomplete Codex action is not always a sign the model misunderstood the task; sandbox mode and approval policy commonly block actions silently in ways that look like a model failure but are actually working as configured.
  • If early outputs look plausible, the approach is working and deeper evaluation can safely be skipped.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Troubleshooting in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. Which trade-off in this chapter would you defend in a design review, and what evidence would change your mind?
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Codex sandboxing

Codex lab

The Codex lab is a hands-on exercise to run one real, moderately complex task through Codex end to end, from a scoped prompt through test verification, then reflect on what sandbox, approval, or AGENTS.md configuration would improve the next run.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to picking one real repository task, writing a scoped AGENTS.md and prompt, running it with a chosen sandbox mode, verifying with tests, and writing down what needed human correction and why.

It is like a supervised trial run that ends with a debrief on what to configure differently next time, not just whether the task succeeded.

The brief

Run one real, non-trivial task through Codex including test verification, and write down what AGENTS.md rule or sandbox setting would have prevented the correction you had to make.

A worked reference

A lab might take a real open issue, write a project AGENTS.md with the test command and one constraint, run the task in workspace-write mode, verify with tests, and note one thing that needed manual correction.

Starting point in code

# Lab: run one real issue through the full loop
codex --sandbox workspace-write \
  "read issue #142, propose a plan, implement it, and run the test
  suite. Stop and ask before touching any file outside src/orders/."
# Afterward: what needed correction, and what AGENTS.md rule would
# have prevented it?

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: Using a real, messy task teaches more about the tool's actual limits than a clean toy example, but it takes longer and can surface unrelated repository issues that distract from the learning goal

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • The lab's goal is not a perfect diff on the first attempt; it's identifying exactly where you had to intervene and why, since that's the judgment production usage of an agentic tool actually requires.

Going deeper

References

  • Codex AGENTS.md convention

Mini project

The mini project ships one small, complete Codex workflow artifact for a real repository, such as an AGENTS.md plus a documented review checklist, that a teammate could adopt directly.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining a project AGENTS.md with real conventions and constraints, a chosen sandbox and approval policy with a stated reason, and a short review checklist into one reusable, documented setup.

It is like handing off a fully labeled starter kit instead of a working setup only you know how to reproduce.

The brief

Write an AGENTS.md and a short review checklist for a real repository, then have a teammate use both without additional explanation.

A worked reference

A mini project might produce an AGENTS.md for a real repository plus a one-page checklist covering when to use workspace-write versus read-only mode and what always needs human review.

Starting point in code

# AGENTS.md + review-checklist.md for a real repo
Test command: npm run test:ci
Sandbox default: workspace-write, on-request approval
Always human-reviewed: anything touching auth/** or billing/**

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A narrowly scoped, well-documented artifact is more likely to actually get adopted by a team than an ambitious, unfinished framework

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • A working AGENTS.md on your own machine is not the same as a shareable mini project; if a teammate can't use it without your explanation, the work isn't finished.

Going deeper

References

  • Codex AGENTS.md convention

Capstone

The capstone designs a complete operating model for using Codex across a real engineering team: which tasks run autonomously versus require review, what sandbox and approval settings apply by default, shared AGENTS.md standards, and the metrics that prove it's actually helping.

What you're building, and why

In practice it comes down to combining task-scope rules for autonomous versus human-reviewed work, default sandbox and approval settings matched to repository risk, a shared and maintained AGENTS.md, and adoption-plus-outcome metrics into one documented operating model.

It is like writing the operating manual for a new automated capability before rolling it out organization-wide, not after something goes wrong.

The brief

Write a one-page Codex operating model for a real team: autonomous task scope, default sandbox and approval settings, and one metric that would show the rollout is working.

A worked reference

A capstone might specify that Codex can autonomously fix lint failures and update dependencies in workspace-write mode, but any change touching billing or auth requires read-only mode and a named human reviewer, with monthly reporting on merged-without-rework rate.

Starting point in code

const operatingModel = {
  autonomous: ["fix lint failures", "update dependencies"],
  needsReview: ["auth changes", "billing changes"],
  defaultSandbox: "workspace-write",
  defaultApproval: "on-request",
  metric: "% PRs merged without rework"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Trade-offs to document

  • The core tension: A comprehensive operating model takes real design effort, but it prevents the common failure of ad hoc, inconsistent usage with no visibility into whether it's actually improving delivery or just increasing risk

Definition of done

Watch out for

  • Rolling out an agentic coding tool is not complete once it's installed; without agreed scope, default safety settings, and success metrics, usage quality and risk both become invisible and inconsistent across the team.

Going deeper

References

  • Codex sandboxing
  • Codex AGENTS.md convention

Interview pack

The Codex interview pack prepares you to discuss agentic coding tool adoption credibly: sandbox and approval configuration, AGENTS.md design, and rollout risk management, backed by a concrete story rather than a feature list.

In plain terms

It is like preparing to explain a real safety-configuration decision and its outcome, not just naming that the tool exists.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR-style stories about a real Codex rollout or incident you were part of, paired with technical answers distinguishing sandbox modes, approval policies, and AGENTS.md discovery order.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how would you evaluate whether to give this agent more autonomy describes a specific sandbox and approval configuration, a staged rollout, and a concrete metric that justified the change, not just general enthusiasm for agentic coding.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Try it in code

const answer = {
  situation: "Agent had danger-full-access with no approval gate",
  risk: "a wrong autonomous action could reach main unreviewed",
  fix: "moved to workspace-write + on-request approval, staged rollout",
  result: "zero unreviewed high-risk changes in the following quarter"
};

Change the inputs and watch which assumption breaks first. The gap between what you expected and what you got is where the learning is.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a time an agentic coding tool's configuration, sandbox, approval policy, or instructions file, prevented or failed to prevent a real problem, and what you changed afterward.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that Codex has sandbox modes and an AGENTS.md convention is not the same as being able to justify which settings fit a specific real repository's risk profile; interviewers probe for that applied judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Knowing that Codex has sandbox modes and an AGENTS.md convention is not the same as being able to justify which settings fit a specific real repository's risk profile; interviewers probe for that applied judgment specifically.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Explain Interview pack in two minutes to a senior engineer who has not shipped AI systems.
  2. You must pick a side of "Breadth vs depth" for a real product. Defend your choice, and name the signal that would make you reverse it.
  3. What breaks first in production, and how would you detect it before users do?

References

  • Codex sandboxing

Interview Preparation

Track outcomes

By the end of this track, you should be able to reason from first principles, design the architecture, identify failure modes, build a working slice, and explain how the system behaves in production.

In plain words: Interview preparation is like training for a match: you practice fundamentals, drills, real scenarios, and post-game review. AI engineering interviews test both concepts and judgment: explaining trade-offs matters as much as definitions.

Interview strategy

Interview strategy is the plan for showing the right evidence of skill across behavioral, coding, system design, AI depth, and product judgment rounds.

In plain terms

It is like planning a portfolio review: each story and answer should prove a different capability.

How it works

In practice it comes down to role rubric, story bank, technical themes, mock practice, gap analysis, and follow-up preparation.

A concrete example

A candidate maps one project to ownership, one to conflict, one to system design, and one to AI evaluation depth.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create a preparation matrix with interview type, signal, story, and practice score.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Do not memorize scripts; prepare structures and evidence.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Do not memorize scripts; prepare structures and evidence.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for Interview strategy using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?

References

  • Amazon STAR interview method
  • Google interviewing guide
  • GitHub engineering career resources

Behavioral

Behavioral interview preparation for AI roles means having concrete, structured stories about your actual decisions, actions, and outcomes, using the STAR structure, situation, task, action, result, ready to answer questions about ownership, conflict, and judgment under pressure.

In plain terms

It is like an incident review that walks through what happened, what you personally decided, and what you'd do differently, rather than a general summary of what the team accomplished.

How it works

In practice it comes down to preparing a small set of specific stories in advance, each built around situation, task, action, and result, focusing the majority of the answer on your specific actions and decisions rather than team context, and preparing for likely follow-up questions about what you'd do differently.

A concrete example

A strong behavioral answer about handling conflict briefly sets the context in one sentence, then spends most of the answer on the specific disagreement, what evidence you gathered, the decision you made, and closes with a measurable result and what you learned.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Hands-on lab

Task: Write one STAR story about a real conflict or difficult decision you were part of, keeping the situation and task to two sentences so the action and result get the most attention.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • A behavioral answer is not a biography or a team accomplishment summary; interviewers are specifically listening for your personal ownership, decisions, and judgment, which gets diluted if the story stays too general or credits the team for everything.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • Correct answer: A behavioral answer is not a biography or a team accomplishment summary; interviewers are specifically listening for your personal ownership, decisions, and judgment, which gets diluted if the story stays too general or credits the team for everything.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for Behavioral using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?

References

  • Amazon STAR interview method
  • Google interviewing guide
  • GitHub engineering career resources

System design

System design interviews evaluate how you turn ambiguous product goals into scalable, reliable, safe architecture with clear trade-offs.

In plain terms

It is like whiteboarding a city plan while explaining why roads, utilities, and emergency routes go where they do.

How it works

In practice it comes down to requirements, traffic estimates, APIs, data model, components, bottlenecks, failure modes, and trade-offs.

A concrete example

For an AI support assistant, define users, documents, retrieval, model gateway, guardrails, evals, and observability.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Going deep shows expertise but too much detail can lose the interviewer if the frame is unclear.

Hands-on lab

Task: Practice a 35-minute AI system design with requirements, architecture, bottlenecks, and risks.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Do not start drawing boxes before clarifying requirements and success metrics.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • Correct answer: Do not start drawing boxes before clarifying requirements and success metrics.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for System design using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?

  • Amazon STAR interview method
  • Google interviewing guide
  • GitHub engineering career resources

Coding

Coding interviews evaluate problem decomposition, correctness, complexity, communication, testing, and debugging under time pressure.

In plain terms

They are like pair-programming on a small but revealing problem.

How it works

In practice it comes down to problem restatement, examples, algorithm choice, edge cases, complexity, implementation, and tests.

A concrete example

A strong answer explains the invariant, codes incrementally, tests edge cases, and fixes bugs out loud.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Optimized solutions impress only after correctness is clear.

Hands-on lab

Task: Solve one problem and write the examples, edge cases, complexity, and tests before coding.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Do not go silent while coding; communication is part of the signal.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • Correct answer: Do not go silent while coding; communication is part of the signal.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for Coding using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?

LLM internals

LLM internals interviews test whether you understand how tokens, embeddings, attention, training, decoding, and inference constraints shape product behavior.

In plain terms

It is like explaining the engine enough to diagnose why the car behaves strangely.

How it works

In practice it comes down to tokenization, embeddings, transformer blocks, training objectives, context windows, decoding, and serving constraints.

A concrete example

A good answer connects hallucination risk to pretraining objective, retrieval, decoding, and evaluation rather than blaming 'the model'.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Depth helps but only if tied to product consequences.

Hands-on lab

Task: Explain KV cache, temperature, and RAG grounding in two minutes each.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Do not recite transformer math without explaining operational impact.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • Correct answer: Do not recite transformer math without explaining operational impact.

Going deeper

  1. Give a two-minute answer for LLM internals using a clear structure.

Agents

Agent interview questions test whether you understand agentic system design, task decomposition, tool use, failure handling, and safe autonomy, not just that you know agents can use tools and take multiple steps.

In plain terms

It is like being asked not just what an autopilot does, but how you'd decide when it should hand control back to a human pilot.

How it works

In practice it comes down to preparing to explain a real or realistic agent system's task decomposition, how it coordinates steps, what happens when a tool call fails, and where human checkpoints are placed, backed by specific reasoning rather than a general description of agent capabilities.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how would you design an agent for this task describes specific subtask boundaries, a defined step or cost budget, a failure-handling path, and a human checkpoint before any high-risk action, not just that the agent would use tools to complete the task.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Describing an agent's exciting capabilities is easy and quick; interviewers give far more credit for describing concrete safety, cost, and failure-handling decisions, which takes more preparation to have ready

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a real or realistic agent system, focusing on how you decomposed the task, handled a tool failure, and where you placed a human checkpoint.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that agents can plan, act, and observe is not the same as being able to design specific task decomposition, failure handling, and safety checkpoints for a described scenario; interviewers probe for that applied design judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • Correct answer: Knowing that agents can plan, act, and observe is not the same as being able to design specific task decomposition, failure handling, and safety checkpoints for a described scenario; interviewers probe for that applied design judgment specifically.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for Agents using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?

RAG

RAG interview questions test your understanding of chunking, retrieval, ranking, and grounding trade-offs, not just that you know retrieval-augmented generation retrieves documents before generating an answer.

In plain terms

It is like being asked not just that a research assistant uses source material, but how you'd decide which sources to trust and how to handle contradictory ones.

How it works

In practice it comes down to preparing to explain a real or realistic RAG system's chunking strategy, retrieval and ranking approach, citation and grounding design, and evaluation methodology, backed by specific reasoning tied to a concrete use case.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how would you build a RAG system for this product names a specific chunking approach with a reason, describes a citation and grounding-check design, and proactively raises how you'd evaluate faithfulness without being asked.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Saying a system uses RAG sounds knowledgeable but proves little on its own; interviewers give far more credit for describing concrete chunking, ranking, and faithfulness-evaluation decisions from a real or realistic system

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story or design walkthrough for a RAG system, covering your chunking strategy, retrieval approach, and how you'd evaluate whether answers are actually faithful to retrieved evidence.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that RAG retrieves documents before generating an answer is not the same as understanding how to design chunking, ranking, and faithfulness evaluation for a specific use case; interviewers probe for that applied judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • Correct answer: Knowing that RAG retrieves documents before generating an answer is not the same as understanding how to design chunking, ranking, and faithfulness evaluation for a specific use case; interviewers probe for that applied judgment specifically.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for RAG using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?

Security

Security interview questions for AI roles test whether you can identify and defend against LLM-specific threats, prompt injection, data leakage, excessive tool permissions, not just general software security knowledge.

In plain terms

It is like being asked not just that locks exist, but which specific lock you'd choose for which specific door given the actual threat.

How it works

In practice it comes down to preparing to name specific attack categories relevant to a described AI system, such as prompt injection through retrieved content or excessive agent tool permissions, and describing a specific, tested defense for each rather than a general statement that security matters.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how would you secure this agentic coding assistant names specific attack categories like prompt injection and excessive permissions, describes a tested defense for each, and proactively raises an ongoing testing cadence without being asked.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Naming AI security risks like prompt injection sounds knowledgeable but proves little; interviewers give far more credit for describing a specific tested attack and verified defense from a real or realistic system

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about an AI security vulnerability you found or would anticipate in a described system, how you'd test for it, and the specific defense you'd implement.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that prompt injection and data leakage are risks is not the same as knowing how to test for and defend against them in a specific described system; interviewers probe for that applied, tested judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Knowing that prompt injection and data leakage are risks is not the same as knowing how to test for and defend against them in a specific described system; interviewers probe for that applied, tested judgment specifically.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for Security using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?

  • Google interviewing guide
  • GitHub engineering career resources

Architecture

Architecture interview questions test your ability to design a complete AI system, connecting the application, model or model gateway, retrieval, guardrails, and observability into one coherent design, not just describing which model API to call.

In plain terms

It is like being asked not just which engine to use, but how to design the entire vehicle around it, chassis, safety systems, and controls included.

How it works

In practice it comes down to preparing to sketch a request's full flow through a real or realistic AI system, from user input through retrieval or tool calls, model invocation, guardrail checks, and back to the user, with clear reasoning for each component's boundary and role.

A concrete example

A strong answer to design an AI system for this product sketches the full request flow, names specific components like a guardrail layer and an observability step, and explains why each boundary is where it is, not just which model would be called.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Describing a single model call is quick but shallow; interviewers give far more credit for architecting the complete system, including the parts that determine reliability and safety, which takes more structured thinking to lay out

Hands-on lab

Task: Practice sketching a complete request flow for one real or hypothetical AI product, naming every major component from user input to final response.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • An AI system's architecture is not just which model API you call; the retrieval, guardrail, and observability components around that call are what interviewers are actually probing for when they ask about architecture.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: An AI system's architecture is not just which model API you call; the retrieval, guardrail, and observability components around that call are what interviewers are actually probing for when they ask about architecture.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for Architecture using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?

  • Google interviewing guide

Leadership

Leadership interviews evaluate ownership, influence, conflict handling, judgment, and ability to improve teams and systems.

In plain terms

They are like incident reviews for your professional decisions.

How it works

In practice it comes down to STAR stories, scope, stakeholders, constraints, decisions, outcomes, reflection, and follow-up depth.

A concrete example

A strong story names a hard trade-off, what you personally did, measurable impact, and what changed afterward.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare three leadership stories: conflict, failure, and ambiguous ownership.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Do not answer only with team accomplishments; interviewers need your contribution.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • Correct answer: Do not answer only with team accomplishments; interviewers need your contribution.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.

Cloud

Cloud interview questions for AI roles test your ability to justify specific service choices, compute, storage, identity, for a real deployment, not just name AWS or Azure services you're aware of.

In plain terms

It is like being asked not just which brands of tools exist in a hardware store, but which specific tool you'd actually pick for a specific job and why.

How it works

In practice it comes down to preparing to justify specific compute, storage, and identity choices for a described AI deployment scenario, tied to real constraints like latency, cost, or compliance, rather than listing service names without reasoning.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how would you deploy this RAG system on AWS names a specific compute choice with a reason, a specific retrieval store with a reason, and proactively raises IAM scoping and cost controls without being asked.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Naming many cloud services sounds impressive but proves little; interviewers give far more credit for justified trade-offs between two specific services tied to real constraints

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story or design walkthrough justifying a specific cloud compute and storage choice for a real or hypothetical AI deployment, including one identity or cost control decision.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing that Bedrock, OpenSearch, and IAM exist is not the same as being able to justify which one fits a specific real workload; interviewers probe for that applied judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Knowing that Bedrock, OpenSearch, and IAM exist is not the same as being able to justify which one fits a specific real workload; interviewers probe for that applied judgment specifically.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for Cloud using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?
