Prompt engineering

Prompt engineering interview questions test your ability to design, test, and version prompts reliably in production, not just that you know techniques like few-shot examples or chain-of-thought.

In plain terms

It is like being asked not just that recipes exist, but how you'd test and version a recipe used across an entire restaurant chain.

How it works

In practice it comes down to preparing to describe a specific prompt's structure, a labeled test set covering edge cases, and a versioning process that lets a regression be rolled back, tied to a real prompt you've built or maintained.

A concrete example

A strong answer to how would you make sure this prompt doesn't break describes a specific labeled test set, a structured output format, and a versioning process that lets you roll back a regression, not just general enthusiasm for prompt engineering.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Describing prompting tricks in the abstract is easy; interviewers give far more credit for describing a concrete test set, structure decision, or versioning process from a real prompt you've maintained

Hands-on lab

Task: Prepare one story about a prompt regression you encountered or would anticipate, its root cause, and the specific fix, whether a test set gap, a structure problem, or a missing versioning process.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Knowing prompting techniques like few-shot examples or chain-of-thought is not the same as understanding how to test, structure, and version a prompt reliably in production; interviewers probe for that applied engineering judgment specifically.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • Correct answer: Knowing prompting techniques like few-shot examples or chain-of-thought is not the same as understanding how to test, structure, and version a prompt reliably in production; interviewers probe for that applied engineering judgment specifically.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for Prompt engineering using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?

References

  • Amazon STAR interview method
  • Google interviewing guide
  • GitHub engineering career resources

Mock interviews

Mock interviews simulate real interview pressure so you can measure and improve structure, correctness, timing, and recovery from follow-ups.

In plain terms

They are like sparring before a match.

How it works

In practice it comes down to realistic prompts, timing, interviewer interruptions, rubric scoring, recording, and rewrite cycles.

A concrete example

A mock system-design round reveals that your architecture is strong but requirements framing takes too long.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Hands-on lab

Task: Run one timed mock, score it, and rewrite the weakest five minutes.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Do not only practice with friendly prompts you already know.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • Correct answer: Do not only practice with friendly prompts you already know.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for Mock interviews using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?

References

  • Amazon STAR interview method
  • Google interviewing guide
  • GitHub engineering career resources

Interview lab

The interview lab is a hands-on exercise to answer one real technical interview question out loud under a time limit, record it, and score your own answer against a rubric before rewriting the weakest part.

In plain terms

It is like a sparring session before a real match, where the value comes from honest self-scoring and correction, not just going through the motions.

How it works

In practice it comes down to picking one real interview question, answering it out loud within a set time limit, recording the answer, scoring it against a rubric for structure, correctness, and trade-off judgment, and rewriting the weakest scoring section.

A concrete example

A lab might answer a system-design question in 15 minutes, record it, score it against a rubric covering requirements clarification, architecture, and trade-offs, and find that trade-off discussion was the weakest part, then rewrite that section.

Trade-offs

  • Breadth vs depth: Cover enough examples to show range, but go deep enough that interviewers can see your judgment.
  • Humility vs ownership: A strong answer acknowledges team context while clearly naming your own decisions.
  • Polish vs authenticity: Prepared stories help, but over-scripted answers sound evasive under follow-up questions.

Hands-on lab

Task: Answer one real interview question out loud within a time limit, record it, score it against a rubric, and rewrite the weakest-scoring part.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Practicing an answer in your head is not the same as timed, recorded practice with honest scoring; the specific weaknesses that show up under real time pressure and structured scoring often aren't visible until you actually do it.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Practicing an answer in your head is not the same as timed, recorded practice with honest scoring; the specific weaknesses that show up under real time pressure and structured scoring often aren't visible until you actually do it.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for Interview lab using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?

References

  • Amazon STAR interview method
  • Google interviewing guide
  • GitHub engineering career resources

Question bank A

Question bank A is a curated first set of realistic AI engineering interview questions spanning system design, coding, and applied reasoning, meant to be practiced with timed, structured answers rather than passively read.

In plain terms

It is like a set of practice exam questions meant to be worked through under real conditions, not just skimmed the night before.

How it works

In practice it comes down to collecting realistic questions representative of what's actually asked in AI engineering interviews, organized so they can be practiced one at a time with a timed, structured answer and self-scoring against a rubric, rather than read through as reference material.

A concrete example

Question bank A might include a system-design question about building a RAG-based support assistant, a coding question about implementing retry logic with backoff, and an applied-reasoning question about diagnosing a model that's overfitting.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Practicing a curated question bank under real timed conditions takes more discipline than reading through questions and their ideal answers passively, but it's what actually builds the ability to perform under real interview pressure

Hands-on lab

Task: Pick three questions from a real or assembled question bank, answer each under a time limit, and score your answers against a rubric.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Reading through a list of interview questions and their ideal answers is not the same as being able to produce a strong answer yourself under time pressure; the value of a question bank comes from active, timed practice, not passive review.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • Correct answer: Reading through a list of interview questions and their ideal answers is not the same as being able to produce a strong answer yourself under time pressure; the value of a question bank comes from active, timed practice, not passive review.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for Question bank A using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?

References

  • Amazon STAR interview method
  • Google interviewing guide
  • GitHub engineering career resources

Question bank B

Question bank B is a second curated set of realistic AI engineering interview questions covering behavioral, architecture, and safety-and-security topics, complementing question bank A's more technical focus, meant for the same active, timed practice.

In plain terms

It is like a second practice exam covering a different but equally important part of the syllabus, judgment and communication rather than pure technical execution.

How it works

In practice it comes down to collecting realistic behavioral, architecture, and security-focused questions, organized for the same timed, structured, self-scored practice approach as question bank A, so preparation covers both deep technical reasoning and judgment-focused scenarios.

A concrete example

Question bank B might include a behavioral question about a time you disagreed with a technical decision, an architecture question about designing a guardrail layer, and a security question about defending against prompt injection in a specific described system.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Covering behavioral and security topics with the same rigor as technical questions takes more preparation time, but skipping this half of preparation leaves a real gap, since most real interview loops include both technical and judgment-focused rounds

Hands-on lab

Task: Pick two questions from a real or assembled question bank covering behavioral and security topics, answer each under a time limit, and score your answers against a rubric.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Technical preparation alone is not sufficient interview preparation; behavioral and security-judgment questions are a standard part of most real interview loops and need the same deliberate, timed practice as system design or coding questions.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • Correct answer: Technical preparation alone is not sufficient interview preparation; behavioral and security-judgment questions are a standard part of most real interview loops and need the same deliberate, timed practice as system design or coding questions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for Question bank B using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?

  • Amazon STAR interview method
  • Google interviewing guide
  • GitHub engineering career resources

Evaluation rubric

An evaluation rubric defines the criteria used to score an interview answer, AI output, or engineering artifact consistently.

In plain terms

It is like a grading sheet that turns vague feedback into actionable dimensions.

How it works

In practice it comes down to criteria, levels, examples, weights, calibration, and feedback notes.

A concrete example

A system-design rubric scores requirements, architecture, trade-offs, reliability, safety, and communication.

Trade-offs

  • The core tension: Rubrics improve consistency but can oversimplify nuanced judgment.

Hands-on lab

Task: Create a rubric for a behavioral answer with four scoring dimensions.

  1. Draft the answer with situation, task, action, and result kept separate.
  2. Add two measurable outcomes: latency reduced, cost saved, incidents avoided, revenue protected, or team impact.
  3. Write one likely follow-up question and answer it without changing the underlying facts.

Done when: you can deliver the answer in under two minutes and the follow-up answer doesn't contradict the original story.

Common mistakes

  • Do not use a rubric without examples of strong, medium, and weak answers.

Check yourself

Which of these is true about strong interview answers?

  • Correct answer: Do not use a rubric without examples of strong, medium, and weak answers.
  • If you know the material deeply, structure doesn't matter — depth will come across on its own.
  • The best answers cover every detail of the project so the interviewer has full context before you get to your decisions.

Going deeper

Questions interviewers ask

  1. Give a two-minute answer for Evaluation rubric using a clear structure.
  2. What follow-up question would expose whether your answer is shallow?
  3. What would you cut if the interviewer asked for a shorter answer?

  • Google interviewing guide
  • GitHub engineering career resources

Glossary

Abstention — An AI system explicitly declining to answer, or flagging low confidence, rather than generating an unsupported guess.

Activation function — A nonlinear function applied to a neuron's weighted input sum, letting networks model more than linear relationships.

Agent loop — A repeated cycle where an AI system plans, acts, observes the result, and decides the next step.

AGENTS.md — A convention for repository instructions read by coding agents such as Codex, layered from global to project-specific scope.

Approval policy — A configured rule for which agent actions require explicit human confirmation before executing.

Attention — A mechanism that lets a model weigh how relevant every other token is when processing a given token.

Autoregressive generation — Producing output one token or element at a time, each conditioned on everything generated so far.

Backpropagation — The algorithm that computes gradients through a neural network so parameters can be updated during training.

Batch normalization — A technique that rescales intermediate activations to stabilize and speed up deep network training.

Bias (model) — A systematic skew in a model's predictions that produces different outcomes across groups, usually inherited from training data.

BM25 — A lexical ranking function commonly used in search systems and hybrid RAG retrieval.

Canary release — Rolling out a change to a small percentage of traffic first, expanding only after it proves safe.

Circuit breaker — A safeguard that stops calling a failing dependency after repeated errors, instead of retrying indefinitely.

CLAUDE.md — A project memory file Claude Code reads at the start of every session for conventions, commands, and constraints.

Context compression — Summarizing or trimming conversation history to fit within a model's context window without losing key information.

Context window — The maximum input and output token span a model can process in one request.

Diffusion model — A generative model that produces content by iteratively removing noise from a random starting point.

Embedding — A dense vector representation that captures semantic similarity for search, clustering, and retrieval.

Episodic memory — Stored records of specific past events or interactions, retrievable by time, topic, or outcome.

EU AI Act — A risk-based EU regulatory framework that classifies AI systems into risk tiers with obligations scaled to each tier.

Evaluation set (golden set) — A labeled set of representative queries and correct answers used to measure whether an AI system actually works.

Explainability — The ability to describe why an AI system produced a specific output in terms a human can understand and act on.

Faithfulness — Whether a generated claim is actually supported by the evidence or source it's attributed to.

Feature engineering — Selecting or transforming raw data into the specific signals a model actually learns from.

Fine-tuning — Adapting an existing pretrained model to a narrower task or behavior, far cheaper than training from scratch.

Grounding — Constraining model output to explicit evidence, sources, policies, or tool results.

Guardrail — A runtime or design control that validates inputs, outputs, tool use, data access, or policy compliance.

Hallucination — A model output that is unsupported, fabricated, or inconsistent with available evidence.

HNSW — Hierarchical Navigable Small World, a graph-based approximate nearest-neighbor index used by most vector databases.

Human-in-the-loop — A design pattern where a person reviews or can override an AI decision before it takes effect.

Hybrid search — Retrieval that combines lexical matching such as BM25 with vector similarity search.

Idempotency — A property where calling an operation multiple times with the same input produces the same result, safe under retries.

Instruction tuning — Training a pretrained model on instruction-response examples so it follows user intent instead of just continuing text.

Jailbreak — A prompting technique intended to bypass a model's safety training or stated policy constraints.

KV cache — A transformer inference optimization that stores previous attention keys and values to avoid recomputation.

Latent space — The compressed internal representation a generative model uses before decoding it into full output.

Least privilege — Granting an agent, service, or user only the specific permissions required for its task, nothing broader.

LLM-as-judge — Using a language model to score or compare the outputs of another model against a rubric.

LoRA — A parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique that trains low-rank adapter weights instead of all model weights.

Managed identity — A cloud-native identity assigned to a compute resource so it can authenticate without an embedded credential.

MCP — Model Context Protocol, a protocol for connecting AI applications to tools, resources, and prompts through servers.

MoE — Mixture of Experts, an architecture that routes tokens to specialized subnetworks for efficient scale.

Multi-tenancy — Isolating one customer or team's data from another's within a shared system or index.

Multimodal system — A model or pipeline that processes or generates more than one content type, such as text, image, and audio, together.

Observability — The combination of metrics, logs, and traces that let a team understand a system's real-time behavior.

Overfitting — A model learning training data too precisely, including its noise, and performing worse on new, unseen data.

Positional encoding — Information added to token embeddings so a transformer knows the order of tokens in a sequence.

Prompt injection — An attack where untrusted content attempts to override developer or system instructions.

Quantization — Storing model weights with fewer bits to reduce memory and speed inference, usually with a small quality cost.

RAG — Retrieval-Augmented Generation, a pattern that retrieves external context before generating an answer.

Rate limiting — Capping how many requests a client or feature can make in a given time window to protect shared infrastructure.

Reasoning model — A model trained to produce an extended internal reasoning trace before its final answer, trading latency for accuracy.

Red teaming — Deliberately attempting to find an AI system's failure modes or vulnerabilities before real attackers do.

Re-ranking — A second-stage retrieval step that reorders candidate documents using a stronger relevance model.

Residual connection — A shortcut that adds a layer's input to its output, making very deep neural networks trainable.

RLHF — Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, a method for aligning model behavior with preference signals.

Sandbox mode — A configured boundary on what an agent's filesystem, network, or command access is allowed to touch.

Semantic memory — Durable facts about a user or domain stored independently of any single conversation.

SLO (service level objective) — A target reliability or latency threshold a team commits to for a production system.

Speculative decoding — An inference technique where a smaller draft model proposes tokens that a larger model verifies.

Structured output — Model output constrained to a schema so downstream software can validate and consume it safely.

Subagent — A delegated agent session with its own context window that returns a summarized result to the parent conversation.

Temperature — A sampling parameter that controls how random or deterministic a model's next-token choice is.

Tokenization — Splitting text into the discrete units, tokens, that a language model actually processes.

Tool schema — A structured, typed definition of a tool's name and parameters that lets a model generate a valid call.

Top-P — Nucleus sampling that selects from the smallest token set whose cumulative probability exceeds a threshold.

Working memory — The temporary scratchpad an agent uses during one task, discarded once the task completes.
